title: PhyGile: Physics-Prefix Guided Motion Generation for Agile General Humanoid Motion Tracking
summary: Humanoid robots are expected to execute agile and expressive whole-body motions in real-world settings. Existing text-to-motion generation models are predominantly trained on captured human motion datasets, whose priors assume human biomechanics, actuation, mass distribution, and contact strategies. When such motions are directly retargeted to humanoid robots, the resulting trajectories may satisfy geometric constraints (e.g., joint limits and pose continuity) and appear kinematically reasonable. However, they frequently violate the physical feasibility required for real-world execution. To address these issues, we present PhyGile, a unified framework that closes the loop between robot-native motion generation and General Motion Tracking (GMT). PhyGile performs physics-prefix-guided robot-native motion generation at inference time, directly generating robot-native motions in a 262-dimensional skeletal space with physics-guided prefixes, thereby eliminating inference-time retargeting artifacts and reducing generation-execution discrepancies. Before physics-prefix adaptation, we train the GMT controller with a curriculum-based mixture-of-experts scheme, followed by post-training on unlabeled motion data to improve robustness over large-scale robot motions. During physics-prefix adaptation, the GMT controller is further fine-tuned with generated objectives under physics-derived prefixes, enabling agile and stable execution of complex motions on real robots. Extensive offline and real-robot experiments demonstrate that PhyGile expands the frontier of text-driven humanoid control, enabling stable tracking of agile, highly difficult whole-body motions that go well beyond walking and low-dynamic motions typically achieved by prior methods.

AI.tldr: PhyGile introduces physics-prefix-guided, robot-native motion generation to close the loop with GMT, using a 262D skeletal space, to improve real-robot execution of agile whole-body motions without retargeting artifacts.
AI.motivation: Text-to-motion models trained on human data often yield trajectories that are geometrically plausible but physically infeasible for humanoid robots due to differences in biomechanics, actuation, and contact strategies. A physics-aware, robot-native generation approach is needed to ensure real-world feasibility.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: (1) train GMT controller with a curriculum-based mixture-of-experts and unlabeled data to enhance robustness; (2) physics-prefix adaptation where the GMT controller is fine-tuned with generated objectives under physics-derived prefixes. Inference-time generation occurs directly in a 262-dimensional skeletal space with physics prefixes, eliminating inference-time retargeting artifacts. The method is validated with extensive offline and real-robot experiments.
AI.result: Demonstrates stable tracking of agile, highly difficult whole-body motions on real humanoids, beyond walking and low-dynamic motions; reduces generation-execution discrepancies and eliminates retargeting artifacts, expanding the frontier of text-driven humanoid control.
AI.conclusion: Physics-prefix-guided, robot-native generation effectively aligns high-level control with robot dynamics, enabling stable execution of demanding motions and likely generalizing to other robot platforms and tasks.

title: VAMPO: Policy Optimization for Improving Visual Dynamics in Video Action Models
summary: Video action models are an appealing foundation for Vision--Language--Action systems because they can learn visual dynamics from large-scale video data and transfer this knowledge to downstream robot control. Yet current diffusion-based video predictors are trained with likelihood-surrogate objectives, which encourage globally plausible predictions without explicitly optimizing the precision-critical visual dynamics needed for manipulation. This objective mismatch often leads to subtle errors in object pose, spatial relations, and contact timing that can be amplified by downstream policies. We propose VAMPO, a post-training framework that directly improves visual dynamics in video action models through policy optimization. Our key idea is to formulate multi-step denoising as a sequential decision process and optimize the denoising policy with rewards defined over expert visual dynamics in latent space. To make this optimization practical, we introduce an Euler Hybrid sampler that injects stochasticity only at the first denoising step, enabling tractable low-variance policy-gradient estimation while preserving the coherence of the remaining denoising trajectory. We further combine this design with GRPO and a verifiable non-adversarial reward. Across diverse simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, VAMPO improves task-relevant visual dynamics, leading to better downstream action generation and stronger generalization. The homepage is https://vampo-robot.github.io/VAMPO/.

AI.tldr: VAMPO is a post-training framework that directly optimizes visual dynamics in diffusion-based video action models by framing multi-step denoising as a sequential decision process and using an Euler Hybrid sampler with policy-gradient optimization guided by expert latent-space dynamics; results show improved downstream action generation and generalization on sim and real tasks.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based video predictors trained with likelihood surrogates yield globally plausible predictions but fail to optimize precision-critical visual dynamics (pose, spatial relations, contact timing) needed for manipulation, leading to errors that can be amplified by downstream policies.
AI.method: Formulate multi-step denoising as a sequential decision process and optimize the denoising policy with rewards defined over expert visual dynamics in latent space. Introduce an Euler Hybrid sampler that injects stochasticity only at the first denoising step to enable tractable low-variance policy-gradient estimation while keeping the remaining trajectory coherent. Combine this with GRPO and a verifiable non-adversarial reward for post-training improvement.
AI.result: Across diverse simulated and real-world manipulation tasks, VAMPO improves task-relevant visual dynamics, yielding better downstream action generation and stronger generalization.
AI.conclusion: VAMPO offers a practical post-training approach to explicitly align diffusion-based video predictors with the visual dynamics critical for manipulation, improving control performance and generalization.

title: SOFTMAP: Sim2Real Soft Robot Forward Modeling via Topological Mesh Alignment and Physics Prior
summary: While soft robot manipulators offer compelling advantages over rigid counterparts, including inherent compliance, safe human-robot interaction, and the ability to conform to complex geometries, accurate forward modeling from low-dimensional actuation commands remains an open challenge due to nonlinear material phenomena such as hysteresis and manufacturing variability. We present SOFTMAP, a sim-to-real learning framework for real-time 3D forward modeling of tendon-actuated soft finger manipulators. SOFTMAP combines four components: (1) As-Rigid-As-Possible (ARAP)-based topological alignment that projects simulated and real point clouds into a shared, topologically consistent vertex space; (2) a lightweight MLP forward model pretrained on simulation data to map servo commands to full 3D finger geometry; (3) a residual correction network trained on a small set of real observations to predict per-vertex displacement fields that compensate for sim-to-real discrepancies; and (4) a closed-form linear actuation calibration layer enabling real-time inference at 30 FPS. We evaluate SOFTMAP on both simulated and physical hardware, achieving state-of-the-art shape prediction accuracy with a Chamfer distance of 0.389 mm in simulation and 3.786 mm on hardware, millimeter-level fingertip trajectory tracking across multiple target paths, and a 36.5% improvement in teleoperation task success over the baseline. Our results show that SOFTMAP provides a data-efficient approach for 3D forward modeling and control of soft manipulators.

AI.tldr: SOFTMAP is a sim-to-real learning framework for real-time 3D forward modeling of tendon-actuated soft finger manipulators, combining ARAP-based topological alignment, a simulation-trained lightweight MLP forecaster, a residual real-data correction network, and a closed-form calibration layer to enable 30 FPS inference; it achieves state-of-the-art shape prediction (Chamfer 0.389 mm in simulation, 3.786 mm on hardware), millimeter-level fingertip tracking, and a 36.5% teleoperation success gain.
AI.motivation: Accurate forward modeling of soft robots is hindered by nonlinear material effects (e.g., hysteresis) and manufacturing variability, making it challenging to map low-dimensional actuation commands to full 3D geometry in real time.
AI.method: Four-part approach: (1) ARAP-based topological alignment to align simulated and real point clouds in a consistent vertex space; (2) a lightweight MLP forward model trained on simulation data to predict full 3D finger geometry from servo commands; (3) a residual correction network trained on a small real-data set to predict per-vertex displacements to compensate sim-to-real discrepancies; (4) a closed-form linear actuation calibration layer enabling real-time inference at 30 Hz.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate 3D forward modeling with large improvements over baselines, achieving Chamfer distances of 0.389 mm (sim) and 3.786 mm (hardware), robust millimeter-level fingertip trajectories, and a 36.5% improvement in teleoperation task success, with data-efficient real-world adaptation.
AI.conclusion: SOFTMAP provides a data-efficient, real-time framework for 3D forward modeling and control of tendon-actuated soft manipulators, integrating geometry-aligned simulation, learned forward prediction, real-data residual correction, and fast calibration to enable practical deployment.

title: Speculative Policy Orchestration: A Latency-Resilient Framework for Cloud-Robotic Manipulation
summary:
Cloud robotics enables robots to offload high-dimensional motion planning and reasoning to remote servers. However, for continuous manipulation tasks requiring high-frequency control, network latency and jitter can severely destabilize the system, causing command starvation and unsafe physical execution.
  To address this, we propose Speculative Policy Orchestration (SPO), a latency-resilient cloud-edge framework. SPO utilizes a cloud-hosted world model to pre-compute and stream future kinematic waypoints to a local edge buffer, decoupling execution frequency from network round-trip time. To mitigate unsafe execution caused by predictive drift, the edge node employs an $ε$-tube verifier that strictly bounds kinematic execution errors. The framework is coupled with an Adaptive Horizon Scaling mechanism that dynamically expands or shrinks the speculative pre-fetch depth based on real-time tracking error.
  We evaluate SPO on continuous RLBench manipulation tasks under emulated network delays. Results show that even when deployed with learned models of modest accuracy, SPO reduces network-induced idle time by over 60% compared to blocking remote inference. Furthermore, SPO discards approximately 60% fewer cloud predictions than static caching baselines. Ultimately, SPO enables fluid, real-time cloud-robotic control while maintaining bounded physical safety.

AI.tldr: SPO combines cloud-generated world modeling with an edge ε-tube verifier and adaptive horizon scaling to decouple control frequency from network latency in cloud robotics, improving real-time performance while maintaining safety in high-frequency manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Network latency and jitter in cloud-robot systems destabilize high-frequency manipulation and can cause unsafe execution. There is a need to decouple execution from RTT while ensuring bounded safety under predictive drift.
AI.method: A cloud-hosted world model pre-computes future kinematic waypoints and streams them to an edge buffer; the edge applies an ε-tube verifier to bound execution errors; Adaptive Horizon Scaling modulates speculative pre-fetch depth based on real-time tracking error.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on RLBench manipulation tasks with emulated delays shows SPO reduces network-induced idle time by >60% versus blocking remote inference and discards ~60% fewer cloud predictions than static caching baselines.
AI.conclusion: SPO enables fluid, real-time cloud-robotic control with bounded safety, demonstrating how speculative pre-fetching combined with safety-verification and adaptive horizon control can mitigate latency issues in cloud robotics.

title: Speculating Experts Accelerates Inference for Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have gained popularity as a means of scaling the capacity of large language models (LLMs) while maintaining sparse activations and reduced per-token compute. However, in memory-constrained inference settings, expert weights must be offloaded to CPU, creating a performance bottleneck from CPU-GPU transfers during decoding. We propose an expert prefetching scheme that leverages currently computed internal model representations to speculate future experts, enabling memory transfers to overlap with computation. Across multiple MoE architectures, we demonstrate that future experts can be reliably predicted by these internal representations. We also demonstrate that executing speculated experts generally maintains downstream task accuracy, thus preserving more effective compute-memory overlap by eliminating the need to re-fetch true router-selected experts. Integrated into an optimized inference engine, our approach achieves up to 14\% reduction in time per output token (TPOT) over on-demand loading of experts from CPU memory. For MoEs where speculative execution alone yields suboptimal accuracy, we further examine lightweight estimators that improve expert prediction hit rates, thereby reducing performance degradation. Our code is released in open-source at https://github.com/axonn-ai/yalis/tree/offload_prefetch.

AI.tldr: Prefetches MoE expert weights from CPU by predicting future experts from internal activations to overlap compute with data transfer, reducing TPOT by up to 14%; accuracy largely preserved; includes estimators for lower hit-rate cases; open-source.
AI.motivation: Mitigate CPU-GPU transfer bottlenecks in memory-constrained MoE inference while preserving the model's sparse activations and scalability.
AI.method: Leverage currently computed internal representations during decoding to speculate future router-selected experts. Prefetch (and sometimes execute) those speculated experts to overlap memory transfers with computation. Evaluates across multiple MoE architectures; introduces lightweight estimators to boost hit rate when speculation alone harms accuracy; integrated into an optimized inference engine; code released.
AI.result: Demonstrates reliable prediction of future experts from internal activations. Speculative execution maintains downstream task accuracy in most cases. Achieves up to 14% reduction in time per output token (TPOT) compared with on-demand CPU-loading. For cases with suboptimal accuracy, estimators improve hit rates and reduce degradation. Open-source implementation available.
AI.conclusion: Memory-transfer bottlenecks in MoE inference can be mitigated by speculative prefetching of expert weights guided by internal model signals, enabling better compute-memory overlap with minimal accuracy loss. The approach generalizes across MoE architectures and benefits from simple estimators when needed.

title: When both Grounding and not Grounding are Bad -- A Partially Grounded Encoding of Planning into SAT (Extended Version)
summary: Classical planning problems are typically defined using lifted first-order representations, which offer compactness and generality. While most planners ground these representations to simplify reasoning, this can cause an exponential blowup in size. Recent approaches instead operate directly on the lifted level to avoid full grounding. We explore a middle ground between fully lifted and fully grounded planning by introducing three SAT encodings that keep actions lifted while partially grounding predicates. Unlike previous SAT encodings, which scale quadratically with plan length, our approach scales linearly, enabling better performance on longer plans. Empirically, our best encoding outperforms the state of the art in length-optimal planning on hard-to-ground domains.

AI.tldr: Presents three SAT encodings that keep actions lifted and partially ground predicates, achieving linear (vs quadratic) scaling with plan length and improving length-optimal planning on hard-to-ground domains.
AI.motivation: Grounding often causes exponential blowup; aim to retain lifted structure while avoiding full grounding, balancing compactness and reasoning efficiency.
AI.method: Develop three SAT encodings operating at lifted action level with partial predicate grounding; analyze scaling and compare to existing encodings; evaluate on length-optimal planning benchmarks.
AI.result: Encodings scale linearly with plan length; the best encoding outperforms state-of-the-art length-optimal planners on challenging domains.
AI.conclusion: Partial grounding in SAT encodings effectively bridges lifted and grounded planning, offering better scalability for long plans.

title: Diffusion-Guided Semantic Consistency for Multimodal Heterogeneity
summary: Federated learning (FL) is severely challenged by non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) client data, a problem that degrades global model performance, especially in multimodal perception settings. Conventional methods often fail to address the underlying semantic discrepancies between clients, leading to suboptimal performance for multimedia systems requiring robust perception. To overcome this, we introduce SemanticFL, a novel framework that leverages the rich semantic representations of pre-trained diffusion models to provide privacy-preserving guidance for local training. Our approach leverages multi-layer semantic representations from a pre-trained Stable Diffusion model (including VAE-encoded latents and U-Net hierarchical features) to create a shared latent space that aligns heterogeneous clients, facilitated by an efficient client-server architecture that offloads heavy computation to the server. A unified consistency mechanism, employing cross-modal contrastive learning, further stabilizes convergence. We conduct extensive experiments on benchmarks including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and TinyImageNet under diverse heterogeneity scenarios. Our results demonstrate that SemanticFL surpasses existing federated learning approaches, achieving accuracy gains of up to 5.49% over FedAvg, validating its effectiveness in learning robust representations for heterogeneous and multimodal data for perception tasks.

AI.tldr: SemanticFL leverages pre-trained diffusion-model semantics to align non-IID FL clients via a server-assisted framework with cross-modal contrastive learning, achieving up to 5.49% accuracy gains on CIFAR-10/100 and TinyImageNet over FedAvg.
AI.motivation: Non-IID data and semantic misalignment hinder federated learning performance in multimodal perception; there is a need for privacy-preserving guidance and robust representation learning across heterogeneous clients.
AI.method: Uses multi-layer semantic representations from a pre-trained Stable Diffusion model (VAE latents and U-Net features) to construct a shared latent space; employs a client-server architecture that offloads heavy computation to the server; introduces a unified consistency mechanism via cross-modal contrastive learning to stabilize convergence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and TinyImageNet under diverse heterogeneity scenarios show that SemanticFL outperforms baselines, achieving up to 5.49% accuracy gains over FedAvg.
AI.conclusion: SemanticFL demonstrates improved robustness for heterogeneous and multimodal data in federated learning and is effective for perception tasks, indicating the potential of diffusion-model–based semantic guidance in FL.

title: Unlabeled Multi-Robot Motion Planning with Improved Separation Trade-offs
summary:
We study unlabeled multi-robot motion planning for unit-disk robots in a polygonal environment. Although the problem is hard in general, polynomial-time solutions exist under appropriate separation assumptions on start and target positions. Banyassady et al. (SoCG'22) guarantee feasibility in simple polygons under start--start and target--target distances of at least $4$, and start--target distances of at least $3$, but without optimality guarantees. Solovey et al. (RSS'15) provide a near-optimal solution in general polygonal domains, under stricter conditions: start/target positions must have pairwise distance at least $4$, and at least $\sqrt{5}\approx2.236$ from obstacles. This raises the question of whether polynomial-time algorithms can be obtained in even more densely packed environments.
  In this paper we present a generalized algorithm that achieve different trade-offs on the robots-separation and obstacles-separation bounds, all significantly improving upon the state of the art. Specifically, we obtain polynomial-time constant-approximation algorithms to minimize the total path length when (i) the robots-separation is $2\tfrac{2}{3}$ and the obstacles-separation is $1\tfrac{2}{3}$, or (ii) the robots-separation is $\approx3.291$ and the obstacles-separation $\approx1.354$. Additionally, we introduce a different strategy yielding a polynomial-time solution when the robots-separation is only $2$, and the obstacles-separation is $3$. Finally, we show that without any robots-separation assumption, obstacles-separation of at least $1.5$ may be necessary for a solution to exist.

AI.tldr: Generalized polynomial-time constant-approximation algorithms for unlabeled multi-robot motion planning with unit disks in polygonal environments, enabling several trade-offs between robots-separation and obstacles-separation that improve prior state-of-the-art; includes regimes with 2 2/3 / 1 2/3 separation, ~3.291 / ~1.354 separation, a separate approach for separation 2 / 3, and a necessary condition showing obstacle separation ≥ 1.5 without robots-separation.
AI.motivation: Dense packing of unit-disk robots in polygonal domains; unlabeled planning; existing methods require fairly large separations and lack optimality guarantees; aim to push feasible regimes closer to dense packing while providing performance guarantees.
AI.method: Develop a generalized framework producing polynomial-time constant-factor approximations for total path length under multiple separation bounds. The method likely combines space partitioning, separation-aware feasibility tests, and constructive routing that ensures collision-free labeled assignments while controlling total length; provides multiple algorithms tuned to different separation regimes and proves correctness and approximation bounds; also derives a lower bound showing necessity of obstacle separation 1.5 in absence of robot separation.
AI.result: Three main regimes with proven constant-factor approximations: (i) robot separation 2 2/3, obstacle separation 1 2/3; (ii) robot separation ~3.291, obstacle separation ~1.354; and a separate polynomial-time algorithm for robot separation 2 and obstacle separation 3. The work also establishes a necessary condition: without robot separation assumptions, obstacle separation must be at least 1.5 for feasibility in general. All results are polynomial-time and focus on minimizing total path length.
AI.conclusion: Significantly improves the feasible density for unlabeled multi-robot motion planning with unit disks, offering several trade-offs between robot and obstacle separations and achieving constant-factor optimizations in polynomial time. Opens up avenues for even tighter bounds and extensions to broader robot models and environments.

title: A Closed-Form CLF-CBF Controller for Whole-Body Continuum Soft Robot Collision Avoidance
summary:
Safe operation is essential for deploying robots in human-centered 3D environments. Soft continuum manipulators provide passive safety through mechanical compliance, but still require active control to achieve reliable collision avoidance. Existing approaches, such as sampling-based planning, are often computationally expensive and lack formal safety guarantees, which limits their use for real-time whole-body avoidance.
  This paper presents a closed-form Control Lyapunov Function--Control Barrier Function (CLF--CBF) controller for real-time 3D obstacle avoidance in soft continuum manipulators without online optimization. By analytically embedding safety constraints into the control input, the proposed method ensures stability and safety under the stated modeling assumptions, while avoiding feasibility issues commonly encountered in online optimization-based methods. The resulting controller is up to $10\times$ faster than standard CLF--CBF quadratic-programming approaches and up to $100\times$ faster than traditional sampling-based planners.
  Simulation and hardware experiments on a tendon-driven soft manipulator demonstrate accurate 3D trajectory tracking and robust obstacle avoidance in cluttered environments. These results show that the proposed framework provides a scalable and provably safe control strategy for soft robots operating in dynamic, safety-critical settings.

AI.tldr: A closed-form CLF-CBF controller enables real-time, provably safe 3D obstacle avoidance for soft continuum manipulators without online optimization, outperforming optimization-based and sampling-based methods.
AI.motivation: Soft robots offer passive safety but require reliable collision avoidance in human-centered, 3D environments. Existing planning methods are computationally heavy and lack formal safety guarantees, hindering real-time deployment.
AI.method: Develop a closed-form Control Lyapunov Function–Control Barrier Function (CLF-CBF) controller that embeds safety constraints analytically into the control input, eliminating the need for online optimization. Demonstrates on a tendon-driven soft manipulator with 3D obstacle avoidance and tracking.
AI.result: Achieves up to 10× faster performance than standard CLF-CBF QP approaches and up to 100× faster than sampling-based planners. Simulation and hardware experiments show accurate trajectory tracking and robust obstacle avoidance in cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a scalable, provably safe control strategy for soft robots in dynamic, safety-critical settings and enables real-time, whole-body obstacle avoidance without online optimization.

title: A Visualization for Comparative Analysis of Regression Models
summary: As regression is a widely studied problem, many methods have been proposed to solve it, each of them often requiring setting different hyper-parameters. Therefore, selecting the proper method for a given application may be very difficult and relies on comparing their performances. Performance is usually measured using various metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE), or R-squared (R${}^2$). These metrics provide a numerical summary of predictive accuracy by quantifying the difference between predicted and actual values. However, while these metrics are widely used in the literature for summarizing model performance and useful to distinguish between models performing poorly and well, they often aggregate too much information. This article addresses these limitations by introducing a novel visualization approach that highlights key aspects of regression model performance. The proposed method builds upon three main contributions: (1) considering the residuals in a 2D space, which allows for simultaneous evaluation of errors from two models, (2) leveraging the Mahalanobis distance to account for correlations and differences in scale within the data, and (3) employing a colormap to visualize the percentile-based distribution of errors, making it easier to identify dense regions and outliers. By graphically representing the distribution of errors and their correlations, this approach provides a more detailed and comprehensive view of model performance, enabling users to uncover patterns that traditional aggregate metrics may obscure. The proposed visualization method facilitates a deeper understanding of regression model performance differences and error distributions, enhancing the evaluation and comparison process.

AI.tldr: A regression evaluation framework that uses a 2D residual space for comparing two models, Mahalanobis distance to capture residual correlations and scale differences, and a percentile-based colormap to visualize error distributions, offering richer insights beyond traditional metrics.
AI.motivation: Traditional regression metrics (MAE, RMSE, R^2) summarize performance but obscure distributional details and inter-model error relationships. A visualization that captures joint error structure and density can enhance model comparison and detect patterns/outliers.
AI.method: Construct a 2D residual plane comparing two models' errors, apply Mahalanobis distance to quantify joint deviation considering correlations and scale, and use a colormap representing percentile-based error distribution to identify dense regions and outliers.
AI.result: Proposes a novel visualization technique for regression evaluation that provides a more detailed view of error distributions and model differences, potentially revealing patterns not captured by aggregate metrics.
AI.conclusion: The visualization approach should improve understanding and comparison of regression models by highlighting error structure, correlations, and outliers beyond conventional metrics.

title: Hyperagents
summary: Self-improving AI systems aim to reduce reliance on human engineering by learning to improve their own learning and problem-solving processes. Existing approaches to self-improvement rely on fixed, handcrafted meta-level mechanisms, fundamentally limiting how fast such systems can improve. The Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM) demonstrates open-ended self-improvement in coding by repeatedly generating and evaluating self-modified variants. Because both evaluation and self-modification are coding tasks, gains in coding ability can translate into gains in self-improvement ability. However, this alignment does not generally hold beyond coding domains. We introduce \textbf{hyperagents}, self-referential agents that integrate a task agent (which solves the target task) and a meta agent (which modifies itself and the task agent) into a single editable program. Crucially, the meta-level modification procedure is itself editable, enabling metacognitive self-modification, improving not only the task-solving behavior, but also the mechanism that generates future improvements. We instantiate this framework by extending DGM to create DGM-Hyperagents (DGM-H), eliminating the assumption of domain-specific alignment between task performance and self-modification skill to potentially support self-accelerating progress on any computable task. Across diverse domains, the DGM-H improves performance over time and outperforms baselines without self-improvement or open-ended exploration, as well as prior self-improving systems. Furthermore, the DGM-H improves the process by which it generates new agents (e.g., persistent memory, performance tracking), and these meta-level improvements transfer across domains and accumulate across runs. DGM-Hyperagents offer a glimpse of open-ended AI systems that do not merely search for better solutions, but continually improve their search for how to improve.

AI.tldr: DGM-Hyperagents extend Darwin Gödel Machine by making the meta-modification process itself editable, enabling open-ended self-improvement across tasks; they separate task-solving and meta-improvement to avoid domain-specific alignment, and show progressive gains and transfer of meta-improvements across domains and runs.
AI.motivation: To overcome fixed, handcrafted meta-level mechanisms in self-improving AI, which limit the speed and scope of self-improvement and hinder domain generality.
AI.method: Introduce hyperagents that combine a task agent with an editable meta-agent; extend Darwin Gödel Machine (DGM) to DGM-Hyperagents (DGM-H) so that meta-level modification is itself modifiable, enabling metacognitive self-modification and self-improvement of how to improve. Implement across diverse domains and evaluate performance, self-improvement rate, and transfer of meta-improvement mechanisms.
AI.result: DGM-H improves task performance over time, outperforms baselines with/without self-improvement and prior self-improving systems, and enhances the process of generating new agents (memory, performance tracking). Meta-improvements transfer across domains and runs, suggesting potential for open-ended, self-accelerating progress in computable tasks.
AI.conclusion: DGM-Hyberagents offer a pathway to open-ended AI systems that continually improve both task solving and the means of improving, potentially enabling domain-general self-improvement without relying on domain-specific alignment between task performance and self-improvement skill.

authors: L. D. M. S. Sai Teja
title: AURORA: Adaptive Unified Representation for Robust Ultrasound Analysis
summary: Ultrasound images vary widely across scanners, operators, and anatomical targets, which often causes models trained in one setting to generalize poorly to new hospitals and clinical conditions. The Foundation Model Challenge for Ultrasound Image Analysis (FMC-UIA) reflects this difficulty by requiring a single model to handle multiple tasks, including segmentation, detection, classification, and landmark regression across diverse organs and datasets. We propose a unified multi-task framework based on a transformer visual encoder from the Qwen3-VL family. Intermediate token features are projected into spatial feature maps and fused using a lightweight multi-scale feature pyramid, enabling both pixel-level predictions and global reasoning within a shared representation. Each task is handled by a small task-specific prediction head, while training uses task-aware sampling and selective loss balancing to manage heterogeneous supervision and reduce task imbalance. Our method is designed to be simple to optimize and adaptable across a wide range of ultrasound analysis tasks. The performance improved from 67% to 85% on the validation set and achieved an average score of 81.84% on the official test set across all tasks. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/saitejalekkala33/FMCUIA-ISBI.git

AI.tldr: Unified multi-task ultrasound analysis model (FMC-UIA) using a Qwen3-VL transformer encoder, enabling segmentation, detection, classification, and landmark regression across diverse datasets with a shared representation and task-specific heads.
AI.motivation: Ultrasound variability across scanners, operators, and anatomical targets leads to poor cross-domain generalization; a single model that can handle multiple tasks across diverse datasets is desirable.
AI.method: A transformer visual encoder from the Qwen3-VL family processes image inputs; intermediate token features are projected into spatial feature maps and fused with a lightweight multi-scale feature pyramid to support pixel-level predictions and global reasoning. Task-specific heads handle segmentation, detection, classification, and landmark regression. Training uses task-aware sampling and selective loss balancing to address heterogeneous supervision and task imbalance.
AI.result: Validation performance improved from 67% to 85% and the model achieved an average score of 81.84% on the official test set across all tasks; code released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a simple-to-optimize, adaptable solution for broad ultrasound analysis tasks, demonstrating effective cross-task generalization across diverse data sources.

title: Locality Sensitive Hashing in Hyperbolic Space
summary: For a metric space $(X, d)$, a family $\mathcal{H}$ of locality sensitive hash functions is called $(r, cr, p_1, p_2)$ sensitive if a randomly chosen function $h\in \mathcal{H}$ has probability at least $p_1$ (at most $p_2$) to map any $a, b\in X$ in the same hash bucket if $d(a, b)\leq r$ (or $d(a, b)\geq cr$). Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) is one of the most popular techniques for approximate nearest-neighbor search in high-dimensional spaces, and has been studied extensively for Hamming, Euclidean, and spherical geometries. An $(r, cr, p_1, p_2)$-sensitive hash function enables approximate nearest neighbor search (i.e., returning a point within distance $cr$ from a query $q$ if there exists a point within distance $r$ from $q$) with space $O(n^{1+ρ})$ and query time $O(n^ρ)$ where $ρ=\frac{\log 1/p_1}{\log 1/p_2}$. But LSH for hyperbolic spaces $\mathbb{H}^d$ remains largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first LSH construction native to hyperbolic space. For the hyperbolic plane $(d=2)$, we show a construction achieving $ρ\leq 1/c$, based on the hyperplane rounding scheme. For general hyperbolic spaces $(d \geq 3)$, we use dimension reduction from $\mathbb{H}^d$ to $\mathbb{H}^2$ and the 2D hyperbolic LSH to get $ρ\leq 1.59/c$. On the lower bound side, we show that the lower bound on $ρ$ of Euclidean LSH extends to the hyperbolic setting via local isometry, therefore giving $ρ\geq 1/c^2$.

AI.tldr: First locality-sensitive hashing (LSH) for hyperbolic spaces. It yields bounds ρ ≤ 1/c in H^2 and ρ ≤ 1.59/c in H^d (d≥3) via reduction to H^2; a matching Euclidean lower bound gives ρ ≥ 1/c^2.
AI.motivation: Hyperbolic spaces naturally model hierarchical and scale-free data; enabling efficient approximate nearest neighbor search in hyperbolic geometry fills a gap in LSH literature and could improve ANN in such spaces.
AI.method: Introduce a hyperplane-rounding based LSH in H^2; extend to H^d (d≥3) by reducing to H^2 via a dimension-reduction technique; analyze collision probabilities to bound ρ = log(1/p1)/log(1/p2); derive lower bound by transferring Euclidean LSH lower bounds via local isometry between spaces.
AI.result: A native LSH construction for hyperbolic space with ρ ≤ 1/c in H^2 and ρ ≤ 1.59/c in general H^d; lower bound ρ ≥ 1/c^2 obtained from local isometry with Euclidean LSH. For LSH performance, space is O(n^{1+ρ}) and query time is O(n^ρ).
AI.conclusion: The work establishes the first LSH framework for hyperbolic spaces, providing both upper and lower bounds on ρ and enabling efficient ANN in hyperbolic geometry. It highlights potential for further refinements and practical evaluation, and suggests that hyperbolic LSH can be competitive with Euclidean LSH under suitable regimes.

title: Can LLMs Prove Robotic Path Planning Optimality? A Benchmark for Research-Level Algorithm Verification
summary: Robotic path planning problems are often NP-hard, and practical solutions typically rely on approximation algorithms with provable performance guarantees for general cases. While designing such algorithms is challenging, formally proving their approximation optimality is even more demanding, which requires domain-specific geometric insights and multi-step mathematical reasoning over complex operational constraints. Recent Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, yet their ability to assist with research-level optimality proofs in robotic path planning remains under-explored. In this work, we introduce the first benchmark for evaluating LLMs on approximation-ratio proofs of robotic path planning algorithms. The benchmark consists of 34 research-grade proof tasks spanning diverse planning problem types and complexity levels, each requiring structured reasoning over algorithm descriptions, problem constraints, and theoretical guarantees. Our evaluation of state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source LLMs reveals that even the strongest models struggle to produce fully valid proofs without external domain knowledge. However, providing LLMs with task-specific in-context lemmas substantially improves reasoning quality, a factor that is more effective than generic chain-of-thought prompting or supplying the ground-truth approximation ratio as posterior knowledge. We further provide fine-grained error analysis to characterize common logical failures and hallucinations, and demonstrate how each error type can be mitigated through targeted context augmentation.

AI.tldr: A 34-task benchmark for evaluating LLMs on proving approximation ratios in robotic path planning; findings show current models struggle to produce fully valid proofs without domain knowledge, but task-specific in-context lemmas substantially improve reasoning; error analysis reveals key failure modes and suggests mitigation via targeted context augmentation.
AI.motivation: Assess the capability of large language models to assist with formal, domain-specific optimality proofs in robotics, a gap not addressed by existing math or NLP benchmarks.
AI.method: Construct 34 research-grade proof tasks covering diverse planning problems; evaluate state-of-the-art proprietary and open-source LLMs; compare prompting strategies (chain-of-thought, in-context lemmas, and using the ground-truth ratio as posterior knowledge); perform fine-grained error analysis and propose mitigation via context augmentation.
AI.result: LLMs struggle to produce fully valid proofs without domain knowledge; incorporating task-specific in-context lemmas substantially improves reasoning quality, outperforming generic chain-of-thought or simply supplying the ratio as posterior knowledge.
AI.conclusion: First benchmark for approximation-ratio proofs in robotic path planning; targeted context via lemmas is a robust method to improve LLM-assisted proofs; provides detailed error taxonomy and mitigation guidance through context augmentation.

title: Maximizing mutual information between user-contexts and responses improve LLM personalization with no additional data
summary: While post-training has successfully improved large language models (LLMs) across a variety of domains, these gains heavily rely on human-labeled data or external verifiers. Existing data has already been exploited, and new high-quality data is expensive to collect. More fundamentally, true intelligence goes far beyond tasks that are easily verifiable. Therefore, we need self-improvement frameworks that allow models to improve without external oversight. We propose *Mutual Information Preference Optimization (MIPO)*, a contrastive data augmentation method that constructs preference pairs by generating a positive response conditioning on the correct prompt, and a negative response by conditioning on a random, unrelated prompt. We show that using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to learn from this paired data maximizes pointwise conditional mutual information (MI) (under the base LLM) between prompts and model responses. Empirical results with various-sized Llama- and Qwen-Instruct models show that when used to maximize MI between user context and response, MIPO provides an effective personalization technique, achieving 3-40% improvements on personalization tasks using real-user datasets compared to strong baselines. Surprisingly, MIPO can also be applied to improve performance on math and multiple-choice problems, yielding 1-18% **without any additional data or human supervision**. These results suggest a promising direction for self-improvement.

AI.tldr: Proposes Mutual Information Preference Optimization (MIPO): a self-supervised data augmentation method that creates preference pairs by conditioning responses on the correct prompt (positive) and on a random unrelated prompt (negative), optimized with Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to maximize pointwise conditional mutual information between prompts and responses, enabling self-improvement without external supervision.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on human-labeled data and external verifiers for post-training gains; enable learning signals from tasks not easily verifiable; explore self-improvement through maximizing mutual information between user prompts and model responses.
AI.method: Construct preference pairs: positive response conditioned on the correct prompt; negative response conditioned on a random unrelated prompt. Use Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to train, aiming to maximize the pointwise conditional mutual information (MI) between prompts and model responses under the base LLM.
AI.result: Empirical results on Llama- and Qwen-Instruct models show 3-40% improvements on personalization tasks with real-user datasets vs strong baselines; also 1-18% gains on math and multiple-choice problems without additional data or human supervision.
AI.conclusion: MI-based data augmentation via MIPO offers a promising direction for self-improvement, enabling personalization and performance gains without extra labeled data or external oversight.

title: Teaching an Agent to Sketch One Part at a Time
summary: We develop a method for producing vector sketches one part at a time. To do this, we train a multi-modal language model-based agent using a novel multi-turn process-reward reinforcement learning following supervised fine-tuning. Our approach is enabled by a new dataset we call ControlSketch-Part, containing rich part-level annotations for sketches, obtained using a novel, generic automatic annotation pipeline that segments vector sketches into semantic parts and assigns paths to parts with a structured multi-stage labeling process. Our results indicate that incorporating structured part-level data and providing agent with the visual feedback through the process enables interpretable, controllable, and locally editable text-to-vector sketch generation.

AI.tldr: A multimodal agent generates vector sketches piecewise with reinforcement learning, guided by a part-level annotated dataset (ControlSketch-Part); results show the approach yields interpretable, controllable, and locally editable text-to-vector sketch generation via structured data and process-level visual feedback.
AI.motivation: Enable fine-grained, controllable vector sketch synthesis with interpretability; existing methods lack part-level guidance and scalable automatic annotation for semantic segmentation of sketches.
AI.method: Train a multi-modal language-model–based agent using a novel multi-turn process-reward reinforcement learning framework after supervised fine-tuning; introduce ControlSketch-Part, a dataset with rich part-level annotations. An automatic generic annotation pipeline segments vector sketches into semantic parts and assigns paths to parts via multi-stage labeling.
AI.result: Incorporating structured part-level data and process-based visual feedback yields interpretable, controllable, and locally editable text-to-vector sketch generation.
AI.conclusion: Structured part-level annotations plus a process-reward feedback loop enhance controllability and interpretability in vector sketch synthesis; the dataset and annotation pipeline enable further research into controllable vector-generation systems.

title: Factored Levenberg-Marquardt for Diffeomorphic Image Registration: An efficient optimizer for FireANTs
summary: FireANTs introduced a novel Eulerian descent method for plug-and-play behavior with arbitrary optimizers adapted for diffeomorphic image registration as a test-time optimization problem, with a GPU-accelerated implementation. FireANTs uses Adam as its default optimizer for fast and more robust optimization. However, Adam requires storing state variables (i.e. momentum and squared-momentum estimates), each of which can consume significant memory, prohibiting its use for significantly large images. In this work, we propose a modified Levenberg-Marquardt (LM) optimizer that requires only a single scalar damping parameter as optimizer state, that is adaptively tuned using a trust region approach. The resulting optimizer reduces memory by up to 24.6% for large volumes, and retaining performance across all four datasets. A single hyperparameter configuration tuned on brain MRI transfers without modification to lung CT and cross-modal abdominal registration, matching or outperforming Adam on three of four benchmarks. We also perform ablations on the effectiveness of using Metropolis-Hastings style rejection step to prevent updates that worsen the loss function.

AI.tldr: Memory-efficient LM optimizer for FireANTs that preserves performance in diffeomorphic image registration, enabling scalable test-time optimization with a single hyperparameter and modest MH-based robustness checks.
AI.motivation: Adam-based optimization offers robust convergence but incurs high memory usage for large volumes, hindering scalability for plug-and-play registration across datasets.
AI.method: Replace Adam with a Levenberg–Marquardt optimizer with a single scalar damping parameter tuned via a trust-region approach; GPU-accelerated, plug-and-play with arbitrary optimizers; include Metropolis-Hastings style rejection ablation; evaluate transferability of a single hyperparameter across brain MRI, lung CT, and cross-modal abdominal data.
AI.result: Memory usage reduced up to 24.6% for large volumes; performance maintained across four datasets; a single hyperparameter configuration trained on brain MRI transfers to other modalities, matching or outperforming Adam on 3 of 4 benchmarks; ablations explored the MH rejection step.
AI.conclusion: A memory-efficient, adaptive LM-based optimizer can replace Adam in FireANTs for diffeomorphic registration, enabling scalable test-time optimization with robust cross-dataset transfer and optional MH-based safeguards.

title: Better Sampling Bounds for Restricted Delaunay Triangulations and a Star-Shaped Property for Restricted Voronoi Cells
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures. Extended version (with complete proofs) of a paper that will appear in the 42nd International Symposium on Computational Geometry
summary: The restricted Delaunay triangulation of a closed surface $Σ$ and a finite point set $V \subset Σ$ is a subcomplex of the Delaunay tetrahedralization of $V$ whose triangles approximate $Σ$. It is well known that if $V$ is a sufficiently dense sample of a smooth $Σ$, then the union of the restricted Delaunay triangles is homeomorphic to $Σ$. We show that an $ε$-sample with $ε\leq 0.3245$ suffices. By comparison, Dey proves it for a $0.18$-sample; our improved sampling bound reduces the number of sample points required by a factor of $3.25$. More importantly, we improve a related sampling bound of Cheng et al. for Delaunay surface meshing, reducing the number of sample points required by a factor of $21$. The first step of our homeomorphism proof is particularly interesting: we show that for a $0.44$-sample, the restricted Voronoi cell of each site $v \in V$ is homeomorphic to a disk, and the orthogonal projection of the cell onto $T_vΣ$ (the plane tangent to $Σ$ at $v$) is star-shaped.

AI.tldr: Improved sampling bounds for restricted Delaunay triangulations of a sampled surface; ε-sample ≤ 0.3245 suffices; previously 0.18; improved by factor 3.25; plus improved Delaunay surface meshing sampling by a factor 21; key steps show restricted Voronoi cells are disks for ε=0.44 and their projections are star-shaped.
AI.motivation: To guarantee that restricted Delaunay triangulation approximates the surface topology (homeomorphism) for finite samples; reducing required sample density reduces data and computation.
AI.method: Geometric/topological analysis of restricted Voronoi cells, projection onto tangent planes, and proofs of disk homeomorphism and star-shapedness; deriving sampling bounds through geometric inequalities.
AI.result: Proves that ε ≤ 0.3245 suffices for homeomorphism between union of restricted Delaunay triangles and surface; shows 0.44-sample restricted Voronoi cells are disks; improves Delaunay surface meshing sampling by a factor 21.
AI.conclusion: Tighter sampling criteria yield fewer sample points while preserving topological correctness; contributes to practical surface meshing by reducing data requirements; approach may inspire further refinement in Delaunay-based meshing theory.

title: Real-Time Optical Communication Using Event-Based Vision with Moving Transmitters
comment: 8 pages, 7 Figures, Submitted to IROS 2026 - Under Review
summary: In multi-robot systems, traditional radio frequency (RF) communication struggles with contention and jamming. Optical communication offers a strong alternative. However, conventional frame-based cameras suffer from limited frame rates, motion blur, and reduced robustness under high dynamic range lighting. Event cameras support microsecond temporal resolution and high dynamic range, making them extremely sensitive to scene changes under fast relative motion with an optical transmitter. Leveraging these strengths, we develop a complete optical communication system capable of tracking moving transmitters and decoding messages in real time. Our system achieves over $95\%$ decoding accuracy for text transmission during motion by implementing a Geometry-Aware Unscented Kalman Filter (GA-UKF), achieving 7x faster processing speed compared to the previous state-of-the-art method, while maintaining equivalent tracking accuracy at transmitting frequencies $\geq$ 1 kHz.

AI.tldr: A complete optical-communication system using event cameras and a Geometry-Aware Unscented Kalman Filter (GA-UKF) tracks moving transmitters and decodes text in real time with high accuracy and speed.
AI.motivation: RF-based multi-robot communication faces contention and jamming; conventional frame-based cameras are limited by frame rate and motion blur. Optical communication with event cameras offers microsecond timing and high dynamic range to handle fast relative motion.
AI.method: Integrate event-camera based optical signaling with a GA-UKF to track the transmitter geometry and decode messages in real time; achieves high decoding accuracy at transmission frequencies ≥ 1 kHz and is ~7x faster than prior state-of-the-art.
AI.result: Achieves >95% decoding accuracy for text during motion; GA-UKF provides ~7x faster processing while maintaining equivalent tracking accuracy at ≥1 kHz transmissions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable, real-time optical communication pipeline for moving transmitters using event cameras and geometry-aware filtering, addressing limitations of RF channels and traditional frame-based vision.

title: BrainSCL: Subtype-Guided Contrastive Learning for Brain Disorder Diagnosis
summary: Mental disorder populations exhibit pronounced heterogeneity -- that is, the significant differences between samples -- poses a significant challenge to the definition of positive pairs in contrastive learning. To address this, we propose a subtype-guided contrastive learning framework that models patient heterogeneity as latent subtypes and incorporates them as structural priors to guide discriminative representation learning. Specifically, we construct multi-view representations by combining patients' clinical text with graph structure adaptively learned from BOLD signals, to uncover latent subtypes via unsupervised spectral clustering. A dual-level attention mechanism is proposed to construct prototypes for capturing stable subtype-specific connectivity patterns. We further propose a subtype-guided contrastive learning strategy that pulls samples toward their subtype prototype graph, reinforcing intra-subtype consistency for providing effective supervisory signals to improve model performance. We evaluate our method on Major Depressive Disorder (MDD), Bipolar Disorder (BD), and Autism Spectrum Disorders (ASD). Experimental results confirm the effectiveness of subtype prototype graphs in guiding contrastive learning and demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art approaches. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BrainSCL-06D7.

AI.tldr: Subtype-guided contrastive learning for psychiatric disorders by modeling latent subtypes via spectral clustering on multi-view representations (clinical text + BOLD-derived graphs) and prototype-aware contrastive learning.
AI.motivation: Mental disorders are heterogeneous; standard contrastive learning struggles with defining positive pairs. Introducing latent subtypes as structural priors can enforce intra-subtype consistency and provide robust supervisory signals.
AI.method: Build multi-view representations combining clinical text and BOLD-derived graphs; use unsupervised spectral clustering to infer latent subtypes; apply a dual-level attention to form subtype prototypes; use a subtype-guided contrastive loss pulling samples toward their subtype prototype graph.
AI.result: Datasets include MDD, BD, ASD; results show subtype prototype graphs improve performance over state-of-the-art methods; code provided.
AI.conclusion: Leverage latent subtypes to guide contrastive learning yields improved representations and performance in heterogeneous psychiatric populations.

title: Learning to Disprove: Formal Counterexample Generation with Large Language Models
summary: Mathematical reasoning demands two critical, complementary skills: constructing rigorous proofs for true statements and discovering counterexamples that disprove false ones. However, current AI efforts in mathematics focus almost exclusively on proof construction, often neglecting the equally important task of finding counterexamples. In this paper, we address this gap by fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) to reason about and generate counterexamples. We formalize this task as formal counterexample generation, which requires LLMs not only to propose candidate counterexamples but also to produce formal proofs that can be automatically verified in the Lean 4 theorem prover. To enable effective learning, we introduce a symbolic mutation strategy that synthesizes diverse training data by systematically extracting theorems and discarding selected hypotheses, thereby producing diverse counterexample instances. Together with curated datasets, this strategy enables a multi-reward expert iteration framework that substantially enhances both the effectiveness and efficiency of training LLMs for counterexample generation and theorem proving. Experiments on three newly collected benchmarks validate the advantages of our approach, showing that the mutation strategy and training framework yield significant performance gains.

AI.tldr: Fine-tune LLMs to generate counterexamples and formal proofs in Lean 4 using a symbolic mutation strategy for diverse training data, enabling a multi-reward expert-iteration training framework; achieves significant gains on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the gap in AI mathematics where counterexample generation is underexplored; formal verification in Lean 4 provides rigorous validation for counterexamples and proofs; enhancing both discovery and verification strengthens mathematical reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce a symbolic mutation strategy that synthesizes diverse training data by extracting theorems and discarding selected hypotheses to produce varied counterexample instances; curate datasets; train LLMs with a multi-reward expert-iteration framework to jointly optimize counterexample generation and theorem proving.
AI.result: Experiments on three newly collected benchmarks show substantial performance gains; the mutation strategy and the multi-reward training framework improve both effectiveness and efficiency of counterexample generation and formal proof generation/verifications.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that counterexample-centric training is a viable complement to proof-centric AI in mathematics; data synthesis via mutation plus a multi-reward learning paradigm enhances LLM capabilities for producing verifiable Lean 4 proofs and counterexamples, with potential broad impact on AI-assisted mathematics.

title: LoFi: Location-Aware Fine-Grained Representation Learning for Chest X-ray
summary: Fine-grained representation learning is crucial for retrieval and phrase grounding in chest X-rays, where clinically relevant findings are often spatially confined. However, the lack of region-level supervision in contrastive models and the limited ability of large vision language models to capture fine-grained representations in external validation lead to suboptimal performance on these tasks. To address these limitations, we propose Location-aware Fine-grained representation learning (LoFi), which jointly optimizes sigmoid, captioning, and location-aware captioning losses using a lightweight large language model. The location-aware captioning loss enables region-level supervision through grounding and dense captioning objectives, thereby facilitating fine-grained representation learning. Building upon these representations, we integrate a fine-grained encoder into retrieval-based in-context learning to enhance chest X-ray grounding across diverse settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method achieves superior retrieval and phrase grounding performance on MIMIC-CXR and PadChest-GR.

AI.tldr: LoFi is a fine-grained representation learning framework for chest X-rays that uses location-aware captioning and three losses to enable region-level supervision and improve retrieval and grounding; it leverages a lightweight LLM and a fine-grained encoder in retrieval-based in-context learning, and achieves state-of-the-art results on MIMIC-CXR and PadChest-GR.
AI.motivation: Current contrastive models lack region-level supervision, limiting fine-grained representations; external validation shows large vision-language models struggle to capture fine-grained cues in radiographs; thus improved grounding and dense captioning are needed for chest X-ray retrieval and phrase grounding.
AI.method: Jointly optimize sigmoid, captioning, and location-aware captioning losses using a lightweight large language model. The location-aware captioning loss provides region-level supervision via grounding and dense captioning objectives. A fine-grained encoder is integrated into retrieval-based in-context learning to boost grounding across diverse settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate superior retrieval and phrase grounding performance on MIMIC-CXR and PadChest-GR.
AI.conclusion: LoFi enables fine-grained representation learning by incorporating region-level supervision through location-aware captioning and grounding, and improves retrieval-grounding performance across datasets when combined with retrieval-based in-context learning.

title: The Voronoi Diagram of Four Lines in $\mathbb{R}^3$
comment: Accepted at the Symposium on Computational Geometry (SoCG) 2026
summary: We consider the Voronoi diagram of lines in $\mathbb{R}^3$ under the Euclidean metric, and give a full classification of its structure in the base case of four lines in general position. We first show that the number of vertices in the Voronoi diagram of four lines in general position is always even, between 0 and 8, and all such numbers can be realized. We identify a key structure for the diagram formation, called a \emph{twist}, which is a pair of consecutive intersections among trisector branches; only two types of twists are possible, so-called \emph{full} and \emph{partial} twists. A full twist is a purely local structure, which can be inserted or removed without affecting the rest of the diagram. Assuming no full twists, the nearest and the farthest Voronoi diagrams of four lines, each have 15 distinct topologies, which are in one-to-one correspondence; the two-dimensional faces are all unbounded, and the total number of vertices is at most six. The unbounded features of the farthest diagram, encoded in a two-dimensional spherical map, are also in one-to-one correspondence. The identified topologies are all realizable. Any Voronoi diagram of four lines in general position in $\mathbb{R}^3$ can be obtained from one of these topologies by inserting full twists; each twist induces a bounded face of exactly two vertices in both the nearest and farthest diagrams. We obtain the classification by an exhaustive search algorithm using some new structural and combinatorial observations of line Voronoi diagrams.

AI.tldr: Complete combinatorial classification of Voronoi diagrams of four lines in R^3 under the Euclidean metric. Key findings include: vertex counts are even (0–8), all values realized; introduction of twists (full vs partial) governing diagram formation; with no full twists, 15 topologies for nearest and farthest diagrams, bijective correspondence; all topologies realizable; inserting full twists yields all other diagrams, with each twist creating a bounded two-vertex face; classification achieved via an exhaustive search leveraging new structural/combinatorial observations.
AI.motivation: To understand and enumerate the full combinatorial landscape of line-based Voronoi diagrams in 3D, focusing on the tractable base case of four lines, and to reveal how local structural features (twists) control global diagram topology.
AI.method: Introduce twist structure as pairs of consecutive intersections among trisector branches, categorizing into full and partial twists. Prove that full twists are local and can be inserted/removed without changing the rest. Analyze the no-full-twist case to enumerate 15 topologies for both nearest and farthest diagrams; use a two-dimensional facial/vertices bound (≤6 vertices) and a spherical map for unbounded farthest features. Use exhaustive search with new combinatorial observations to realize all identified topologies and to validate that any diagram arises from one topology via insertion of full twists.
AI.result: Parity and bounds: the number of Voronoi vertices for four lines is always even, in {0,2,4,6,8}. For the no-full-twist regime, there are exactly 15 topologies for the nearest diagram and 15 corresponding topologies for the farthest diagram, forming a one-to-one correspondence; all two-dimensional faces are unbounded and the total vertex count is ≤6. Unbounded features of the farthest diagram are encoded by a spherical map in bijection with the topologies. All identified topologies are realizable. Any diagram (in general position) can be obtained from one of these topologies by inserting full twists, with each twist creating a bounded face of exactly two vertices in both the nearest and farthest diagrams.
AI.conclusion: The authors provide a complete, realizable classification of Voronoi diagrams of four lines in R^3 (general position). The twist-based framework reduces the problem to a finite set of topologies and a local operation (twist insertion/removal) that generates all diagrams. The approach combines structural insight with an exhaustive search, delivering a constructive and extensible classification.

title: Zero Shot Deformation Reconstruction for Soft Robots Using a Flexible Sensor Array and Cage Based 3D Gaussian Modeling
summary: We present a zero-shot deformation reconstruction framework for soft robots that operates without any visual supervision at inference time. In this work, zero-shot deformation reconstruction is defined as the ability to infer object-wide deformations on previously unseen soft robots without collecting object-specific deformation data or performing any retraining during deployment. Our method assumes access to a static geometric proxy of the undeformed object, which can be obtained from a STL model. During operation, the system relies exclusively on tactile sensing, enabling camera-free deformation inference. The proposed framework integrates a flexible piezoresistive sensor array with a geometry-aware, cage-based 3D Gaussian deformation model. Local tactile measurements are mapped to low-dimensional cage control signals and propagated to dense Gaussian primitives to generate globally consistent shape deformations. A graph attention network regresses cage displacements from tactile input, enforcing spatial smoothness and structural continuity via boundary-aware propagation. Given only a nominal geometric proxy and real-time tactile signals, the system performs zero-shot deformation reconstruction of unseen soft robots in bending and twisting motions, while rendering photorealistic RGB in real time. It achieves 0.67 IoU, 0.65 SSIM, and 3.48 mm Chamfer distance, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization through explicit coupling of tactile sensing and structured geometric deformation.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot deformation reconstruction for unseen soft robots using tactile sensing, with a geometry-aware cage-based 3D Gaussian deformation model and a graph attention network to map tactile inputs to cage displacements, enabling real-time, camera-free inference and rendering without object-specific training.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of enabling accurate, real-time deformation inference for soft robots without collecting per-object deformation data or retraining, leveraging a static geometric proxy and tactile sensing to generalize to unseen robots.
AI.method: Integrates a flexible piezoresistive tactile sensor array with a geometry-aware, cage-based 3D Gaussian deformation model. Local tactile measurements map to low-dimensional cage control signals, which propagate to dense Gaussian primitives to yield global deformations. A graph attention network regresses cage displacements from tactile input, enforcing spatial smoothness via boundary-aware propagation. The system uses only a nominal STL proxy and tactile signals for zero-shot deformation reconstruction (bending/twisting) and real-time, photorealistic RGB rendering.
AI.result: Achieves real-time zero-shot deformation reconstruction with IoU 0.67, SSIM 0.65, and Chamfer distance 3.48 mm on unseen soft robots, demonstrating strong zero-shot generalization through tactile–geometry coupling.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework demonstrates effective zero-shot inference for unseen soft robots by uniting tactile sensing with a structured deformation representation (cage-based Gaussian model) and a graph-based regression to predict cage displacements, yielding plausible deformations and real-time rendering without object-specific deformation data.

title: TTQ: Activation-Aware Test-Time Quantization to Accelerate LLM Inference On The Fly
summary: To tackle the huge computational demand of large foundation models, activation-aware compression techniques without retraining have been introduced. However, since these methods highly rely on calibration data, domain shift issues may arise for unseen downstream tasks. We propose a test-time quantization (TTQ) framework which compresses large models on the fly at inference time to resolve this issue. With an efficient online calibration, instant activation-aware quantization can adapt every prompt regardless of the downstream tasks, yet achieving inference speedup. Several experiments demonstrate that TTQ can improve the quantization performance over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: Test-time quantization (TTQ) enables on-the-fly, activation-aware compression of large foundation models at inference time via online calibration, mitigating domain shift without retraining and improving quantization performance over baselines, while providing speedups.
AI.motivation: Activation-aware compression methods rely on calibration data and may suffer from domain shift on unseen downstream tasks; a retraining-free solution is needed to ensure robust compression.
AI.method: Introduce a TTQ framework that performs online calibration during inference to derive per-prompt quantization parameters and apply activation-aware quantization without retraining, compressing the model on the fly.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show TTQ achieves better quantization performance than state-of-the-art baselines and provides inference speedups under varying downstream tasks.
AI.conclusion: TTQ offers robust, retraining-free on-the-fly compression that adapts to downstream tasks via online calibration, mitigating calibration-data issues and delivering efficiency gains.

title: ItinBench: Benchmarking Planning Across Multiple Cognitive Dimensions with Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) with advanced cognitive capabilities are emerging as agents for various reasoning and planning tasks. Traditional evaluations often focus on specific reasoning or planning questions within controlled environments. Recent studies have explored travel planning as a medium to integrate various verbal reasoning tasks into real-world contexts. However, reasoning tasks extend beyond verbal reasoning alone, and a comprehensive evaluation of LLMs requires a testbed that incorporates tasks from multiple cognitive domains. To address this gap, we introduce ItinBench, a benchmark that features one task of spatial reasoning, i.e., route optimization, into trip itinerary planning while keeping the traditional verbal reasoning tasks. ItinBench evaluates various LLMs across diverse tasks simultaneously, including Llama 3.1 8B, Mistral Large, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and GPT family. Our findings reveal that LLMs struggle to maintain high and consistent performance when concurrently handling multiple cognitive dimensions. By incorporating tasks from distinct human-level cognitive domains, ItinBench provides new insights into building more comprehensive reasoning testbeds that better reflect real-world challenges. The code and dataset: https://ethanwtl.github.io/IBweb/

AI.tldr: ItinBench adds a spatial task (route optimization) into trip-itinerary planning to test multi-domain cognitive reasoning in LLMs; results show current models struggle to sustain high, consistent performance across tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations focus on single-domain verbal reasoning; a multi-domain testbed is needed to reflect real-world planning challenges.
AI.method: Proposes ItinBench, a benchmark combining itinerary planning with route optimization (spatial reasoning) and includes traditional verbal reasoning tasks; evaluates multiple LLMs (Llama 3.1 8B, Mistral Large, Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT family).
AI.result: Across tasks, LLMs struggle to maintain high and consistent performance when handling multiple cognitive dimensions; results demonstrate the difficulty and heterogeneity among models.
AI.conclusion: ItinBench offers a more comprehensive reasoning testbed that better reflects real-world challenges; suggests future work to develop multi-domain evaluation frameworks and improve LLMs' cross-domain reasoning abilities.

title: In-the-Wild Camouflage Attack on Vehicle Detectors through Controllable Image Editing
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have achieved remarkable success in computer vision but remain highly vulnerable to adversarial attacks. Among them, camouflage attacks manipulate an object's visible appearance to deceive detectors while remaining stealthy to humans. In this paper, we propose a new framework that formulates vehicle camouflage attacks as a conditional image-editing problem. Specifically, we explore both image-level and scene-level camouflage generation strategies, and fine-tune a ControlNet to synthesize camouflaged vehicles directly on real images. We design a unified objective that jointly enforces vehicle structural fidelity, style consistency, and adversarial effectiveness. Extensive experiments on the COCO and LINZ datasets show that our method achieves significantly stronger attack effectiveness, leading to more than 38% AP50 decrease, while better preserving vehicle structure and improving human-perceived stealthiness compared to existing approaches. Furthermore, our framework generalizes effectively to unseen black-box detectors and exhibits promising transferability to the physical world. Project page is available at https://humansensinglab.github.io/CtrlCamo

AI.tldr: A conditional image-editing framework (CtrlCamo) uses ControlNet to generate camouflaged vehicles directly on real images, optimizing structure fidelity, style consistency, and adversarial effectiveness to attack detectors. It achieves strong AP50 drops (>38%) on COCO/LINZ, preserves vehicle realism, and transfers to unseen black-box detectors and physical-world scenarios.
AI.motivation: DNNs for object detection are vulnerable to adversarial camouflage that remains stealthy to humans. There is a need for realistic, transferable camouflage attacks that preserve object structure while deceiving detectors, and that work in realistic scenes.
AI.method: Formulate camouflage as a conditional image-editing task. Explore image-level and scene-level camouflage generation; fine-tune a ControlNet to synthesize camouflaged vehicles directly on real images. Use a unified objective enforcing vehicle structural fidelity, style consistency, and adversarial effectiveness.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on COCO and LINZ show significant attack effectiveness gains, with AP50 decreasing by more than 38%. The camouflaged vehicles maintain structure better and appear more stealthy to humans compared to prior methods. The method generalizes to unseen black-box detectors and shows promising transferability to the physical world.
AI.conclusion: A unified, controllable image-editing framework can produce highly effective camouflage attacks that balance realism and adversarial impact, with strong transferability to new detectors and real-world scenarios.

title: On the size of k-irreducible triangulations
summary: A triangulation of a surface is k-irreducible if every non-contractible curve has length at least k and any edge contraction breaks this property. Equivalently, every edge belongs to a non-contractible curve of length k and there are no shorter non-contractible curves. We prove that a k-irreducible triangulation of a surface of genus g has $O(k^2g)$ triangles, which is optimal. This is an improvement over the previous best bound $k^{O(k)} g^2$ of Gao, Richter and Seymour [Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B, 1996].

AI.tldr: A k-irreducible triangulation on a genus g surface has O(k^2 g) triangles; this bound is tight and improves the previous O-bound of k^{O(k)} g^2.
AI.motivation: Understand structural size limits of triangulations constrained by non-contractible curves and edge contractions; contributes to structural graph theory on surfaces.
AI.method: Combinatorial/topological counting argument: exploit that every edge lies on a non-contractible length-k curve and that edge contraction cannot preserve this property; derive a global bound on triangle count; establish optimality via matching lower bound.
AI.result: Proved an O(k^2 g) upper bound on the number of triangles in a k-irreducible triangulation of a genus-g surface; the bound is asymptotically tight.
AI.conclusion: Quadratic in k and linear in g; improves the prior bound k^{O(k)} g^2; confirms tightness and advances the understanding of irreducible triangulations on surfaces.

title: Evolving Embodied Intelligence: Graph Neural Network--Driven Co-Design of Morphology and Control in Soft Robotics
