AI.motivation: Fill the lack of structured, real-clinical multimodal datasets for gastric cancer to advance VLMs that can reason across vision, text, and biochemical data within realistic workflows.
AI.method: Assemble 1.7k cases including resting/dynamic CT, endoscopy images, biochemical indicators, expert notes, and tumor bounding boxes. Systematically evaluate recent VLMs on five tasks—VQA, report generation, cross-modal retrieval, disease classification, and lesion localization—designed to mirror clinical workflow and reasoning.
AI.result: Not reported in the abstract; the work establishes a benchmark and evaluation framework to assess VLM capabilities and their ability to link biochemical signals with spatial and textual clinical information.
AI.conclusion: Gastric-X is proposed as a step toward aligning medical VLMs with physicians' cognitive and evidential reasoning, serving as a resource to guide future development of multimodal medical AI.

title: Real-Time Structural Detection for Indoor Navigation from 3D LiDAR Using Bird's-Eye-View Images
summary: Efficient structural perception is essential for mapping and autonomous navigation on resource-constrained robots. Existing 3D methods are computationally prohibitive, while traditional 2D geometric approaches lack robustness. This paper presents a lightweight, real-time framework that projects 3D LiDAR data into 2D Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) images to enable efficient detection of structural elements relevant to mapping and navigation. Within this representation, we systematically evaluate several feature extraction strategies, including classical geometric techniques (Hough Transform, RANSAC, and LSD) and a deep learning detector based on YOLO-OBB. The resulting detections are integrated through a spatiotemporal fusion module that improves stability and robustness across consecutive frames. Experiments conducted on a standard mobile robotic platform highlight clear performance trade-offs. Classical methods such as Hough and LSD provide fast responses but exhibit strong sensitivity to noise, with LSD producing excessive segment fragmentation that leads to system congestion. RANSAC offers improved robustness but fails to meet real-time constraints. In contrast, the YOLO-OBB-based approach achieves the best balance between robustness and computational efficiency, maintaining an end-to-end latency (satisfying 10 Hz operation) while effectively filtering cluttered observations in a low-power single-board computer (SBC) without using GPU acceleration. The main contribution of this work is a computationally efficient BEV-based perception pipeline enabling reliable real-time structural detection from 3D LiDAR on resource-constrained robotic platforms that cannot rely on GPU-intensive processing.

AI.tldr: Lightweight BEV-LiDAR perception pipeline for real-time structural element detection on resource-constrained robots; compares classical 2D methods (Hough, RANSAC, LSD) and a YOLO-OBB detector; achieves 10 Hz on SBC without GPU; YOLO-OBB offers best robustness-efficiency trade-off.
AI.motivation: Efficient and robust 3D perception is essential for mapping and autonomous navigation on resource-constrained robots. Full 3D methods are computationally heavy, while 2D geometric approaches can be brittle to noise. A BEV projection of LiDAR aims to balance accuracy and efficiency.
AI.method: Project 3D LiDAR data into 2D Bird's-Eye-View images. Systematically evaluate feature extraction strategies including Hough Transform, RANSAC, LSD, and a deep learning detector based on YOLO-OBB. Fuse detections through a spatiotemporal module to improve stability across frames. Validate on a mobile robot platform, comparing latency and robustness, particularly on a low-power SBC without GPU acceleration.
AI.result: Hough and LSD provide fast responses but are sensitive to noise; LSD causes excessive fragmentation leading to congestion. RANSAC offers better robustness but does not meet real-time constraints. YOLO-OBB achieves the best trade-off, delivering end-to-end 10 Hz latency on a low-power SBC without GPU and effectively filtering cluttered observations.
AI.conclusion: A BEV-based, computationally efficient perception pipeline enables reliable real-time structural detection from 3D LiDAR on resource-constrained robots that cannot rely on GPU acceleration.

title: LeWorldModel: Stable End-to-End Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture from Pixels
summary: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) offer a compelling framework for learning world models in compact latent spaces, yet existing methods remain fragile, relying on complex multi-term losses, exponential moving averages, pre-trained encoders, or auxiliary supervision to avoid representation collapse. In this work, we introduce LeWorldModel (LeWM), the first JEPA that trains stably end-to-end from raw pixels using only two loss terms: a next-embedding prediction loss and a regularizer enforcing Gaussian-distributed latent embeddings. This reduces tunable loss hyperparameters from six to one compared to the only existing end-to-end alternative. With ~15M parameters trainable on a single GPU in a few hours, LeWM plans up to 48x faster than foundation-model-based world models while remaining competitive across diverse 2D and 3D control tasks. Beyond control, we show that LeWM's latent space encodes meaningful physical structure through probing of physical quantities. Surprise evaluation confirms that the model reliably detects physically implausible events.

AI.tldr: LeWorldModel (LeWM) is a JEPA that trains end-to-end from raw pixels using only two losses, enabling stable training with fewer hyperparameters, a small model (~15M params), fast single-GPU training, and competitive performance on 2D/3D control tasks, with latent space that encodes physics and detects implausible events.
AI.motivation: Address instability and hyperparameter fragility of existing JEPA-based world models that rely on complex losses, exponential moving averages, pre-trained encoders, or external supervision; enable stable, end-to-end pixel-based world modeling with minimal supervision.
AI.method: Propose LeWM, a JEPA variant trained end-to-end from raw pixels using two loss terms: next-embedding prediction and a Gaussian latent-regularizer to prevent collapse. ~15M parameters, trainable on a single GPU in hours; claims up to 48× faster than foundation-model-based world models. Evaluated on diverse 2D and 3D control tasks; latent space is probeable for physical quantities and supports detection of physically implausible events.
AI.result: LeWM achieves competitive performance across 2D and 3D control tasks with stable end-to-end training from pixels, compact parameter count, and efficient training. The latent space encodes meaningful physical structure and supports surprise-like detection of implausible events; reduces loss hyperparameters from six to one.
AI.conclusion: LeWM demonstrates that a minimal two-term JEPA loss suffices for stable end-to-end world modeling from raw pixels, offering efficiency benefits and interpretable latent physics; further work could explore broader tasks, ablations, and robustness.

title: Utility-Guided Agent Orchestration for Efficient LLM Tool Use
summary: Tool-using large language model (LLM) agents often face a fundamental tension between answer quality and execution cost. Fixed workflows are stable but inflexible, while free-form multi-step reasoning methods such as ReAct may improve task performance at the expense of excessive tool calls, longer trajectories, higher token consumption, and increased latency. In this paper, we study agent orchestration as an explicit decision problem rather than leaving it entirely to prompt-level behavior. We propose a utility-guided orchestration policy that selects among actions such as respond, retrieve, tool call, verify, and stop by balancing estimated gain, step cost, uncertainty, and redundancy. Our goal is not to claim universally best task performance, but to provide a controllable and analyzable policy framework for studying quality-cost trade-offs in tool-using LLM agents. Experiments across direct answering, threshold control, fixed workflows, ReAct, and several policy variants show that explicit orchestration signals substantially affect agent behavior. Additional analyses on cost definitions, workflow fairness, and redundancy control further demonstrate that lightweight utility design can provide a defensible and practical mechanism for agent control.

AI.tldr: A utility-guided orchestration policy for tool-using LLM agents selects among respond, retrieve, tool call, verify, and stop to balance estimated gain, step cost, uncertainty, and redundancy, enabling controllable study of quality-cost trade-offs without claiming universal best.
AI.motivation: Addresses the tension between answer quality and execution cost in tool-using LLM agents. Fixed workflows are reliable but inflexible; free-form methods (e.g., ReAct) can improve task performance but incur higher tool usage, tokens, and latency. The paper advocates an explicit, analyzable decision framework for agent orchestration.
AI.method: Proposes an orchestration policy that considers actions (respond, retrieve, tool call, verify, stop) and selects them based on estimated gain, step cost, uncertainty, and redundancy. Evaluates across direct answering, threshold control, fixed workflows, ReAct, and various policy variants, and analyzes cost definitions, workflow fairness, and redundancy.
AI.result: Explicit orchestration signals significantly influence agent behavior. A lightweight utility design yields a defensible, practical mechanism for agent control and enables systematic study of quality-cost trade-offs across diverse workflows.
AI.conclusion: Utility-guided orchestration is a viable, controllable framework for researching quality-cost trade-offs in tool-using LLM agents. It does not claim universal best task performance but offers a flexible approach for analyzing decision-making under cost and uncertainty constraints.

title: ReXInTheWild: A Unified Benchmark for Medical Photograph Understanding
summary: Everyday photographs taken with ordinary cameras are already widely used in telemedicine and other online health conversations, yet no comprehensive benchmark evaluates whether vision-language models can interpret their medical content. Analyzing these images requires both fine-grained natural image understanding and domain-specific medical reasoning, a combination that challenges both general-purpose and specialized models. We introduce ReXInTheWild, a benchmark of 955 clinician-verified multiple-choice questions spanning seven clinical topics across 484 photographs sourced from the biomedical literature. When evaluated on ReXInTheWild, leading multimodal large language models show substantial performance variation: Gemini-3 achieves 78% accuracy, followed by Claude Opus 4.5 (72%) and GPT-5 (68%), while the medical specialist model MedGemma reaches only 37%. A systematic error analysis also reveals four categories of common errors, ranging from low-level geometric errors to high-level reasoning failures and requiring different mitigation strategies. ReXInTheWild provides a challenging, clinically grounded benchmark at the intersection of natural image understanding and medical reasoning. The dataset is available on HuggingFace.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark, ReXInTheWild, assesses vision-language models on everyday clinical photographs requiring medical reasoning; results show substantial gaps among models (Gemini-3 ~78%, Claude Opus 4.5 ~72%, GPT-5 ~68%), while a medical specialist model underperforms (~37%); four error categories identified; dataset released on HuggingFace.
AI.motivation: To test whether general-purpose and medical-focused vision-language models can interpret clinically relevant content in everyday photographs used in telemedicine, addressing the gap between natural image understanding and domain-specific medical reasoning.
AI.method: Benchmark dataset: 955 clinician-verified multiple-choice questions across 7 clinical topics, 484 photographs sourced from biomedical literature. Evaluated leading multimodal LLMs (e.g., Gemini-3, Claude Opus 4.5, GPT-5) and a medical specialist model (MedGemma). Included systematic error analysis identifying four error categories. Dataset available on HuggingFace.
AI.result: Performance varies substantially: Gemini-3 78% accuracy, Claude Opus 4.5 72%, GPT-5 68%, MedGemma 37%. Four error categories revealed, spanning low-level geometric errors to high-level reasoning failures, suggesting different mitigation needs.
AI.conclusion: ReXInTheWild provides a challenging, clinically grounded benchmark at the intersection of natural image understanding and medical reasoning and will guide future improvements in both general and medical-specific vision-language models; dataset is publicly accessible on HuggingFace.

title: Multi-Agent Motion Planning on Industrial Magnetic Levitation Platforms: A Hybrid ADMM-HOCBF approach
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures, accepted to the European Control Conference 2026
summary: This paper presents a novel hybrid motion planning method for holonomic multi-agent systems. The proposed decentralised model predictive control (MPC) framework tackles the intractability of classical centralised MPC for a growing number of agents while providing safety guarantees. This is achieved by combining a decentralised version of the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) with a centralised high-order control barrier function (HOCBF) architecture. Simulation results show significant improvement in scalability over classical centralised MPC. We validate the efficacy and real-time capability of the proposed method by developing a highly efficient C++ implementation and deploying the resulting trajectories on a real industrial magnetic levitation platform.

AI.tldr: Hybrid decentralized MPC using ADMM and centralized HOCBF for scalable, safe motion planning in holonomic multi-agent systems; validated in simulation and on a real magnetic levitation platform with real-time C++ implementation.
AI.motivation: Centralized MPC becomes intractable as the number of agents grows; need scalable planning with safety guarantees. A decentralised optimization (ADMM) coupled with a centralised safety layer (HOCBF) aims to retain guarantees while reducing computation.
AI.method: Integrates a decentralised ADMM-based decomposition of the MPC problem with a centralised high-order control barrier function (HOCBF) safety architecture; applicable to holonomic multi-agent systems; implemented in C++ for real-time performance.
AI.result: Simulation results indicate scalability improvements over classical centralized MPC; hardware validation demonstrates real-time capability on an industrial magnetic levitation platform.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields scalable, safe, real-time motion planning for multi-agent systems, with successful hardware validation and potential for industrial deployment.

title: DPxFin: Adaptive Differential Privacy for Anti-Money Laundering Detection via Reputation-Weighted Federated Learning
comment: Accepted at AI FOR FINANCIAL FRAUD DETECTION & PREVENTION AT ACM ICAIF-25
summary: In the modern financial system, combating money laundering is a critical challenge complicated by data privacy concerns and increasingly complex fraud transaction patterns. Although federated learning (FL) is a promising problem-solving approach as it allows institutions to train their models without sharing their data, it has the drawback of being prone to privacy leakage, specifically in tabular data forms like financial data. To address this, we propose DPxFin, a novel federated framework that integrates reputation-guided adaptive differential privacy. Our approach computes client reputation by evaluating the alignment between locally trained models and the global model. Based on this reputation, we dynamically assign differential privacy noise to client updates, enhancing privacy while maintaining overall model utility. Clients with higher reputations receive lower noise to amplify their trustworthy contributions, while low-reputation clients are allocated stronger noise to mitigate risk. We validate DPxFin on the Anti-Money Laundering (AML) dataset under both IID and non-IID settings using Multi Layer Perceptron (MLP). Experimental analysis established that our approach has a more desirable trade-off between accuracy and privacy than those of traditional FL and fixed-noise Differential Privacy (DP) baselines, where performance improvements were consistent, even though on a modest scale. Moreover, DPxFin does withstand tabular data leakage attacks, proving its effectiveness under real-world financial conditions.

AI.tldr: A reputation-guided adaptive differential privacy (DP) approach in federated learning (FL) for AML on tabular data improves the privacy-utility trade-off by weighting client contributions with a reputation score, reducing noise for trustworthy clients and increasing noise for less trusted ones; outperforms standard FL and fixed-noise DP and is robust to tabular leakage attacks.
AI.motivation: In financial tabular data, FL faces privacy leakage risks; fixed DP noise can degrade utility, and naive FL can expose sensitive information. Adaptive, reputation-based DP aims to preserve model performance while strengthening privacy.
AI.method: Compute client reputation from the alignment between locally trained models and the global model, then adaptively assign DP noise to client updates based on reputation (high-reputation clients receive less noise, low-reputation clients receive more). Evaluated on an AML dataset using an MLP under IID and non-IID settings.
AI.result: DPxFin achieves a more favorable accuracy-privacy trade-off than traditional FL and fixed-noise DP baselines; improvements are consistent though modest in scale; also demonstrates resistance to tabular data leakage attacks.
AI.conclusion: DPxFin demonstrates that reputation-guided adaptive DP within FL can enhance privacy without sacrificing utility in tabular financial data and is robust to leakage attacks, indicating potential for real-world AML fraud prevention, with scalability considerations for larger deployments.

title: On the Ability of Transformers to Verify Plans
summary: Transformers have shown inconsistent success in AI planning tasks, and theoretical understanding of when generalization should be expected has been limited. We take important steps towards addressing this gap by analyzing the ability of decoder-only models to verify whether a given plan correctly solves a given planning instance. To analyse the general setting where the number of objects -- and thus the effective input alphabet -- grows at test time, we introduce C*-RASP, an extension of C-RASP designed to establish length generalization guarantees for transformers under the simultaneous growth in sequence length and vocabulary size. Our results identify a large class of classical planning domains for which transformers can provably learn to verify long plans, and structural properties that significantly affects the learnability of length generalizable solutions. Empirical experiments corroborate our theory.

AI.tldr: Decouples input length and vocabulary growth to study when decoder-only transformers can verify long planning solutions; introduces C*-RASP to obtain length generalization guarantees under simultaneous sequence-length and vocabulary growth; identifies conditions and domains where provable verification is feasible and shows empirical support.
AI.motivation: A theoretical and empirical study of when transformer-based decoders can generalize to verify long planning solutions as problem size (number of objects) grows, addressing the gap in understanding planning generalization.
AI.method: Extend C-RASP to C*-RASP to enable length generalization guarantees under joint growth of sequence length and vocabulary; analyze classical planning domains to identify properties that enable learnability; derive provable guarantees for plan verification; run empirical experiments to validate theory.
AI.result: A large class of classical planning domains admits provable learnability of long-plan verification by transformers; specific structural properties significantly affect learnability; empirical results corroborate the theoretical guarantees.
AI.conclusion: The work provides theoretical guarantees for length generalization in decoder-only transformers for plan verification in planning tasks and highlights domain properties that enable or hinder learnability, supported by experiments.

title: Recognising BSL Fingerspelling in Continuous Signing Sequences
summary: Fingerspelling is a critical component of British Sign Language (BSL), used to spell proper names, technical terms, and words that lack established lexical signs. Fingerspelling recognition is challenging due to the rapid pace of signing and common letter omissions by native signers, while existing BSL fingerspelling datasets are either small in scale or temporally and letter-wise inaccurate. In this work, we introduce a new large-scale BSL fingerspelling dataset, FS23K, constructed using an iterative annotation framework. In addition, we propose a fingerspelling recognition model that explicitly accounts for bi-manual interactions and mouthing cues. As a result, with refined annotations, our approach halves the character error rate (CER) compared to the prior state of the art on fingerspelling recognition. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of our method and highlight its potential to support future research in sign language understanding and scalable, automated annotation pipelines. The project page can be found at https://taeinkwon.com/projects/fs23k/.

AI.tldr: Introduces FS23K, a large-scale British Sign Language fingerspelling dataset built via iterative annotation; proposes a biomanchual and mouthing-aware recognition model; reports a 2x CER reduction versus prior SOTA, enabling scalable automated annotation pipelines.
AI.motivation: BSL fingerspelling recognition suffers from rapid signing, letter omissions, and limited, unreliable datasets; a larger, well-annotated dataset and a model that leverages bi-manual and mouthing cues can significantly advance sign language understanding.
AI.method: Construct FS23K using an iterative annotation framework to ensure scalability and accuracy; develop a recognition model that explicitly models bi-manual interactions and mouthing cues; refine annotations to improve CER.
AI.result: CER is halved compared to prior state of the art on fingerspelling recognition; improved performance demonstrates the effectiveness of the dataset and model; suggests potential for scalable automated annotation pipelines and future sign-language research.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that large, well-annotated datasets combined with perception models that incorporate bi-manual and mouthing information can substantially improve fingerspelling recognition and enable scalable sign-language understanding research.

title: Beyond detection: cooperative multi-agent reasoning for rapid onboard EO crisis response
comment: Accepted for presentation at the ESA's 4S Symposium 2026 Conference (see https://atpi.eventsair.com/4s-symposium-2026/)
summary:
Rapid identification of hazardous events is essential for next-generation Earth Observation (EO) missions supporting disaster response. However, current monitoring pipelines remain largely ground-centric, introducing latency due to downlink limitations, multi-source data fusion constraints, and the computational cost of exhaustive scene analysis.
  This work proposes a hierarchical multi-agent architecture for onboard EO processing under strict resource and bandwidth constraints. The system enables the exploitation of complementary multimodal observations by coordinating specialized AI agents within an event-driven decision pipeline. AI agents can be deployed across multiple nodes in a distributed setting, such as satellite platforms. An Early Warning agent generates fast hypotheses from onboard observations and selectively activates domain-specific analysis agents, while a Decision agent consolidates the evidence to issue a final alert. The architecture combines vision-language models, traditional remote sensing analysis tools, and role-specialized agents to enable structured reasoning over multimodal observations while minimizing unnecessary computation.
  A proof-of-concept implementation was executed on the engineering model of an edge-computing platform currently deployed in orbit, using representative satellite data. Experiments on wildfire and flood monitoring scenarios show that the proposed routing-based pipeline significantly reduces computational overhead while maintaining coherent decision outputs, demonstrating the feasibility of distributed agent-based reasoning for future autonomous EO constellations.

AI.tldr: An onboard, hierarchical multi-agent system coordinates fast Early Warning hypotheses with domain-specific analyzers to enable low-latency, multimodal hazard detection on resource-constrained satellites, reducing compute while preserving decision quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome latency and computational bottlenecks of ground-centric disaster monitoring by moving processing onboard under strict resource and bandwidth limits, leveraging multimodal data and distributed AI agents.
AI.method: Proposes a distributed, event-driven pipeline: Early Warning agent generates fast hypotheses from onboard observations and activates specialized analysis agents; a Decision agent aggregates evidence to issue alerts. Combines vision-language models with traditional remote sensing tools across multi-node onboard platforms; validated via a proof-of-concept on an orbit-deployed edge computer using wildfire and flood data.
AI.result: Significant reduction in computational overhead with coherent decision outputs; demonstrates feasibility of distributed agent-based reasoning for autonomous EO constellations; proof-of-concept on an orbital edge platform with representative satellite data.
AI.conclusion: Distributed onboard, multimodal, agent-based reasoning is viable for next-generation EO missions, enabling rapid hazard detection under stringent resources while maintaining decision quality.

title: MSNet and LS-Net: Scalable Multi-Scale Multi-Representation Networks for Time Series Classification
summary:
Time series classification (TSC) performance depends not only on architectural design but also on the diversity of input representations. In this work, we propose a scalable multi-scale convolutional framework that systematically integrates structured multi-representation inputs for univariate time series.
  We introduce two architectures: MSNet, a hierarchical multi-scale convolutional network optimized for robustness and calibration, and LS-Net, a lightweight variant designed for efficiency-aware deployment. In addition, we adapt LiteMV -- originally developed for multivariate inputs -- to operate on multi-representation univariate signals, enabling cross-representation interaction.
  We evaluate all models across 142 benchmark datasets under a unified experimental protocol. Critical Difference analysis confirms statistically significant performance differences among the top models. Results show that LiteMV achieves the highest mean accuracy, MSNet provides superior probabilistic calibration (lowest NLL), and LS-Net offers the best efficiency-accuracy tradeoff. Pareto analysis further demonstrates that multi-representation multi-scale modeling yields a flexible design space that can be tuned for accuracy-oriented, calibration-oriented, or resource-constrained settings.
  These findings establish scalable multi-representation multi-scale learning as a principled and practical direction for modern TSC. Reference implementation of MSNet and LS-Net is available at: https://github.com/alagoz/msnet-lsnet-tsc

AI.tldr: Multi-representation, multi-scale framework for univariate time series with MSNet and LS-Net; LiteMV adapted for multi-representation inputs. Evaluated on 142 datasets; LiteMV top on mean accuracy, MSNet best calibration, LS-Net best efficiency; Pareto analysis shows flexible design space; open-source implementation available.
AI.motivation: TSC performance depends on input representations; aim to harmonize accuracy, calibration, and efficiency via scalable multi-representation multi-scale learning.
AI.method: Propose MSNet (hierarchical multi-scale CNN) and LS-Net (lightweight variant); adapt LiteMV to univariate multi-representation inputs for cross-representation interaction; comprehensive evaluation on 142 datasets; use Critical Difference and Pareto analyses; provide public code.
AI.result: LiteMV achieves highest mean accuracy; MSNet has superior probabilistic calibration (lowest NLL); LS-Net offers best efficiency-accuracy tradeoff; multi-representation multi-scale modeling yields flexible design space; Pareto analysis confirms tunable settings for different goals.
AI.conclusion: Scalable multi-representation multi-scale learning is a principled, practical direction for modern time series classification; reference implementations available at the linked GitHub repository.

title: Experience is the Best Teacher: Motivating Effective Exploration in Reinforcement Learning for LLMs
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) with rubric-based rewards has recently shown remarkable progress in enhancing general reasoning capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet still suffers from ineffective exploration confined to curent policy distribution. In fact, RL optimization can be viewed as steering the policy toward an ideal distribution that maximizes the rewards, while effective exploration should align efforts with desired target. Leveraging this insight, we propose HeRL, a Hindsight experience guided Reinforcement Learning framework to bootstrap effective exploration by explicitly telling LLMs the desired behaviors specified in rewards. Concretely, HeRL treats failed trajectories along with their unmet rubrics as hindsight experience, which serves as in-context guidance for the policy to explore desired responses beyond its current distribution. Additionally, we introduce a bonus reward to incentivize responses with greater potential for improvement under such guidance. HeRL facilitates effective learning from desired high quality samples without repeated trial-and-error from scratch, yielding a more accurate estimation of the expected gradient theoretically. Extensive experiments across various benchmarks demonstrate that HeRL achieves superior performance gains over baselines, and can further benefit from experience guided self-improvement at test time. Our code is available at https://github.com/sikelifei/HeRL.

AI.tldr: HeRL uses hindsight experience to guide RL with rubric-based rewards for LLMs, adding bonus rewards to improve exploration; treats failed trajectories as guidance to improve policy, leading to better gradient estimates and improved performance.
AI.motivation: Mitigate ineffective exploration in RL when optimizing rubric-based rewards for LLMs; align exploration with desired target behaviors; leverage hindsight from failed attempts to reuse information.
AI.method: Hindsight experience guided Reinforcement Learning (HeRL): convert unmet rubrics in failed trajectories into hindsight guidance via in-context prompts; treat failed outcomes as guidance to explore desired responses beyond current policy; add a bonus reward to incentivize improvements; learn from high-quality samples to yield more accurate gradient estimates; supports experience-guided self-improvement at test time.
AI.result: Empirical results across benchmarks show HeRL achieves superior performance gains over baselines; demonstrates effectiveness of hindsight guidance and bonus reward; code released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: HeRL enables effective learning from high-quality samples, reduces extensive trial-and-error, improves gradient estimation, and can be combined with experience-guided self-improvement at test time.

title: SurfaceXR: Fusing Smartwatch IMUs and Egocentric Hand Pose for Seamless Surface Interactions
comment: Accepted to IEEE VR 2026 as a TVCG journal paper
summary: Mid-air gestures in Extended Reality (XR) often cause fatigue and imprecision. Surface-based interactions offer improved accuracy and comfort, but current egocentric vision methods struggle due to hand tracking challenges and unreliable surface plane estimation. We introduce SurfaceXR, a sensor fusion approach combining headset-based hand tracking with smartwatch IMU data to enable robust inputs on everyday surfaces. Our insight is that these modalities are complementary: hand tracking provides 3D positional data while IMUs capture high-frequency motion. A 21-participant study validates SurfaceXR's effectiveness for touch tracking and 8-class gesture recognition, demonstrating significant improvements over single-modality approaches.

AI.tldr: SurfaceXR fuses headset hand tracking with smartwatch IMU to enable robust touch and 8-class gestures on everyday surfaces, outperforming single-modality baselines.
AI.motivation: Mid-air XR gestures cause fatigue and imprecision; surface-based interactions are more accurate and comfortable, but egocentric vision struggles with hand tracking and reliable surface plane estimation on everyday surfaces.
AI.method: Multimodal sensor fusion combining headset-based 3D hand tracking with high-frequency smartwatch IMU data; integration yields robust input on everyday surfaces; evaluated with 21 participants for touch tracking and 8-class gesture recognition.
AI.result: Significant improvements over single-modality approaches for touch tracking and 8-class gesture recognition across the 21 participants.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that complementary headset and smartwatch sensors enable robust, comfortable surface-based XR interactions on everyday surfaces, mitigating fatigue and precision issues of mid-air gestures.

title: Radar-Inertial Odometry with Online Spatio-Temporal Calibration via Continuous-Time IMU Modeling
summary: Radar-Inertial Odometry (RIO) has emerged as a robust alternative to vision- and LiDAR-based odometry in challenging conditions such as low light, fog, featureless environments, or in adverse weather. However, many existing RIO approaches assume known radar-IMU extrinsic calibration or rely on sufficient motion excitation for online extrinsic estimation, while temporal misalignment between sensors is often neglected or treated independently. In this work, we present a RIO framework that performs joint online spatial and temporal calibration within a factor-graph optimization formulation, based on continuous-time modeling of inertial measurements using uniform cubic B-splines. The proposed continuous-time representation of acceleration and angular velocity accurately captures the asynchronous nature of radar-IMU measurements, enabling reliable convergence of both the temporal offset and extrinsic calibration parameters, without relying on scan matching, target tracking, or environment-specific assumptions.

AI.tldr: A continuous-time radar–inertial odometry framework that jointly estimates online spatial (extrinsic) calibration and temporal offset between radar and IMU using a factor-graph and uniform cubic B-splines to model inertial signals.
AI.motivation: RIO methods often assume known extrinsics or require sufficient motion for online extrinsic estimation, and they typically ignore or treat temporally misaligned data separately. There is a need for online joint spatial-temporal calibration that works in challenging conditions without relying on scan matching or environment-specific cues.
AI.method: The approach performs joint online spatial and temporal calibration within a factor-graph optimization, using continuous-time modeling of inertial measurements with uniform cubic B-splines to represent acceleration and angular velocity. This captures radar–IMU asynchrony and enables convergence of both temporal offset and extrinsic calibration without scan matching, target tracking, or environment-specific assumptions.
AI.result: The framework enables reliable convergence of the temporal offset and extrinsic calibration parameters and does not rely on scan matching, target tracking, or environment-specific assumptions, while operating online.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a unified continuous-time RIO framework that handles asynchronous radar–IMU data and enables online joint calibration of spatial and temporal offsets, enhancing robustness in challenging sensing conditions.

title: Ternary Gamma Semirings: From Neural Implementation to Categorical Foundations
summary: This paper establishes a theoretical framework connecting neural network learning with abstract algebraic structures. We first present a minimal counterexample demonstrating that standard neural networks completely fail on compositional generalization tasks (0% accuracy). By introducing a logical constraint -- the Ternary Gamma Semiring -- the same architecture learns a perfectly structured feature space, achieving 100% accuracy on novel combinations. We prove that this learned feature space constitutes a finite commutative ternary $Γ$-semiring, whose ternary operation implements the majority vote rule. Comparing with the recently established classification of Gokavarapu et al., we show that this structure corresponds precisely to the Boolean-type ternary $Γ$-semiring with $|T|=4$, $|Γ|=1$}, which is unique up to isomorphism in their enumeration. Our findings reveal three profound conclusions: (i) the success of neural networks can be understood as an approximation of mathematically ``natural'' structures; (ii) learned representations generalize because they internalize algebraic axioms (symmetry, idempotence, majority property); (iii) logical constraints guide networks to converge to these canonical forms. This work provides a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding neural network generalization and inaugurates the new interdisciplinary direction of Computational $Γ$-Algebra.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework linking neural networks to a finite commutative ternary Γ-semiring via a Ternary Gamma Semiring constraint, showing 0% baseline on compositional generalization and 100% accuracy on novel combinations, with canonical algebraic structure and Computational Γ-Algebra direction.
AI.motivation: Understand neural network generalization through algebraic axioms; explain compositional generalization gap; unify machine learning with abstract algebra under a Γ-semiring framework.
AI.method: Present a minimal counterexample to standard networks; introduce a logical constraint named the Ternary Gamma Semiring; prove the learned feature space forms a finite commutative ternary Γ-semiring; show its ternary operation implements majority vote; compare to the classification in Gokavarapu et al. and identify the corresponding Boolean-type ternary Γ-semiring (|T|=4, |Γ|=1), unique up to isomorphism.
AI.result: Under the constraint, the architecture achieves 100% accuracy on novel compositions; mathematically proves the learned feature space is a finite commutative ternary Γ-semiring with a majority-operation; aligns with the specific Boolean-type semiring class in prior work; establishes a canonical algebraic form for learned representations.
AI.conclusion: Three key takeaways: (i) neural networks may approximate mathematically natural algebraic structures; (ii) representation learning generalizes by internalizing algebraic axioms (symmetry, idempotence, majority); (iii) logical constraints steer networks toward canonical algebraic forms, inaugurating Computational Γ-Algebra as an interdisciplinary research direction.

title: DIAL-KG: Schema-Free Incremental Knowledge Graph Construction via Dynamic Schema Induction and Evolution-Intent Assessment
summary:
Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are foundational to applications such as search, question answering, and recommendation. Conventional knowledge graph construction methods are predominantly static, rely ing on a single-step construction from a fixed corpus with a prede f
ined schema. However, such methods are suboptimal for real-world sce narios where data arrives dynamically, as incorporating new informa tion requires complete and computationally expensive graph reconstruc tions. Furthermore, predefined schemas hinder the flexibility of knowl edge graph construction. To address these limitations, we introduce DIAL KG, a closed-loop framework for incremental KG construction orches trated by a Meta-Knowledge Base (MKB). The framework oper ates in a three-stage cycle: (i) Dual-Track Extraction, which ensures knowledge completeness by defaulting to triple generation and switching to event extraction for complex knowledge; (ii) Governance Adjudica tion, which ensures the fidelity and currency of extracted facts to prevent hallucinations and knowledge staleness; and (iii) Schema Evolution, in which new schemas are induced from validated knowledge to guide subsequent construction cycles, and knowledge from the current round is incrementally applied to the existing KG. Extensive experiments demon strate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in the quality of both the constructed graph and the induced schemas.

AI.tldr: DIAL KG is a closed-loop, incremental KG construction framework driven by a Meta-Knowledge Base (MKB), featuring a three-stage cycle—Dual-Track Extraction, Governance Adjudication, and Schema Evolution—that achieves state-of-the-art quality in both constructed graphs and induced schemas.
AI.motivation: Conventional KG construction is static and costly when data arrives dynamically, and predefined schemas limit flexibility; hallucinations and knowledge staleness undermine quality. An incremental, schema-evolving framework is needed to maintain up-to-date, high-quality KGs with adaptable schemas.
AI.method: Three-stage cycle: (i) Dual-Track Extraction—default triple generation for completeness, switching to event extraction for complex knowledge; (ii) Governance Adjudication—checks fidelity and currency to prevent hallucinations and staleness; (iii) Schema Evolution—induces new schemas from validated knowledge to guide future rounds; integrates current round knowledge into the existing KG via the MKB.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance in both the quality of the constructed graph and the induced schemas.
AI.conclusion: DIAL KG provides an effective closed-loop, incremental KG construction paradigm with adaptive schema evolution and governance, improving quality and adaptability in dynamic data settings.

title: dinov3.seg: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation with DINOv3
summary: Open-Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation (OVSS) assigns pixel-level labels from an open set of text-defined categories, demanding reliable generalization to unseen classes at inference. Although modern vision-language models (VLMs) support strong open-vocabulary recognition, their representations learned through global contrastive objectives remain suboptimal for dense prediction, prompting many OVSS methods to depend on limited adaptation or refinement of image-text similarity maps. This, in turn, restricts spatial precision and robustness in complex, cluttered scenes. We introduce dinov3.seg, extending dinov3.txt into a dedicated framework for OVSS. Our contributions are four-fold. First, we design a task-specific architecture tailored to this backbone, systematically adapting established design principles from prior open-vocabulary segmentation work. Second, we jointly leverage text embeddings aligned with both the global [CLS] token and local patch-level visual features from ViT-based encoder, effectively combining semantic discrimination with fine-grained spatial locality. Third, unlike prior approaches that rely primarily on post hoc similarity refinement, we perform early refinement of visual representations prior to image-text interaction, followed by late refinement of the resulting image-text correlation features, enabling more accurate and robust dense predictions in cluttered scenes. Finally, we propose a high-resolution local-global inference strategy based on sliding-window aggregation, which preserves spatial detail while maintaining global context. We conduct extensive experiments on five widely adopted OVSS benchmarks to evaluate our approach. The results demonstrate its effectiveness and robustness, consistently outperforming current state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: dinov3.seg extends dinov3.txt to open-vocabulary semantic segmentation with a task-specific architecture, fusing global CLS and patch-level text-aligned features, applying early visual refinement before image-text interaction and late refinement of cross-modal features, plus sliding-window high-resolution local-global inference. It achieves state-of-the-art performance across five OVSS benchmarks, showing robustness in cluttered scenes.
AI.motivation: Open-vocabulary semantic segmentation (OVSS) requires reliable generalization to unseen classes. Current VLMs trained with global contrastive objectives are suboptimal for dense prediction and rely on limited adaptation, leading to poor spatial precision in cluttered scenes.
AI.method: Introduce dinov3.seg, a task-tailored architecture for OVSS based on the dinov3 backbone. Jointly align text embeddings with both the global [CLS] token and local patch-level visual features from a ViT encoder. Perform early refinement of visual representations before image-text interaction, followed by late refinement of image-text correlation features. Employ a high-resolution local-global inference strategy via sliding-window aggregation to preserve spatial detail while maintaining global context.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five widely adopted OVSS benchmarks show that dinov3.seg consistently outperforms current state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating robustness in cluttered scenes and strong open-vocabulary segmentation performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively leverages both global and local semantic information, with staged refinement and sliding-window inference enabling precise dense predictions in open-vocabulary segmentation. This sets a new strong baseline and suggests a promising direction for open-vocabulary dense vision tasks.

title: GustPilot: A Hierarchical DRL-INDI Framework for Wind-Resilient Quadrotor Navigation
summary: Wind disturbances remain a key barrier to reliable autonomous navigation for lightweight quadrotors, where the rapidly varying airflow can destabilize both planning and tracking. This paper introduces GustPilot, a hierarchical wind-resilient navigation stack in which a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) policy generates inertial-frame velocity reference for gate traversal. At the same time, a geometric Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) controller provides low-level tracking with fast residual disturbance rejection. The INDI layer achieves this by providing incremental feedback on both specific linear acceleration and angular acceleration rate, using onboard sensor measurements to reject wind disturbances rapidly. Robustness is obtained through a two-level strategy, wind-aware planning learned via fan-jet domain randomization during training, and rapid execution-time disturbance rejection by the INDI tracking controller. We evaluate GustPilot in real flights on a 50g quad-copter platform against a DRL-PID baseline across four scenarios ranging from no-wind to fully dynamic conditions with a moving gate and a moving disturbance source. Despite being trained only in a minimal single-gate and single-fan setup, the policy generalizes to significantly more complex environments (up to six gates and four fans) without retraining. Across 80 experiments, DRL-INDI achieves a 94.7% versus 55.0% for DRL-PID as average Overall Success Rate (OSR), reduces tracking RMSE up to 50%, and sustains speeds up to 1.34 m/s under wind disturbances up to 3.5 m/s. These results demonstrate that combining DRL-based velocity planning with structured INDI disturbance rejection provides a practical and generalizable approach to wind-resilient autonomous flight navigation.

AI.tldr: A wind-resilient navigation stack, GustPilot, combines DRL-planned inertial-frame velocity with an INDI controller for fast disturbance rejection, achieving robust, real-world performance and generalization in wind-rich gate navigation.
AI.motivation: Lightweight quadrotors are highly sensitive to rapidly varying wind, which destabilizes planning and tracking. A robust architecture balancing learning-based planning with structured control is needed to ensure reliability in windy, dynamic environments.
AI.method: A hierarchical stack where a DRL policy outputs inertial-frame velocity references for gate traversal, while a geometric INDI controller provides fast, incremental disturbance rejection using onboard sensors. Robustness is enhanced by wind-aware planning trained with fan-jet domain randomization; real-flights validate performance against a DRL-PID baseline across varied wind and moving gate scenarios.
AI.result: In 80 flights, DRL-INDI achieved an average Overall Success Rate of 94.7% compared with 55.0% for DRL-PID. Tracking RMSE reduced by up to 50%. The system sustained speeds up to 1.34 m/s under wind disturbances up to 3.5 m/s. The policy trained in a minimal single-gate, single-fan setup generalized to environments with up to six gates and four fans.
AI.conclusion: Combining DRL-based velocity planning with a structured INDI disturbance rejection yields a practical, generalizable approach to wind-resilient autonomous quadrotor navigation, enabling reliable flight in dynamic wind fields with limited training setups.

title: A General Deep Learning Framework for Wireless Resource Allocation under Discrete Constraints
summary: While deep learning (DL)-based methods have achieved remarkable success in continuous wireless resource allocation, efficient solutions for problems involving discrete variables remain challenging. This is primarily due to the zero-gradient issue in backpropagation, the difficulty of enforcing intricate constraints with discrete variables, and the inability in generating solutions with non-same-parameter-same-decision (non-SPSD) property. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a general DL framework by introducing the support set to represent the discrete variables. We model the elements of the support set as random variables and learn their joint probability distribution. By factorizing the joint probability as the product of conditional probabilities, each conditional probability is sequentially learned. This probabilistic modeling directly tackles all the aforementioned challenges of DL for handling discrete variables. By operating on probability distributions instead of hard binary decisions, the framework naturally avoids the zero-gradient issue. During the learning of the conditional probabilities, discrete constraints can be seamlessly enforced by masking out infeasible solutions. Moreover, with a dynamic context embedding that captures the evolving discrete solutions, the non-SPSD property is inherently provided by the proposed framework. We apply the proposed framework to two representative mixed-discrete wireless resource allocation problems: (a) joint user association and beamforming in cell-free systems, and (b) joint antenna positioning and beamforming in movable antenna-aided systems. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed DL framework consistently outperforms existing baselines in terms of both system performance and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: A general DL framework for discrete variables in wireless resource allocation using a probabilistic support-set model, factorized joint distribution, and sequential learning of conditional probabilities; leverages distribution-level learning, constraint masking, and dynamic context embedding to address zero-gradient, feasibility enforcement, and non-SPSD properties; validated on two mixed-discrete problems with improved performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address the core challenges of applying deep learning to problems with discrete decision variables in wireless resource allocation: zero-gradient backpropagation, difficulty enforcing complex discrete constraints, and ensuring solutions exhibit non-same-parameter-same-decision (non-SPSD) property.
AI.method: Represent discrete variables via a support set treated as random variables and model their joint distribution. Factorize the joint distribution into products of conditional probabilities and learn these conditionals sequentially. Operate on probability distributions rather than hard decisions to avoid zero-gradient issues. Enforce discrete constraints by masking infeasible solutions during learning. Use a dynamic context embedding to capture evolving discrete solutions, inherently providing the non-SPSD property. Apply framework to two problems: (a) joint user association and beamforming in cell-free systems; (b) joint antenna positioning and beamforming in movable antenna-aided systems.
AI.result: Simulation results show the proposed framework consistently outperforms existing baselines in both system performance and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed probabilistic, sequentially learned discrete-variable DL framework provides a general solution to the key challenges of DL for discrete wireless resource allocation, delivering performance gains and improved efficiency while inherently enforcing constraints and ensuring non-SPSD behavior.

title: Pitfalls in Evaluating Interpretability Agents
summary: Automated interpretability systems aim to reduce the need for human labor and scale analysis to increasingly large models and diverse tasks. Recent efforts toward this goal leverage large language models (LLMs) at increasing levels of autonomy, ranging from fixed one-shot workflows to fully autonomous interpretability agents. This shift creates a corresponding need to scale evaluation approaches to keep pace with both the volume and complexity of generated explanations. We investigate this challenge in the context of automated circuit analysis -- explaining the roles of model components when performing specific tasks. To this end, we build an agentic system in which a research agent iteratively designs experiments and refines hypotheses. When evaluated against human expert explanations across six circuit analysis tasks in the literature, the system appears competitive. However, closer examination reveals several pitfalls of replication-based evaluation: human expert explanations can be subjective or incomplete, outcome-based comparisons obscure the research process, and LLM-based systems may reproduce published findings via memorization or informed guessing. To address some of these pitfalls, we propose an unsupervised intrinsic evaluation based on the functional interchangeability of model components. Our work demonstrates fundamental challenges in evaluating complex automated interpretability systems and reveals key limitations of replication-based evaluation.

AI.tldr: An LLM-based agentic system for automated circuit analysis shows competitive results with human experts but reveals evaluation pitfalls; proposes an unsupervised intrinsic evaluation via component interchangeability to address replication-based evaluation limits.
AI.motivation: To scale interpretability evaluation for increasingly autonomous LLM-driven analysis and avoid biases and limitations of human-centric, replication-based assessments; using automated circuit analysis as a testbed to study how explanations of model components can be evaluated.
AI.method: Develop an agentic research agent that iteratively designs experiments and refines hypotheses. Compare its explanations to human experts across six circuit-analysis tasks. Critically analyze replication-based evaluation; propose an unsupervised intrinsic evaluation based on functional interchangeability of model components.
AI.result: The agentic system appears competitive with human expert explanations on six tasks, but deeper analysis uncovers pitfalls of replication-based evaluation, including subjectivity/incompleteness of human explanations, outcome-focused comparisons that ignore the research process, and the risk of memorization or guessing by LLMs. The authors propose an unsupervised intrinsic evaluation using functional interchangeability to address these issues.
AI.conclusion: Replication-based evaluation has fundamental limitations for complex automated interpretability systems. An intrinsic, unsupervised evaluation framework based on component interchangeability can better assess how different parts of the system contribute to explanations and robustness across tasks.

title: Pedestrian Crossing Intent Prediction via Psychological Features and Transformer Fusion
comment: Accepted to IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV) 2026. 8 pages, 3 figures
summary: Pedestrian intention prediction needs to be accurate for autonomous vehicles to navigate safely in urban environments. We present a lightweight, socially informed architecture for pedestrian intention prediction. It fuses four behavioral streams (attention, position, situation, and interaction) using highway encoders, a compact 4-token Transformer, and global self-attention pooling. To quantify uncertainty, we incorporate two complementary heads: a variational bottleneck whose KL divergence captures epistemic uncertainty, and a Mahalanobis distance detector that identifies distributional shift. Together, these components yield calibrated probabilities and actionable risk scores without compromising efficiency. On the PSI 1.0 benchmark, our model outperforms recent vision language models by achieving 0.9 F1, 0.94 AUC-ROC, and 0.78 MCC by using only structured, interpretable features. On the more diverse PSI 2.0 dataset, where, to the best of our knowledge, no prior results exist, we establish a strong initial baseline of 0.78 F1 and 0.79 AUC-ROC. Selective prediction based on Mahalanobis scores increases test accuracy by up to 0.4 percentage points at 80% coverage. Qualitative attention heatmaps further show how the model shifts its cross-stream focus under ambiguity. The proposed approach is modality-agnostic, easy to integrate with vision language pipelines, and suitable for risk-aware intent prediction on resource-constrained platforms.

AI.tldr: Lightweight, socially informed pedestrian intention predictor using four behavioral streams fused with highway encoders and a compact Transformer, plus dual uncertainty heads (variational bottleneck with KL and a Mahalanobis detector). Achieves strong calibrated risks on PSI 1.0/2.0 with selective prediction and interpretability via attention heatmaps; modality-agnostic and suitable for resource-constrained platforms.
AI.motivation: Safe autonomous navigation in urban environments requires accurate, calibrated pedestrian intention prediction and uncertainty estimation, while maintaining efficiency on resource-constrained platforms.
AI.method: Four streams (attention, position, situation, interaction) fused via highway encoders into a compact 4-token Transformer with global self-attention pooling. Two uncertainty heads: a variational bottleneck (KL divergence for epistemic uncertainty) and a Mahalanobis distance detector for distributional shift. Selective prediction based on Mahalanobis scores. Qualitative attention heatmaps show cross-stream focus shifts under ambiguity.
AI.result: PSI 1.0: 0.9 F1, 0.94 AUC-ROC, 0.78 MCC using only structured features. PSI 2.0: 0.78 F1, 0.79 AUC-ROC (baseline). Selective prediction improves test accuracy by up to 0.4 percentage points at 80% coverage. Model is modality-agnostic, integrates with vision–language pipelines, and is efficient.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields calibrated probabilities and actionable risk scores with maintained efficiency, offering a strong, interpretable baseline for risk-aware pedestrian intent prediction and easy integration with existing perception systems.

title: Uncertainty Matters: Structured Probabilistic Online Mapping for Motion Prediction in Autonomous Driving
summary: Online map generation and trajectory prediction are critical components of the autonomous driving perception-prediction-planning pipeline. While modern vectorized mapping models achieve high geometric accuracy, they typically treat map estimation as a deterministic task, discarding structural uncertainty. Existing probabilistic approaches often rely on diagonal covariance matrices, which assume independence between points and fail to capture the strong spatial correlations inherent in road geometry. To address this, we propose a structured probabilistic formulation for online map generation. Our method explicitly models intra-element dependencies by predicting a dense covariance matrix, parameterized via a Low-Rank plus Diagonal (LRPD) covariance decomposition. This formulation represents uncertainty as a combination of a low-rank component, which captures global spatial structure, and a diagonal component representing independent local noise, thereby capturing geometric correlations without the prohibitive computational cost of full covariance matrices. Evaluations on the nuScenes dataset demonstrate that our uncertainty-aware framework yields consistent improvements in online map generation quality compared to deterministic baselines. Furthermore, our approach establishes new state-of-the-art performance for map-based motion prediction, highlighting the critical role of uncertainty in planning tasks. Code is published under link-available-soon.

AI.tldr: Proposes a structured probabilistic framework for online map generation using a Low-Rank plus Diagonal (LRPD) covariance to model intra-element dependencies, improving map generation quality and achieving state-of-the-art map-based motion prediction on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: Current vectorized maps are treated as deterministic or rely on diagonal (independent) covariances, which fail to capture strong spatial correlations in road geometry. A structured uncertainty model is needed to better reflect geometry and support planning.
AI.method: Predict a dense covariance via Low-Rank plus Diagonal decomposition to capture global spatial structure (low-rank) and local noise (diagonal). Integrates into online map generation and improves trajectory prediction. Computationally efficient relative to full covariance by avoiding full dense matrices.
AI.result: On nuScenes, the uncertainty-aware approach consistently improves online map generation vs. deterministic baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance in map-based motion prediction; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: Structured uncertainty modeling via LRPD covariance effectively captures spatial correlations in road geometry and enhances planning-relevant predictions, offering a practical balance between expressive power and computational cost.

title: Target Concept Tuning Improves Extreme Weather Forecasting
summary: Deep learning models for meteorological forecasting often fail in rare but high-impact events such as typhoons, where relevant data is scarce. Existing fine-tuning methods typically face a trade-off between overlooking these extreme events and overfitting them at the expense of overall performance. We propose TaCT, an interpretable concept-gated fine-tuning framework that solves the aforementioned issue by selective model improvement: models are adapted specifically for failure cases while preserving performance in common scenarios. To this end, TaCT automatically discovers failure-related internal concepts using Sparse Autoencoders and counterfactual analysis, and updates parameters only when the corresponding concepts are activated, rather than applying uniform adaptation. Experiments show consistent improvements in typhoon forecasting across different regions without degrading other meteorological variables. The identified concepts correspond to physically meaningful circulation patterns, revealing model biases and supporting trustworthy adaptation in scientific forecasting tasks. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Concept-Gated-Fine-tune-62AC.

AI.tldr: TaCT selectively tunes models for rare high-impact events by updating only when failure-related internal concepts are activated, discovered via Sparse Autoencoders and counterfactual analysis, improving typhoon forecasts while preserving general performance.
AI.motivation: Rare extreme weather events suffer from data scarcity and model biases; standard fine-tuning often harms overall performance or fails to capture failure cases.
AI.method: Identify failure-related internal concepts with Sparse Autoencoders and counterfactual analysis; apply parameter updates only when the corresponding concepts are activated (concept-gated fine-tuning).
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent typhoon-forecast improvements across regions without degrading other meteorological variables; learned concepts align with physically meaningful circulation patterns, revealing model biases and enabling trustworthy adaptation; code available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Concept-Gated-Fine-tune-62AC.
AI.conclusion: TaCT provides an interpretable, selective fine-tuning framework that improves rare-event forecasting while preserving general performance, with concepts offering physical interpretability.

title: Learning Dynamic Belief Graphs for Theory-of-mind Reasoning
summary: Theory of Mind (ToM) reasoning with Large Language Models (LLMs) requires inferring how people's implicit, evolving beliefs shape what they seek and how they act under uncertainty -- especially in high-stakes settings such as disaster response, emergency medicine, and human-in-the-loop autonomy. Prior approaches either prompt LLMs directly or use latent-state models that treat beliefs as static and independent, often producing incoherent mental models over time and weak reasoning in dynamic contexts. We introduce a structured cognitive trajectory model for LLM-based ToM that represents mental state as a dynamic belief graph, jointly inferring latent beliefs, learning their time-varying dependencies, and linking belief evolution to information seeking and decisions. Our model contributes (i) a novel projection from textualized probabilistic statements to consistent probabilistic graphical model updates, (ii) an energy-based factor graph representation of belief interdependencies, and (iii) an ELBO-based objective that captures belief accumulation and delayed decisions. Across multiple real-world disaster evacuation datasets, our model significantly improves action prediction and recovers interpretable belief trajectories consistent with human reasoning, providing a principled module for augmenting LLMs with ToM in high-uncertainty environment. https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ICML_submission-6373/

AI.tldr: Introduces a structured cognitive trajectory model for LLM-based Theory of Mind, using a dynamic belief graph to capture evolving latent beliefs and their influence on information seeking and decisions; achieves improved action prediction on disaster data.
AI.motivation: Address incoherence and static nature of prior latent-state ToM models; need dynamic, interpretable belief evolution in high-stakes uncertainty.
AI.method: Construct a dynamic belief graph as energy-based factor graph; project textual probabilistic statements into graphical model updates; ELBO-based objective capturing belief accumulation and delayed decisions; joint inference of latent beliefs and time-varying dependencies; links beliefs to actions.
AI.result: Significant improvement in action prediction and recovery of interpretable belief trajectories on disaster evacuation datasets; demonstrates coherent ToM reasoning and potential augmentation of LLMs in high-uncertainty environments.
AI.conclusion: Provides principled module to augment LLMs with ToM in dynamic, high-uncertainty settings; generalizable to real-world disaster response scenarios.

title: MoCA3D: Monocular 3D Bounding Box Prediction in the Image Plane
summary: Monocular 3D object understanding has largely been cast as a 2D RoI-to-3D box lifting problem. However, emerging downstream applications require image-plane geometry (e.g., projected 3D box corners) which cannot be easily obtained without known intrinsics, a problem for object detection in the wild. We introduce MoCA3D, a Monocular, Class-Agnostic 3D model that predicts projected 3D bounding box corners and per-corner depths without requiring camera intrinsics at inference time. MoCA3D formulates pixel-space localization and depth assignment as dense prediction via corner heatmaps and depth maps. To evaluate image-plane geometric fidelity, we propose Pixel-Aligned Geometry (PAG), which directly measures image-plane corner and depth consistency. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MoCA3D achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving image-plane corner PAG by 22.8% while remaining comparable on 3D IoU, using up to 57 times fewer trainable parameters. Finally, we apply MoCA3D to downstream tasks which were previously impractical under unknown intrinsics, highlighting its utility beyond standard baseline models.

AI.tldr: MoCA3D is a monocular, class-agnostic 3D detector that directly predicts image-plane projected 3D corners and per-corner depths without requiring camera intrinsics at inference. It uses dense corner heatmaps and depth maps, and introduces Pixel-Aligned Geometry (PAG) to measure image-plane geometric fidelity. It achieves state-of-the-art PAG gains (22.8% improvement) while maintaining comparable 3D IoU and using up to 57x fewer parameters, enabling downstream tasks under unknown intrinsics.
AI.motivation: Traditional monocular 3D detection relies on lifting 2D RoIs to 3D boxes with known camera intrinsics, which is impractical for in-the-wild scenarios where intrinsics are unavailable. There is a need for image-plane geometry and intrinsics-free inference to support downstream tasks.
AI.method: MoCA3D predicts the projected 3D bounding box corners and per-corner depths directly in image space via dense pixel-wise predictions (corner heatmaps and depth maps). It omits the requirement for camera intrinsics at inference, formulates localization and depth as pixel-space dense predictions, and introduces Pixel-Aligned Geometry (PAG) to evaluate image-plane corner consistency and depth fidelity.
AI.result: MoCA3D achieves state-of-the-art performance on image-plane geometry with a 22.8% improvement in PAG; maintains competitive 3D IoU; uses up to 57× fewer trainable parameters; demonstrates utility on downstream tasks previously impractical under unknown intrinsics.
AI.conclusion: MoCA3D enables image-plane geometry-aware monocular 3D understanding without intrinsics, offering better image-plane fidelity, parameter efficiency, and practical applicability to real-world tasks where camera intrinsics are unavailable.

title: Not an Obstacle for Dog, but a Hazard for Human: A Co-Ego Navigation System for Guide Dog Robots
summary: Guide dogs offer independence to Blind and Low-Vision (BLV) individuals, yet their limited availability leaves the vast majority of BLV users without access. Quadruped robotic guide dogs present a promising alternative, but existing systems rely solely on the robot's ground-level sensors for navigation, overlooking a critical class of hazards: obstacles that are transparent to the robot yet dangerous at human body height, such as bent branches. We term this the viewpoint asymmetry problem and present the first system to explicitly address it. Our Co-Ego system adopts a dual-branch obstacle avoidance framework that integrates the robot-centric ground sensing with the user's elevated egocentric perspective to ensure comprehensive navigation safety. Deployed on a quadruped robot, the system is evaluated in a controlled user study with sighted participants under blindfold across three conditions: unassisted, single-view, and cross-view fusion. Results demonstrate that cross-view fusion significantly reduces collision times and cognitive load, verifying the necessity of viewpoint complementarity for safe robotic guide dog navigation.

AI.tldr: A dual-branch Co-Ego system fuses robot-ground sensing with the user’s elevated egocentric view to address viewpoint asymmetry in quadruped robotic guide dogs; cross-view fusion improves safety performance in a controlled user study.
AI.motivation: Robot-only ground sensors miss hazards at human body height (viewpoint asymmetry) leading to unsafe navigation; a complementary elevated user perspective can enhance obstacle avoidance.
AI.method: A dual-branch obstacle avoidance framework that combines robot-centric ground sensing with the user’s elevated egocentric perspective, fused via a cross-view mechanism; implemented on a quadruped robot and evaluated in a controlled user study with sighted participants under blindfold across three conditions (unassisted, single-view, cross-view fusion).
AI.result: Cross-view fusion significantly reduces collision times and cognitive load compared to single-view or unassisted conditions, demonstrating the importance of viewpoint complementarity for safe navigation.
AI.conclusion: Viewpoint asymmetry is a critical factor in robotic guide navigation; integrating elevated human perspective with robot ground sensing via cross-view fusion enhances safety and efficiency, supporting the feasibility of safer robotic guide dogs.

title: FalconBC: Flow matching for Amortized inference of Latent-CONditioned physiologic Boundary Conditions
summary: Boundary condition tuning is a fundamental step in patient-specific cardiovascular modeling. Despite an increase in offline training cost, recent methods in data-driven variational inference can efficiently estimate the joint posterior distribution of boundary conditions, with amortization of training efforts over clinical targets. However, even the most modern approaches fall short in two important scenarios: open-loop models with known mean flow and assumed waveform shapes, and anatomies affected by vascular lesions where segmentation influences the reachability of pressure or flow split targets. In both cases, boundary conditions cannot be tuned in isolation. We introduce a general amortized inference framework based on probabilistic flow that treats clinical targets, inflow features, and point cloud embeddings of patient-specific anatomies as either conditioning variables or quantities to be jointly estimated. We demonstrate the approach on two patient-specific models: an aorto-iliac bifurcation with varying stenosis locations and severity, and a coronary arterial tree.

AI.tldr: Proposes an amortized probabilistic-flow framework to jointly infer boundary conditions with clinical targets and patient geometries, addressing open-loop and lesion-affected anatomies; validated on an aorto-iliac stenosis model and a coronary tree.
AI.motivation: Boundary conditions in patient-specific cardiovascular modeling are interdependent with flow targets and anatomy. Open-loop scenarios with known mean flow and lesion-affected anatomies complicate independent tuning. Data-driven amortized inference offers efficiency but must support joint estimation across targets and geometry.
AI.method: A general amortized inference framework based on probabilistic flow that treats clinical targets, inflow features, and point-cloud embeddings of patient-specific anatomies as conditioning variables or quantities to be jointly estimated.
AI.result: Demonstrated feasibility on two patient-specific models: an aorto-iliac bifurcation with varying stenosis locations and severities, and a coronary arterial tree, showing joint, conditioned estimation of boundary conditions despite anatomical lesions.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a unified, scalable approach for joint boundary-condition inference in patient-specific cardiovascular models, accommodating anatomy-driven constraints and multiple targets, and enabling amortized training across clinical scenarios.

title: SeeClear: Reliable Transparent Object Depth Estimation via Generative Opacification
summary: Monocular depth estimation remains challenging for transparent objects, where refraction and transmission are difficult to model and break the appearance assumptions used by depth networks. As a result, state-of-the-art estimators often produce unstable or incorrect depth predictions for transparent materials. We propose SeeClear, a novel framework that converts transparent objects into generative opaque images, enabling stable monocular depth estimation for transparent objects. Given an input image, we first localize transparent regions and transform their refractive appearance into geometrically consistent opaque shapes using a diffusion-based generative opacification module. The processed image is then fed into an off-the-shelf monocular depth estimator without retraining or architectural changes. To train the opacification model, we construct SeeClear-396k, a synthetic dataset containing 396k paired transparent-opaque renderings. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets show that SeeClear significantly improves depth estimation for transparent objects. Project page: https://heyumeng.com/SeeClear-web/

AI.tldr: Converts transparent regions to diffusion-generated opaque surrogates to enable stable monocular depth estimation without retraining.
AI.motivation: Monocular depth estimation struggles with transparent objects due to refraction and transmission, leading to unstable or incorrect predictions; a generative opacification step can convert transparent regions into appearance-consistent opaque shapes.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: localize transparent regions and apply a diffusion-based opacification module to produce geometrically consistent opaque shapes, then feed into an off-the-shelf monocular depth estimator. Training uses SeeClear-396k, a synthetic dataset of paired transparent-opaque renderings; no changes to the depth estimator's architecture or training.
AI.result: Significant improvements in depth estimation for transparent objects on both synthetic and real-world datasets; demonstrates compatibility with existing depth models without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Transforming transparent regions into opaque equivalents enables robust monocular depth estimation for transparent objects without altering depth-model design, leveraging large synthetic paired data for training.

title: KUKAloha: A General, Low-Cost, and Shared-Control based Teleoperation Framework for Construction Robot Arm
summary: This paper presents KUKAloha, a general, low-cost, and shared-control teleoperation framework designed for construction robot arms. The proposed system employs a leader-follower paradigm in which a lightweight leading arm enables intuitive human guidance for coarse robot motion, while an autonomous perception module based on AprilTag detection performs precise alignment and grasp execution. By explicitly decoupling human control from fine manipulation, KUKAloha improves safety and repeatability when operating large-scale manipulators.
