We implement the framework on a KUKA robot arm and conduct a usability study with representative construction manipulation tasks. Experimental results demonstrate that KUKAloha reduces operator workload, improves task completion efficiency, and provides a practical solution for scalable demonstration collection and shared human-robot control in construction environments.

AI.tldr: KUKAloha is a low-cost, shared-control teleoperation framework for large construction robot arms, combining leader-follower guidance with autonomous AprilTag-based alignment for precise grasping.
AI.motivation: Improve safety, repeatability, and efficiency in remote or shared control of large-scale construction manipulators while enabling scalable data collection for demonstrations.
AI.method: A leader-follower teleoperation setup where a lightweight leading arm captures coarse human guidance; an autonomous perception module detects AprilTags to perform precise alignment and grasp execution; decouples coarse control from fine manipulation; implemented on a KUKA arm and evaluated via usability tasks.
AI.result: The approach reduces operator workload and increases task completion efficiency; demonstrates practicality for scalable demonstration collection and shared human-robot control in construction environments.
AI.conclusion: KUKAloha offers a practical, low-cost solution that enhances safety and efficiency in construction robotics by separating coarse human input from fine manipulation and leveraging AprilTag-based precision for grasping.

title: StreetForward: Perceiving Dynamic Street with Feedforward Causal Attention
summary: Feedforward reconstruction is crucial for autonomous driving applications, where rapid scene reconstruction enables efficient utilization of large-scale driving datasets in closed-loop simulation and other downstream tasks, eliminating the need for time-consuming per-scene optimization. We present StreetForward, a pose-free and tracker-free feedforward framework for dynamic street reconstruction. Building upon the alternating attention mechanism from Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT), we propose a simple yet effective temporal mask attention module that captures dynamic motion information from image sequences and produces motion-aware latent representations. Static content and dynamic instances are represented uniformly with 3D Gaussian Splatting, and are optimized jointly by cross-frame rendering with spatio-temporal consistency, allowing the model to infer per-pixel velocities and produce high-fidelity novel views at new poses and times. We train and evaluate our model on the Waymo Open Dataset, demonstrating superior performance on novel view synthesis and depth estimation compared to existing methods. Furthermore, zero-shot inference on CARLA and other datasets validates the generalization capability of our approach. More visualizations are available on our project page: https://streetforward.github.io.

AI.tldr: StreetForward provides pose-free, tracker-free feedforward dynamic street reconstruction via 3D Gaussian Splatting and a temporal mask attention, achieving strong novel view synthesis and depth with zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Rapid, scalable scene reconstruction for autonomous driving and simulation without per-scene optimization, enabling efficient use of large driving datasets and downstream tasks.
AI.method: Extends VGGT's alternating attention with a temporal mask attention module to extract motion from image sequences; represents static and dynamic content using 3D Gaussian Splatting; jointly optimizes via cross-frame rendering with spatio-temporal consistency to infer per-pixel velocities and render novel views; trained/evaluated on Waymo Open Dataset; zero-shot tests on CARLA.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods on novel view synthesis and depth estimation on Waymo; demonstrates zero-shot generalization to CARLA and other datasets; provides motion-aware latent representations and high-fidelity rendering.
AI.conclusion: A simple, effective feedforward framework for dynamic street reconstruction that achieves motion-aware, pose- and tracker-free reconstruction with good generalization and rendering quality.

title: AGILE: A Comprehensive Workflow for Humanoid Loco-Manipulation Learning
summary:
Recent advances in reinforcement learning (RL) have enabled impressive humanoid behaviors in simulation, yet transferring these results to new robots remains challenging. In many real deployments, the primary bottleneck is no longer simulation throughput or algorithm design, but the absence of systematic infrastructure that links environment verification, training, evaluation, and deployment in a coherent loop.
  To address this gap, we present AGILE, an end-to-end workflow for humanoid RL that standardizes the policy-development lifecycle to mitigate common sim-to-real failure modes. AGILE comprises four stages: (1) interactive environment verification, (2) reproducible training, (3) unified evaluation, and (4) descriptor-driven deployment via robot/task configuration descriptors. For evaluation stage, AGILE supports both scenario-based tests and randomized rollouts under a shared suite of motion-quality diagnostics, enabling automated regression testing and principled robustness assessment. AGILE also incorporates a set of training stabilizations and algorithmic enhancements in training stage to improve optimization stability and sim-to-real transfer.
  With this pipeline in place, we validate AGILE across five representative humanoid skills spanning locomotion, recovery, motion imitation, and loco-manipulation on two hardware platforms (Unitree G1 and Booster T1), achieving consistent sim-to-real transfer. Overall, AGILE shows that a standardized, end-to-end workflow can substantially improve the reliability and reproducibility of humanoid RL development.

AI.tldr: AGILE is an end-to-end humanoid reinforcement learning workflow that links environment verification, reproducible training, unified evaluation, and descriptor-driven deployment to improve sim-to-real transfer; it integrates scenario-based testing and randomized rollouts with motion-quality diagnostics, and is validated on two hardware platforms across five skills.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of integrated infrastructure that connects environment verification, training, evaluation, and deployment in humanoid RL, aiming to reduce sim-to-real failure modes and enhance reproducibility and reliability.
AI.method: Four-stage pipeline: (1) interactive environment verification; (2) reproducible training with stabilizations and enhancements; (3) unified evaluation with scenario-based tests and randomized rollouts using motion-quality diagnostics; (4) descriptor-driven deployment via robot/task configuration descriptors.
AI.result: Empirically validated across five humanoid skills (locomotion, recovery, motion imitation, loco-manipulation) on two hardware platforms (Unitree G1, Booster T1) with consistent sim-to-real transfer.
AI.conclusion: A standardized, end-to-end workflow can substantially improve reliability and reproducibility of humanoid RL development by linking verification, training, evaluation, and deployment in a coherent loop.

title: DAPA: Distribution Aware Piecewise Activation Functions for On-Device Transformer Inference and Training
summary: Non-linear activation functions play a pivotal role in on-device inference and training, as they not only consume substantial hardware resources but also impose a significant impact on system performance and energy efficiency. In this work, we propose Distribution-Aware Piecewise Activation (DAPA), a differentiable and hardware-friendly activation function for Transformer architectures by exploiting the distribution of pre-activation data. DAPA employs a non-uniform piecewise approximation that allocates finer segments to high-probability regions of the distribution, improving generalizability over prior piecewise linear methods. The resulting approximation is further quantized using Distribution-Weighted Mean Square Error to reduce latency and resource utilization for hardware deployment. Our HLS implementation demonstrates that DAPA speeds up GELU computation by 16$\times$ and decreases DSP utilization by 16$\times$ while maintaining comparable or better performance across vision Transformers and GPT-2 models.

AI.tldr: Proposes a distribution-aware, differentiable, non-uniform piecewise activation (DAPA) for Transformers, with distribution-guided refinement and Distribution-Weighted MSE quantization to optimize on-device inference; shows 16x speedup and 16x lower DSP usage for GELU, with comparable/better accuracy on ViT and GPT-2.
AI.motivation: Reduce hardware cost and energy of activation functions in on-device Transformer workloads without sacrificing accuracy by exploiting the distribution of pre-activation values to create a calibrated, hardware-friendly approximation.
AI.method: Introduce DAPA, a differentiable non-uniform piecewise activation that allocates more segments to high-probability regions of the pre-activation distribution; train to fit the distribution; quantize via Distribution-Weighted MSE for hardware deployment; implement in high-level synthesis (HLS) to evaluate on hardware; validate on Vision Transformers and GPT-2.
AI.result: DAPA yields up to 16× faster GELU computation and 16× lower DSP utilization with comparable or improved performance across ViT and GPT-2 models.
AI.conclusion: DAPA provides a distribution-aware, hardware-friendly activation that improves on-piecewise approximations, enabling faster and more energy-efficient on-device Transformer inference/training while maintaining accuracy.

title: Dual-Domain Representation Alignment: Bridging 2D and 3D Vision via Geometry-Aware Architecture Search
summary: Modern computer vision requires balancing predictive accuracy with real-time efficiency, yet the high inference cost of large vision models (LVMs) limits deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Although Evolutionary Neural Architecture Search (ENAS) is well suited for multi-objective optimization, its practical use is hindered by two issues: expensive candidate evaluation and ranking inconsistency among subnetworks. To address them, we propose EvoNAS, an efficient distributed framework for multi-objective evolutionary architecture search. We build a hybrid supernet that integrates Vision State Space and Vision Transformer (VSS-ViT) modules, and optimize it with a Cross-Architecture Dual-Domain Knowledge Distillation (CA-DDKD) strategy. By coupling the computational efficiency of VSS blocks with the semantic expressiveness of ViT modules, CA-DDKD improves the representational capacity of the shared supernet and enhances ranking consistency, enabling reliable fitness estimation during evolution without extra fine-tuning. To reduce the cost of large-scale validation, we further introduce a Distributed Multi-Model Parallel Evaluation (DMMPE) framework based on GPU resource pooling and asynchronous scheduling. Compared with conventional data-parallel evaluation, DMMPE improves efficiency by over 70% through concurrent multi-GPU, multi-model execution. Experiments on COCO, ADE20K, KITTI, and NYU-Depth v2 show that the searched architectures, termed EvoNets, consistently achieve Pareto-optimal trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency. Compared with representative CNN-, ViT-, and Mamba-based models, EvoNets deliver lower inference latency and higher throughput under strict computational budgets while maintaining strong generalization on downstream tasks such as novel view synthesis. Code is available at https://github.com/EMI-Group/evonas

AI.tldr: Proposes EvoNAS, an efficient distributed evolutionary NAS framework for multi-objective optimization in vision models. It combines a hybrid VSS-ViT supernet with cross-architecture dual-domain knowledge distillation to stabilize ranking and improve expressiveness, and introduces distributed multi-model parallel evaluation to cut validation cost. EvoNets achieve Pareto-optimal accuracy-efficiency trade-offs with lower latency and higher throughput on several benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Reduce the high inference cost of large vision models and the evaluation/ranking inefficiencies in evolutionary NAS, enabling reliable, real-time deployment on resource-constrained devices.
AI.method: Construct a hybrid supernet integrating Vision State Space (VSS) blocks and Vision Transformer (ViT) modules. Apply Cross-Architecture Dual-Domain Knowledge Distillation (CA-DDKD) to align representations across architectures and improve ranking consistency. Introduce Distributed Multi-Model Parallel Evaluation (DMMPE) leveraging GPU pooling and asynchronous scheduling to accelerate large-scale validation. Use an evolutionary search to identify EvoNets under efficiency constraints without requiring post-hoc fine-tuning for ranking.
AI.result: EvoNets consistently achieve Pareto-optimal trade-offs between accuracy and efficiency, with lower inference latency and higher throughput than representative CNN-, ViT-, and Mamba-based models under strict budgets, while generalizing well to downstream tasks like novel view synthesis. DMMPE reduces validation cost by over 70% via concurrent multi-GPU, multi-model execution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed EvoNAS framework enables efficient, reliable multi-objective neural architecture search for vision models by coupling a hybrid, expressive supernet with a fast, scalable evaluation pipeline; EvoNets provide deployable architectures with strong performance under resource constraints; code is publicly available.

title: HortiMulti: A Multi-Sensor Dataset for Localisation and Mapping in Horticultural Polytunnels
summary: Agricultural robotics is gaining increasing relevance in both research and real-world deployment. As these systems are expected to operate autonomously in more complex tasks, the availability of representative real-world datasets becomes essential. While domains such as urban and forestry robotics benefit from large and established benchmarks, horticultural environments remain comparatively under-explored despite the economic significance of this sector. To address this gap, we present HortiMulti, a multimodal, cross-season dataset collected in commercial strawberry and raspberry polytunnels across an entire growing season, capturing substantial appearance variation, dynamic foliage, specular reflections from plastic covers, severe perceptual aliasing, and GNSS-unreliable conditions, all of which directly degrade existing localisation and perception algorithms. The sensor suite includes two 3D LiDARs, four RGB cameras, an IMU, GNSS, and wheel odometry. Ground truth trajectories are derived from a combination of Total Station surveying, AprilTag fiducial markers, and LiDAR-inertial odometry, spanning dense, sparse, and marker-free coverage to support evaluation under both controlled and realistic conditions. We release time-synchronised raw measurements, calibration files, reference trajectories, and baseline benchmarks for visual, LiDAR, and multi-sensor SLAM, with results confirming that current state-of-the-art methods remain inadequate for reliable polytunnel deployment, establishing HortiMulti as a one-stop resource for developing and testing robotic perception systems in horticulture environments.

AI.tldr: A multimodal cross-season dataset (HortiMulti) for polytunnel horticulture, with dual LiDARs, four RGB cameras, IMU, GNSS, and wheel odometry, plus ground-truth via Total Station, AprilTag markers, and LiDAR-inertial odometry; demonstrates SLAM limitations in polytunnels and provides baselines and data for evaluation.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of representative, real-world, cross-season horticulture data to improve perception and localization robustness in controlled environments with dynamic foliage and challenging lighting.
AI.method: Data collection across an entire growing season in commercial strawberry and raspberry polytunnels. Sensor suite includes 2x LiDARs, 4x RGB cameras, IMU, GNSS, wheel odometry. Ground truth via Total Station, AprilTag fiducials, and LiDAR-Inertial Odometry. Time-synchronised measurements, calibration files, reference trajectories, and baseline benchmarks for visual, LiDAR, and multi-sensor SLAM.
AI.result: Confirms current state-of-the-art SLAM/perception methods are inadequate for reliable polytunnel deployment; releases a comprehensive dataset with baselines to foster development and evaluation in horticulture robotics.
AI.conclusion: HortiMulti serves as a comprehensive, cross-season resource for developing and testing robotic perception systems in horticulture, enabling evaluation under both controlled and realistic in-situ conditions.

title: Beyond Weighted Summation: Learnable Nonlinear Aggregation Functions for Robust Artificial Neurons
summary: Weighted summation has remained the default input aggregation mechanism in artificial neurons since the earliest neural network models. While computationally efficient, this design implicitly behaves like a mean-based estimator and is therefore sensitive to noisy or extreme inputs. This paper investigates whether replacing fixed linear aggregation with learnable nonlinear alternatives can improve neural network robustness without sacrificing trainability. Two differentiable aggregation mechanisms are introduced: an F-Mean neuron based on a learnable power-weighted aggregation rule, and a Gaussian Support neuron based on distance-aware affinity weighting. To preserve the optimisation stability of standard neurons, hybrid neurons are proposed that interpolate between linear and nonlinear aggregation through a learnable blending parameter. Evaluated in multilayer perceptrons and convolutional neural networks on CIFAR-10 and a noisy CIFAR-10 variant with additive Gaussian corruption, hybrid neurons consistently improve robustness under noise while F-Mean hybrids also yield modest gains on clean data. The three-way hybrid achieves robustness scores of up to 0.991 compared to 0.890 for the standard baseline, and learned parameters converge consistently to sub-linear aggregation (p $\approx$ 0.43--0.50) and high novelty utilisation ($α$ $\approx$ 0.69--0.79). These findings suggest that neuron-level aggregation is a meaningful and underexplored design dimension for building more noise-tolerant neural networks.

AI.tldr: Learnable nonlinear neuron aggregations (F-Mean and Gaussian Support), plus hybrids blending linear and nonlinear aggregation, improve robustness to noisy inputs in MLPs/CNNs with CIFAR-10, sometimes at a modest cost to clean data performance. Three-way hybrids achieve high robustness (up to 0.991 vs 0.890 baseline). Learned aggregation parameters converge to sub-linear means (p ~ 0.43–0.50) and high novelty usage (α ~ 0.69–0.79).
AI.motivation: Fixed linear summation in neurons acts like a mean-based estimator, making networks vulnerable to noisy or extreme inputs. Introducing differentiable, learnable nonlinear aggregation could enhance robustness without harming trainability.
AI.method: Introduce two differentiable aggregations: F-Mean with a learnable power-weighted rule, and Gaussian Support with distance-aware affinity weighting. Also propose hybrid neurons with a learnable blending parameter to interpolate between linear and nonlinear aggregation. Evaluate in multilayer perceptrons and convolutional networks on CIFAR-10 and a noisy CIFAR-10 variant with Gaussian corruption.
AI.result: Hybrid neurons consistently improve robustness under noise; F-Mean hybrids give modest gains on clean data. The trio of hybrid aggregations achieves robustness scores up to 0.991, compared to 0.890 for the standard baseline. Learned parameters converge to sub-linear aggregation (p ≈ 0.43–0.50) and high novelty utilization (α ≈ 0.69–0.79).
AI.conclusion: Neuron-level aggregation is a meaningful, underexplored design dimension for building more noise-tolerant neural networks; sub-linear, novelty-aware aggregation can yield robust performance with competitive trainability.

title: PFM-VEPAR: Prompting Foundation Models for RGB-Event Camera based Pedestrian Attribute Recognition
summary: Event-based pedestrian attribute recognition (PAR) leverages motion cues to enhance RGB cameras in low-light and motion-blur scenarios, enabling more accurate inference of attributes like age and emotion. However, existing two-stream multimodal fusion methods introduce significant computational overhead and neglect the valuable guidance from contextual samples. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an Event Prompter. Discarding the computationally expensive auxiliary backbone, this module directly applies extremely lightweight and efficient Discrete Cosine Transform (DCT) and Inverse DCT (IDCT) operations to the event data. This design extracts frequency-domain event features at a minimal computational cost, thereby effectively augmenting the RGB branch. Furthermore, an external memory bank designed to provide rich prior knowledge, combined with modern Hopfield networks, enables associative memory-augmented representation learning. This mechanism effectively mines and leverages global relational knowledge across different samples. Finally, a cross-attention mechanism fuses the RGB and event modalities, followed by feed-forward networks for attribute prediction. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets fully validate the effectiveness of the proposed RGB-Event PAR framework. The source code of this paper will be released on https://github.com/Event-AHU/OpenPAR

AI.tldr: A lightweight RGB–Event PAR framework that uses DCT/IDCT on event streams, an external Hopfield-memory module for global relational knowledge, and cross-attention fusion to improve pedestrian attribute recognition with reduced computation.
AI.motivation: Heavy two-stream fusion pipelines incur high computational cost and underutilize contextual/global cues; leveraging event cues in the frequency domain and an external memory can enhance robustness in low-light/motion-blur scenarios.
AI.method: Discard expensive backbone; apply DCT/IDCT to event data to obtain frequency-domain features; employ an external memory bank with modern Hopfield networks for associative memory across samples; fuse RGB and event features via cross-attention; use feed-forward nets for attribute prediction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets validate the approach's effectiveness; results suggest improved accuracy/robustness with lower computation; source code to be released.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively augments RGB-based PAR by incorporating efficient event-based features and a global-relational memory, enabling robust and efficient attribute recognition.

title: The Robot's Inner Critic: Self-Refinement of Social Behaviors through VLM-based Replanning
comment: Accepted to ICRA 2026. 8 pages, 9 figures, Project page: https://limjiyu99.github.io/inner-critic/
summary:
Conventional robot social behavior generation has been limited in flexibility and autonomy, relying on predefined motions or human feedback. This study proposes CRISP (Critique-and-Replan for Interactive Social Presence), an autonomous framework where a robot critiques and replans its own actions by leveraging a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a `human-like social critic.' CRISP integrates (1) extraction of movable joints and constraints by analyzing the robot's description file (e.g., MJCF), (2) generation of step-by-step behavior plans based on situational context, (3) generation of low-level joint control code by referencing visual information (joint range-of-motion visualizations), (4) VLM-based evaluation of social appropriateness and naturalness, including pinpointing erroneous steps, and (5) iterative refinement of behaviors through reward-based search. This approach is not tied to a specific robot API; it can generate subtly different, human-like motions on various platforms using only the robot's structure file. In a user study involving five different robot types and 20 scenarios, including mobile manipulators and humanoids, our proposed method achieved significantly higher preference and situational appropriateness ratings compared to previous methods. This research presents a general framework that minimizes human intervention while expanding the robot's autonomous interaction capabilities and cross-platform applicability.
  Detailed result videos and supplementary information regarding this work are available at: https://limjiyu99.github.io/inner-critic/

AI.tldr: CRISP uses a Vision-Language Model as a social critic to autonomously critique and replan robot actions, extracting joint constraints from robot models, generating plans, producing low-level joint code, and iteratively refining via reward-based search; cross-platform, API-agnostic, with improved user-perceived social performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome rigidity of predefined, human-feedback–dependent social robot behavior by enabling autonomous, flexible, cross-platform social interaction.
AI.method: Five-step pipeline: (1) extract movable joints/constraints from robot description (e.g., MJCF); (2) generate step-by-step behavior plans from situational context; (3) produce low-level joint control code using visual joint-range-of-motion cues; (4) evaluate social appropriateness/naturalness with a VLM and identify erroneous steps; (5) iteratively refine behaviors via reward-based search; framework is not tied to a specific robot API.
AI.result: In a user study with five robot types and 20 scenarios (mobile manipulators and humanoids), CRISP achieved significantly higher preference and situational appropriateness ratings than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: CRISP provides a general, autonomous, cross-platform framework for social robot behavior generation, reducing human intervention and broadening autonomous interaction capabilities.

title: Anatomical Heterogeneity in Transformer Language Models
comment: 11 pages, 10 tables. Independent research. Code available at https://github.com/tomaszwi66
summary: Current transformer language models are trained with uniform computational budgets across all layers, implicitly assuming layer homogeneity. We challenge this assumption through empirical analysis of SmolLM2-135M, a 30-layer, 135M-parameter causal language model, using five diagnostic metrics: weight predictability (R2), ablation degradation, recovery speed, weight manipulation robustness, and structural analysis. We find profound anatomical heterogeneity: (1) Layer weights follow strong mathematical regularity (R2 = 0.91) with a universal oscillatory delta pattern (correlation ~= -0.50), yet predicted weights cause catastrophic failure due to nonlinear error accumulation. (2) Layer importance spans a 10^7 range, from a critical core (L8-11, up to +63,419% PPL degradation) to anti-layers (L14, L17) whose removal improves performance. (3) Recovery speed correlates with layer importance, indicating differential training requirements. (4) Only weight scaling (alpha = 0.9) preserves model quality among five tested manipulation strategies. (5) Growth Transformer Training, allocating budget by layer importance, achieves ~54% cost reduction. A proof-of-concept experiment confirms this: 4.7x lower validation loss than uniform training at identical parameter count, while being 13% faster.

AI.tldr: Layer-wise heterogeneity exists in a 30-layer LM; adaptive budget allocation by layer importance can dramatically reduce training cost while preserving or improving performance.
AI.motivation: Uniform computation across layers assumes homogeneity; empirical evidence is needed to justify layer-aware budgets and to unlock potential efficiency gains.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of SmolLM2-135M (30 layers, 135M params) using five metrics: weight predictability (R2), ablation degradation, recovery speed, weight manipulation robustness, and structural analysis; identify layer importance, core vs anti-layers, oscillatory weight patterns; test Growth Transformer Training with layer-aware budgeting; validate with a proof-of-concept.
AI.result: Layer weights show strong regularity (R2≈0.91) and a universal oscillatory delta pattern; predicted weights can cause nonlinear error accumulation leading to catastrophic failure. Layer importance spans ~10^7, from a critical core (L8-11) to anti-layers (L14, L17). Recovery speed correlates with importance; only weight scaling (alpha≈0.9) preserves quality among manipulation strategies. Growth Transformer Training achieves ~54% cost reduction; proof-of-concept shows 4.7x lower validation loss at same parameter count and ~13% faster.
AI.conclusion: There is profound layer-level heterogeneity in transformer LMs; allocating training budget by layer importance yields substantial efficiency gains and performance benefits, validating layer-aware training strategies and growth approaches.

title: IndoorR2X: Indoor Robot-to-Everything Coordination with LLM-Driven Planning
summary: Although robot-to-robot (R2R) communication improves indoor scene understanding beyond what a single robot can achieve, R2R alone cannot overcome partial observability without substantial exploration overhead or scaling team size. In contrast, many indoor environments already include low-cost Internet of Things (IoT) sensors (e.g., cameras) that provide persistent, building-wide context beyond onboard perception. We therefore introduce IndoorR2X, the first benchmark and simulation framework for Large Language Model (LLM)-driven multi-robot task planning with Robot-to-Everything (R2X) perception and communication in indoor environments. IndoorR2X integrates observations from mobile robots and static IoT devices to construct a global semantic state that supports scalable scene understanding, reduces redundant exploration, and enables high-level coordination through LLM-based planning. IndoorR2X provides configurable simulation environments, sensor layouts, robot teams, and task suites to systematically evaluate high-level semantic coordination strategies. Extensive experiments across diverse settings demonstrate that IoT-augmented world modeling improves multi-robot efficiency and reliability, and we highlight key insights and failure modes for advancing LLM-based collaboration between robot teams and indoor IoT sensors.

AI.tldr: IndoorR2X provides a benchmark and simulation framework for LLM-driven multi-robot planning using Robot-to-Everything (R2X) perception in indoor scenes; it integrates mobile robots and static IoT sensors to build a global semantic state, improving coordination efficiency and reliability.
AI.motivation: Partial observability and exploration overhead limit indoor multi-robot systems; IoT sensors offer persistent, building-wide context to augment onboard perception and enable scalable semantic coordination.
AI.method: Develop IndoorR2X benchmark and simulator; fuse observations from mobile robots and static IoT devices into a global semantic state; enable LLM-based high-level planning; provide configurable environments, sensor layouts, robot teams, and task suites to evaluate semantic coordination strategies.
AI.result: IoT-augmented world modeling improves multi-robot efficiency and reliability; the work also identifies insights and failure modes for advancing LLM-based collaboration between robot teams and indoor IoT sensors.
AI.conclusion: IoT-enhanced perception combined with LLM planning supports scalable indoor multi-robot coordination and establishes a platform for evaluating and advancing R2X collaboration with IoT sensors; future work should address observed failure modes and generalization to real-world deployments.

title: A Mathematical Theory of Understanding
summary: Generative AI has transformed the economics of information production, making explanations, proofs, examples, and analyses available at very low cost. Yet the value of information still depends on whether downstream users can absorb and act on it. A signal conveys meaning only to a learner with the structural capacity to decode it: an explanation that clarifies a concept for one user may be indistinguishable from noise to another who lacks the relevant prerequisites. This paper develops a mathematical model of that learner-side bottleneck. We model the learner as a mind, an abstract learning system characterized by a prerequisite structure over concepts. A mind may represent a human learner, an artificial learner such as a neural network, or any agent whose ability to interpret signals depends on previously acquired concepts. Teaching is modeled as sequential communication with a latent target. Because instructional signals are usable only when the learner has acquired the prerequisites needed to parse them, the effective communication channel depends on the learner's current state of knowledge and becomes more informative as learning progresses. The model yields two limits on the speed of learning and adoption: a structural limit determined by prerequisite reachability and an epistemic limit determined by uncertainty about the target. The framework implies threshold effects in training and capability acquisition. When the teaching horizon lies below the prerequisite depth of the target, additional instruction cannot produce successful completion of teaching; once that depth is reached, completion becomes feasible. Across heterogeneous learners, a common broadcast curriculum can be slower than personalized instruction by a factor linear in the number of learner types.

AI.tldr: A mind-based model of information transmission reveals learning speed is limited by learner prerequisites and epistemic uncertainty; threshold effects imply a horizon must exceed prerequisite depth for teaching to succeed, and broadcast curricula may be inefficient relative to personalized instruction, scaling with the number of learner types.
AI.motivation: Explain why the utility of generated explanations and analyses depends on downstream interpretability, and formalize learner bottlenecks to guide curriculum design and personalization across diverse agents.
AI.method: Develop a mathematical model of a 'mind' with a prerequisite concept structure. Model teaching as sequential communication to a latent target, with an effective channel that depends on the learner's current knowledge. Derive limits on learning speed: a structural limit from prerequisite reachability and an epistemic limit from uncertainty about the target; analyze threshold behavior and compare broadcast vs personalized instruction."
AI.result: Two fundamental limits on learning/adoption speed: (i) structural limit dictated by how prerequisites constrain what can be taught or decoded now; (ii) epistemic limit governed by uncertainty about the target. The framework exhibits threshold effects: if teaching horizon is shallower than the target's prerequisite depth, teaching cannot succeed; once depth is reached, completion becomes feasible. Across heterogeneous learners, a single broadcast curriculum can be slower than personalization by a factor that scales linearly with the number of learner types.
AI.conclusion: The framework clarifies how learner architecture shapes information value and teaching efficiency. It suggests curriculum design should align to learner prerequisites, and that personalization can dramatically improve throughput, especially in heterogeneous populations; it also highlights threshold phenomena that govern when instruction becomes feasible.

title: Efficiency Follows Global-Local Decoupling
summary: Modern vision models must capture image-level context without sacrificing local detail while remaining computationally affordable. We revisit this tradeoff and advance a simple principle: decouple the roles of global reasoning and local representation. To operationalize this principle, we introduce ConvNeur, a two-branch architecture in which a lightweight neural memory branch aggregates global context on a compact set of tokens, and a locality-preserving branch extracts fine structure. A learned gate lets global cues modulate local features without entangling their objectives. This separation yields subquadratic scaling with image size, retains inductive priors associated with local processing, and reduces overhead relative to fully global attention. On standard classification, detection, and segmentation benchmarks, ConvNeur matches or surpasses comparable alternatives at similar or lower compute and offers favorable accuracy versus latency trade-offs at similar budgets. These results support the view that efficiency follows global-local decoupling.

AI.tldr: ConvNeur introduces a two-branch vision model that decouples global reasoning from local representation to achieve subquadratic scaling while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Efficiently capturing image-level context without sacrificing local detail and reducing compute compared to fully global attention.
AI.method: A two-branch architecture: a lightweight neural memory branch aggregates global context on a compact token set; a locality-preserving branch extracts fine structure; a learned gate modulates local features with global cues, enabling global-local decoupling and subquadratic scaling.
AI.result: Matches or surpasses comparable methods on classification, detection, and segmentation benchmarks at similar compute; favorable accuracy-latency trade-offs; reduced overhead relative to fully global attention.
AI.conclusion: Global-local decoupling is an effective efficiency principle for vision models, enabling efficient global context without eroding local inductive priors.

title: Warm-Start Flow Matching for Guaranteed Fast Text/Image Generation
summary: Current auto-regressive (AR) LLMs, diffusion-based text/image generative models, and recent flow matching (FM) algorithms are capable of generating premium quality text/image samples. However, the inference or sample generation in these models is often very time-consuming and computationally demanding, mainly due to large numbers of function evaluations corresponding to the lengths of tokens or the numbers of diffusion steps. This also necessitates heavy GPU resources, time, and electricity. In this work we propose a novel solution to reduce the sample generation time of flow matching algorithms by a guaranteed speed-up factor, without sacrificing the quality of the generated samples. Our key idea is to utilize computationally lightweight generative models whose generation time is negligible compared to that of the target AR/FM models. The draft samples from a lightweight model, whose quality is not satisfactory but fast to generate, are regarded as an initial distribution for a FM algorithm. Unlike conventional usage of FM that takes a pure noise (e.g., Gaussian or uniform) initial distribution, the draft samples are already of decent quality, so we can set the starting time to be closer to the end time rather than 0 in the pure noise FM case. This will significantly reduce the number of time steps to reach the target data distribution, and the speed-up factor is guaranteed. Our idea, dubbed {\em Warm-Start FM} or WS-FM, can essentially be seen as a {\em learning-to-refine} generative model from low-quality draft samples to high-quality samples. As a proof of concept, we demonstrate the idea on some synthetic toy data as well as real-world text and image generation tasks, illustrating that our idea offers guaranteed speed-up in sample generation without sacrificing the quality of the generated samples.

AI.tldr: Warm-Start FM (WS-FM) speeds up flow-matching sampling by seeding the process with fast, low-quality draft samples from a lightweight model, achieving guaranteed speed-up without sacrificing sample quality; validated on toy data and real text/image tasks.
AI.motivation: Inference latency and high compute cost of autoregressive, diffusion, and flow-matching models due to long sequences of steps; need a guaranteed method to reduce generation time while maintaining quality.
AI.method: Replace the pure-noise initial condition in flow matching with draft samples from a lightweight generative model to form an improved initial distribution. Start the FM process at a later time (closer to the target) since the draft samples are already near the desired distribution. This constitutes a learning-to-refine framework labeled Warm-Start FM (WS-FM).
AI.result: The approach yields a guaranteed speed-up in sample generation, with no observed degradation in sample quality in both synthetic toy data and real-world text/image generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: WS-FM is a practical, theoretically grounded enhancement to flow-matching: it leverages fast, low-quality drafts to substantially reduce inference time while preserving output fidelity, and can be viewed as a general learning-to-refine paradigm for generative modeling.

title: Accelerating Diffusion Decoders via Multi-Scale Sampling and One-Step Distillation
summary: Image tokenization plays a central role in modern generative modeling by mapping visual inputs into compact representations that serve as an intermediate signal between pixels and generative models. Diffusion-based decoders have recently been adopted in image tokenization to reconstruct images from latent representations with high perceptual fidelity. In contrast to diffusion models used for downstream generation, these decoders are dedicated to faithful reconstruction rather than content generation. However, their iterative sampling process introduces significant latency, making them impractical for real-time or large-scale applications. In this work, we introduce a two-stage acceleration framework to address this inefficiency. First, we propose a multi-scale sampling strategy, where decoding begins at a coarse resolution and progressively refines the output by doubling the resolution at each stage, achieving a theoretical speedup of $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ compared to standard full-resolution sampling. Second, we distill the diffusion decoder at each scale into a single-step denoising model, enabling fast and high-quality reconstructions in a single forward pass per scale. Together, these techniques yield an order-of-magnitude reduction in decoding time with little degradation in output quality. Our approach provides a practical pathway toward efficient yet expressive image tokenizers. We hope it serves as a foundation for future work in efficient visual tokenization and downstream generation.

AI.tldr: A two-stage acceleration for diffusion-based image tokenizers: (1) multi-scale coarse-to-fine decoding with O(log n) speedup, and (2) per-scale distillation of the diffusion decoder into a single denoising step, enabling fast one-pass reconstructions while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-based decoders yield high-fidelity image reconstructions for tokenization but incur significant latency due to iterative sampling. There is a need for real-time or large-scale image tokenizers that maintain perceptual quality.
AI.method: Introduce a multi-scale sampling strategy that starts from a coarse resolution and doubles the resolution at each stage, combined with distilling the diffusion decoder at each scale into a single-step denoiser to produce fast, high-quality reconstructions per scale.
AI.result: The approach achieves an order-of-magnitude reduction in decoding time with only minor degradation in output quality, demonstrating a practical path toward efficient yet expressive image tokenizers.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework offers practical acceleration for diffusion-based image tokenizers and lays groundwork for future research in efficient visual tokenization and downstream generation.

title: Optimizing Resource-Constrained Non-Pharmaceutical Interventions for Multi-Cluster Outbreak Control Using Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
summary: Non-pharmaceutical interventions (NPIs), such as diagnostic testing and quarantine, are crucial for controlling infectious disease outbreaks but are often constrained by limited resources, particularly in early outbreak stages. In real-world public health settings, resources must be allocated across multiple outbreak clusters that emerge asynchronously, vary in size and risk, and compete for a shared resource budget. Here, a cluster corresponds to a group of close contacts generated by a single infected index case. Thus, decisions must be made under uncertainty and heterogeneous demands, while respecting operational constraints. We formulate this problem as a constrained restless multi-armed bandit and propose a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework. A global controller learns a continuous action cost multiplier that adjusts global resource demand, while a generalized local policy estimates the marginal value of allocating resources to individuals within each cluster. We evaluate the proposed framework in a realistic agent-based simulator of SARS-CoV-2 with dynamically arriving clusters. Across a wide range of system scales and testing budgets, our method consistently outperforms RMAB-inspired and heuristic baselines, improving outbreak control effectiveness by 20%-30%. Experiments on up to 40 concurrently active clusters further demonstrate that the hierarchical framework is highly scalable and enables faster decision-making than the RMAB-inspired method.

AI.tldr: A hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for allocating non-pharmaceutical interventions under resource constraints across asynchronously arriving outbreak clusters, outperforming RMAB-inspired baselines by 20-30% and scaling to 40 clusters.
AI.motivation: Resource-limited NPIs (e.g., testing, quarantine) must be allocated across multiple asynchronous outbreak clusters with varying size and risk, under uncertainty and operational constraints. The goal is to improve outbreak control during the early stages when resources are tight.
AI.method: Formulate as a constrained restless multi-armed bandit. Propose a hierarchical RL framework: a global controller learns a continuous action cost multiplier to regulate global resource demand; a generalized local policy estimates the marginal value of allocating resources to individuals within each cluster. Evaluation uses a realistic agent-based SARS-CoV-2 simulator with dynamically arriving clusters.
AI.result: The framework consistently outperforms RMAB-inspired and heuristic baselines across various system scales and testing budgets, achieving 20%-30% improvement in outbreak control. It scales to up to 40 concurrently active clusters and enables faster decision-making than the RMAB-inspired method.
AI.conclusion: A scalable hierarchical RL approach effectively allocates scarce NPIs under asynchronous demands and uncertainty, yielding substantial improvements in outbreak control and computational efficiency compared to RMAB-inspired approaches.

title: CurveStream: Boosting Streaming Video Understanding in MLLMs via Curvature-Aware Hierarchical Visual Memory Management
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models have achieved significant success in offline video understanding, yet their application to streaming videos is severely limited by the linear explosion of visual tokens, which often leads to Out-of-Memory (OOM) errors or catastrophic forgetting. Existing visual retention and memory management methods typically rely on uniform sampling, low-level physical metrics, or passive cache eviction. However, these strategies often lack intrinsic semantic awareness, potentially disrupting contextual coherence and blurring transient yet critical semantic transitions. To address these limitations, we propose CurveStream, a training-free, curvature-aware hierarchical visual memory management framework. Our approach is motivated by the key observation that high-curvature regions along continuous feature trajectories closely align with critical global semantic transitions. Based on this geometric insight, CurveStream evaluates real-time semantic intensity via a Curvature Score and integrates an online K-Sigma dynamic threshold to adaptively route frames into clear and fuzzy memory states under a strict token budget. Evaluations across diverse temporal scales confirm that this lightweight framework, CurveStream, consistently yields absolute performance gains of over 10% (e.g., 10.69% on StreamingBench and 13.58% on OVOBench) over respective baselines, establishing new state-of-the-art results for streaming video perception.The code will be released at https://github.com/streamingvideos/CurveStream.

AI.tldr: CurveStream is a training-free, curvature-aware memory management framework for streaming video LLMs that uses a curvature score to identify semantic transitions and an online K-Sigma threshold to route frames into memory states under a token budget, achieving ~10%+ gains on StreamingBench and OVOBench.
AI.motivation: Streaming video understanding with LLMs is hampered by token explosion and memory constraints; existing memory methods lack semantic-awareness, risking disruption of contextual coherence and missing transient transitions.
AI.method: Compute real-time semantic intensity via curvature score along continuous feature trajectories; an online K-Sigma adaptive threshold routes frames into clear vs fuzzy memory under a strict token budget; no training required; lightweight, streaming-friendly.
AI.result: Empirical gains of 10.69% on StreamingBench and 13.58% on OVOBench over baselines; establishes new state-of-the-art results for streaming video perception; code to be released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: Curvature-aware memory management is effective for streaming video perception and offers a training-free solution with strong performance gains; potential for broad applicability beyond the reported benchmarks.

title: LoD-Loc v3: Generalized Aerial Localization in Dense Cities using Instance Silhouette Alignment
summary: We present LoD-Loc v3, a novel method for generalized aerial visual localization in dense urban environments. While prior work LoD-Loc v2 achieves localization through semantic building silhouette alignment with low-detail city models, it suffers from two key limitations: poor cross-scene generalization and frequent failure in dense building scenes. Our method addresses these challenges through two key innovations. First, we develop a new synthetic data generation pipeline that produces InsLoD-Loc - the largest instance segmentation dataset for aerial imagery to date, comprising 100k images with precise instance building annotations. This enables trained models to exhibit remarkable zero-shot generalization capability. Second, we reformulate the localization paradigm by shifting from semantic to instance silhouette alignment, which significantly reduces pose estimation ambiguity in dense scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LoD-Loc v3 outperforms existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines, achieving superior performance in both cross-scene and dense urban scenarios with a large margin. The project is available at https://nudt-sawlab.github.io/LoD-Locv3/.

AI.tldr: LoD-Loc v3 introduces a synthetic instance-level aerial dataset (InsLoD-Loc) and shifts localization from semantic silhouettes to instance silhouettes, achieving strong cross-scene generalization and robustness in dense urban scenes, outperforming SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: LoD-Loc v2 struggles with cross-scene generalization and dense-building failures due to reliance on low-detail semantic silhouette alignment with city models; there is a need for better generalization and disambiguation in dense urban localization.
AI.method: Two key innovations: (1) InsLoD-Loc, a synthetic data pipeline producing the largest instance-building segmentation dataset for aerial imagery (100k images with precise instance annotations) enabling zero-shot generalization; (2) reformulation from semantic silhouette alignment to instance silhouette alignment to significantly reduce pose estimation ambiguity in dense scenes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show LoD-Loc v3 outperforms existing SOTA baselines by large margins in both cross-scene and dense urban scenarios.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves cross-scene generalization and robustness for aerial localization; the synthetic dataset enables zero-shot transfer; instance silhouette alignment reduces pose ambiguity in dense environments; code/data available at the provided project URL.

title: MagicSeg: Open-World Segmentation Pretraining via Counterfactural Diffusion-Based Auto-Generation
summary: Open-world semantic segmentation presently relies significantly on extensive image-text pair datasets, which often suffer from a lack of fine-grained pixel annotations on sufficient categories. The acquisition of such data is rendered economically prohibitive due to the substantial investments of both human labor and time. In light of the formidable image generation capabilities of diffusion models, we introduce a novel diffusion model-driven pipeline for automatically generating datasets tailored to the needs of open-world semantic segmentation, named "MagicSeg". Our MagicSeg initiates from class labels and proceeds to generate high-fidelity textual descriptions, which in turn serve as guidance for the diffusion model to generate images. Rather than only generating positive samples for each label, our process encompasses the simultaneous generation of corresponding negative images, designed to serve as paired counterfactual samples for contrastive training. Then, to provide a self-supervised signal for open-world segmentation pretraining, our MagicSeg integrates an open-vocabulary detection model and an interactive segmentation model to extract object masks as precise segmentation labels from images based on the provided category labels. By applying our dataset to the contrastive language-image pretraining model with the pseudo mask supervision and the auxiliary counterfactual contrastive training, the downstream model obtains strong performance on open-world semantic segmentation. We evaluate our model on PASCAL VOC, PASCAL Context, and COCO, achieving SOTA with performance of 62.9%, 26.7%, and 40.2%, respectively, demonstrating our dataset's effectiveness in enhancing open-world semantic segmentation capabilities. Project website: https://github.com/ckxhp/magicseg.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-model-driven pipeline (MagicSeg) generates synthetic image-text data and pseudo masks to pretrain open-world semantic segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art results on VOC, Pascal Context, and COCO by combining positive/negative samples, open-vocabulary detection, and counterfactual contrastive learning.
AI.motivation: Open-world semantic segmentation requires fine-grained pixel-annotated data across many categories, but collecting such data is costly. Diffusion models enable high-fidelity image synthesis guided by textual descriptions, offering scalable data generation for diverse categories.
AI.method: From class labels, generate descriptive text prompts to steer a diffusion model in producing images. Simultaneously generate negative (counterfactual) images. Use an open-vocabulary detector and an interactive segmentation model to extract object masks from generated images according to the labels, providing pseudo segmentation labels. Train a contrastive language–image model with pseudo mask supervision and auxiliary counterfactual contrastive learning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks: PASCAL VOC 62.9%, PASCAL Context 26.7%, COCO 40.2%, demonstrating effectiveness of synthetic-data-driven open-world segmentation.
AI.conclusion: A diffusion-driven synthetic-data pipeline with pseudo mask supervision and counterfactual contrastive learning can substantially enhance open-world semantic segmentation, reducing reliance on costly real pixel-level annotations while maintaining competitive accuracy.

title: DynFlowDrive: Flow-Based Dynamic World Modeling for Autonomous Driving
summary: Recently, world models have been incorporated into the autonomous driving systems to improve the planning reliability. Existing approaches typically predict future states through appearance generation or deterministic regression, which limits their ability to capture trajectory-conditioned scene evolution and leads to unreliable action planning. To address this, we propose DynFlowDrive, a latent world model that leverages flow-based dynamics to model the transition of world states under different driving actions. By adopting the rectifiedflow formulation, the model learns a velocity field that describes how the scene state changes under different driving actions, enabling progressive prediction of future latent states. Building upon this, we further introduce a stability-aware multi-mode trajectory selection strategy that evaluates candidate trajectories according to the stability of the induced scene transitions. Extensive experiments on the nuScenes and NavSim benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements across diverse driving frameworks without introducing additional inference overhead. Source code will be abaliable at https://github.com/xiaolul2/DynFlowDrive.

AI.tldr: Latent flow-based world model (DynFlowDrive) for autonomous driving that models action-conditioned scene evolution via a velocity field learned in rectifiedflow, enabling progressive latent-state prediction, paired with a stability-aware multi-mode trajectory selector; improves planning across nuScenes/NavSim without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Existing methods rely on appearance-based generation or deterministic regression to predict future states, which poorly capture how scenes evolve conditioned on actions, limiting planning reliability.
AI.method: Introduce DynFlowDrive: a latent world model with flow-based dynamics; use rectifiedflow to learn a velocity field governing state transitions under driving actions and enable progressive latent-state prediction; add stability-aware multi-mode trajectory selection evaluating candidate trajectories by the stability of the induced scene transitions.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on nuScenes and NavSim shows consistent performance gains across diverse driving frameworks, without increasing inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: Flow-based latent dynamics combined with a stability-aware multi-modal planning strategy enhances planning reliability and efficiency for autonomous driving; code will be released at GitHub.

title: Deep Hilbert--Galerkin Methods for Infinite-Dimensional PDEs and Optimal Control
summary:
We develop deep learning-based approximation methods for fully nonlinear second-order PDEs on separable Hilbert spaces, such as HJB equations for infinite-dimensional control, by parameterizing solutions via Hilbert--Galerkin Neural Operators (HGNOs). We prove the first Universal Approximation Theorems (UATs) which are sufficiently powerful to address these problems, based on novel topologies for Hessian terms and corresponding novel continuity assumptions on the fully nonlinear operator. These topologies are non-sequential and non-metrizable, making the problem delicate. In particular, we prove UATs for functions on Hilbert spaces, together with their Fréchet derivatives up to second order, and for unbounded operators applied to the first derivative, ensuring that HGNOs are able to approximate all the PDE terms. For control problems, we further prove UATs for optimal feedback controls in terms of our approximating value function HGNO.
  We develop numerical training methods, which we call Deep Hilbert--Galerkin and Hilbert Actor-Critic (reinforcement learning) Methods, for these problems by minimizing the $L^2_μ(H)$-norm of the residual of the PDE on the whole Hilbert space, not just a projected PDE to finite dimensions. This is the first paper to propose such an approach. The models considered arise in many applied sciences, such as functional differential equations in physics and Kolmogorov and HJB PDEs related to controlled PDEs, SPDEs, path-dependent systems, partially observed stochastic systems, and mean-field SDEs. We numerically solve examples of Kolmogorov and HJB PDEs related to the optimal control of deterministic and stochastic heat and Burgers' equations, demonstrating the promise of our deep learning-based approach.

AI.tldr: HGNOs (Hilbert--Galerkin Neural Operators) provide universal approximation for fully nonlinear second-order PDEs on Hilbert spaces, with new non-sequential, non-metrizable Hessian topologies; introduce training via residual minimization on the whole Hilbert space (Deep Hilbert–Galerkin, Hilbert Actor-Critic); validated on Kolmogorov and HJB PDEs in infinite-dimensional control and related SPDEs.
AI.motivation: To enable neural-operator based approximation of fully nonlinear PDEs in infinite-dimensional settings (Hilbert spaces), overcoming the lack of finite-dimensional reduction and ensuring universal approximation for operators involving second-order derivatives and unbounded first-derivative terms; address control, SPDEs, mean-field problems.
AI.method: Define Hilbert-Galerkin Neural Operators to parameterize solutions; establish universal approximation theorems for Hilbert-space valued functions and their Fréchet derivatives up to second order, including unbounded operators on first derivatives, using novel non-sequential, non-metrizable Hessian topologies and continuity assumptions; train by minimizing L2_μ(H) residual over the entire Hilbert space via Deep Hilbert-Galerkin and Hilbert Actor-Critic methods.
AI.result: First UATs for functions on Hilbert spaces and their second-order Fréchet derivatives with unbounded first-derivative operators; numerical training framework that minimizes the PDE residual on the whole Hilbert space; successful numerical experiments on Kolmogorov and HJB PDEs tied to optimal control problems of deterministic and stochastic heat and Burgers’ equations.
AI.conclusion: HGNOs offer a rigorous, scalable framework to approximate fully nonlinear PDEs in infinite dimensions, with broad applicability to functional differential equations, SPDEs, mean-field systems, and controlled PDEs; the proposed training paradigm is novel and promising, though practical performance and computational demands remain to be explored across more problems.

title: FlowScene: Style-Consistent Indoor Scene Generation with Multimodal Graph Rectified Flow
summary: Scene generation has extensive industrial applications, demanding both high realism and precise control over geometry and appearance. Language-driven retrieval methods compose plausible scenes from a large object database, but overlook object-level control and often fail to enforce scene-level style coherence. Graph-based formulations offer higher controllability over objects and inform holistic consistency by explicitly modeling relations, yet existing methods struggle to produce high-fidelity textured results, thereby limiting their practical utility. We present FlowScene, a tri-branch scene generative model conditioned on multimodal graphs that collaboratively generates scene layouts, object shapes, and object textures. At its core lies a tight-coupled rectified flow model that exchanges object information during generation, enabling collaborative reasoning across the graph. This enables fine-grained control of objects' shapes, textures, and relations while enforcing scene-level style coherence across structure and appearance. Extensive experiments show that FlowScene outperforms both language-conditioned and graph-conditioned baselines in terms of generation realism, style consistency, and alignment with human preferences.

AI.tldr: FlowScene introduces a tri-branch, graph-conditioned generative model with a rectified flow that jointly generates scene layouts, object shapes, and textures, achieving superior realism and style coherence compared with language- and graph-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Current scene-generation methods either rely on language prompts, which lack object-level control, or graph-based approaches, which struggle with high-fidelity textures. There's a need for fine-grained control over geometry and appearance while maintaining scene-level style coherence.
AI.method: FlowScene uses a tri-branch architecture that separately generates layouts, shapes, and textures conditioned on multimodal graphs. A tight-coupled rectified flow model exchanges object information during generation to enable collaborative reasoning across the graph, enforcing consistency of structure and appearance at the scene level.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FlowScene surpasses both language-conditioned and graph-conditioned baselines in realism, style consistency, and alignment with human preferences.
AI.conclusion: FlowScene delivers fine-grained control over object geometry and textures with holistic scene coherence, improving practical utility for texture-rich, controllable scene generation.

title: LIORNet: Self-Supervised LiDAR Snow Removal Framework for Autonomous Driving under Adverse Weather Conditions
summary: LiDAR sensors provide high-resolution 3D perception and long-range detection, making them indispensable for autonomous driving and robotics. However, their performance significantly degrades under adverse weather conditions such as snow, rain, and fog, where spurious noise points dominate the point cloud and lead to false perception. To address this problem, various approaches have been proposed: distance-based filters exploiting spatial sparsity, intensity-based filters leveraging reflectance distributions, and learning-based methods that adapt to complex environments. Nevertheless, distance-based methods struggle to distinguish valid object points from noise, intensity-based methods often rely on fixed thresholds that lack adaptability to changing conditions, and learning-based methods suffer from the high cost of annotation, limited generalization, and computational overhead. In this study, we propose LIORNet, which eliminates these drawbacks and integrates the strengths of all three paradigms. LIORNet is built upon a U-Net++ backbone and employs a self-supervised learning strategy guided by pseudo-labels generated from multiple physical and statistical cues, including range-dependent intensity thresholds, snow reflectivity, point sparsity, and sensing range constraints. This design enables LIORNet to distinguish noise points from environmental structures without requiring manual annotations, thereby overcoming the difficulty of snow labeling and the limitations of single-principle approaches. Extensive experiments on the WADS and CADC datasets demonstrate that LIORNet outperforms state-of-the-art filtering algorithms in both accuracy and runtime while preserving critical environmental features. These results highlight LIORNet as a practical and robust solution for LiDAR perception in extreme weather, with strong potential for real-time deployment in autonomous driving systems.

AI.tldr: LIORNet is a self-supervised LiDAR noise-filtering network built on U-Net++ that integrates distance-, intensity-, and learning-based cues with pseudo-labels to filter spurious points under winter conditions, avoiding manual annotations and achieving superior accuracy and speed on WADS and CADC datasets.
AI.motivation: LiDAR perception deteriorates under adverse weather due to spurious noise; existing filters either struggle to separate noise from real points, rely on fixed thresholds, or require costly annotations and have limited generalization. A unified, self-supervised framework leveraging multiple cues can improve robustness and efficiency.
AI.method: LIORNet uses a U-Net++ backbone with self-supervised learning guided by pseudo-labels derived from (i) range-dependent intensity thresholds, (ii) snow reflectivity, (iii) point sparsity, and (iv) sensing range constraints. These cues enable per-point labeling without manual annotation, enabling learning-based filtering that respects physical and statistical properties of the scene.
AI.result: Experiments on the WADS and CADC datasets show that LIORNet outperforms state-of-the-art filtering methods in both accuracy and runtime, while preserving essential environmental features.
AI.conclusion: LIORNet offers a practical, robust solution for LiDAR perception in extreme weather with potential for real-time deployment in autonomous driving systems, addressing annotation cost, generalization, and computational concerns.

title: Global Convergence of Multiplicative Updates for the Matrix Mechanism: A Collaborative Proof with Gemini 3
summary:
We analyze a fixed-point iteration $v \leftarrow φ(v)$ arising in the optimization of a regularized nuclear norm objective involving the Hadamard product structure, posed in~\cite{denisov} in the context of an optimization problem over the space of algorithms in private machine learning. We prove that the iteration $v^{(k+1)} = \text{diag}((D_{v^{(k)}}^{1/2} M D_{v^{(k)}}^{1/2})^{1/2})$ converges monotonically to the unique global optimizer of the potential function $J(v) = 2 \text{Tr}((D_v^{1/2} M D_v^{1/2})^{1/2}) - \sum v_i$, closing a problem left open there.
  The bulk of this proof was provided by Gemini 3, subject to some corrections and interventions. Gemini 3 also sketched the initial version of this note. Thus, it represents as much a commentary on the practical use of AI in mathematics as it represents the closure of a small gap in the literature. As such, we include a small narrative description of the prompting process, and some resulting principles for working with AI to prove mathematics.

AI.tldr: Proves a fixed-point iteration for a Hadamard-structured, regularized nuclear-norm objective converges monotonically to the unique global minimizer; closes an open problem and discusses AI-assisted mathematical practice.
AI.motivation: Address optimization over algorithms in private machine learning by analyzing a fixed-point scheme for a nuclear-norm objective with Hadamard product; resolve a problem left open in Denisov.
AI.method: Analyze the iteration v^{(k+1)} = diag((D_{v^{(k)}}^{1/2} M D_{v^{(k)}}^{1/2})^{1/2}); establish monotone convergence to the unique minimizer of the potential J(v) = 2 Tr((D_v^{1/2} M D_v^{1/2})^{1/2}) - ∑ v_i; correct and finalize a prior partial proof (Gemini 3) and provide AI-driven commentary.
AI.result: Monotone convergence to the unique global optimizer of J(v); the open problem is closed; the bulk of the proof is attributed to Gemini 3 with subsequent corrections; the note also presents an AI-assisted narrative describing prompting process and practical principles for using AI in proving mathematics.
AI.conclusion: The fixed-point scheme yields a robust, globally optimal solution for the Hadamard-structured nuclear-norm problem; the work also exemplifies AI-assisted mathematical practice and offers guidance on how AI can support rigorous proofs and literature closure.

title: K-GMRF: Kinetic Gauss-Markov Random Field for First-Principles Covariance Tracking on Lie Groups
summary: Tracking non-stationary covariance matrices is fundamental to vision yet hindered by existing estimators that either neglect manifold constraints or rely on first-order updates, incurring inevitable phase lag during rapid evolution. We propose K-GMRF, an online, training-free framework for covariance tracking that reformulates the problem as forced rigid-body motion on Lie groups. Derived from the Euler-Poincaré equations, our method interprets observations as torques driving a latent angular velocity, propagated via a structure-preserving symplectic integrator. We theoretically prove that this second-order dynamics achieves zero steady-state error under constant rotation, strictly superior to the proportional lag of first-order baselines. Validation across three domains demonstrates robust tracking fidelity: (i) on synthetic ellipses, K-GMRF reduces angular error by 30x compared to Riemannian EMA while maintaining stability at high speeds; (ii) on SO(3) stabilization with 20% dropout, it decreases geodesic error from 29.4° to 9.9°; and (iii) on OTB motion-blur sequences, it improves loU from 0.55 to 0.74 on BlurCar2 with a 96% success rate. As a fully differentiable symplectic module, K-GMRF provides a plug-and-play geometric prior for data-constrained scenarios and an interpretable layer within modern deep architectures.

AI.tldr: Online, training-free covariance tracking on Lie groups using second-order Euler–Poincaré dynamics (K-GMRF). Interprets observations as torques driving latent angular velocity, propagated with a structure-preserving symplectic integrator; achieves zero steady-state error under constant rotation and outperforms first-order baselines across three domains; fully differentiable and plug-and-play as a geometric prior.
AI.motivation: Non-stationary covariance tracking is essential for vision tasks but current estimators either ignore manifold constraints or rely on first-order updates, leading to phase lag during rapid evolution.
AI.method: Reformulate covariance tracking as forced rigid-body motion on Lie groups and derive second-order dynamics from Euler–Poincaré equations. Observations act as torques driving a latent angular velocity; propagation uses a structure-preserving symplectic integrator. The approach is training-free and differentiable, serving as a geometric prior module.
AI.result: Synthetic ellipses: angular error reduced ~30x vs Riemannian EMA at high speeds. SO(3) stabilization with 20% dropout: geodesic error 29.4°→9.9°. OTB BlurCar2: loU 0.55→0.74 with 96% success. Demonstrates robust tracking fidelity and stability across domains.
AI.conclusion: K-GMRF provides a fully differentiable, plug-and-play geometric prior for covariance tracking on Lie groups, offering interpretable second-order dynamics with zero steady-state error under constant rotation and broad applicability in data-constrained scenarios.

title: A Unified Platform and Quality Assurance Framework for 3D Ultrasound Reconstruction with Robotic, Optical, and Electromagnetic Tracking
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Three-dimensional (3D) Ultrasound (US) can facilitate diagnosis, treatment planning, and image-guided therapy. However, current studies rarely provide a comprehensive evaluation of volumetric accuracy and reproducibility, highlighting the need for robust Quality Assurance (QA) frameworks, particularly for tracked 3D US reconstruction using freehand or robotic acquisition. This study presents a QA framework for 3D US reconstruction and a flexible open source platform for tracked US research. A custom phantom containing geometric inclusions with varying symmetry properties enables straightforward evaluation of optical, electromagnetic, and robotic kinematic tracking for 3D US at different scanning speeds and insonation angles. A standardised pipeline performs real-time segmentation and 3D reconstruction of geometric targets (DSC = 0.97, FPS = 46) without GPU acceleration, followed by automated registration and comparison with ground-truth geometries. Applying this framework showed that our robotic 3D US achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction performance (DSC-3D = 0.94 +- 0.01, HD95 = 1.17 +- 0.12), approaching the spatial resolution limit imposed by the transducer. This work establishes a flexible experimental platform and a reproducible validation methodology for 3D US reconstruction. The proposed framework enables robust cross-platform comparisons and improved reporting practices, supporting the safe and effective clinical translation of 3D ultrasound in diagnostic and image-guided therapy applications.

AI.tldr: QA framework and open-source platform for tracked 3D ultrasound reconstruction with a custom phantom; real-time segmentation and 3D reconstruction; validated against ground-truth geometries; robust cross-platform comparisons and potential clinical translation.
AI.motivation: Current 3D US lacks comprehensive evaluation of volumetric accuracy and reproducibility, necessitating standardized QA frameworks for tracked 3D US (freehand or robotic) reconstruction.
AI.method: Developed a flexible QA framework and open-source platform. Employed a custom phantom with geometric inclusions to evaluate optical, electromagnetic, and robotic kinematics across scanning speeds and insonation angles. Implemented real-time segmentation and 3D reconstruction of geometric targets (DSC=0.97, FPS=46) without GPU acceleration, followed by automated registration and comparison with ground-truth geometries.
AI.result: Robotic 3D US achieved state-of-the-art reconstruction performance (DSC-3D=0.94±0.01, HD95=1.17±0.12), approaching the spatial resolution limit of the transducer.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a flexible experimental platform and reproducible validation methodology for 3D US reconstruction, enabling robust cross-platform comparisons and improved reporting to support safe and effective clinical translation of 3D ultrasound in diagnostic and image-guided therapy.

title: Adaptive Layerwise Perturbation: Unifying Off-Policy Corrections for LLM RL
summary: Off-policy problems such as policy staleness and training-inference mismatch, has become a major bottleneck for training stability and further exploration for LLM RL. To enhance inference efficiency, the distribution gap between the inference and updated policy grows, leading to heavy-tailed importance ratios. Heavy-tailed ratios arise when the policy is locally sharp, which further inflates sharp gradients and can push updates outside the trust region. To address this, we propose Adaptive Layerwise Perturbation(ALP) by injecting small learnable perturbations into input hidden states of each layer during updates, which is used as the numerator of the importance ratio against the unchanged inference policy in the objective. Intuitively, by adding controlled noise to intermediate representations, ALP prevents the updated policy from deviating too sharply from the inference policy, and enlarges the policy family to cover the inference policy family with mismatch noises. Hence, the flattened distribution can naturally tighten the updated and inference policy gap and reduce the tail of importance ratios, thus maintaining training stability. This is further validated empirically. Experiments on single-turn math and multi-turn tool-integrated reasoning tasks show that ALP not only improves final performance, but also avoid blow up of importance ratio tail and KL spikes during iterative training, along with boosted exploration. Ablations show that representation-level perturbations across all layers are most effective, substantially outperforming partial-layer and logits-only variants.

AI.tldr: Adaptive Layerwise Perturbation (ALP) mitigates heavy-tailed importance ratios in off-policy LLM RL by injecting small learnable perturbations into layer inputs during updates, aligning updated and inference policies and improving stability and exploration; full-layer representation perturbations outperform partial-layer or logits-only variants.
AI.motivation: Off-policy issues like policy staleness and training-inference mismatch hinder training stability and exploration in LLM RL. Heavy-tailed importance weights arise when the updated policy becomes locally sharp, risking large policy updates and trust-region violations.
