AI.method: Introduce ALP: add small, learnable perturbations to hidden states at each layer during parameter updates; use these perturbations as the numerator of the importance ratio against the unchanged inference policy in the objective; train perturbations jointly to tighten the gap between updated and inference policies and flatten the importance-weight tail. Ablation compares perturbation locations (all layers vs. partial layers vs. logits-only).
AI.result: Empirical validation on single-turn math and multi-turn tool-integrated reasoning tasks shows ALP improves final performance and prevents blow-ups in the tail of importance ratios and KL spikes during iterative training, while boosting exploration.
AI.conclusion: Representation-level perturbations across all layers are most effective, substantially outperforming partial-layer and logits-only variants, and provide training stability and enhanced exploration without altering inference policy.

title: Beyond Quadratic: Linear-Time Change Detection with RWKV
summary: Existing paradigms for remote sensing change detection are caught in a trade-off: CNNs excel at efficiency but lack global context, while Transformers capture long-range dependencies at a prohibitive computational cost. This paper introduces ChangeRWKV, a new architecture that reconciles this conflict. By building upon the Receptance Weighted Key Value (RWKV) framework, our ChangeRWKV uniquely combines the parallelizable training of Transformers with the linear-time inference of RNNs. Our approach core features two key innovations: a hierarchical RWKV encoder that builds multi-resolution feature representation, and a novel Spatial-Temporal Fusion Module (STFM) engineered to resolve spatial misalignments across scales while distilling fine-grained temporal discrepancies. ChangeRWKV not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on the LEVIR-CD benchmark, with an 85.46% IoU and 92.16% F1 score, but does so while drastically reducing parameters and FLOPs compared to previous leading methods. This work demonstrates a new, efficient, and powerful paradigm for operational-scale change detection. Our code and model are publicly available.

AI.tldr: ChangeRWKV introduces a RWKV-based architecture that merges Transformer-like parallel training with RNN-like linear-time inference for efficient, scalable remote-sensing change detection. It uses a hierarchical RWKV encoder and a Spatial-Temporal Fusion Module (STFM) to achieve multi-scale spatial-temporal alignment, delivering state-of-the-art LEVIR-CD results with reduced parameters and FLOPs, and releasing code publicly.
AI.motivation: The paper tackles the CNN-vs-Transformer trade-off in remote-sensing change detection: achieve global context and long-range dependencies without prohibitive computation, enabling operational-scale deployment.
AI.method: Proposes two main innovations: (1) a hierarchical RWKV encoder to build multi-resolution feature representations, and (2) a Spatial-Temporal Fusion Module (STFM) to resolve spatial misalignments across scales while distilling fine-grained temporal differences. The architecture leverages the RWKV framework to combine Transformer-like parallelism with RNN-like linear-time inference.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art LEVIR-CD metrics with IoU of 85.46% and F1 score of 92.16%, while drastically reducing parameters and FLOPs relative to prior leading methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates an efficient, scalable, and powerful paradigm for operational-scale change detection, balancing accuracy and efficiency; public release of code and model supports reproducibility and practical adoption.

title: Spectral Alignment in Forward-Backward Representations via Temporal Abstraction
summary: Forward-backward (FB) representations provide a powerful framework for learning the successor representation (SR) in continuous spaces by enforcing a low-rank factorization. However, a fundamental spectral mismatch often exists between the high-rank transition dynamics of continuous environments and the low-rank bottleneck of the FB architecture, making accurate low-rank representation learning difficult. In this work, we analyze temporal abstraction as a mechanism to mitigate this mismatch. By characterizing the spectral properties of the transition operator, we show that temporal abstraction acts as a low-pass filter that suppresses high-frequency spectral components. This suppression reduces the effective rank of the induced SR while preserving a formal bound on the resulting value function error. Empirically, we show that this alignment is a key factor for stable FB learning, particularly at high discount factors where bootstrapping becomes error-prone. Our results identify temporal abstraction as a principled mechanism for shaping the spectral structure of the underlying MDP and enabling effective long-horizon representations in continuous control.

AI.tldr: Temporal abstraction acts as a low-pass filter on the transition spectrum, reducing the effective rank of the successor representation and enabling stable forward-backward learning for long-horizon control in continuous spaces.
AI.motivation: To address the spectral mismatch between the high-rank transition dynamics of continuous environments and the low-rank bottleneck of forward-backward architectures, and to improve stability of long-horizon SR learning (especially at high discount factors).
AI.method: Perform theoretical spectral analysis of the transition operator to show how temporal abstraction suppresses high-frequency components, derive a bound on the induced value-function error, and provide empirical validation of stability and performance in continuous-control tasks.
AI.result: Temporal abstraction reduces the effective rank of the induced SR by filtering high-frequency spectral components, while preserving a formal bound on value-function error; empirically, this alignment yields more stable FB learning, particularly at high discount factors.
AI.conclusion: Temporal abstraction is a principled mechanism to shape the spectral structure of the underlying MDP, enabling effective long-horizon representations in continuous control by mitigating spectral mismatch with FB representations.

title: TRACE: Trajectory Recovery with State Propagation Diffusion for Urban Mobility
comment: This article is accepted by WWW 2026, Dubai, United Arab Emirates
summary: High-quality GPS trajectories are essential for location-based web services and smart city applications, including navigation, ride-sharing and delivery. However, due to low sampling rates and limited infrastructure coverage during data collection, real-world trajectories are often sparse and feature unevenly distributed location points. Recovering these trajectories into dense and continuous forms is essential but challenging, given their complex and irregular spatio-temporal patterns. In this paper, we introduce a novel diffusion model for trajectory recovery named TRACE, which reconstruct dense and continuous trajectories from sparse and incomplete inputs. At the core of TRACE, we propose a State Propagation Diffusion Model (SPDM), which integrates a novel memory mechanism, so that during the denoising process, TRACE can retain and leverage intermediate results from previous steps to effectively reconstruct those hard-to-recover trajectory segments. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world datasets show that TRACE outperforms the state-of-the-art, offering $>$26\% accuracy improvement without significant inference overhead. Our work strengthens the foundation for mobile and web-connected location services, advancing the quality and fairness of data-driven urban applications. Code is available at: https://github.com/JinmingWang/TRACE

AI.tldr: TRACE is a diffusion-based trajectory recovery method that densifies sparse GPS data using a State Propagation Diffusion Model with a memory mechanism, achieving substantial accuracy gains with efficient inference; code released.
AI.motivation: Real-world GPS trajectories are sparse due to low sampling rates and uneven coverage, hindering reliable location-based services and urban analytics; dense, continuous trajectories are needed for accurate modeling and decision-making.
AI.method: A diffusion model-based framework named TRACE incorporating a novel memory-enabled State Propagation Diffusion Model (SPDM) that retains and leverages intermediate denoising states across steps to better reconstruct hard-to-recover trajectory segments.
AI.result: TRACE outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by over 26% in accuracy on multiple real-world datasets, with no significant inference overhead.
AI.conclusion: The approach strengthens data-driven urban applications by improving trajectory quality and fairness, and provides a publicly available codebase for reproducibility.

title: Physion-Eval: Evaluating Physical Realism in Generated Video via Human Reasoning
summary: Video generation models are increasingly used as world simulators for storytelling, simulation, and embodied AI. As these models advance, a key question arises: do generated videos obey the physical laws of the real world? Existing evaluations largely rely on automated metrics or coarse human judgments such as preferences or rubric-based checks. While useful for assessing perceptual quality, these methods provide limited insight into when and why generated dynamics violate real-world physical constraints. We introduce Physion-Eval, a large-scale benchmark of expert human reasoning for diagnosing physical realism failures in videos generated by five state-of-the-art models across egocentric and exocentric views, containing 10,990 expert reasoning traces spanning 22 fine-grained physical categories. Each generated video is derived from a corresponding real-world reference video depicting a clear physical process, and annotated with temporally localized glitches, structured failure categories, and natural-language explanations of the violated physical behavior. Using this dataset, we reveal a striking limitation of current video generation models: in physics-critical scenarios, 83.3% of exocentric and 93.5% of egocentric generated videos exhibit at least one human-identifiable physical glitch. We hope Physion-Eval will set a new standard for physical realism evaluation and guide the development of physics-grounded video generation. The benchmark is publicly available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/PhysionLabs/Physion-Eval.

AI.tldr: Physion-Eval: a large-scale, expert-driven benchmark to diagnose physical realism failures in video generation. It pairs generated videos from five SOTA models (egocentric and exocentric views) with real references, annotates localized glitches across 22 physical categories, and gathers 10,990 expert reasoning traces to quantify and explain physics violations.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics capture perceptual quality but miss structural physics failures in generated videos. A human-expert benchmark with localized glitches and explanations is needed to diagnose when and why physics constraints fail and guide physics-grounded video generation.
AI.method: Construct Physion-Eval by collecting generated videos from five state-of-the-art models across egocentric and exocentric views, each paired with a corresponding real-world reference video. Expert annotators produce temporally localized glitches, structured failure categories across 22 physical factors, and natural-language explanations. The dataset contains 10,990 expert reasoning traces.
AI.result: In physics-critical scenarios, a large majority of videos contain at least one human-identifiable physical glitch: 83.3% of exocentric and 93.5% of egocentric videos. The dataset is publicly available and aims to set a new standard for physics-grounded evaluation of video generation.
AI.conclusion: Physion-Eval provides a comprehensive, human-expertally labeled benchmark that enables diagnosing, comparing, and eventually steering the development of physics-aware video generation models.

title: MeanFlow Meets Control: Scaling Sampled-Data Control for Swarms
summary: Steering large-scale swarms in only a few control updates is challenging because real systems operate in sampled-data form: control inputs are updated intermittently and applied over finite intervals. In this regime, the natural object is not an instantaneous velocity field, but a finite-window control quantity that captures the system response over each sampling interval. Inspired by MeanFlow, we introduce a control-space learning framework for swarm steering under linear time-invariant dynamics. The learned object is the coefficient that parameterizes the finite-horizon minimum-energy control over each interval. We show that this coefficient admits both an integral representation and a local differential identity along bridge trajectories, which leads to a simple stop-gradient training objective. At implementation time, the learned coefficient is used directly in sampled-data updates, so the prescribed dynamics and actuation map are respected by construction. The resulting framework provides a scalable approach to few-step swarm steering that is consistent with the sampled-data structure of real control systems.

AI.tldr: Learn a finite-horizon control coefficient for mean-flow–inspired swarm steering under linear time-invariant dynamics, enabling stop-gradient training and direct sampled-data updates for scalable, few-step control.
AI.motivation: Real swarm systems operate in sampled-data form; control inputs update intermittently over finite intervals. The natural object is a finite-window control quantity rather than an instantaneous velocity field, motivating a learning framework that respects the discretized actuation and its horizon.
AI.method: Introduce a control-space learning framework where the learned object is a coefficient that parameterizes the finite-horizon minimum-energy control for each sampling interval. Prove an integral representation and a local differential identity along bridge trajectories, yielding a simple stop-gradient training objective. Implement by applying the learned coefficient directly in sampled-data updates to preserve the prescribed dynamics and actuation map.
AI.result: A scalable, few-step swarm-steering approach that is consistent with the sampled-data structure of real control systems and leverages a learned finite-horizon control coefficient.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a practical, dynamics-consistent method for steering large-scale swarms under LTI dynamics in sampled-data settings, with a simple training objective and direct applicability to real-time updates.

title: Any-Subgroup Equivariant Networks via Symmetry Breaking
summary: The inclusion of symmetries as an inductive bias, known as equivariance, often improves generalization on geometric data (e.g. grids, sets, and graphs). However, equivariant architectures are usually highly constrained, designed for symmetries chosen a priori, and not applicable to datasets with other symmetries. This precludes the development of flexible, multi-modal foundation models capable of processing diverse data equivariantly. In this work, we build a single model -- the Any-Subgroup Equivariant Network (ASEN) -- that can be simultaneously equivariant to several groups, simply by modulating a certain auxiliary input feature. In particular, we start with a fully permutation-equivariant base model, and then obtain subgroup equivariance by using a symmetry-breaking input whose automorphism group is that subgroup. However, finding an input with the desired automorphism group is computationally hard. We overcome this by relaxing from exact to approximate symmetry breaking, leveraging the notion of 2-closure to derive fast algorithms. Theoretically, we show that our subgroup-equivariant networks can simulate equivariant MLPs, and their universality can be guaranteed if the base model is universal. Empirically, we validate our method on symmetry selection for graph and image tasks, as well as multitask and transfer learning for sequence tasks, showing that a single network equivariant to multiple permutation subgroups outperforms both separate equivariant models and a single non-equivariant model.

AI.tldr: ASEN is a single base model that achieves equivariance to multiple permutation subgroups by modulating an auxiliary input, using approximate symmetry breaking via 2-closure, with universality preserved if the base model is universal; it outperforms separate or non-equivariant baselines.
AI.motivation: Equivariant architectures improve generalization by encoding symmetries but are usually fixed to a chosen set of symmetries, hindering flexible, multi-modal models that must handle diverse data with different symmetries.
AI.method: Start from a fully permutation-equivariant base model. induce subgroup equivariance by conditioning on a symmetry-breaking auxiliary input whose automorphism group equals the desired subgroup. Since finding exact inputs is hard, employ approximate symmetry breaking using 2-closure to derive fast algorithms. Prove that subgroup-equivariant networks can simulate equivariant MLPs and that universality holds if the base model is universal.
AI.result: The approach is theoretically sound and empirically validated: a single network equivariant to multiple permutation subgroups can outperform both several separate equivariant models and a single non-equivariant model on tasks involving graphs, images, and sequences, with successful symmetry selection, multitask, and transfer learning.
AI.conclusion: ASEN enables flexible, multi-subgroup equivariant foundation models by replacing exact symmetry breaking with efficient approximate methods, preserving universality and delivering practical gains across diverse data modalities.

title: FB-CLIP: Fine-Grained Zero-Shot Anomaly Detection with Foreground-Background Disentanglement
summary: Fine-grained anomaly detection is crucial in industrial and medical applications, but labeled anomalies are often scarce, making zero-shot detection challenging. While vision-language models like CLIP offer promising solutions, they struggle with foreground-background feature entanglement and coarse textual semantics. We propose FB-CLIP, a framework that enhances anomaly localization via multi-strategy textual representations and foreground-background separation. In the textual modality, it combines End-of-Text features, global-pooled representations, and attention-weighted token features for richer semantic cues. In the visual modality, multi-view soft separation along identity, semantic, and spatial dimensions, together with background suppression, reduces interference and improves discriminability. Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR) aligns image features with normal and abnormal textual prototypes, suppressing uncertain matches and enlarging semantic gaps. Experiments show that FB-CLIP effectively distinguishes anomalies from complex backgrounds, achieving accurate fine-grained anomaly detection and localization under zero-shot settings.

AI.tldr: FB-CLIP is a zero-shot, fine-grained anomaly detection framework that strengthens CLIP-based localization via multi-strategy textual cues, foreground-background separation, and semantic consistency regularization, achieving improved detection and localization.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot anomaly detection is hampered by scarce labeled anomalies and CLIP's foreground-background entanglement and coarse semantics; better localization and semantic discrimination are needed for complex backgrounds in industrial/medical settings.
AI.method: Textual modality: End-of-Text features, global-pooled representations, and attention-weighted token features for richer semantics. Visual modality: multi-view soft separation along identity, semantic, and spatial dimensions with background suppression to reduce interference. Semantic Consistency Regularization (SCR) aligns image features with normal and abnormal textual prototypes, suppressing uncertain matches and enlarging semantic gaps.
AI.result: Empirical results show FB-CLIP improves anomaly detection and localization under zero-shot settings, demonstrating robustness to complex backgrounds and achieving finer-grained discrimination.
AI.conclusion: FB-CLIP effectively mitigates foreground-background interference and enhances semantic alignment for zero-shot, fine-grained anomaly detection and localization, with potential applications in industrial and medical domains.

title: ICLAD: In-Context Learning for Unified Tabular Anomaly Detection Across Supervision Regimes
summary: Anomaly detection on tabular data is commonly studied under three supervision regimes, including one-class settings that assume access to anomaly-free training samples, fully unsupervised settings with unlabeled and potentially contaminated training data, and semi-supervised settings with limited anomaly labels. Existing deep learning approaches typically train dataset-specific models under the assumption of a single supervision regime, which limits their ability to leverage shared structures across anomaly detection tasks and to adapt to different supervision levels. We propose ICLAD, an in-context learning foundation model for tabular anomaly detection that generalizes across both datasets and supervision regimes. ICLAD is trained via meta-learning on synthetic tabular anomaly detection tasks, and at inference time, the model assigns anomaly scores by conditioning on the training set without updating model weights. Comprehensive experiments on 57 tabular datasets from ADBench show that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance across three supervision regimes, establishing a unified framework for tabular anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: ICLAD is a meta-learned, in-context learning foundation model for tabular anomaly detection that generalizes across datasets and supervision regimes, scoring anomalies by conditioning on the training set without updating weights, and achieving state-of-the-art performance on 57 ADBench datasets.
AI.motivation: Fill the gap of dataset- and regime-specific anomaly detectors by learning a unified model that can handle one-class, unsupervised, and semi-supervised settings, leveraging shared structures across tasks.
AI.method: Meta-learning on synthetic tabular anomaly detection tasks to train an in-context scoring model; at inference, condition on the given training set to compute anomaly scores without gradient updates to model weights.
AI.result: Reported state-of-the-art performance across three supervision regimes on 57 tabular datasets from ADBench.
AI.conclusion: ICLAD offers a unified, in-context learning-based foundation for tabular anomaly detection that generalizes across datasets and supervision regimes and enables efficient anomaly scoring without retraining.

title: Stochastic Sequential Decision Making over Expanding Networks with Graph Filtering
summary: Graph filters leverage topological information to process networked data with existing methods mainly studying fixed graphs, ignoring that graphs often expand as nodes continually attach with an unknown pattern. The latter requires developing filter-based decision-making paradigms that take evolution and uncertainty into account. Existing approaches rely on either pre-designed filters or online learning, limited to a myopic view considering only past or present information. To account for future impacts, we propose a stochastic sequential decision-making framework for filtering networked data with a policy that adapts filtering to expanding graphs. By representing filter shifts as agents, we model the filter as a multi-agent system and train the policy following multi-agent reinforcement learning. This accounts for long-term rewards and captures expansion dynamics through sequential decision-making. Moreover, we develop a context-aware graph neural network to parameterize the policy, which tunes filter parameters based on information of both the graph and agents. Experiments on synthetic and real datasets from cold-start recommendation to COVID prediction highlight the benefits of using a sequential decision-making perspective over batch and online filtering alternatives.

AI.tldr: Introduces a stochastic sequential decision-making framework for adaptive filtering on expanding graphs, modeling filter shifts as agents and training a MARL-based policy via a context-aware GNN; demonstrates superiority over batch/online filtering on cold-start recommendation and COVID prediction.
AI.motivation: Graphs grow with unknown attachment patterns; existing filters are fixed or myopic; need to account for future impacts and long-term rewards in filtering decisions on evolving networks.
AI.method: Represent filter shifts as agents in a multi-agent reinforcement learning setup; optimize long-term rewards; parameterize the policy with a context-aware graph neural network using both graph structure and agent information.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and real datasets show that the sequential decision-making framework yields better performance than batch or online filtering approaches.
AI.conclusion: Sequential decision-making for filtering on expanding graphs is beneficial and effectively captures expansion dynamics and long-term effects; the approach generalizes across domains like recommendations and epidemiology.

title: ParallelVLM: Lossless Video-LLM Acceleration with Visual Alignment Aware Parallel Speculative Decoding
summary: Although current Video-LLMs achieve impressive performance in video understanding tasks, their autoregressive decoding efficiency remains constrained by the massive number of video tokens. Visual token pruning can partially ease this bottleneck, yet existing approaches still suffer from information loss and yield only modest acceleration in decoding. In this paper, we propose ParallelVLM, a training-free draft-then-verify speculative decoding framework that overcomes both mutual waiting and limited speedup-ratio problems between draft and target models in long-video settings. ParallelVLM features two parallelized stages that maximize hardware utilization and incorporate an Unbiased Verifier-Guided Pruning strategy to better align the draft and target models by eliminating the positional bias in attention-guided pruning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ParallelVLM effectively expands the draft window by $1.6\sim1.8\times$ with high accepted lengths, and accelerates various video understanding benchmarks by 3.36$\times$ on LLaVA-Onevision-72B and 2.42$\times$ on Qwen2.5-VL-32B compared with vanilla autoregressive decoding.

AI.tldr: ParallelVLM introduces a training-free draft-then-verify speculative decoding framework for long-video autoregressive decoding. It uses two parallel stages and an Unbiased Verifier-Guided Pruning strategy to expand the draft window and achieve substantial speedups (e.g., 3.36x on LLaVA-Onevision-72B, 2.42x on Qwen2.5-VL-32B) compared with vanilla decoding.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video LLMs are bottlenecked by the large number of video tokens; although visual token pruning helps, it incurs information loss and modest speedups. A training-free method to better align draft and target models for long videos is needed.
AI.method: ParallelVLM uses two parallelized stages to maximize hardware utilization and employs an Unbiased Verifier-Guided Pruning strategy to mitigate positional bias in attention-guided pruning, enabling a draft–verify speculative decoding process without training.
AI.result: The framework expands the draft window by about 1.6–1.8× with high accepted lengths and yields substantial speedups on benchmarks: 3.36× on LLaVA-Onevision-72B and 2.42× on Qwen2.5-VL-32B, relative to vanilla autoregressive decoding.
AI.conclusion: ParallelVLM demonstrates that training-free, draft–verify speculative decoding with unbiased pruning can effectively mitigate mutual waiting and speedup bottlenecks in long-video decoding, achieving notable efficiency gains across multiple video understanding benchmarks.

title: Scalable Cross-Facility Federated Learning for Scientific Foundation Models on Multiple Supercomputers
summary: Artificial Intelligence for scientific applications increasingly requires training large models on data that cannot be centralized due to privacy constraints, data sovereignty, or the sheer volume of data generated. Federated learning (FL) addresses this by enabling collaborative training without centralizing raw data, but scientific applications demand model scales that requires extensive computing resources, typically offered at High Performance Computing (HPC) facilities. Deploying FL experiments across HPC facilities introduces challenges beyond cloud or enterprise settings. We present a comprehensive cross-facility FL framework for heterogeneous HPC environments, built on Advanced Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning (APPFL) framework with Globus Compute and Transfer orchestration, and evaluate it across four U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) leadership-class supercomputers. We demonstrate that FL experiments across HPC facilities are practically achievable, characterize key sources of heterogeneity impacting the training performance, and show that algorithmic choices matter significantly under realistic HPC scheduling conditions. We validate the scientific applicability by fine-tuning a large language model on a chemistry instruction dataset, and identify scheduler-aware algorithm design as a critical open challenge for future deployments.

AI.tldr: Cross-facility federated learning framework for heterogeneous HPC environments built on Advanced Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning (APPFL) with Globus Compute and Transfer orchestration; evaluated on four DOE leadership-class supercomputers; demonstrates practical feasibility, identifies key heterogeneity sources affecting training, and shows scheduler-aware algorithm design as a critical factor; validated by fine-tuning a large language model on a chemistry instruction dataset.
AI.motivation: Scientific ML increasingly requires training large models on distributed data under privacy or data governance constraints; HPC resources enable scaling, but cross-facility FL faces scheduling, heterogeneity, and orchestration challenges.
AI.method: Integrates APPFL with Globus Compute and Transfer orchestration to enable cross-facility FL across heterogeneous HPC facilities; evaluates performance under realistic HPC scheduling conditions on four DOE leadership-class supercomputers; analyzes heterogeneity sources and the impact of algorithmic choices; demonstrates applicability by fine-tuning a chemistry-domain LLM.
AI.result: Demonstrates that cross-HPC FL experiments are practically achievable; characterizes key heterogeneity sources that impact training performance; shows that algorithmic choices significantly affect training performance under realistic HPC scheduling; validates the approach by fine-tuning a large language model on a chemistry instruction dataset.
AI.conclusion: Scheduler-aware algorithm design is a critical open challenge for future cross-facility FL deployments; cross-facility FL on HPCs is feasible and valuable for scientific research, with ongoing work needed to optimize performance under diverse scheduling and privacy constraints.

title: OrbitNVS: Harnessing Video Diffusion Priors for Novel View Synthesis
summary: Novel View Synthesis (NVS) aims to generate unseen views of a 3D object given a limited number of known views. Existing methods often struggle to synthesize plausible views for unobserved regions, particularly under single-view input, and still face challenges in maintaining geometry- and appearance-consistency. To address these issues, we propose OrbitNVS, which reformulates NVS as an orbit video generation task. Through tailored model design and training strategies, we adapt a pre-trained video generation model to the NVS task, leveraging its rich visual priors to achieve high-quality view synthesis. Specifically, we incorporate camera adapters into the video model to enable accurate camera control. To enhance two key properties of 3D objects, geometry and appearance, we design a normal map generation branch and use normal map features to guide the synthesis of the target views via attention mechanism, thereby improving geometric consistency. Moreover, we apply a pixel-space supervision to alleviate blurry appearance caused by spatial compression in the latent space. Extensive experiments show that OrbitNVS significantly outperforms previous methods on the GSO and OmniObject3D benchmarks, especially in the challenging single-view setting (\eg, +2.9 dB and +2.4 dB PSNR).

title: Subspace Kernel Learning on Tensor Sequences
comment: Accepted at the Fourteenth International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Learning from structured multi-way data, represented as higher-order tensors, requires capturing complex interactions across tensor modes while remaining computationally efficient. We introduce Uncertainty-driven Kernel Tensor Learning (UKTL), a novel kernel framework for $M$-mode tensors that compares mode-wise subspaces derived from tensor unfoldings, enabling expressive and robust similarity measure. To handle large-scale tensor data, we propose a scalable Nyström kernel linearization with dynamically learned pivot tensors obtained via soft $k$-means clustering. A key innovation of UKTL is its uncertainty-aware subspace weighting, which adaptively down-weights unreliable mode components based on estimated confidence, improving robustness and interpretability in comparisons between input and pivot tensors. Our framework is fully end-to-end trainable and naturally incorporates both multi-way and multi-mode interactions through structured kernel compositions. Extensive evaluations on action recognition benchmarks (NTU-60, NTU-120, Kinetics-Skeleton) show that UKTL achieves state-of-the-art performance, superior generalization, and meaningful mode-wise insights. This work establishes a principled, scalable, and interpretable kernel learning paradigm for structured multi-way and multi-modal tensor sequences.

AI.tldr: UKTL introduces uncertainty-driven kernel learning for M-mode tensors by comparing mode-wise subspaces from unfoldings, with scalable Nyström linearization using pivot tensors learned via soft k-means. It includes uncertainty-aware subspace weighting to down-weight unreliable modes, is end-to-end trainable, and reports state-of-the-art results on action recognition benchmarks with interpretable mode-wise insights.
AI.motivation: Address the need to capture complex inter-mode interactions in high-order tensors while maintaining scalability for large-scale structured data; provide robust, interpretable similarity measures for multi-way and multi-modal tensor sequences.
AI.method: A kernel framework that computes mode-wise subspace similarities from tensor unfoldings, combines them via structured kernel compositions, uses Nyström-based kernel linearization with dynamically learned pivot tensors via soft k-means, and incorporates uncertainty-aware weighting to down-weight unreliable mode components; end-to-end trainable.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on action recognition benchmarks NTU-60, NTU-120, Kinetics-Skeleton reports state-of-the-art performance, improved generalization, and meaningful mode-wise insights.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a principled, scalable, and interpretable kernel learning paradigm for structured multi-way and multi-modal tensor sequences, enabling effective comparisons and robust representations across tensor modes.

title: UniPR: Unified Object-level Real-to-Sim Perception and Reconstruction from a Single Stereo Pair
summary: Perceiving and reconstructing objects from images are critical for real-to-sim transfer tasks, which are widely used in the robotics community. Existing methods rely on multiple submodules such as detection, segmentation, shape reconstruction, and pose estimation to complete the pipeline. However, such modular pipelines suffer from inefficiency and cumulative error, as each stage operates on only partial or locally refined information while discarding global context. To address these limitations, we propose UniPR, the first end-to-end object-level real-to-sim perception and reconstruction framework. Operating directly on a single stereo image pair, UniPR leverages geometric constraints to resolve the scale ambiguity. We introduce Pose-Aware Shape Representation to eliminate the need for per-category canonical definitions and to bridge the gap between reconstruction and pose estimation tasks. Furthermore, we construct a large-vocabulary stereo dataset, LVS6D, comprising over 6,300 objects, to facilitate large-scale research in this area. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniPR reconstructs all objects in a scene in parallel within a single forward pass, achieving significant efficiency gains and preserves true physical proportions across diverse object types, highlighting its potential for practical robotic applications.

AI.tldr: UniPR is an end-to-end, stereo-based real-to-sim perception framework that reconstructs all scene objects in a single forward pass using Pose-Aware Shape Representation and geometric constraints, and introduces LVS6D for large-vocabulary stereo perception; it improves efficiency and preserves real-world proportions.
AI.motivation: Current modular pipelines for perception and reconstruction incur inefficiency and error accumulation due to stage-wise processing, partial information, and loss of global context. An end-to-end, object-level approach could leverage global information and resolve scale ambiguity.
AI.method: End-to-end architecture operating on a single stereo image pair to reconstruct multiple objects in parallel. It uses geometric constraints to resolve scale ambiguity. Introduces Pose-Aware Shape Representation to bridge reconstruction and pose estimation without relying on per-category canonical definitions. Also presents LVS6D, a large-vocabulary stereo dataset with over 6,300 objects for large-scale research.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant efficiency gains, with all objects in a scene reconstructed in parallel during a single forward pass. The method preserves true physical proportions across diverse object types, indicating robust real-to-sim transfer potential for practical robotics.
AI.conclusion: UniPR demonstrates that end-to-end, object-level real-to-sim perception can be both efficient and accurate, mitigating the drawbacks of modular pipelines. The LVS6D dataset supports broad exploration in this area and paves the way for practical robotic applications.

title: Neural Uncertainty Principle: A Unified View of Adversarial Fragility and LLM Hallucination
summary: Adversarial vulnerability in vision and hallucination in large language models are conventionally viewed as separate problems, each addressed with modality-specific patches. This study first reveals that they share a common geometric origin: the input and its loss gradient are conjugate observables subject to an irreducible uncertainty bound. Formalizing a Neural Uncertainty Principle (NUP) under a loss-induced state, we find that in near-bound regimes, further compression must be accompanied by increased sensitivity dispersion (adversarial fragility), while weak prompt-gradient coupling leaves generation under-constrained (hallucination). Crucially, this bound is modulated by an input-gradient correlation channel, captured by a specifically designed single-backward probe. In vision, masking highly coupled components improves robustness without costly adversarial training; in language, the same prefill-stage probe detects hallucination risk before generating any answer tokens. NUP thus turns two seemingly separate failure taxonomies into a shared uncertainty-budget view and provides a principled lens for reliability analysis. Guided by this NUP theory, we propose ConjMask (masking high-contribution input components) and LogitReg (logit-side regularization) to improve robustness without adversarial training, and use the probe as a decoding-free risk signal for LLMs, enabling hallucination detection and prompt selection. NUP thus provides a unified, practical framework for diagnosing and mitigating boundary anomalies across perception and generation tasks.

AI.tldr: Neural Uncertainty Principle (NUP) links adversarial vulnerability and LLM hallucination via conjugate input-loss observables, enabling unified reliability analysis with ConjMask, LogitReg, and a single-backward probe.
AI.motivation: Reveal a common geometric origin for robustness and generation failures to enable unified, non-training-based mitigation across vision and language tasks.
AI.method: Formulate NUP under a loss-induced state; analyze near-bound regimes where compression increases sensitivity dispersion; quantify input-gradient correlation with a single-backward probe; propose ConjMask (mask high-contribution inputs) and LogitReg (logit-side regularization); use the probe as a decoding-free risk signal for LLMs.
AI.result: Demonstrates in vision that masking highly coupled input components improves robustness without adversarial training; in language, the probe detects hallucination risk before token generation; offers a unified uncertainty-budget framework for reliability analysis.
AI.conclusion: NUP provides a unified, practical framework for diagnosing and mitigating boundary anomalies across perception and generation tasks; ConjMask and LogitReg enable robustness without adversarial training, and the probe supports hallucination detection and prompt selection in LLMs.

title: Disentangle-then-Align: Non-Iterative Hybrid Multimodal Image Registration via Cross-Scale Feature Disentanglement
summary: Multimodal image registration is a fundamental task and a prerequisite for downstream cross-modal analysis. Despite recent progress in shared feature extraction and multi-scale architectures, two key limitations remain. First, some methods use disentanglement to learn shared features but mainly regularize the shared part, allowing modality-private cues to leak into the shared space. Second, most multi-scale frameworks support only a single transformation type, limiting their applicability when global misalignment and local deformation coexist. To address these issues, we formulate hybrid multimodal registration as jointly learning a stable shared feature space and a unified hybrid transformation. Based on this view, we propose HRNet, a Hybrid Registration Network that couples representation disentanglement with hybrid parameter prediction. A shared backbone with Modality-Specific Batch Normalization (MSBN) extracts multi-scale features, while a Cross-scale Disentanglement and Adaptive Projection (CDAP) module suppresses modality-private cues and projects shared features into a stable subspace for matching. Built on this shared space, a Hybrid Parameter Prediction Module (HPPM) performs non-iterative coarse-to-fine estimation of global rigid parameters and deformation fields, which are fused into a coherent deformation field. Extensive experiments on four multimodal datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on rigid and non-rigid registration tasks. The code is available at the project website.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Registration Network (HRNet) for multimodal image registration that jointly learns a stable shared feature space and a unified hybrid transformation, addressing both cross-modal feature leakage and the coexistence of global misalignment and local deformation.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal registration methods struggle with leakage of modality-private cues into the shared representation due to incomplete disentanglement and are usually limited to a single transformation type in multi-scale frameworks, hindering simultaneous handling of global rigid misalignment and local non-rigid deformation.
AI.method: HRNet uses a shared backbone with Modality-Specific Batch Normalization (MSBN) to extract multi-scale features, a Cross-scale Disentanglement and Adaptive Projection (CDAP) module to suppress modality-private cues and project features into a stable shared subspace, and a Hybrid Parameter Prediction Module (HPPM) to non-iteratively estimate global rigid parameters and deformation fields in a coarse-to-fine manner, fusing them into a coherent deformation field.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four multimodal datasets show state-of-the-art performance on both rigid and non-rigid registration tasks; code is available at the project website.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a unified framework that addresses both representation disentanglement and transformation flexibility in multimodal registration, achieving state-of-the-art results across rigid and non-rigid tasks and enabling practical applicability through a non-iterative, hybrid transformation approach.

title: Wearable Foundation Models Should Go Beyond Static Encoders
summary: Wearable foundation models (WFMs), trained on large volumes of data collected by affordable, always-on devices, have demonstrated strong performance on short-term, well-defined health monitoring tasks, including activity recognition, fitness tracking, and cardiovascular signal assessment. However, most existing WFMs primarily map short temporal windows to predefined labels via static encoders, emphasizing retrospective prediction rather than reasoning over evolving personal history, context, and future risk trajectories. As a result, they are poorly suited for modeling chronic, progressive, or episodic health conditions that unfold over weeks, months or years. Hence, we argue that WFMs must move beyond static encoders and be explicitly designed for longitudinal, anticipatory health reasoning. We identify three foundational shifts required to enable this transition: (1) Structurally rich data, which goes beyond isolated datasets or outcome-conditioned collection to integrated multimodal, long-term personal trajectories, and contextual metadata, ideally supported by open and interoperable data ecosystems; (2) Longitudinal-aware multimodal modeling, which prioritizes long-context inference, temporal abstraction, and personalization over cross-sectional or population-level prediction; and (3) Agentic inference systems, which move beyond static prediction to support planning, decision-making, and clinically grounded intervention under uncertainty. Together, these shifts reframe wearable health monitoring from retrospective signal interpretation toward continuous, anticipatory, and human-aligned health support.

AI.tldr: Proposes three foundational shifts to evolve wearable foundation models toward longitudinal, anticipatory health reasoning.
AI.motivation: WFMs currently excel in short-term, static predictions but fail to capture evolving personal histories, context, and long-term health trajectories; a need for longitudinal data ecosystems, long-context modeling, and planning under uncertainty.
AI.method: Three foundational shifts: (1) Structurally rich data with multimodal, long-term personal trajectories and interoperable ecosystems; (2) Longitudinal-aware multimodal modeling emphasizing long-context inference, temporal abstraction, and personalization; (3) Agentic inference systems enabling planning, decision-making, and clinically grounded interventions under uncertainty.
AI.result: Provides a conceptual framework and roadmap for moving WFMs from retrospective signal interpretation to continuous, anticipatory, and human-aligned health support.
AI.conclusion: Adopting these shifts reframes wearable health monitoring from retrospective signal interpretation toward proactive, longitudinal health support for chronic and episodic conditions.

title: IUP-Pose: Decoupled Iterative Uncertainty Propagation for Real-time Relative Pose Regression via Implicit Dense Alignment v1
summary: Relative pose estimation is fundamental for SLAM, visual localization, and 3D reconstruction. Existing Relative Pose Regression (RPR) methods face a key trade-off: feature-matching pipelines achieve high accuracy but block gradient flow via non-differentiable RANSAC, while ViT-based regressors are end-to-end trainable but prohibitively expensive for real-time deployment. We identify the core bottlenecks as the coupling between rotation and translation estimation and insufficient cross-view feature alignment. We propose IUP-Pose, a geometry-driven decoupled iterative framework with implicit dense alignment. A lightweight Multi-Head Bi-Cross Attention (MHBC) module aligns cross-view features without explicit matching supervision. The aligned features are processed by a decoupled rotation-translation pipeline: two shared-parameter rotation stages iteratively refine rotation with uncertainty, and feature maps are realigned via rotational homography H_inf before translation prediction. IUP-Pose achieves 73.3% AUC@20deg on MegaDepth1500 with full end-to-end differentiability, 70 FPS throughput, and only 37M parameters, demonstrating a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for real-time edge deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes IUP-Pose, a geometry-driven decoupled iterative RPR framework with implicit dense alignment; achieves end-to-end differentiable, real-time performance with strong accuracy.
AI.motivation: Addresses core bottlenecks in relative pose regression (RPR): (i) coupling between rotation and translation estimation, and (ii) insufficient cross-view feature alignment, which limit accuracy and end-to-end trainability. Existing pipelines rely on non-differentiable RANSAC (high accuracy but blocked gradients) or ViT-based regressors (end-to-end but too slow).
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight Multi-Head Bi-Cross Attention (MHBC) module to align cross-view features without explicit matching supervision. Use a decoupled rotation-translation pipeline with two shared-parameter rotation stages that iteratively refine rotation with quantified uncertainty. Realign feature maps via rotational homography H_inf before predicting translation. End-to-end differentiable.
AI.result: 74? Actually the abstract states 73.3% AUC@20deg on MegaDepth1500; 70 FPS throughput; 37M parameters.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off for real-time edge deployment, offering real-time performance with competitive pose accuracy and a small model size.

title: Dual Prompt-Driven Feature Encoding for Nighttime UAV Tracking
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation 2026
summary: Robust feature encoding constitutes the foundation of UAV tracking by enabling the nuanced perception of target appearance and motion, thereby playing a pivotal role in ensuring reliable tracking. However, existing feature encoding methods often overlook critical illumination and viewpoint cues, which are essential for robust perception under challenging nighttime conditions, leading to degraded tracking performance. To overcome the above limitation, this work proposes a dual prompt-driven feature encoding method that integrates prompt-conditioned feature adaptation and context-aware prompt evolution to promote domain-invariant feature encoding. Specifically, the pyramid illumination prompter is proposed to extract multi-scale frequency-aware illumination prompts. %The dynamic viewpoint prompter adapts the sampling to different viewpoints, enabling the tracker to learn view-invariant features. The dynamic viewpoint prompter modulates deformable convolution offsets to accommodate viewpoint variations, enabling the tracker to learn view-invariant features. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of the proposed dual prompt-driven tracker (DPTracker) in tackling nighttime UAV tracking. Ablation studies highlight the contribution of each component in DPTracker. Real-world tests under diverse nighttime UAV tracking scenarios further demonstrate the robustness and practical utility. The code and demo videos are available at https://github.com/yiheng-wang-duke/DPTracker.

AI.tldr: A dual prompt-driven feature encoding framework (DPTracker) for robust nighttime UAV tracking, combining a pyramid illumination prompter for multi-scale illumination cues with a dynamic viewpoint prompter to handle viewpoint changes, enabling domain-invariant features; validated by ablations and real-world nighttime tests, code released.
AI.motivation: Nighttime UAV tracking suffers from severe illumination and viewpoint variation, causing degraded perception and tracking performance. Existing feature encoding methods often overlook these cues, hindering robustness under challenging nocturnal conditions.
AI.method: DPTracker introduces dual prompt-driven feature encoding: (1) prompt-conditioned feature adaptation and (2) context-aware prompt evolution. A pyramid illumination prompter extracts multi-scale, frequency-aware illumination prompts to align features across lighting conditions. A dynamic viewpoint prompter (described as adapting sampling and deformable convolution offsets) targets viewpoint variations to learn view-invariant features, with the overall framework enabling prompt-guided adaptation to domain shifts.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and ablation studies validate the effectiveness of DPTracker in nighttime UAV tracking; the proposed components contribute additively to performance, and real-world nighttime tests demonstrate robustness. The authors also provide code and demo videos for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dual prompt-driven framework advances robust, domain-invariant feature encoding for nighttime UAV tracking by leveraging illumination- and viewpoint-aware prompts. It shows practical applicability and sets a foundation for prompt-based adaptation in challenging imaging conditions.

title: Demonstrations, CoT, and Prompting: A Theoretical Analysis of ICL
summary: In-Context Learning (ICL) enables pretrained LLMs to adapt to downstream tasks by conditioning on a small set of input-output demonstrations, without any parameter updates. Although there have been many theoretical efforts to explain how ICL works, most either rely on strong architectural or data assumptions, or fail to capture the impact of key practical factors such as demonstration selection, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, the number of demonstrations, and prompt templates. We address this gap by establishing a theoretical analysis of ICL under mild assumptions that links these design choices to generalization behavior. We derive an upper bound on the ICL test loss, showing that performance is governed by (i) the quality of selected demonstrations, quantified by Lipschitz constants of the ICL loss along paths connecting test prompts to pretraining samples, (ii) an intrinsic ICL capability of the pretrained model, and (iii) the degree of distribution shift. Within the same framework, we analyze CoT prompting as inducing a task decomposition and show that it is beneficial when demonstrations are well chosen at each substep and the resulting subtasks are easier to learn. Finally, we characterize how ICL performance sensitivity to prompt templates varies with the number of demonstrations. Together, our study shows that pretraining equips the model with the ability to generalize beyond observed tasks, while CoT enables the model to compose simpler subtasks into more complex ones, and demonstrations and instructions enable it to retrieve similar or complex tasks, including those that can be composed into more complex ones, jointly supporting generalization to unseen tasks. All theoretical insights are corroborated by experiments.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for In-Context Learning (ICL) in pretrained LLMs, linking how demonstration quality, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, the number of demonstrations, and prompt templates affect generalization. It yields an upper bound on ICL test loss dependent on demonstration Lipschitz constants, intrinsic ICL capability, and distribution shift. CoT is beneficial when demonstrations decompose tasks into easier subtasks; template sensitivity varies with the number of demonstrations. Experiments corroborate the theory.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in understanding how practical ICL factors—demonstration selection, CoT prompting, number of demos, and prompt templates—influence generalization, beyond existing strong architectural/data assumptions.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework to bound ICL test loss. Model the effect of prompts via Lipschitz paths from test prompts to pretraining samples; interpret CoT as task decomposition; analyze dependence of template sensitivity on the number of demonstrations; validate with experiments.
AI.result: Derived an upper bound on ICL test loss governed by (i) quality of selected demonstrations (Lipschitz constants along prompt-to-pretraining paths), (ii) intrinsic ICL capability of the pretrained model, and (iii) distribution shift. CoT is beneficial when substeps are well chosen and easier to learn; template sensitivity to prompts varies with the number of demonstrations. Empirical results validate the theoretical insights.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining equips models to generalize beyond observed tasks; CoT enables composing simpler subtasks into complex ones; demonstrations and instructions enable retrieval of similar or complex tasks, enabling generalization to unseen tasks. The theory and experiments jointly support broad generalization to unseen tasks in ICL.

title: UniBioTransfer: A Unified Framework for Multiple Biometrics Transfer
summary: Deepface generation has traditionally followed a task-driven paradigm, where distinct tasks (e.g., face transfer and hair transfer) are addressed by task-specific models. Nevertheless, this single-task setting severely limits model generalization and scalability. A unified model capable of solving multiple deepface generation tasks in a single pass represents a promising and practical direction, yet remains challenging due to data scarcity and cross-task conflicts arising from heterogeneous attribute transformations. To this end, we propose UniBioTransfer, the first unified framework capable of handling both conventional deepface tasks (e.g., face transfer and face reenactment) and shape-varying transformations (e.g., hair transfer and head transfer). Besides, UniBioTransfer naturally generalizes to unseen tasks, like lip, eye, and glasses transfer, with minimal fine-tuning. Generally, UniBioTransfer addresses data insufficiency in multi-task generation through a unified data construction strategy, including a swapping-based corruption mechanism designed for spatially dynamic attributes like hair. It further mitigates cross-task interference via an innovative BioMoE, a mixture-of-experts based model coupled with a novel two-stage training strategy that effectively disentangles task-specific knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness, generalization, and scalability of UniBioTransfer, outperforming both existing unified models and task-specific methods across a wide range of deepface generation tasks. Project page is at https://scy639.github.io/UniBioTransfer.github.io/

AI.tldr: UniBioTransfer unifies multiple deepface generation tasks (conventional and shape-changing) into a single BioMoE-based framework with unified data augmentation, enabling generalization to unseen tasks with minimal fine-tuning and surpassing task-specific and existing unified methods.
AI.motivation: Task-specific deepface models suffer from limited generalization and scalability; cross-task conflicts and data scarcity hinder multi-task learning.
AI.method: Introduce UniBioTransfer: (1) unified data construction with a swapping-based corruption for spatially dynamic attributes like hair; (2) BioMoE (mixture-of-experts) with a two-stage training strategy to disentangle task-specific knowledge; (3) supports both conventional tasks (face transfer, face reenactment) and shape-varying tasks (hair transfer, head transfer) and generalizes to unseen tasks (lip, eye, glasses) with minimal fine-tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show improved effectiveness, generalization, and scalability relative to both task-specific methods and existing unified models across a broad set of deepface generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: UniBioTransfer offers a practical, scalable unified framework for multi-task deepface generation, addressing data insufficiency and cross-task interference while enabling rapid generalization to new tasks.

title: On Performance Guarantees for Federated Learning with Personalized Constraints
summary: Federated learning (FL) has emerged as a communication-efficient algorithmic framework for distributed learning across multiple agents. While standard FL formulations capture unconstrained or globally constrained problems, many practical settings involve heterogeneous resource or model constraints, leading to optimization problems with agent-specific feasible sets. Here, we study a personalized constrained federated optimization problem in which each agent is associated with a convex local objective and a private constraint set. We propose PC-FedAvg, a method in which each agent maintains cross-estimates of the other agents' variables through a multi-block local decision vector. Each agent updates all blocks locally, penalizing infeasibility only in its own block. Moreover, the cross-estimate mechanism enables personalization without requiring consensus or sharing constraint information among agents. We establish communication-complexity rates of $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-2})$ for suboptimality and $\mathcal{O}(ε^{-1})$ for agent-wise infeasibility. Preliminary experiments on the MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets validate our theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: PC-FedAvg enables personalized Federated Learning with agent-specific convex constraints by using cross-estimates of other agents’ variables and local block-wise feasibility penalties, avoiding sharing constraint info and consensus. It achieves suboptimality and infeasibility rates of O(ε^{-2}) and O(ε^{-1}) respectively, with preliminary MNIST/CIFAR-10 experiments supporting theory.
AI.motivation: In practical FL, agents often have heterogeneous resource constraints and private feasibility sets. A method is needed to personalize optimization while respecting agent-specific constraints without requiring global consensus or disclosure of constraint information.
AI.method: Introduce PC-FedAvg, where each agent maintains a multi-block local decision vector and cross-estimates of other agents’ variables. Each agent updates all blocks locally and imposes feasibility penalties only on its own block. The cross-estimate mechanism preserves personalization and avoids sharing constraint details or achieving global consensus.
AI.result: Proven communication-complexity rates: O(ε^{-2}) for suboptimality and O(ε^{-1}) for agent-wise infeasibility. Empirical validation on MNIST and CIFAR-10 corroborates theoretical predictions.
AI.conclusion: PC-FedAvg provides a practical framework for personalized constrained federated optimization, achieving competitive convergence guarantees without requiring consensus or exposure of private constraint information, with experimental results aligning with theory.

title: OmniDiT: Extending Diffusion Transformer to Omni-VTON Framework
summary: Despite the rapid advancement of Virtual Try-On (VTON) and Try-Off (VTOFF) technologies, existing VTON methods face challenges with fine-grained detail preservation, generalization to complex scenes, complicated pipeline, and efficient inference. To tackle these problems, we propose OmniDiT, an omni Virtual Try-On framework based on the Diffusion Transformer, which combines try-on and try-off tasks into one unified model. Specifically, we first establish a self-evolving data curation pipeline to continuously produce data, and construct a large VTON dataset Omni-TryOn, which contains over 380k diverse and high-quality garment-model-tryon image pairs and detailed text prompts. Then, we employ the token concatenation and design an adaptive position encoding to effectively incorporate multiple reference conditions. To relieve the bottleneck of long sequence computation, we are the first to introduce Shifted Window Attention into the diffusion model, thus achieving a linear complexity. To remedy the performance degradation caused by local window attention, we utilize multiple timestep prediction and an alignment loss to improve generation fidelity. Experiments reveal that, under various complex scenes, our method achieves the best performance in both the model-free VTON and VTOFF tasks and a performance comparable to current SOTA methods in the model-based VTON task.

AI.tldr: OmniDiT introduces an omni VTON/VTOFF diffusion Transformer that unifies both tasks in a single model, supported by a self-evolving data curation pipeline yielding Omni-TryOn (~380k pairs) and novel architectural choices (token concatenation, adaptive positional encoding, Shifted Window Attention) to achieve linear-time diffusion and higher fidelity, with state-of-the-art results on model-free VTON/VTOFF and competitive model-based VTON.
AI.motivation: Address core limitations of current VTON methods: loss of fine-grained detail, poor generalization to complex scenes, complex pipelines, and slow inference. Propose a single unified model for VTON and VTOFF, backed by a large, high-quality dataset and computation-efficient diffusion transformers.
AI.method: 1) Self-evolving data curation pipeline to continuously generate data, 2) Build Omni-TryOn dataset with ~380k garment-model-tryon pairs and prompts, 3) Use token concatenation and adaptive position encoding to integrate multiple reference conditions, 4) Introduce Shifted Window Attention in diffusion model to achieve linear complexity, 5) Mitigate local-window attention bottlenecks via multiple timestep prediction and alignment loss to improve fidelity.
AI.result: Empirical results show OmniDiT achieves state-of-the-art performance for model-free VTON and VTOFF, and competitive performance versus SOTA for model-based VTON across diverse and complex scenes.
AI.conclusion: OmniDiT provides a unified, efficient, and high-fidelity VTON/VTOFF framework backed by a large-scale dataset and novel architectural components, enabling superior performance in free-form scenes with linear-time inference.

title: DeepStock: Reinforcement Learning with Policy Regularizations for Inventory Management
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) provides a general-purpose methodology for training inventory policies that can leverage big data and compute. However, off-the-shelf implementations of DRL have seen mixed success, often plagued by high sensitivity to the hyperparameters used during training. In this paper, we show that by imposing policy regularizations, grounded in classical inventory concepts such as "Base Stock", we can significantly accelerate hyperparameter tuning and improve the final performance of several DRL methods. We report details from a 100% deployment of DRL with policy regularizations on Alibaba's e-commerce platform, Tmall. We also include extensive synthetic experiments, which show that policy regularizations reshape the narrative on what is the best DRL method for inventory management.

AI.tldr: Policy regularizations grounded in Base Stock concepts accelerate DRL-based inventory policies and enable full deployment on Alibaba's Tmall, while synthetic experiments challenge prevailing views on which DRL methods are best for inventory management.
AI.motivation: DRL inventory control is highly sensitive to training hyperparameters. Incorporating classical inventory theory via regularizations aims to stabilize learning, improve sample efficiency, and boost real-world performance. A real-world deployment on a major platform (Alibaba/Tmall) provides strong validation.
AI.method: Impose policy regularization terms tied to Base Stock principles within DRL objectives, and evaluate across multiple DRL algorithms. Conduct extensive synthetic experiments to assess generality, and report a 100% deployment outcome on Alibaba's e-commerce platform.
AI.result: Regularizations substantially speed up hyperparameter tuning and improve final performance across DRL methods. The work reports successful full deployment on Alibaba's Tmall. Synthetic experiments indicate that these regularizations can alter the perceived strength of different DRL methods for inventory tasks.
AI.conclusion: Policy regularizations that embed classical inventory concepts into DRL can enhance performance and deployment feasibility in inventory management, and may redefine which DRL methods are considered most effective.

title: GravCal: Single-Image Calibration of IMU Gravity Priors with Per-Sample Confidence
summary:
Gravity estimation is fundamental to visual-inertial perception, augmented reality, and robotics, yet gravity priors from IMUs are often unreliable under linear acceleration, vibration, and transient motion. Existing methods often estimate gravity directly from images or assume reasonably accurate inertial input, leaving the practical problem of correcting a noisy gravity prior from a single image largely unaddressed.
  We present GravCal, a feedforward model for single-image gravity prior calibration. Given one RGB image and a noisy gravity prior, GravCal predicts a corrected gravity direction and a per-sample confidence score. The model combines two complementary predictions, including a residual correction of the input prior and a prior-independent image estimate, and uses a learned gate to fuse them adaptively.
  Extensive experiments show strong gains over raw inertial priors: GravCal reduces mean angular error from 22.02° (IMU prior) to 14.24°, with larger improvements when the prior is severely corrupted. We also introduce a novel dataset of over 148K frames with paired VIO-derived ground-truth gravity and Mahony-filter IMU priors across diverse scenes and arbitrary camera orientations. The learned gate also correlates with prior quality, making it a useful confidence signal for downstream systems.

AI.tldr: GravCal is a feedforward single-image gravity calibration method that fuses a residual correction of a noisy IMU gravity prior with a prior-free image estimate via a learned gate, improving gravity direction accuracy and providing per-sample confidence.
AI.motivation: IMU gravity priors are unreliable under linear acceleration, vibration, and transient motion; there is a need to correct noisy priors from a single image without relying solely on image-only gravity estimation.
AI.method: Two complementary predictions are produced: (i) a residual correction of the input gravity prior and (ii) a prior-independent image-based gravity estimate. A learned gate adaptively fuses these predictions. The model takes one RGB image and a noisy gravity prior and outputs a corrected gravity direction and a per-sample confidence score. A new dataset with paired VIO-derived gravity and Mahony priors is introduced.
AI.result: GravCal reduces mean angular error from 22.02° (with the raw IMU prior) to 14.24°, with larger gains when the prior is severely corrupted. The learned gate's output correlates with prior quality, providing a useful confidence signal for downstream systems.
AI.conclusion: GravCal offers robust, real-time-capable calibration of gravity priors from a single image by combining prior correction and image-based estimation with adaptive fusion, and provides a confidence measure. The accompanying dataset enables broader evaluation.

title: Continual Learning for Food Category Classification Dataset: Enhancing Model Adaptability and Performance
summary: Conventional machine learning pipelines often struggle to recognize categories absent from the original trainingset. This gap typically reduces accuracy, as fixed datasets rarely capture the full diversity of a domain. To address this, we propose a continual learning framework for text-guided food classification. Unlike approaches that require retraining from scratch, our method enables incremental updates, allowing new categories to be integrated without degrading prior knowledge. For example, a model trained on Western cuisines could later learn to classify dishes such as dosa or kimchi. Although further refinements are needed, this design shows promise for adaptive food recognition, with applications in dietary monitoring and personalized nutrition planning.

AI.tldr: Continual learning framework for text-guided food classification enabling incremental addition of new categories without retraining from scratch.
AI.motivation: Fixed training sets rarely capture full domain diversity; unseen categories reduce accuracy; need lifelong learning in food recognition.
AI.method: Proposes a text-guided continual learning approach that supports adding new classes incrementally while preserving prior knowledge, avoiding catastrophic forgetting; example includes adding dosa or kimchi to Western cuisine trained model.
AI.result: Preliminary results indicate potential; robustness and scalability require further refinement; not yet demonstrated in-depth performance.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates promise of adaptive, text-guided food classification for dietary monitoring and personalized nutrition; further work needed to realize practical deployment.

title: Alternating Diffusion for Proximal Sampling with Zeroth Order Queries
summary: This work introduces a new approximate proximal sampler that operates solely with zeroth-order information of the potential function. Prior theoretical analyses have revealed that proximal sampling corresponds to alternating forward and backward iterations of the heat flow. The backward step was originally implemented by rejection sampling, whereas we directly simulate the dynamics. Unlike diffusion-based sampling methods that estimate scores via learned models or by invoking auxiliary samplers, our method treats the intermediate particle distribution as a Gaussian mixture, thereby yielding a Monte Carlo score estimator from directly samplable distributions. Theoretically, when the score estimation error is sufficiently controlled, our method inherits the exponential convergence of proximal sampling under isoperimetric conditions on the target distribution. In practice, the algorithm avoids rejection sampling, permits flexible step sizes, and runs with a deterministic runtime budget. Numerical experiments demonstrate that our approach converges rapidly to the target distribution, driven by interactions among multiple particles and by exploiting parallel computation.

AI.tldr: Introduces a zeroth-order proximal sampler using Gaussian-mixture intermediates to simulate proximal steps; achieves fast, deterministic convergence without rejection sampling, leveraging multi-particle interactions and parallelism.
AI.motivation: To enable efficient sampling from complex distributions using only zeroth-order (function-value) information, avoiding score estimation and rejection-based steps in diffusion/proximal schemes.
AI.method: Develops an approximate proximal sampler that directly simulates forward/backward heat-flow steps with isoperimetric target distributions. Models intermediate distribution as a Gaussian mixture and builds a Monte Carlo score estimator from accessible samples; eliminates rejection sampling; supports flexible step sizes and fixed runtime; enables parallel, multi-particle interactions.
AI.result: Under controlled score estimation error, achieves exponential convergence similar to proximal sampling under isoperimetric conditions; empirically demonstrates rapid convergence driven by particle interactions and parallel computation; deterministic runtime budget.
AI.conclusion: The proposed zeroth-order proximal sampler provides practical, efficient sampling with strong theoretical guarantees and favorable empirical performance compared to diffusion-based or rejection-based methods.

title: Semantic Audio-Visual Navigation in Continuous Environments
comment: This paper has been accepted to CVPR 2026
summary: Audio-visual navigation enables embodied agents to navigate toward sound-emitting targets by leveraging both auditory and visual cues. However, most existing approaches rely on precomputed room impulse responses (RIRs) for binaural audio rendering, restricting agents to discrete grid positions and leading to spatially discontinuous observations. To establish a more realistic setting, we introduce Semantic Audio-Visual Navigation in Continuous Environments (SAVN-CE), where agents can move freely in 3D spaces and perceive temporally and spatially coherent audio-visual streams. In this setting, targets may intermittently become silent or stop emitting sound entirely, causing agents to lose goal information. To tackle this challenge, we propose MAGNet, a multimodal transformer-based model that jointly encodes spatial and semantic goal representations and integrates historical context with self-motion cues to enable memory-augmented goal reasoning. Comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MAGNet significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to a 12.1\% absolute improvement in success rate. These results also highlight its robustness to short-duration sounds and long-distance navigation scenarios. The code is available at https://github.com/yichenzeng24/SAVN-CE.

AI.tldr: MAGNet enables memory-augmented, multimodal navigation in continuous 3D environments (SAVN-CE) by fusing spatial/semantic goals with historical self-motion context, outperforming state-of-the-art in audio-visual navigation with up to 12.1% absolute gain.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of discrete-grid, RIR-based binaural rendering that yield spatially discontinuous observations and unrealistic constraints; a continuous, temporally-coherent AV navigation setting is needed where targets may intermittently go silent.
AI.method: A multimodal transformer model (MAGNet) that jointly encodes spatial and semantic goal representations and integrates historical context with self-motion cues to perform memory-augmented goal reasoning within the SAVN-CE framework.
AI.result: MAGNet significantly surpasses state-of-the-art methods, achieving up to 12.1% absolute improvement in success rate and showing robustness to short-duration sounds and long-distance navigation; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: SAVN-CE provides a realistic benchmark for audio-visual navigation, and MAGNet's memory-augmented reasoning improves robustness and performance in continuous, dynamic auditory scenes.

title: RiboSphere: Learning Unified and Efficient Representations of RNA Structures
summary: Accurate RNA structure modeling remains difficult because RNA backbones are highly flexible, non-canonical interactions are prevalent, and experimentally determined 3D structures are comparatively scarce. We introduce \emph{RiboSphere}, a framework that learns \emph{discrete} geometric representations of RNA by combining vector quantization with flow matching. Our design is motivated by the modular organization of RNA architecture: complex folds are composed from recurring structural motifs. RiboSphere uses a geometric transformer encoder to produce SE(3)-invariant (rotation/translation-invariant) features, which are discretized with finite scalar quantization (FSQ) into a finite vocabulary of latent codes.
