Conditioned on these discrete codes, a flow-matching decoder reconstructs atomic coordinates, enabling high-fidelity structure generation. We find that the learned code indices are enriched for specific RNA motifs, suggesting that the model captures motif-level compositional structure rather than acting as a purely compressive bottleneck. Across benchmarks, RiboSphere achieves strong performance in structure reconstruction (RMSD 1.25\,Å, TM-score 0.84), and its pretrained discrete representations transfer effectively to inverse folding and RNA--ligand binding prediction, with robust generalization in data-scarce regimes.

AI.tldr: RiboSphere learns discrete geometric representations of RNA by combining vector quantization with flow matching, using a SE(3)-invariant geometric transformer to produce discrete latent codes that condition a flow-based decoder for high-fidelity RNA structure generation. The learned codes are motif-enriched and transfer to inverse folding and RNA–ligand prediction, with strong generalization in data-scarce settings.
AI.motivation: RNA structure modeling remains challenging due to backbone flexibility, prevalence of non-canonical interactions, and limited experimentally determined 3D structures. The work aims to capture motif-level compositional structure via discrete latent representations to improve reconstruction and enable transfer to downstream tasks.
AI.method: A geometric transformer encoder yields SE(3)-invariant features, which are discretized through finite scalar quantization into a finite vocabulary of latent codes. A flow-matching decoder reconstructs atomic coordinates conditioned on these codes. Training emphasizes reconstruction fidelity and analyzes the relation between code indices and RNA motifs.
AI.result: Strong reconstruction performance (RMSD ~1.25 Å, TM-score ~0.84). Discrete codes are enriched for specific RNA motifs, indicating motif-level capture rather than mere compression. Pretrained representations transfer to inverse folding and RNA–ligand binding prediction, with robust generalization in low-data regimes.
AI.conclusion: Discrete, motif-aware latent representations enable effective RNA structure generation and beneficial transfer to related tasks, supporting the idea that modular motif composition underpins RNA folding and can be captured via discrete geometric codes.

title: Toward High-Fidelity Visual Reconstruction: From EEG-Based Conditioned Generation to Joint-Modal Guided Rebuilding
summary: Human visual reconstruction aims to reconstruct fine-grained visual stimuli based on subject-provided descriptions and corresponding neural signals. As a widely adopted modality, Electroencephalography (EEG) captures rich visual cognition information, encompassing complex spatial relationships and chromatic details within scenes. However, current approaches are deeply coupled with an alignment framework that forces EEG features to align with text or image semantic representation. The dependency may condense the rich spatial and chromatic details in EEG that achieved mere conditioned image generation rather than high-fidelity visual reconstruction. To address this limitation, we propose a novel Joint-Modal Visual Reconstruction (JMVR) framework. It treats EEG and text as independent modalities for joint learning to preserve EEG-specific information for reconstruction. It further employs a multi-scale EEG encoding strategy to capture both fine- and coarse-grained features, alongside image augmentation to enhance the recovery of perceptual details. Extensive experiments on the THINGS-EEG dataset demonstrate that JMVR achieves SOTA performance against six baseline methods, specifically exhibiting superior capabilities in modeling spatial structure and chromatic fidelity.

AI.tldr: The JMVR framework proposes independent multi-modal learning for EEG- and text-based visual reconstruction, using multi-scale EEG encoding and image augmentation to surpass six baselines on THINGS-EEG, with notably improved spatial and color fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current EEG-to-image approaches rely on alignment frameworks that force EEG features to match text/image semantics, potentially discarding rich EEG spatial/chromatic details essential for high-fidelity reconstruction.
AI.method: Introduce Joint-Modal Visual Reconstruction (JMVR) that treats EEG and text as separate modalities for joint learning; implements multi-scale EEG encoding to capture fine- and coarse-grained features; uses image augmentation to enhance perceptual detail recovery.
AI.result: On THINGS-EEG, JMVR achieves state-of-the-art performance relative to six baselines, especially in modeling spatial structure and chromatic fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Preserving EEG-specific information via independent multi-modal learning and multi-scale encoding yields higher-fidelity visual reconstructions, suggesting joint learning of independent modalities plus augmentation as a promising direction.

title: Heavy-Tailed and Long-Range Dependent Noise in Stochastic Approximation: A Finite-Time Analysis
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
summary: Stochastic approximation (SA) is a fundamental iterative framework with broad applications in reinforcement learning and optimization. Classical analyses typically rely on martingale difference or Markov noise with bounded second moments, but many practical settings, including finance and communications, frequently encounter heavy-tailed and long-range dependent (LRD) noise. In this work, we study SA for finding the root of a strongly monotone operator under these non-classical noise models. We establish the first finite-time moment bounds in both settings, providing explicit convergence rates that quantify the impact of heavy tails and temporal dependence. Our analysis employs a noise-averaging argument that regularizes the impact of noise without modifying the iteration. Finally, we apply our general framework to stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and gradient play, and corroborate our finite-time analysis through numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: Finite-time moment bounds for stochastic approximation (SA) to find roots of strongly monotone operators under heavy-tailed and long-range dependent noise; introduces a noise-averaging technique that regularizes noise without altering the iteration; demonstrates applicability to SGD and gradient play with numerical validation.
AI.motivation: Standard SA analyses assume well-behaved noise (martingale differences or bounded second moments). In finance, communications, and other domains, noise can be heavy-tailed and exhibit long-range dependence (LRD). This work seeks finite-time guarantees and explicit rates under these non-classical noise models.
AI.method: Formulate SA for finding zeros of strongly monotone operators under heavy-tailed and LRD noise. Develop a noise-averaging argument that mitigates noise impact without modifying the update rule. Derive finite-time moment bounds; instantiate the framework for stochastic gradient descent (SGD) and gradient play; support results with numerical experiments.
AI.result: First finite-time moment bounds established for SA under both heavy-tailed and long-range dependent noise models. The bounds yield explicit convergence rates that quantify the influence of tail heaviness and temporal dependence. The proposed noise-averaging technique regularizes noise without altering the iterate. The framework is demonstrated on SGD and gradient play and validated numerically.
AI.conclusion: Non-classical noise models can be accommodated with finite-time guarantees via noise-averaging in SA. The framework is general, applies to SGD and gradient play, and provides quantitative rates that disentangle effects of heavy tails and LRD. Numerical experiments corroborate the theoretical findings.

title: Making Video Models Adhere to User Intent with Minor Adjustments
summary: With the recent drastic advancements in text-to-video diffusion models, controlling their generations has drawn interest. A popular way for control is through bounding boxes or layouts. However, enforcing adherence to these control inputs is still an open problem. In this work, we show that by slightly adjusting user-provided bounding boxes we can improve both the quality of generations and the adherence to the control inputs. This is achieved by simply optimizing the bounding boxes to better align with the internal attention maps of the video diffusion model while carefully balancing the focus on foreground and background. In a sense, we are modifying the bounding boxes to be at places where the model is familiar with. Surprisingly, we find that even with small modifications, the quality of generations can vary significantly. To do so, we propose a smooth mask to make the bounding box position differentiable and an attention-maximization objective that we use to alter the bounding boxes. We conduct thorough experiments, including a user study to validate the effectiveness of our method. Our code is made available on the project webpage to foster future research from the community.

AI.tldr: Optimizing user-provided bounding boxes via a differentiable mask and an attention-maximization objective improves both quality and adherence in text-to-video diffusion, with validation from a user study and public code.
AI.motivation: Controlling text-to-video diffusion with boxes/layouts is beneficial but imperfect; small, principled edits to control inputs can align them with the model's internal attention, boosting fidelity and adherence.
AI.method: Introduce a differentiable mask for bounding-box positions and an attention-maximization objective. Optimize boxes to better align with the model's attention maps while balancing foreground/background emphasis. Validate with comprehensive experiments including a user study; release code.
AI.result: Small, deliberate bounding-box tweaks yield significant improvements in generation quality and adherence, as demonstrated by quantitative and qualitative experiments and supported by user study feedback.
AI.conclusion: Bounding-box optimization via differentiable masks and attention-based objectives provides an effective, simple route to enhance controllability in text-to-video diffusion, with code released for future research.

title: Ensembles-based Feature Guided Analysis
summary: Recent Deep Neural Networks (DNN) applications ask for techniques that can explain their behavior. Existing solutions, such as Feature Guided Analysis (FGA), extract rules on their internal behaviors, e.g., by providing explanations related to neurons activation. Results from the literature show that these rules have considerable precision (i.e., they correctly predict certain classes of features), but the recall (i.e., the number of situations these rule apply) is more limited. To mitigate this problem, this paper presents Ensembles-based Feature Guided Analysis (EFGA). EFGA combines rules extracted by FGA into ensembles. Ensembles aggregate different rules to increase their applicability depending on an aggregation criterion, a policy that dictates how to combine rules into ensembles. Although our solution is extensible, and different aggregation criteria can be developed by users, in this work, we considered three different aggregation criteria. We evaluated how the choice of the criterion influences the effectiveness of EFGA on two benchmarks (i.e., the MNIST and LSC datasets), and found that different aggregation criteria offer alternative trade-offs between precision and recall. We then compare EFGA with FGA. For this experiment, we selected an aggregation criterion that provides a reasonable trade-off between precision and recall. Our results show that EFGA has higher train recall (+28.51% on MNIST, +33.15% on LSC), and test recall (+25.76% on MNIST, +30.81% on LSC) than FGA, with a negligible reduction on the test precision (-0.89% on MNIST, -0.69% on LSC).

AI.tldr: EFGA extends FGA by ensembling extracted rules to improve recall with minimal precision loss; shows recall gains on MNIST and LSC under a selected aggregation criterion.
AI.motivation: FGA rules have high precision but limited recall; ensembling aims to broaden applicability and improve recall without substantially sacrificing precision.
AI.method: Introduce EFGA that aggregates FGA-derived rules into ensembles via user-definable aggregation criteria; evaluate three criteria on MNIST and LSC; compare EFGA to FGA and select a criterion balancing precision and recall; EFGA is presented as extensible.
AI.result: EFGA yields higher recall than FGA: train recall +28.51% (MNIST) and +33.15% (LSC); test recall +25.76% (MNIST) and +30.81% (LSC); precision slightly decreases by -0.89% (MNIST) and -0.69% (LSC).
AI.conclusion: Ensembling FGA rules via EFGA improves recall with negligible precision loss; aggregation criteria offer tunable trade-offs; EFGA is extensible and beneficial for explanation-focused models.

title: The Residual Stream Is All You Need: On the Redundancy of the KV Cache in Transformer Inference
summary: The key-value (KV) cache is widely treated as essential state in transformer inference, and a large body of work engineers policies to compress, evict, or approximate its entries. We prove that this state is entirely redundant: keys and values at every layer are deterministic projections of the residual stream, and recomputing them from a single residual vector per token incurs exactly zero reconstruction error, not approximately, but bit-identically. We verify this across six models from four architecture families (135M to 4B parameters). Cross-task residual patching at every layer produces D_KL = 0 between patched and original output distributions, confirming that the residual stream satisfies a Markov property and is the sole information-carrying state. Removing the cache entirely and recomputing from scratch yields token-identical output under greedy decoding on all models tested. We build on this result with KV-Direct, a bounded-memory inference scheme that checkpoints residual vectors (5 KB per token on Gemma 3-4B) instead of full KV pairs (136 KB), recomputing keys and values on demand. Over 20 conversation turns, KV-Direct holds peak memory at 42 MB while the standard cache grows past 103 MB. Against five eviction baselines (H2O, StreamingLLM, SnapKV, TOVA, window-only), KV-Direct maintains 100% token match at every cache budget; all baselines degrade to 5-28%. A per-operation latency analysis shows recomputation runs up to 5x faster than reading cached tensors at moderate batch sizes. Code is available at https://github.com/Kaleemullahqasim/KV-Direct.

AI.tldr: KV cache in transformer inference is fully redundant; residual streams encode all keys/values bit-identically; a bounded-memory approach, KV-Direct, checkpoints residuals and recomputes K/V on demand, achieving identical outputs with lower memory and favorable latency.
AI.motivation: Challenge the necessity of the KV cache by testing whether the residual stream suffices to generate identical outputs, aiming to reduce memory and compute in inference.
AI.method: Theoretical proof that keys/values are deterministic projections of the residual stream with bit-identical reconstruction; empirical verification across six models (135M–4B) from four architectures; cross-task residual patching yields D_KL=0 between patched and original outputs; remove cache and obtain token-identical outputs under greedy decoding; develop KV-Direct that checkpoints residual vectors (~5 KB/token) and recomputes keys/values on demand; compare against eviction baselines (H2O, StreamingLLM, SnapKV, TOVA, window-only); measure memory, token fidelity, and per-operation latency.
AI.result: Demonstrates that keys/values are projections of the residual stream with zero reconstruction error; residual-based patching yields KL divergence zero, confirming a Markov property with residual as the sole information carrier; removing the cache yields token-identical outputs under greedy decoding; KV-Direct achieves peak memory ~42 MB over 20 turns versus >103 MB for standard cache; maintains 100% token match across budgets while baselines drop to 5–28%; recomputation is up to 5x faster than reading cached tensors at moderate batch sizes.
AI.conclusion: KV cache is unnecessary; the residual stream is the only information-carrying state for token generation. KV-Direct provides a bounded-memory inference mechanism with exact (bit-identical) outputs and substantial memory savings, accompanied by favorable latency characteristics; code released.

title: ATHENA: Adaptive Test-Time Steering for Improving Count Fidelity in Diffusion Models
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models achieve high visual fidelity but surprisingly exhibit systematic failures in numerical control when prompts specify explicit object counts. To address this limitation, we introduce ATHENA, a model-agnostic, test-time adaptive steering framework that improves object count fidelity without modifying model architectures or requiring retraining. ATHENA leverages intermediate representations during sampling to estimate object counts and applies count-aware noise corrections early in the denoising process, steering the generation trajectory before structural errors become difficult to revise. We present three progressively more advanced variants of ATHENA that trade additional computation for improved numerical accuracy, ranging from static prompt-based steering to dynamically adjusted count-aware control. Experiments on established benchmarks and a new visually and semantically complex dataset show that ATHENA consistently improves count fidelity, particularly at higher target counts, while maintaining favorable accuracy-runtime trade-offs across multiple diffusion backbones.

AI.tldr: Athena improves object-count fidelity in text-to-image diffusion without retraining by test-time adaptive count-aware steering. It uses intermediate representations to estimate counts and applies early denoising corrections, offering three variants that trade compute for accuracy across multiple backbones and datasets.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models exhibit systematic numerical control errors when prompts specify explicit object counts, leading to incorrect cardinality and layout; current solutions require retraining or architectural changes. A model-agnostic, test-time method is desirable for practical deployment.
AI.method: Three progressively advanced variants: static prompt-based steering and dynamic, count-aware control. ATHENA estimates object counts from intermediate representations during sampling and injects count-aware noise corrections early in the denoising process to steer generation before errors become irreversible. It remains model-agnostic and does not require retraining.
AI.result: ATHENA consistently improves count fidelity, especially at higher target counts, and maintains favorable accuracy-runtime trade-offs across multiple diffusion backbones on established benchmarks and a new complex dataset.
AI.conclusion: A practical, model-agnostic solution for numerical control in diffusion models: early, count-aware steering during sampling reduces structural count errors, with a tunable compute-accuracy trade-off through three variants, and broad applicability across backbones.

title: Scale-Dependent Radial Geometry and Metric Mismatch in Wasserstein Propagation for Reverse Diffusion
summary: Existing analyses of reverse diffusion often propagate sampling error in the Euclidean geometry underlying \(\Wtwo\) along the entire reverse trajectory. Under weak log-concavity, however, Gaussian smoothing can create contraction first at large separations while short separations remain non-dissipative. The first usable contraction is therefore radial rather than Euclidean, creating a metric mismatch between the geometry that contracts early and the geometry in which the terminal error is measured. We formalize this mismatch through an explicit radial lower profile for the learned reverse drift. Its far-field limit gives a contraction reserve, its near-field limit gives the Euclidean load governing direct \(\Wtwo\) propagation, and admissible switch times are characterized by positivity of the reserve on the remaining smoothing window. We exploit this structure with a one-switch routing argument. Before the switch, reflection coupling yields contraction in a concave transport metric adapted to the radial profile. At the switch, we convert once from this metric back to \(\Wtwo\) under a \(p\)-moment budget, and then propagate the converted discrepancy over the remaining short window in Euclidean geometry. For discretizations of the learned reverse SDE under \(L^2\) score-error control, a one-sided Lipschitz condition of score error, and standard well-posedness and coupling hypotheses, we obtain explicit non-asymptotic end-to-end \(\Wtwo\) guarantees, a scalar switch-selection objective, and a sharp structural limit on the conversion exponent within the affine-tail concave class.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes reverse diffusion under weak log-concavity, showing that contraction occurs radially (not Euclidean) due to Gaussian smoothing. It introduces a radial contractive metric, a one-switch routing strategy that transitions from a radial metric to Euclidean W2, and provides non-asymptotic W2 guarantees for discretized SDEs under score-error control with a scalar switch objective and a sharp exponent bound within an affine-tail concave class.
AI.motivation: To resolve a geometry mismatch in reverse diffusion: contraction occurs in a radial metric at large scales while error is measured in Euclidean W2, leading to suboptimal propagation of sampling error. The work aims to derive explicit, non-asymptotic end-to-end W2 guarantees and guidance for switch design.
AI.method: Model the learned reverse drift with a radial lower profile; analyze far-field (contraction reserve) and near-field (Euclidean load) limits; determine admissible switch times via positivity of the reserve over the smoothing window. Use a one-switch routing: pre-switch with reflection coupling in a concave transport metric adapted to the radial profile; post-switch convert to W2 under a p-moment budget and propagate in Euclidean geometry. Provide discrete-time guarantees for learned SDEs under L2 score-error control with a one-sided Lipschitz condition and standard well-posedness/coupling assumptions.
AI.result: Explicit non-asymptotic end-to-end W2 guarantees for discretized reverse SDEs; a scalar switch-selection objective; a sharp structural limit on the conversion exponent within the affine-tail concave class.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-region contraction framework clarifies how to leverage a radial metric during early smoothing and Euclidean geometry later, enabling practical switch design and rigorous W2 guarantees for reverse-diffusion sampling in settings with weak log-concavity.

title: Vision-Language Attribute Disentanglement and Reinforcement for Lifelong Person Re-Identification
summary: Lifelong person re-identification (LReID) aims to learn from varying domains to obtain a unified person retrieval model. Existing LReID approaches typically focus on learning from scratch or a visual classification-pretrained model, while the Vision-Language Model (VLM) has shown generalizable knowledge in a variety of tasks. Although existing methods can be directly adapted to the VLM, since they only consider global-aware learning, the fine-grained attribute knowledge is underleveraged, leading to limited acquisition and anti-forgetting capacity. To address this problem, we introduce a novel VLM-driven LReID approach named Vision-Language Attribute Disentanglement and Reinforcement (VLADR). Our key idea is to explicitly model the universally shared human attributes to improve inter-domain knowledge transfer, thereby effectively utilizing historical knowledge to reinforce new knowledge learning and alleviate forgetting. Specifically, VLADR includes a Multi-grain Text Attribute Disentanglement mechanism that mines the global and diverse local text attributes of an image. Then, an Inter-domain Cross-modal Attribute Reinforcement scheme is developed, which introduces cross-modal attribute alignment to guide visual attribute extraction and adopts inter-domain attribute alignment to achieve fine-grained knowledge transfer. Experimental results demonstrate that our VLADR outperforms the state-of-the-art methods by 1.9\%-2.2\% and 2.1\%-2.5\% on anti-forgetting and generalization capacity. Our source code is available at https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2026-VLADR

AI.tldr: VLADR is a Vision-Language Model (VLM) based lifelong person re-identification framework that disentangles global and local text attributes and uses inter-domain cross-modal attribute reinforcement to enhance knowledge transfer and mitigate forgetting.
AI.motivation: LReID methods typically learn from scratch or rely on a visual classification-pretrained backbone. While VLMs offer generalizable knowledge, existing adaptations focus on global features and underutilize fine-grained textual attributes, limiting cross-domain transfer and anti-forgetting capacity.
AI.method: Proposes Multi-grain Text Attribute Disentanglement to mine global and diverse local text attributes of an image. Introduces Inter-domain Cross-modal Attribute Reinforcement with cross-modal attribute alignment to guide visual attribute extraction and inter-domain attribute alignment to enable fine-grained knowledge transfer.
AI.result: VLADR outperforms state-of-the-art methods by 1.9–2.2 percentage points in anti-forgetting and 2.1–2.5 percentage points in generalization. The authors also provide source code at the given GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling universally shared human attributes and employing cross-domain attribute reinforcement enhances inter-domain knowledge transfer, preserves historical knowledge, and improves both anti-forgetting and generalization in LReID.

title: GoAgent: Group-of-Agents Communication Topology Generation for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems (MAS) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in solving complex tasks, yet their effectiveness depends heavily on the underlying communication topology that coordinates agent interactions. Within these systems, successful problem-solving often necessitates task-specific group structures to divide and conquer subtasks. However, most existing approaches generate communication topologies in a node-centric manner, leaving group structures to emerge implicitly from local connectivity decisions rather than modeling them explicitly, often leading to suboptimal coordination and unnecessary communication overhead. To address this limitation, we propose GoAgent (Group-of-Agents), a communication topology generation method that explicitly treats collaborative groups as the atomic units of MAS construction. Specifically, GoAgent first enumerates task-relevant candidate groups through an LLM and then autoregressively selects and connects these groups as atomic units to construct the final communication graph, jointly capturing intra-group cohesion and inter-group coordination. To mitigate communication redundancy and noise propagation inherent in expanding topologies, we further introduce a conditional information bottleneck (CIB) objective that compresses inter-group communication, preserving task-relevant signals while filtering out redundant historical noise. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance of GoAgent with 93.84% average accuracy while reducing token consumption by about 17%.

AI.tldr: GoAgent is a group-centric topology method for LLM-based MAS; enumerates candidate groups via LLM and builds a group-level graph autoregressively; adds conditional information bottleneck to compress inter-group communication; achieves SOTA on six benchmarks with 93.84% accuracy and ~17% token savings.
AI.motivation: Address inefficiencies of node-centric topology in MAS where group structures emerge implicitly; improve coordination by explicit group modeling and reduce communication overhead.
AI.method: Enumerate task-relevant candidate groups using LLM; autoregressively select and connect these groups as atomic units to form final communication graph; introduce a conditional information bottleneck objective to compress inter-group communication and filter noise; optimize for intra-group cohesion and inter-group coordination.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on six benchmarks shows state-of-the-art performance, averaging 93.84% accuracy and ~17% token reduction.
AI.conclusion: Explicit group-level topology design enhances coordination efficiency and performance in LLM-based MAS; GoAgent provides a scalable method by combining group enumeration with information bottleneck to mitigate noise.

title: Unbiased Dynamic Multimodal Fusion
summary: Traditional multimodal methods often assume static modality quality, which limits their adaptability in dynamic real-world scenarios. Thus, dynamical multimodal methods are proposed to assess modality quality and adjust their contribution accordingly. However, they typically rely on empirical metrics, failing to measure the modality quality when noise levels are extremely low or high. Moreover, existing methods usually assume that the initial contribution of each modality is the same, neglecting the intrinsic modality dependency bias. As a result, the modality hard to learn would be doubly penalized, and the performance of dynamical fusion could be inferior to that of static fusion. To address these challenges, we propose the Unbiased Dynamic Multimodal Learning (UDML) framework. Specifically, we introduce a noise-aware uncertainty estimator that adds controlled noise to the modality data and predicts its intensity from the modality feature. This forces the model to learn a clear correspondence between feature corruption and noise level, allowing accurate uncertainty measure across both low- and high-noise conditions. Furthermore, we quantify the inherent modality reliance bias within multimodal networks via modality dropout and incorporate it into the weighting mechanism. This eliminates the dual suppression effect on the hard-to-learn modality. Extensive experiments across diverse multimodal benchmark tasks validate the effectiveness, versatility, and generalizability of the proposed UDML. The code is available at https://github.com/shicaiwei123/UDML.

AI.tldr: UDML introduces a noise-aware uncertainty estimator and modality dropout to achieve unbiased, robust dynamic fusion across noise levels, outperforming existing dynamic methods; code available.
AI.motivation: Traditional multimodal fusion assumes static or ad-hoc dynamic modality quality and uses empirical metrics; these fail under extreme noise and ignore intrinsic modality dependency bias, causing misweighting of hard-to-learn modalities.
AI.method: UDML adds controlled noise to each modality and trains a noise-intensity regressor to learn the relation between corruption and noise. It also uses modality dropout to quantify innate modality reliance bias and incorporates this into the fusion weighting to prevent double penalization of hard modalities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse multimodal benchmarks show improved robustness, versatility, and generalizability of UDML compared with prior approaches; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: UDML provides an unbiased, robust dynamic fusion framework that addresses both uncertainty estimation under varying noise and intrinsic modality bias, improving over static and previous dynamic fusion methods across multiple multimodal tasks.

title: Ontology-Based Knowledge Modeling and Uncertainty-Aware Outdoor Air Quality Assessment Using Weighted Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic
summary: Outdoor air pollution is a major concern for the environment and public health, especially in areas where urbanization is taking place rapidly. The Indian Air Quality Index (IND-AQI), developed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), is a standardized reporting system for air quality based on pollutants such as PM2.5, PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO2), sulfur dioxide (SO2), ozone (O3), carbon monoxide (CO), and ammonia (NH3). However, the traditional calculation of the AQI uses crisp thresholds and deterministic aggregation rules, which are not suitable for handling uncertainty and transitions between classes. To address these limitations, this study proposes a hybrid ontology-based uncertainty-aware framework integrating Weighted Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Logic with semantic knowledge modeling. Interval Type-2 fuzzy sets are used to model uncertainty near AQI class boundaries, while pollutant importance weights are determined using Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process (IT2-FAHP) to reflect their relative health impacts. In addition, an OWL-based air quality ontology extending the Semantic Sensor Network (SSN) ontology is developed to represent pollutants, monitoring stations, AQI categories, regulatory standards, and environmental governance actions. Semantic reasoning is implemented using SWRL rules and validated through SPARQL queries to infer AQI categories, health risks, and recommended mitigation actions. Experimental evaluation using CPCB air quality datasets demonstrates that the proposed framework improves AQI classification reliability and uncertainty handling compared with traditional crisp and Type-1 fuzzy approaches, while enabling explainable semantic reasoning and intelligent decision support for air quality monitoring systems

AI.tldr: A hybrid ontology-enabled, uncertainty-aware AQI framework using Interval Type-2 fuzzy logic and semantic reasoning to improve IND-AQI classification and decision support.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty at AQI class boundaries and fixed crisp thresholds limit reliability and interpretability; integrating fuzzy logic with semantic knowledge modeling enables robust, explainable decision support for air quality monitoring.
AI.method: Use Interval Type-2 fuzzy sets to model boundary uncertainty; IT2-FAHP to determine pollutant weights; an OWL-based air quality ontology extending the SSN ontology; SWRL rules for inference; SPARQL queries for reasoning; evaluation on CPCB datasets.
AI.result: Improved AQI classification reliability and uncertainty handling over crisp and Type-1 fuzzy approaches; enhanced explainability and actionable mitigation recommendations through semantic reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a viable, scalable integration of fuzzy uncertainty handling with semantic web technologies for smarter air quality monitoring and governance.

title: 3D Gaussian Splatting with Self-Constrained Priors for High Fidelity Surface Reconstruction
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://takeshie.github.io/GSPrior
summary: Rendering 3D surfaces has been revolutionized within the modeling of radiance fields through either 3DGS or NeRF. Although 3DGS has shown advantages over NeRF in terms of rendering quality or speed, there is still room for improvement in recovering high fidelity surfaces through 3DGS. To resolve this issue, we propose a self-constrained prior to constrain the learning of 3D Gaussians, aiming for more accurate depth rendering. Our self-constrained prior is derived from a TSDF grid that is obtained by fusing the depth maps rendered with current 3D Gaussians. The prior measures a distance field around the estimated surface, offering a band centered at the surface for imposing more specific constraints on 3D Gaussians, such as removing Gaussians outside the band, moving Gaussians closer to the surface, and encouraging larger or smaller opacity in a geometry-aware manner. More importantly, our prior can be regularly updated by the most recent depth images which are usually more accurate and complete. In addition, the prior can also progressively narrow the band to tighten the imposed constraints. We justify our idea and report our superiority over the state-of-the-art methods in evaluations on widely used benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A self-constrained prior based on a TSDF band guides 3D Gaussian surfaces (3DGS) to improve depth rendering by pruning Gaussians outside the surface band, repositioning Gaussians toward the surface, and adapting opacity in a geometry-aware way; the prior updates with newer depth renders and can progressively narrow the band, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To close the fidelity gap between 3DGS and NeRF in surface recovery by enforcing a geometry-aware prior that anchors Gaussians to the estimated surface, thus improving depth rendering accuracy and rendering quality.
AI.method: Construct a TSDF grid by fusing depth maps rendered with the current 3D Gaussians. Define a distance field around the estimated surface forming a band. Use this self-derived prior to constrain Gaussians: remove those outside the band, move Gaussians toward the surface, and adjust opacity according to geometry. Regularly update the prior with the latest depth images and progressively narrow the band to tighten constraints during optimization.
AI.result: The approach is validated with experiments on widely used benchmarks, showing superiority over state-of-the-art methods in depth rendering quality and/or rendering efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A self-constrained TSDF-based prior effectively guides 3D Gaussian surface learning in 3DGS, enabling more accurate surface recovery and improved rendering performance, with the prior being updatable and progressively tightened to ensure convergence.

title: Regret Analysis of Sleeping Competing Bandits
summary: The Competing Bandits framework is a recently emerging area that integrates multi-armed bandits in online learning with stable matching in game theory. While conventional models assume that all players and arms are constantly available, in real-world problems, their availability can vary arbitrarily over time. In this paper, we formulate this setting as Sleeping Competing Bandits. To analyze this problem, we naturally extend the regret definition used in existing competing bandits and derive regret bounds for the proposed model. We propose an algorithm that simultaneously achieves an asymptotic regret bound of $\mathrm{O}\left(NK\log T_{i}/Δ^2\right)$ under reasonable assumptions, where $N$ is the number of players, $K$ is the number of arms, $T_{i}$ is the number of rounds of each player $p_i$, and $Δ$ is the minimum reward gap. We also provide a regret lower bound of $\mathrmΩ\left( N(K-N+1)\log T_{i}/Δ^2 \right)$ under the same assumptions. This implies that our algorithm is asymptotically optimal in the regime where the number of arms $K$ is relatively larger than the number of players $N$.

AI.tldr: Extends competing bandits to Sleeping Competing Bandits where players/arms vary over time; proposes an algorithm with an asymptotic regret of O(NK log Ti / Δ^2) and a lower bound Ω(N(K−N+1) log Ti / Δ^2), showing asymptotic optimality when K ≫ N.
AI.motivation: In real-world online allocation, both players and arms can become unavailable unpredictably. A sleeping setting requires new regret definitions and algorithms that respect dynamic availability while maintaining stable matchings.
AI.method: Extend the regret framework of competing bandits to the sleeping setting, design an algorithm that handles time-varying availability, and derive both upper and lower bounds on regret under standard assumptions (uniform minimum gap Δ, etc.).
AI.result: An algorithm achieving asymptotic regret of O(NK log Ti / Δ^2) for each player with Ti rounds and a matching lower bound Ω(N(K−N+1) log Ti / Δ^2) under the same assumptions; demonstrates asymptotic optimality in the regime K ≫ N.
AI.conclusion: The work generalizes competing bandits to sleeping settings, provides near-optimal performance guarantees in the specified regime, and highlights the importance of considering availability dynamics in online matching problems.

title: TSegAgent: Zero-Shot Tooth Segmentation via Geometry-Aware Vision-Language Agents
summary: Automatic tooth segmentation and identification from intra-oral scanned 3D models are fundamental problems in digital dentistry, yet most existing approaches rely on task-specific 3D neural networks trained with densely annotated datasets, resulting in high annotation cost and limited generalization to scans from unseen sources. Thus, we propose TSegAgent, which addresses these challenges by reformulating dental analysis as a zero-shot geometric reasoning problem rather than a purely data-driven recognition task. The key idea is to combine the representational capacity of general-purpose foundation models with explicit geometric inductive biases derived from dental anatomy. Instead of learning dental-specific features, the proposed framework leverages multi-view visual abstraction and geometry-grounded reasoning to infer tooth instances and identities without task-specific training. By explicitly encoding structural constraints such as dental arch organization and volumetric relationships, the method reduces uncertainty in ambiguous cases and mitigates overfitting to particular shape distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that this reasoning-oriented formulation enables accurate and reliable tooth segmentation and identification with low computational and annotation cost, while exhibiting strong generalization across diverse and previously unseen dental scans.

AI.tldr: A zero-shot geometric reasoning framework (TSegAgent) that uses foundation models and dental anatomy priors for tooth segmentation/identification with low annotation cost and strong generalization.
AI.motivation: Annotation cost and limited generalization of 3D dental segmentation models trained on densely annotated datasets; need a robust approach that generalizes across unseen sources.
AI.method: Zero-shot approach combining multi-view visual abstractions with geometry-grounded reasoning; explicit encoding of structural constraints such as dental arch organization and volumetric relationships; avoids task-specific training.
AI.result: Demonstrates accurate and reliable tooth segmentation and identification with low computation and annotation cost; strong generalization to unseen dental scans.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-informed, zero-shot reasoning provides robust generalization and reduced data burden, with potential to extend to broader dental anatomy tasks.

title: Learning from Similarity/Dissimilarity and Pairwise Comparison
summary: This paper addresses binary classification in scenarios where obtaining explicit instance level labels is impractical, by exploiting multiple weak labels defined on instance pairs. The existing SconfConfDiff classification framework relies on continuous valued probabilistic supervision, including similarity-confidence, the probability of class agreement, and confidence-difference, the difference in positive class probabilities. However, probabilistic labeling requires subjective uncertainty quantification, often leading to unstable supervision. We propose SD-Pcomp classification, a binary judgment based weakly supervised learning framework that relies only on relative judgments, namely class agreement between two instances and pairwise preference toward the positive class. The method employs Similarity/Dissimilarity (SD) labels and Pairwise Comparison (Pcomp) labels, and develops two unbiased risk estimators, (i) a convex combination of SD and Pcomp and (ii) a unified estimator that integrates both labels by modeling their relationship. Theoretical analysis and experimental results show that the proposed approach improves classification performance over methods using a single weak label, and is robust to label noise and uncertainty in class prior estimation.

AI.tldr: Introduces SD-Pcomp: a binary weakly supervised classifier using only relative SD and Pcomp labels with two unbiased risk estimators; achieves improved performance and robustness versus single-label weak supervision.
AI.motivation: Explicit instance-level labels are impractical; probabilistic labels require uncertainty quantification, often causing instability; propose binary judgments based on relative information as a robust alternative.
AI.method: Use Similarity/Dissimilarity (SD) and Pairwise Comparison (Pcomp) labels; derive two unbiased estimators: (i) a convex combination of SD and Pcomp risks; (ii) a unified estimator modeling the relationship between SD and Pcomp; theoretical analysis ensures unbiasedness; experiments validate.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis and experiments show improved performance over single weak-label methods; robust to label noise and uncertainty in class priors.
AI.conclusion: SD-Pcomp provides a robust, binary-judgment weak supervision framework for binary classification, leveraging relative judgments and combining SD and Pcomp to outperform single-label baselines.

title: Demographic-Aware Self-Supervised Anomaly Detection Pretraining for Equitable Rare Cardiac Diagnosis
summary: Rare cardiac anomalies are difficult to detect from electrocardiograms (ECGs) due to their long-tailed distribution with extremely limited case counts and demographic disparities in diagnostic performance. These limitations contribute to delayed recognition and uneven quality of care, creating an urgent need for a generalizable framework that enhances sensitivity while ensuring equity across diverse populations. In this study, we developed an AI-assisted two-stage ECG framework integrating self-supervised anomaly detection with demographic-aware representation learning. The first stage performs self-supervised anomaly detection pretraining by reconstructing masked global and local ECG signals, modeling signal trends, and predicting patient attributes to learn robust ECG representations without diagnostic labels. The pretrained model is then fine-tuned for multi-label ECG classification using asymmetric loss to better handle long-tail cardiac abnormalities, and additionally produces anomaly score maps for localization, with CPU-based optimization enabling practical deployment. Evaluated on a longitudinal cohort of over one million clinical ECGs, our method achieves an AUROC of 94.7% for rare anomalies and reduces the common-rare performance gap by 73%, while maintaining consistent diagnostic accuracy across age and sex groups. In conclusion, the proposed equity-aware AI framework demonstrates strong clinical utility, interpretable anomaly localization, and scalable performance across multiple cohorts, highlighting its potential to mitigate diagnostic disparities and advance equitable anomaly detection in biomedical signals and digital health. Source code is available at https://github.com/MediaBrain-SJTU/Rare-ECG.

AI.tldr: Two-stage AI framework for rare ECG anomalies using self-supervised anomaly detection and demographic-aware representation learning; achieves high AUROC and equitable performance on >1M ECGs.
AI.motivation: Rare cardiac anomalies have a long-tail distribution and demographic disparities in diagnostic performance, leading to delayed recognition and unequal care; a generalizable, equity-focused solution is needed.
AI.method: Stage 1: Self-supervised anomaly-detection pretraining by reconstructing masked global and local ECG signals, modeling signal trends, and predicting patient attributes to learn robust representations without diagnostic labels. Stage 2: Fine-tuning for multi-label ECG classification with asymmetric loss to address long-tail distributions; anomaly score maps for localization; CPU-based optimization for deployment.
AI.result: Evaluated on a longitudinal cohort of over one million ECGs; AUROC 94.7% for rare anomalies; reduces the common–rare performance gap by 73%; diagnostic accuracy remains consistent across age and sex groups.
AI.conclusion: Equity-aware AI framework with interpretable anomaly localization; scalable performance across cohorts; potential to mitigate diagnostic disparities in biomedical signals; source code available at the authors' repository.

title: FedRG: Unleashing the Representation Geometry for Federated Learning with Noisy Clients
summary: Federated learning (FL) suffers from performance degradation due to the inevitable presence of noisy annotations in distributed scenarios. Existing approaches have advanced in distinguishing noisy samples from the dataset for label correction by leveraging loss values. However, noisy samples recognition relying on scalar loss lacks reliability for FL under heterogeneous scenarios. In this paper, we rethink this paradigm from a representation perspective and propose \method~(\textbf{Fed}erated under \textbf{R}epresentation \textbf{G}emometry), which follows \textbf{the principle of ``representation geometry priority''} to recognize noisy labels. Firstly, \method~creates label-agnostic spherical representations by using self-supervision. It then iteratively fits a spherical von Mises-Fisher (vMF) mixture model to this geometry using previously identified clean samples to capture semantic clusters. This geometric evidence is integrated with a semantic-label soft mapping mechanism to derive a distribution divergence between the label-free and annotated label-conditioned feature space, which robustly identifies noisy samples and updates the vMF mixture model with the newly separated clean dataset. Lastly, we employ an additional personalized noise absorption matrix on noisy labels to achieve robust optimization. Extensive experimental results demonstrate that \method~significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods for FL with data heterogeneity under diverse noisy clients scenarios.

AI.tldr: FedRGe introduces a representation-geometry based framework for robust noisy-label handling in federated learning, using self-supervised label-agnostic spherical features and a spherical von Mises–Fisher mixture to identify clean vs. noisy samples, augmented by a semantic-soft mapping and a personalized noise absorption matrix for robust optimization, outperforming state-of-the-art under heterogeneous data and noise conditions.
AI.motivation: In FL with heterogeneous data, noisy annotations degrade performance. Existing loss-based noisy-label detection is unreliable in FL; a representation-centric approach leveraging geometry can more robustly distinguish clean/noisy samples across clients.
AI.method: 1) Generate label-agnostic spherical representations via self-supervision. 2) Iteratively fit a spherical vMF mixture model to capture semantic clusters using known clean samples. 3) Integrate geometry with a semantic-label soft mapping to compute distribution divergence between label-free and label-conditioned feature spaces, identifying noisy samples. 4) Update the vMF model with newly identified clean data. 5) Apply a personalized noise absorption matrix on noisy labels for robust optimization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods for FL with data heterogeneity and noisy clients, across diverse noisy scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A representation-geometry-priority framework effectively detects noisy labels and enables robust FL optimization; combining self-supervised representations, geometric clustering, divergence-based labeling, and personalized correction yields strong empirical gains across heterogeneous FL settings.

title: WorldAgents: Can Foundation Image Models be Agents for 3D World Models?
summary: Given the remarkable ability of 2D foundation image models to generate high-fidelity outputs, we investigate a fundamental question: do 2D foundation image models inherently possess 3D world model capabilities? To answer this, we systematically evaluate multiple state-of-the-art image generation models and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) on the task of 3D world synthesis. To harness and benchmark their potential implicit 3D capability, we propose an agentic framing to facilitate 3D world generation. Our approach employs a multi-agent architecture: a VLM-based director that formulates prompts to guide image synthesis, a generator that synthesizes new image views, and a VLM-backed two-step verifier that evaluates and selectively curates generated frames from both 2D image and 3D reconstruction space. Crucially, we demonstrate that our agentic approach provides coherent and robust 3D reconstruction, producing output scenes that can be explored by rendering novel views. Through extensive experiments across various foundation models, we demonstrate that 2D models do indeed encapsulate a grasp of 3D worlds. By exploiting this understanding, our method successfully synthesizes expansive, realistic, and 3D-consistent worlds.

AI.tldr: 2D foundation image models may inherently encode 3D world models; an agentic multi-agent pipeline demonstrates 3D world generation and 3D‑consistent reconstructions using 2D priors.
AI.motivation: Explore whether 2D image generators and vision–language models possess latent 3D understanding and whether this can be harnessed to synthesize coherent 3D scenes.
AI.method: Propose an agentic framework with three components: a VLM‑based director to formulate prompts guiding synthesis, a generator to create new views, and a VLM‑backed verifier to evaluate and curate frames in 2D and 3D spaces; evaluate across multiple foundation models; render novel views; iterative refinement to ensure 3D consistency.
AI.result: The approach yields coherent, robust 3D reconstructions; produced scenes are explorable via novel views; 2D models demonstrate a grasp of 3D structure and can generate expansive, realistic, 3D‑consistent worlds.
AI.conclusion: 2D foundation models encode latent 3D understanding; the agentic framework can leverage this for scalable 3D world generation, enabling realistic 3D synthesis from 2D priors.

title: FedPDPO: Federated Personalized Direct Preference Optimization for Large Language Model Alignment
summary: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences in federated learning (FL) is challenging due to decentralized, privacy-sensitive, and highly non-IID preference data. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) offers an efficient alternative to reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF), but its direct application in FL suffers from severe performance degradation under non-IID data and limited generalization of implicit rewards. To bridge this gap, we propose FedPDPO (Federated Personalized Direct Preference Optimization), a personalized federated framework for preference alignment of LLMs. It adopts a parameter-efficient fine-tuning architecture where each client maintains a frozen pretrained LLM backbone augmented with a Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapter, enabling communication-efficient aggregation. To address non-IID heterogeneity, we devise (1) the globally shared LoRA adapter with the personalized client-specific LLM head. Moreover, we introduce (2) a personalized DPO training strategy with a client-specific explicit reward head to complement implicit rewards and further alleviate non-IID heterogeneity, and (3) a bottleneck adapter to balance global and local features. We provide theoretical analysis establishing the probabilistic foundation and soundness. Extensive experiments on multiple preference datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, achieving up to 4.80% average accuracy improvements in federated intra-domain and cross-domain settings.

AI.tldr: FedPDPO introduces a federated, personalized DPO framework for LLM alignment under non-IID data, using a globally shared LoRA and client-specific heads plus explicit reward heads and bottleneck adapters; theory plus experiments show state-of-the-art gains.
AI.motivation: Aligning LLMs with human preferences in federated learning is hard due to privacy-preserving, decentralized, and highly non-IID data, and the limitations of RLHF/DPO under such settings.
AI.method: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning with a frozen backbone + LoRA; a globally shared LoRA adapter with personalized client-specific LLM heads; a client-specific explicit reward head to supplement implicit rewards; a bottleneck adapter to balance global vs local features; theoretical analysis of probabilistic foundations; empirical evaluation on multiple preference datasets.
AI.result: Achieves up to 4.80% average accuracy improvement over baselines in both federated intra-domain and cross-domain settings; demonstrated state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: FedPDPO effectively mitigates non-IID issues in federated preference alignment of LLMs, leveraging a balanced architecture and training strategy to provide strong theoretical and empirical performance.

title: BALM: A Model-Agnostic Framework for Balanced Multimodal Learning under Imbalanced Missing Rates
summary: Learning from multiple modalities often suffers from imbalance, where information-rich modalities dominate optimization while weaker or partially missing modalities contribute less. This imbalance becomes severe in realistic settings with imbalanced missing rates (IMR), where each modality is absent with different probabilities, distorting representation learning and gradient dynamics. We revisit this issue from a training-process perspective and propose BALM, a model-agnostic plug-in framework to achieve balanced multimodal learning under IMR. The framework comprises two complementary modules: the Feature Calibration Module (FCM), which recalibrates unimodal features using global context to establish a shared representation basis across heterogeneous missing patterns; the Gradient Rebalancing Module (GRM), which balances learning dynamics across modalities by modulating gradient magnitudes and directions from both distributional and spatial perspectives. BALM can be seamlessly integrated into diverse backbones, including multimodal emotion recognition (MER) models, without altering their architectures. Experimental results across multiple MER benchmarks confirm that BALM consistently enhances robustness and improves performance under diverse missing and imbalance settings. Code available at: https://github.com/np4s/BALM_CVPR2026.git

AI.tldr: BALM is a model-agnostic plug-in framework with two modules (FCM and GRM) that balances multimodal learning under imbalanced missing-rate (IMR) to improve robustness in MER.
AI.motivation: IMR causes information-rich modalities to dominate training, distorting representations and gradient dynamics; a training-process perspective is needed.
AI.method: Introduce Feature Calibration Module (FCM) to recalibrate unimodal features using global context to form a shared basis; Gradient Rebalancing Module (GRM) to balance gradient magnitudes/directions across modalities from distributional and spatial perspectives; plug-in to diverse backbones without architecture changes; demonstrated on MER tasks; code provided.
AI.result: Consistent robustness improvements across MER benchmarks under varied missing and imbalance settings; BALM improves performance with IMR; code available.
AI.conclusion: BALM provides effective, model-agnostic balance in multimodal learning under IMR, improving robustness with minimal integration effort; potential applicability to other multimodal tasks.

title: Dual Path Attribution: Efficient Attribution for SwiGLU-Transformers through Layer-Wise Target Propagation
summary: Understanding the internal mechanisms of transformer-based large language models (LLMs) is crucial for their reliable deployment and effective operation. While recent efforts have yielded a plethora of attribution methods attempting to balance faithfulness and computational efficiency, dense component attribution remains prohibitively expensive. In this work, we introduce Dual Path Attribution (DPA), a novel framework that faithfully traces information flow on the frozen transformer in one forward and one backward pass without requiring counterfactual examples. DPA analytically decomposes and linearizes the computational structure of the SwiGLU Transformers into distinct pathways along which it propagates a targeted unembedding vector to receive the effective representation at each residual position. This target-centric propagation achieves O(1) time complexity with respect to the number of model components, scaling to long input sequences and dense component attribution. Extensive experiments on standard interpretability benchmarks demonstrate that DPA achieves state-of-the-art faithfulness and unprecedented efficiency compared to existing baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes Dual Path Attribution (DPA) for faithful, efficient transformer attribution with one forward and one backward pass, achieving O(1) per component.
AI.motivation: Dense component attribution is expensive; need scalable, faithful interpretability for frozen transformers.
AI.method: Analytically decompose and linearize SwiGLU-based transformer computations into distinct propagation pathways; propagate a targeted unembedding vector to obtain the effective representation at each residual position; one forward and one backward pass; no counterfactuals; O(1) time with respect to model components.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on standard interpretability benchmarks show state-of-the-art faithfulness and unprecedented efficiency compared to baselines; scales to long inputs and dense attribution.
AI.conclusion: DPA provides a practical, faithful, and scalable attribution framework for transformer internals, enabling efficient understanding of LLMs without counterfactual reasoning.

title: Uncertainty-aware Prototype Learning with Variational Inference for Few-shot Point Cloud Segmentation
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables, accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Few-shot 3D semantic segmentation aims to generate accurate semantic masks for query point clouds with only a few annotated support examples. Existing prototype-based methods typically construct compact and deterministic prototypes from the support set to guide query segmentation. However, such rigid representations are unable to capture the intrinsic uncertainty introduced by scarce supervision, which often results in degraded robustness and limited generalization. In this work, we propose UPL (Uncertainty-aware Prototype Learning), a probabilistic approach designed to incorporate uncertainty modeling into prototype learning for few-shot 3D segmentation. Our framework introduces two key components. First, UPL introduces a dual-stream prototype refinement module that enriches prototype representations by jointly leveraging limited information from both support and query samples. Second, we formulate prototype learning as a variational inference problem, regarding class prototypes as latent variables. This probabilistic formulation enables explicit uncertainty modeling, providing robust and interpretable mask predictions. Extensive experiments on the widely used ScanNet and S3DIS benchmarks show that our UPL achieves consistent state-of-the-art performance under different settings while providing reliable uncertainty estimation. The code is available at https://fdueblab-upl.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Probabilistic, uncertainty-aware prototypes for few-shot 3D segmentation; uses dual-stream refinement and variational inference; achieves state-of-the-art on ScanNet and S3DIS with reliable uncertainty estimates.
AI.motivation: Rigid prototypes in existing few-shot 3D segmentation fail to capture uncertainty due to scarce supervision, harming robustness and generalization.
AI.method: A dual-stream prototype refinement module enriches prototypes using information from both support and query samples, and prototypes are modeled as latent variables within a variational inference framework to enable explicit uncertainty estimation.
AI.result: The approach achieves consistent state-of-the-art performance on ScanNet and S3DIS under various settings and provides reliable uncertainty estimates; code is released.
AI.conclusion: Modeling uncertainty in prototype learning improves robustness and generalization for few-shot 3D segmentation and yields interpretable mask predictions.

title: PerformRecast: Expression and Head Pose Disentanglement for Portrait Video Editing
summary: This paper primarily investigates the task of expression-only portrait video performance editing based on a driving video, which plays a crucial role in animation and film industries. Most existing research mainly focuses on portrait animation, which aims to animate a static portrait image according to the facial motion from the driving video. As a consequence, it remains challenging for them to disentangle the facial expression from head pose rotation and thus lack the ability to edit facial expression independently. In this paper, we propose PerformRecast, a versatile expression-only video editing method which is dedicated to recast the performance in existing film and animation. The key insight of our method comes from the characteristics of 3D Morphable Face Model (3DMM), which models the face identity, facial expression and head pose of 3D face mesh with separate parameters. Therefore, we improve the keypoints transformation formula in previous methods to make it more consistent with 3DMM model, which achieves a better disentanglement and provides users with much more fine-grained control. Furthermore, to avoid the misalignment around the boundary of face in generated results, we decouple the facial and non-facial regions of input portrait images and pre-train a teacher model to provide separate supervision for them. Extensive experiments show that our method produces high-quality results which are more faithful to the driving video, outperforming existing methods in both controllability and efficiency. Our code, data and trained models are available at https://youku-aigc.github.io/PerformRecast.

AI.tldr: Proposes PerformRecast, an expression-only portrait video editing method that decouples facial expression from head pose using a 3DMM-consistent keypoint transformation and region-wise supervision (facial vs non-facial).
AI.motivation: To enable independent editing of facial expressions in portrait videos, addressing the entanglement of expression and head pose in prior portrait animation methods, which hampers controllability for film/animation applications.
AI.method: Introduce a 3DMM-consistent keypoint transformation to disentangle identity/expression/pose; decouple facial and non-facial regions for boundary alignment; pre-train a teacher model to provide separate supervision for these regions; train on driving/video data to drive expression edits.
AI.result: High-quality, faithful results that better track the driving video; improved controllability and efficiency compared with existing methods; qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate advantages.
AI.conclusion: Expression-only video editing is feasible with a 3DMM-aligned, region-aware framework; the approach offers finer control and boundary robustness, with released code/data/models for reproducibility.

title: Scalable Learning of Multivariate Distributions via Coresets
summary: Efficient and scalable non-parametric or semi-parametric regression analysis and density estimation are of crucial importance to the fields of statistics and machine learning. However, available methods are limited in their ability to handle large-scale data. We address this issue by developing a novel coreset construction for multivariate conditional transformation models (MCTMs) to enhance their scalability and training efficiency. To the best of our knowledge, these are the first coresets for semi-parametric distributional models. Our approach yields substantial data reduction via importance sampling. It ensures with high probability that the log-likelihood remains within multiplicative error bounds of $(1\pm\varepsilon)$ and thereby maintains statistical model accuracy. Compared to conventional full-parametric models, where coresets have been incorporated before, our semi-parametric approach exhibits enhanced adaptability, particularly in scenarios where complex distributions and non-linear relationships are present, but not fully understood. To address numerical problems associated with normalizing logarithmic terms, we follow a geometric approximation based on the convex hull of input data. This ensures feasible, stable, and accurate inference in scenarios involving large amounts of data. Numerical experiments demonstrate substantially improved computational efficiency when handling large and complex datasets, thus laying the foundation for a broad range of applications within the statistics and machine learning communities.

AI.tldr: Coreset construction for multivariate conditional transformation models (MCTMs) enabling scalable non-parametric/semi-parametric regression and density estimation; first coresets for semi-parametric distributional models; preserves log-likelihood within (1±ε) with high probability; employs importance sampling and a convex-hull-based geometric approximation to stabilize normalization terms; yields substantial data reduction and improved efficiency on large/complex data.
AI.motivation: Need scalable, accurate inference for non-parametric/semi-parametric regression and density estimation; existing methods struggle with large-scale data and semi-parametric models; MCTMs provide flexible distributional modeling but training is computationally intensive.
AI.method: Construct coresets via importance sampling tailored to MCTMs; ensure multiplicative log-likelihood approximation; address numerical issues from normalizing logarithms with a geometric approximation based on the convex hull of inputs.
AI.result: Numerical experiments show substantial computational efficiency gains and maintained accuracy under (1±ε) error bounds; coreset-based semi-parametric models perform robustly on large/complex datasets.
AI.conclusion: This work introduces the first coresets for semi-parametric distributional models, enabling scalable training of MCTMs and broad applicability in statistics and ML; the approach enhances adaptability for complex distributions and nonlinear relations.

title: Learning Hierarchical Orthogonal Prototypes for Generalized Few-Shot 3D Point Cloud Segmentation
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures, 2 tables, Accepted by ICME 2026
summary: Generalized few-shot 3D point cloud segmentation aims to adapt to novel classes from only a few annotations while maintaining strong performance on base classes, but this remains challenging due to the inherent stability-plasticity trade-off: adapting to novel classes can interfere with shared representations and cause base-class forgetting. We present HOP3D, a unified framework that learns hierarchical orthogonal prototypes with an entropy-based few-shot regularizer to enable robust novel-class adaptation without degrading base-class performance. HOP3D introduces hierarchical orthogonalization that decouples base and novel learning at both the gradient and representation levels, effectively mitigating base-novel interference. To further enhance adaptation under sparse supervision, we incorporate an entropy-based regularizer that leverages predictive uncertainty to refine prototype learning and promote balanced predictions. Extensive experiments on ScanNet200 and ScanNet++ demonstrate that HOP3D consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under both 1-shot and 5-shot settings. The code is available at https://fdueblab-hop3d.github.io/.

AI.tldr: HOP3D provides hierarchical orthogonal prototypes with an entropy-based few-shot regularizer to enable robust generalized few-shot 3D point cloud segmentation, decoupling base and novel learning to mitigate forgetting and outperform baselines in 1- and 5-shot on ScanNet datasets.
AI.motivation: Address the stability-plasticity trade-off in generalized few-shot 3D segmentation: adapt to novel classes with few annotations without degrading base-class performance.
AI.method: Introduce hierarchical orthogonalization to decouple base and novel learning at gradient and representation levels; apply entropy-based regularizer using predictive uncertainty to refine prototypes and balance predictions under sparse supervision.
AI.result: Empirical results on ScanNet200 and ScanNet++ show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art baselines in 1-shot and 5-shot settings; code available.
AI.conclusion: HOP3D effectively mitigates base-novel interference and enables robust, data-efficient adaptation for 3D segmentation; approach can generalize to other few-shot segmentation tasks.

title: PhysNeXt: Next-Generation Dual-Branch Structured Attention Fusion Network for Remote Photoplethysmography Measurement
summary: Remote photoplethysmography (rPPG) enables contactless measurement of heart rate and other vital signs by analyzing subtle color variations in facial skin induced by cardiac pulsation. Current rPPG methods are mainly based on either end-to-end modeling from raw videos or intermediate spatial-temporal map (STMap) representations. The former preserves complete spatiotemporal information and can capture subtle heartbeat-related signals, but it also introduces substantial noise from motion artifacts and illumination variations. The latter stacks the temporal color changes of multiple facial regions of interest into compact two-dimensional representations, significantly reducing data volume and computational complexity, although some high-frequency details may be lost. To effectively integrate the mutual strengths, we propose PhysNeXt, a dual-input deep learning framework that jointly exploits video frames and STMap representations. By incorporating a spatio-temporal difference modeling unit, a cross-modal interaction module, and a structured attention-based decoder, PhysNeXt collaboratively enhances the robustness of pulse signal extraction. Experimental results demonstrate that PhysNeXt achieves more stable and fine-grained rPPG signal recovery under challenging conditions, validating the effectiveness of joint modeling of video and STMap representations. The codes will be released.

AI.tldr: PhysNeXt fuses raw video frames and STMap for rPPG via a dual-input network with spatio-temporal difference unit, cross-modal interaction, and structured attention to improve robustness and signal fidelity.
AI.motivation: rPPG extraction is vulnerable to motion and lighting; end-to-end video models preserve details but add noise, while STMap representations reduce data but may lose high-frequency cues. A joint approach leverages the strengths of both representations to improve pulse signal extraction.
AI.method: A dual-input deep learning framework that processes video frames and STMap representations concurrently, incorporating a spatio-temporal difference modeling unit, a cross-modal interaction module, and a structured attention-based decoder to enhance pulse signal recovery.
AI.result: Experimental results show PhysNeXt achieves more stable and fine-grained rPPG signal recovery under challenging conditions, validating the effectiveness of joint modeling of video and STMap representations.
AI.conclusion: Joint modeling of raw video frames and STMap representations with the proposed modules improves robustness and signal fidelity in rPPG, demonstrating the benefits of multi-representation fusion for physiological signal extraction.

title: Quantifying Gate Contribution in Quantum Feature Maps for Scalable Circuit Optimization
summary: Quantum machine learning offers promising advantages for classification tasks, but noise, decoherence, and connectivity constraints in current devices continue to limit the efficient execution of feature map-based circuits. Gate Assessment and Threshold Evaluation (GATE) is presented as a circuit optimization methodology that reduces quantum feature maps using a novel gate significance index. This index quantifies the relevance of each gate by combining fidelity, entanglement, and sensitivity. It is formulated for both simulator/emulator environments, where quantum states are accessible, and for real hardware, where these quantities are estimated from measurement results and auxiliary circuits. The approach iteratively scans a threshold range, eliminates low-contribution gates, generates optimized quantum machine learning models, and ranks them based on accuracy, runtime, and a balanced performance criterion before final testing. The methodology is evaluated on real-world classification datasets using two representative quantum machine learning models, PegasosQSVM and Quantum Neural Network, in three execution scenarios: noise-free simulation, noisy emulation derived from an IBM backend, and real IBM quantum hardware. The structural impact of gate removal in feature maps is examined, compatibility with noise-mitigation techniques is studied, and the scalability of index computation is evaluated using approaches based on density matrices, matrix product states, tensor networks, and real-world devices. The results show consistent reductions in circuit size and runtime and, in many cases, preserved or improved predictive accuracy, with the best trade-offs typically occurring at intermediate thresholds rather than in the baseline circuits or in those compressed more aggressively.

AI.tldr: Gate Assessment and Threshold Evaluation (GATE) provides a gate-significance based pruning method for quantum feature maps, reducing circuit depth and runtime while maintaining or improving accuracy across simulators and real hardware.
AI.motivation: Quantum machine learning feature-map circuits are hindered by noise, decoherence, and connectivity constraints; a principled gate-pruning approach is needed to improve practicality without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: Introduce a gate significance index that combines fidelity, entanglement, and sensitivity; applicable to both simulator-accessible states and hardware-estimated quantities; iteratively sweep a threshold to remove low-contribution gates; generate and rank multiple optimized models by accuracy, runtime, and a balanced performance criterion; evaluate on PegasosQSVM and Quantum Neural Network across: noise-free simulation, noisy IBM-emulated backend, and real IBM hardware; analyze structural impact, noise-mitigation compatibility, and scalability using density matrices, matrix product states, tensor networks, and real devices.
AI.result: Applied across three execution scenarios, the approach yields consistent reductions in circuit size and runtime, with predictive accuracy preserved or improved in many cases; intermediate threshold levels offer the best trade-offs between compression and performance.
AI.conclusion: GATE enables practical, scalable optimization of quantum feature maps, remaining compatible with noise-mitigation techniques and hardware constraints, and delivering favorable accuracy-runtime trade-offs while simplifying circuit structures.

title: Enhancing Alignment for Unified Multimodal Models via Semantically-Grounded Supervision
summary: Unified Multimodal Models (UMMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm that integrates multimodal understanding and generation within a unified modeling framework. However, current generative training paradigms suffer from inherent limitations. We present Semantically-Grounded Supervision (SeGroS), a fine-tuning framework designed to resolve the granularity mismatch and supervisory redundancy in UMMs. At its core, we propose a novel visual grounding map to construct two complementary supervision signals.
