TATAR combines three components: fast--slow task-specific reasoning construction that pairs IQA with concise perceptual rationales and IAA with deliberative aesthetic narratives; two-stage SFT+GRPO learning that establishes task-aware behavioral priors before reward-driven refinement; and asymmetric rewards that apply Gaussian score shaping for IQA and Thurstone-style completion ranking for IAA. Extensive experiments across eight benchmarks demonstrate that TATAR consistently outperforms prior unified baselines on both tasks under in-domain and cross-domain settings, remains competitive with task-specific specialized models, and yields more stable training dynamics for aesthetic assessment. Our results establish task-conditioned post-training as a principled paradigm for unified perceptual scoring. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yinwen2019/TATAR.

AI.tldr: TATAR is a task-aware post-training framework that unifies IQA and IAA by using task-specific reasoning and asymmetric rewards, achieving strong cross-task performance and stable training.
AI.motivation: IQA relies on low-level perceptual cues while IAA requires high-level semantic judgment; a single, task-agnostic reasoning/reward scheme misaligns with each task, hindering performance and training stability.
AI.method: Unified visual-language backbone with (i) fast-slow task-specific reasoning (IQA: concise perceptual rationales; IAA: deliberative aesthetic narratives), (ii) two-stage SFT+GRPO for task-aware priors and reward refinement, (iii) asymmetric rewards (Gaussian shaping for IQA; Thurstone-style completion ranking for IAA).
AI.result: Across eight benchmarks, TATAR outperforms prior unified baselines on both IQA and IAA (in-domain and cross-domain), remains competitive with task-specific models, and yields more stable training for aesthetics.
AI.conclusion: Task-conditioned post-training is a principled paradigm for unified perceptual scoring, enabling effective coupling of low- and high-level judgments in a single model.

title: Fine-tuning Timeseries Predictors Using Reinforcement Learning
summary: This chapter presents three major reinforcement learning algorithms used for fine-tuning financial forecasters. We propose a clear implementation plan for backpropagating the loss of a reinforcement learning task to a model trained using supervised learning, and compare the performance before and after the fine-tuning. We find an increase in performance after fine-tuning, and transfer learning properties to the models, indicating the benefits of fine-tuning. We also highlight the tuning process and empirical results for future implementation by practitioners.

AI.tldr: Three RL algorithms for fine-tuning financial forecasters; presents a plan to backpropagate RL loss into a supervised-trained model; fine-tuning improves performance with transfer learning effects; includes practical tuning guidance for practitioners.
AI.motivation: Enhance financial forecasting by integrating reinforcement learning fine-tuning with supervised models and to assess transferability and practicality.
AI.method: Identify three major reinforcement learning algorithms for fine-tuning; propose an explicit backpropagation plan from the RL objective to a backbone supervised model; compare performance before and after fine-tuning; analyze transfer-learning behavior; report tuning process and empirical results for practitioners.
AI.result: Fine-tuning yields increased performance; observed transfer-learning properties; empirical results provided to guide practitioners.
AI.conclusion: RL-based fine-tuning of financial forecasters is beneficial, with practical implications and actionable guidance for practitioners.

title: Decoupled Sensitivity-Consistency Learning for Weakly Supervised Video Anomaly Detection
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, 4 tables. Accepted by ICME 2026
summary: Recent weakly supervised video anomaly detection methods have achieved significant advances by employing unified frameworks for joint optimization. However, this paradigm is limited by a fundamental sensitivity-stability trade-off, as the conflicting objectives for detecting transient and sustained anomalies lead to either fragmented predictions or over-smoothed responses. To address this limitation, we propose DeSC, a novel Decoupled Sensitivity-Consistency framework that trains two specialized streams using distinct optimization strategies. The temporal sensitivity stream adopts an aggressive optimization strategy to capture high-frequency abrupt changes, whereas the semantic consistency stream applies robust constraints to maintain long-term coherence and reduce noise. Their complementary strengths are fused through a collaborative inference mechanism that reduces individual biases and produces balanced predictions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeSC establishes new state-of-the-art performance by achieving 89.37% AUC on UCF-Crime (+1.29%) and 87.18% AP on XD-Violence (+2.22%). Code is available at https://github.com/imzht/DeSC.

AI.tldr: Decouples sensitivity and consistency in weakly supervised video anomaly detection via two specialized streams—one for temporal sensitivity and one for semantic consistency—fused by collaborative inference to achieve state-of-the-art results on UCF-Crime and XD-Violence.
AI.motivation: Unified weakly supervised frameworks face a fundamental sensitivity-stability trade-off: detecting transient, high-frequency anomalies conflicts with maintaining long-term coherence for sustained events, leading to fragmented or over-smoothed predictions.
AI.method: Introduce DeSC with two dedicated streams: (1) a temporal sensitivity stream optimized aggressively to capture high-frequency changes, and (2) a semantic consistency stream enforcing robust long-term coherence. A collaborative inference mechanism fuses their outputs to mitigate individual biases.
AI.result: achieves state-of-the-art performance: 89.37% AUC on UCF-Crime (+1.29% from prior), and 87.18% AP on XD-Violence (+2.22%), with code released at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Decoupled optimization for sensitivity and consistency, combined via collaborative inference, effectively balances transient and sustained anomaly detection, yielding superior performance.

title: Model-Driven Learning-Based Physical Layer Authentication for Mobile Wi-Fi Devices
summary: The rise of wireless technologies has made the Internet of Things (IoT) ubiquitous, but the broadcast nature of wireless communications exposes IoT to authentication risks. Physical layer authentication (PLA) offers a promising solution by leveraging unique characteristics of wireless channels. As a common approach in PLA, hypothesis testing yields a theoretically optimal Neyman-Pearson (NP) detector, but its reliance on channel statistics limits its practicality in real-world scenarios. In contrast, deep learning-based PLA approaches are practical but tend to be not optimal. To address these challenges, we proposed a learning-based PLA scheme driven by hypothesis testing and conducted extensive simulations and experimental evaluations using Wi-Fi. Specifically, we incorporated conditional statistical models into the hypothesis testing framework to derive a theoretically optimal NP detector. Building on this, we developed LiteNP-Net, a lightweight neural network driven by the NP detector. Simulation results demonstrated that LiteNP-Net could approach the performance of the NP detector even without prior knowledge of the channel statistics. To further assess its effectiveness in practical environments, we deployed an experimental testbed using Wi-Fi IoT development kits in various real-world scenarios. Experimental results demonstrated that the LiteNP-Net outperformed the conventional correlation-based method as well as state-of-the-art Siamese-based methods.

AI.tldr: A learning-based physical layer authentication (PLA) approach, LiteNP-Net, combines NP detector principles with a lightweight neural network to approach optimal NP performance without requiring channel statistics, validated by simulations and a real Wi-Fi IoT testbed.
AI.motivation: Wireless IoT app creates authentication risks due to broadcast nature; NP detector is theoretically optimal but needs channel statistics; deep learning methods are practical but not optimal; a bridge between theory and practice is needed.
AI.method: Introduce conditional statistical models into the hypothesis-testing framework to derive a theoretically optimal NP detector; develop LiteNP-Net, a lightweight NN guided by the NP detector to approximate optimal performance without explicit channel statistics.
AI.result: Simulations show LiteNP-Net closely approaches NP detector performance even without prior channel statistics; Wi-Fi-based experimental testbed demonstrates LiteNP-Net outperforms conventional correlation-based methods and state-of-the-art Siamese-based PLA methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach achieves near-optimal PLA performance without requiring channel statistics and demonstrates practical effectiveness in real-world Wi-Fi IoT settings, surpassing existing methods.

title: The $\mathbf{Y}$-Combinator for LLMs: Solving Long-Context Rot with $λ$-Calculus
summary:
LLMs are increasingly used as general-purpose reasoners, but long inputs remain bottlenecked by a fixed context window. Recursive Language Models (RLMs) address this by externalising the prompt and recursively solving subproblems. Yet existing RLMs depend on an open-ended read-eval-print loop (REPL) in which the model generates arbitrary control code, making execution difficult to verify, predict, and analyse.
  We introduce $λ$-RLM, a framework for long-context reasoning that replaces free-form recursive code generation with a typed functional runtime grounded in $λ$-calculus. It executes a compact library of pre-verified combinators and uses neural inference only on bounded leaf subproblems, turning recursive reasoning into a structured functional program with explicit control flow. We show that $λ$-RLM admits formal guarantees absent from standard RLMs, including termination, closed-form cost bounds, controlled accuracy scaling with recursion depth, and an optimal partition rule under a simple cost model. Empirically, across four long-context reasoning tasks and nine base models, $λ$-RLM outperforms standard RLM in 29 of 36 model-task comparisons, improves average accuracy by up to +21.9 points across model tiers, and reduces latency by up to 4.1x. These results show that typed symbolic control yields a more reliable and efficient foundation for long-context reasoning than open-ended recursive code generation. The complete implementation of $λ$-RLM, is open-sourced for the community at: https://github.com/lambda-calculus-LLM/lambda-RLM.

AI.tldr: Lambda-RLM is a typed, lambda-calculus–based framework for long-context reasoning that replaces free-form recursive code with a pre-verified combinator library, delivering formal guarantees (termination, cost bounds, predictable accuracy) and practical gains (higher accuracy, lower latency) across multiple tasks and models.
AI.motivation: Long-context reasoning in LLMs is bottlenecked by fixed context windows. Recursive Language Models (RLMs) rely on open-ended, potentially opaque code generation, which hampers verification, predictability, and analysis. A structured, typed, symbolic control mechanism can provide verifiable, efficient long-context reasoning.
AI.method: Introduce λ-RLM: a typed functional runtime grounded in λ-calculus that executes a compact library of pre-verified combinators. Neural inference is confined to bounded leaf subproblems, turning recursive reasoning into a structured, explicit-control-flow program. The framework offers formal guarantees (termination, closed-form cost bounds, controllable accuracy with recursion depth, optimal partition under a simple cost model) and includes an open-source implementation.
AI.result: Empirically, across four long-context reasoning tasks and nine base models, λ-RLM outperforms standard RLM in 29 of 36 model-task comparisons, improves average accuracy by up to 21.9 points, and reduces latency by up to 4.1×.
AI.conclusion: Typed symbolic control provides a more reliable and efficient foundation for long-context reasoning than open-ended recursive code generation; the approach yields formal guarantees and practical gains, and the implementation is open-sourced.

title: From Plausibility to Verifiability: Risk-Controlled Generative OCR for Vision-Language Models
summary: Modern vision-language models (VLMs) can act as generative OCR engines, yet open-ended decoding can expose rare but consequential failures. We identify a core deployment misalignment in generative OCR. Autoregressive decoding favors semantic plausibility, whereas OCR requires outputs that are visually grounded and geometrically verifiable. This mismatch produces severe errors, especially over-generation and unsupported substitutions, creating deployment risk even when benchmark accuracy remains high. We therefore formulate frozen VLM OCR as a selective accept/abstain problem and propose a model-agnostic Geometric Risk Controller. The controller probes multiple structured views of the same input, applies lightweight structural screening, and accepts a transcription only when cross-view consensus and stability satisfy predefined criteria, yielding a small family of operating points. Experiments on frozen VLM backbones and standard OCR benchmarks show consistent reductions in extreme-error risk and catastrophic over-generation at predictable coverage costs. Reliable deployment of generative OCR with frozen VLMs benefits from explicit system-level risk control rather than unconstrained generation.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic Geometric Risk Controller reduces extreme OCR errors by gating generative outputs from frozen Vision-Language Models through multi-view structural screening and cross-view consensus, accepting only transcription when stability criteria are met; at the cost of some coverage but with lower risk.
AI.motivation: Generative OCR with frozen VLMs tends to overgenerate or substitute outputs due to semantic-driven decoding that ignores visual/geometric constraints. A system-level guard is needed to align generation with visual grounding and verifiability.
AI.method: Apply multiple structured views of the same input to the frozen VLM OCR, perform lightweight structural screening on each view, and accept a transcription only when cross-view consensus and stability criteria are satisfied; operate in a family of risk-controlled operating points.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent reductions in extreme-error risk and catastrophic over-generation across frozen backbones and standard OCR benchmarks, with predictable trade-offs in coverage.
AI.conclusion: Explicit system-level risk control is beneficial for reliable deployment of generative OCR with frozen VLMs, as it mitigates deployment risks that paraphrase benchmark accuracy.

title: A Super Fast K-means for Indexing Vector Embeddings
summary: We present SuperKMeans: a k-means variant designed for clustering collections of high-dimensional vector embeddings. SuperKMeans' clustering is up to 7x faster than FAISS and Scikit-Learn on modern CPUs and up to 4x faster than cuVS on GPUs (Figure 1), while maintaining the quality of the resulting centroids for vector similarity search tasks. SuperKMeans acceleration comes from reducing data-access and compute overhead by reliably and efficiently pruning dimensions that are not needed to assign a vector to a centroid. Furthermore, we present Early Termination by Recall, a novel mechanism that early-terminates k-means when the quality of the centroids for retrieval tasks stops improving across iterations. In practice, this further reduces runtimes without compromising retrieval quality. We open-source our implementation at https://github.com/cwida/SuperKMeans

AI.tldr: SuperKMeans: a fast k-means variant for high-dimensional embeddings that prunes dimensions during assignment and uses Early Termination by Recall; achieves notable speedups with maintained retrieval quality; open-source.
AI.motivation: Clustering large collections of high-dimensional vector embeddings efficiently is computationally intensive; existing libraries (FAISS, Scikit-Learn, cuVS) may be slow on modern hardware while preserving quality.
AI.method: Reduce data-access and compute by pruning non-essential dimensions during vector-centroid assignment; introduce Early Termination by Recall to stop iterations when retrieval quality stops improving.
AI.result: Reported speedups up to 7x on CPUs vs FAISS/Scikit-Learn and up to 4x on GPUs vs cuVS; maintains centroid quality for vector similarity search; ETR further reduces runtimes without quality loss.
AI.conclusion: Open-source implementation at https://github.com/cwida/SuperKMeans; demonstrates speed-quality trade-off improvement for high-dimensional embedding clustering.

title: Var-JEPA: A Variational Formulation of the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture -- Bridging Predictive and Generative Self-Supervised Learning
summary: The Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) is often seen as a non-generative alternative to likelihood-based self-supervised learning, emphasizing prediction in representation space rather than reconstruction in observation space. We argue that the resulting separation from probabilistic generative modeling is largely rhetorical rather than structural: the canonical JEPA design, coupled encoders with a context-to-target predictor, mirrors the variational posteriors and learned conditional priors obtained when variational inference is applied to a particular class of coupled latent-variable models, and standard JEPA can be viewed as a deterministic specialization in which regularization is imposed via architectural and training heuristics rather than an explicit likelihood. Building on this view, we derive the Variational JEPA (Var-JEPA), which makes the latent generative structure explicit by optimizing a single Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO). This yields meaningful representations without ad-hoc anti-collapse regularizers and allows principled uncertainty quantification in the latent space. We instantiate the framework for tabular data (Var-T-JEPA) and achieve strong representation learning and downstream performance, consistently improving over T-JEPA while remaining competitive with strong raw-feature baselines.

AI.tldr: Recasts JEPA as a variational method and derives Var-JEPA: an ELBO-based framework that provides explicit latent generative structure and uncertainty, instantiated for tabular data as Var-T-JEPA; shows improved representations and competitive downstream performance.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between JEPA's representation-prediction view and probabilistic generative modeling; enable principled uncertainty and a single objective (ELBO) instead of heuristic anti-collapse regularizers.
AI.method: Formulate JEPA as a latent-variable model; derive ELBO-based objective (Variational JEPA); derive Var-T-JEPA for tabular data; use coupled encoders with context-to-target predictor corresponding to variational posteriors and priors; train to optimize ELBO; compare to T-JEPA and baselines.
AI.result: Empirical results show strong representation learning and downstream performance; Var-T-JEPA outperforms T-JEPA and is competitive with strong raw-feature baselines on tabular data.
AI.conclusion: A probabilistic reinterpretation of JEPA yields a unified, principled framework for self-supervised learning with explicit uncertainty in latent space; Var-JEPA can deliver improved representations without ad-hoc regularizers and extends to tabular domains (Var-T-JEPA).

title: Controllable Text-to-Motion Generation via Modular Body-Part Phase Control
summary: Text-to-motion (T2M) generation is becoming a practical tool for animation and interactive avatars. However, modifying specific body parts while maintaining overall motion coherence remains challenging. Existing methods typically rely on cumbersome, high-dimensional joint constraints (e.g., trajectories), which hinder user-friendly, iterative refinement. To address this, we propose Modular Body-Part Phase Control, a plug-and-play framework enabling structured, localized editing via a compact, scalar-based phase interface. By modeling body-part latent motion channels as sinusoidal phase signals characterized by amplitude, frequency, phase shift, and offset, we extract interpretable codes that capture part-specific dynamics. A modular Phase ControlNet branch then injects this signal via residual feature modulation, seamlessly decoupling control from the generative backbone. Experiments on both diffusion- and flow-based models demonstrate that our approach provides predictable and fine-grained control over motion magnitude, speed, and timing. It preserves global motion coherence and offers a practical paradigm for controllable T2M generation. Project page: https://jixiii.github.io/bp-phase-project-page/

AI.tldr: Proposes Modular Body-Part Phase Control for text-to-motion editing using scalar body-part phase signals (amplitude, frequency, phase, offset) and a Phase ControlNet to inject these signals as residual feature modulation, enabling localized, user-friendly control of motion magnitude, speed, and timing while preserving global coherence across diffusion- and flow-based models.
AI.motivation: Existing T2M editing methods rely on high-dimensional joint constraints that are cumbersome for iterative, localized edits. There is a need for a plug-and-play, interpretable, and decoupled control mechanism that can affect specific body parts without breaking overall motion coherence.
AI.method: Represent body-part latent motion as sinusoidal phase signals characterized by amplitude, frequency, phase shift, and offset. Extract part-specific latent codes and feed them into a modular Phase ControlNet branch that modulates features residually, thereby injecting part-level control into the generative backbone without altering it directly. Demonstrated with diffusion- and flow-based models.
AI.result: The framework yields predictable, fine-grained control over motion magnitude, speed, and timing for edited parts, while preserving global motion coherence. It is plug-and-play and compatible with multiple generative backbones, validated on diffusion- and flow-based T2M models; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: Modular Body-Part Phase Control offers a practical, interpretable, and decoupled approach to controllable T2M generation, enabling targeted edits with reliable coherence and broad compatibility across backbones.

title: AgenticRS-EnsNAS: Ensemble-Decoupled Self-Evolving Architecture Search
summary: Neural Architecture Search (NAS) deployment in industrial production systems faces a fundamental validation bottleneck: verifying a single candidate architecture pi requires evaluating the deployed ensemble of M models, incurring prohibitive O(M) computational cost per candidate. This cost barrier severely limits architecture iteration frequency in real-world applications where ensembles (M=50-200) are standard for robustness. This work introduces Ensemble-Decoupled Architecture Search, a framework that leverages ensemble theory to predict system-level performance from single-learner evaluation. We establish the Ensemble-Decoupled Theory with a sufficient condition for monotonic ensemble improvement under homogeneity assumptions: a candidate architecture pi yields lower ensemble error than the current baseline if rho(pi) < rho(pi_old) - (M / (M - 1)) * (Delta E(pi) / sigma^2(pi)), where Delta E, rho, and sigma^2 are estimable from lightweight dual-learner training. This decouples architecture search from full ensemble training, reducing per-candidate search cost from O(M) to O(1) while maintaining O(M) deployment cost only for validated winners. We unify solution strategies across pipeline continuity: (1) closed-form optimization for tractable continuous pi (exemplified by feature bagging in CTR prediction), (2) constrained differentiable optimization for intractable continuous pi, and (3) LLM-driven search with iterative monotonic acceptance for discrete pi. The framework reveals two orthogonal improvement mechanisms -- base diversity gain and accuracy gain -- providing actionable design principles for industrial-scale NAS. All theoretical derivations are rigorous with detailed proofs deferred to the appendix. Comprehensive empirical validation will be included in the journal extension of this work.

AI.tldr: Ensemble-Decoupled Architecture Search (EDAS) predicts system-level ensemble performance from single-learner evaluations to cut NAS search cost from O(M) to O(1) per candidate, while incurring O(M) deployment cost only for validated winners.
AI.motivation: Industrial NAS deployment with large ensembles makes per-candidate evaluation prohibitive. The paper proposes decoupling architecture search from full ensemble training to enable rapid iteration without sacrificing deployment robustness.
AI.method: Develop Ensemble-Decoupled Theory with a sufficient condition for monotonic ensemble improvement under homogeneity: rho(pi) < rho(pi_old) - (M/(M-1)) * (Delta E(pi)/sigma^2(pi)). Estimable from lightweight dual-learner training (Delta E, rho, sigma^2). Provide three solution strategies: (1) closed-form optimization for tractable continuous pi (e.g., feature bagging in CTR); (2) constrained differentiable optimization for intractable continuous pi; (3) LLM-driven search with iterative monotonic acceptance for discrete pi. Identify two orthogonal improvement mechanisms: base diversity gain and accuracy gain.
AI.result: Decouples search from full ensemble training, reducing per-candidate search cost from O(M) to O(1) while retaining O(M) deployment cost for validated winners. Lays out a unified solution space and actionable design principles for industrial NAS; rigorous derivations with proofs relegated to the appendix; empirical validation to appear in journal extension.
AI.conclusion: EDAS offers a practical, theory-backed framework to accelerate industrial NAS by predicting ensemble gains from lightweight metrics, enabling efficient exploration across continuous and discrete architecture spaces while preserving necessary deployment fidelity.

title: Chain-of-Adaptation: Surgical Vision-Language Adaptation with Reinforcement Learning
summary: Conventional fine-tuning on domain-specific datasets can inadvertently alter a model's pretrained multimodal priors, leading to reduced generalization. To address this, we propose Chain-of-Adaptation (CoA), an adaptation framework designed to integrate domain knowledge while maintaining the model's inherent reasoning and perceptual capabilities. CoA introduces a structured reasoning format that enhances domain alignment without sacrificing general multimodal competence by reinforcement learning. Experiments on standard surgical benchmarks, under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, demonstrate that CoA achieves higher accuracy, stronger generalization, and more stable behavior than supervised fine-tuning. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm that CoA effectively preserves the model's core visual-language abilities, providing a reliable pathway for domain specialization in VLMs.

AI.tldr: CoA preserves pretrained multimodal priors while enabling domain-specific adaptation via structured reasoning and reinforcement learning, improving surgical-domain performance and generalization.
AI.motivation: Standard fine-tuning can erode general multimodal priors, reducing generalization; a method is needed to inject domain knowledge without sacrificing core abilities.
AI.method: Chain-of-Adaptation introduces a structured reasoning format and uses reinforcement learning to align domain knowledge with the model's inherent reasoning and perceptual capabilities, preserving general multimodal competence.
AI.result: Outperforms supervised fine-tuning on surgical benchmarks in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings, with higher accuracy, stronger generalization, and more stable behavior; ablations show preservation of core visual-language abilities.
AI.conclusion: CoA provides a robust pathway for domain specialization in vision-language models by balancing domain adaptation with preservation of pretrained priors.

title: Evaluating Vision Foundation Models for Pixel and Object Classification in Microscopy
summary: Deep learning underlies most modern approaches and tools in computer vision, including biomedical imaging. However, for interactive semantic segmentation (often called pixel classification in this context) and interactive object-level classification (object classification), feature-based shallow learning remains widely used. This is due to the diversity of data in this domain, the lack of large pretraining datasets, and the need for computational and label efficiency. In contrast, state-of-the-art tools for many other vision tasks in microscopy - most notably cellular instance segmentation - already rely on deep learning and have recently benefited substantially from vision foundation models (VFMs), particularly SAM. Here, we investigate whether VFMs can also improve pixel and object classification compared to current approaches. To this end, we evaluate several VFMs, including general-purpose models (SAM, SAM2, DINOv3) and domain-specific ones ($μ$SAM, PathoSAM), in combination with shallow learning and attentive probing on five diverse and challenging datasets. Our results demonstrate consistent improvements over hand-crafted features and provide a clear pathway toward practical improvements. Furthermore, our study establishes a benchmark for VFMs in microscopy and informs future developments in this area.

AI.tldr: VFMs (SAM, SAM2, DINOv3, μSAM, PathoSAM) combined with shallow learning and attentive probing improve pixel- and object-classification in microscopy; evaluated on five datasets; results are consistently better than handcrafted features; establishes a benchmarking framework and practical deployment pathway.
AI.motivation: Interactive semantic segmentation and object-level classification in biomedical imaging rely on shallow features due to data diversity, limited pretraining data, and efficiency concerns. VFMs offer a path to improve performance in microscopy for these tasks, enabling better generalization and practical use.
AI.method: Evaluate multiple vision foundation models (general-purpose: SAM, SAM2, DINOv3; domain-specific: μSAM, PathoSAM) in combination with shallow learning and attentive probing across five diverse microscopy datasets; compare against traditional handcrafted features; assess improvements and establish a dataset benchmark.
AI.result: VFMs provide consistent improvements over hand-crafted features when paired with shallow learning and attentive probing; improvements are observed across datasets, supporting practical applicability.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a benchmark for evaluating VFMs in microscopy and highlights their potential to enhance pixel- and object-level classification, guiding future research and development in this domain.

title: ODySSeI: An Open-Source End-to-End Framework for Automated Detection, Segmentation, and Severity Estimation of Lesions in Invasive Coronary Angiography Images
summary: Invasive Coronary Angiography (ICA) is the clinical gold standard for the assessment of coronary artery disease. However, its interpretation remains subjective and prone to intra- and inter-operator variability. In this work, we introduce ODySSeI: an Open-source end-to-end framework for automated Detection, Segmentation, and Severity estimation of lesions in ICA images. ODySSeI integrates deep learning-based lesion detection and lesion segmentation models trained using a novel Pyramidal Augmentation Scheme (PAS) to enhance robustness and real-time performance across diverse patient cohorts (2149 patients from Europe, North America, and Asia). Furthermore, we propose a quantitative coronary angiography-free Lesion Severity Estimation (LSE) technique that directly computes the Minimum Lumen Diameter (MLD) and diameter stenosis from the predicted lesion geometry. Extensive evaluation on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution clinical datasets demonstrates ODySSeI's strong generalizability. Our PAS yields large performance gains in highly complex tasks as compared to relatively simpler ones, notably, a 2.5-fold increase in lesion detection performance versus a 1-3\% increase in lesion segmentation performance over their respective baselines. Our LSE technique achieves high accuracy, with predicted MLD values differing by only $\pm$ 2-3 pixels from the corresponding ground truths. On average, ODySSeI processes a raw ICA image within only a few seconds on a CPU and in a fraction of a second on a GPU and is available as a plug-and-play web interface at swisscardia.epfl.ch. Overall, this work establishes ODySSeI as a comprehensive and open-source framework which supports automated, reproducible, and scalable ICA analysis for real-time clinical decision-making.

AI.tldr: Open-source end-to-end framework (ODySSeI) for automated Detection, Segmentation, and Severity Estimation of lesions in invasive coronary angiography (ICA) images, using a novel Pyramidal Augmentation Scheme (PAS); achieves real-time performance and strong generalizability across diverse cohorts; LSE computes MLD/diameter stenosis without angiography, with high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reduce subjectivity and inter-/intra-operator variability in ICA interpretation and provide a robust, reproducible, and real-time tool for clinical decision-making; address the lack of open-source end-to-end pipelines for ICA analysis.
AI.method: Deep learning-based lesion detection and segmentation using PAS to enhance robustness; trained on 2149 patients from Europe, North America, and Asia. Introduces LSE that directly computes Minimum Lumen Diameter (MLD) and diameter stenosis from lesion geometry without relying on angiography. Evaluated on in-distribution and out-of-distribution datasets; real-time performance on CPU/GPU; available as a plug-and-play web interface.
AI.result: 2.5-fold improvement in lesion detection performance over baselines; lesion segmentation gains of 1–3% over baselines; LSE MLD predictions within ±2–3 pixels of ground truth; runtime: a few seconds per image on CPU and fractions of a second on GPU; demonstrated strong generalizability across diverse cohorts.
AI.conclusion: ODySSeI is a comprehensive, open-source framework enabling automated, reproducible, and scalable ICA analysis for real-time clinical decision-making, with a user-friendly web interface and potential to standardize ICA interpretation across centers.

title: Continual Learning as Shared-Manifold Continuation Under Compatible Shift
summary: Continual learning methods usually preserve old behavior by regularizing parameters, matching old outputs, or replaying previous examples. These strategies can reduce forgetting, but they do not directly specify how the latent representation should evolve. We study a narrower geometric alternative for the regime where old and new data should remain on the same latent support: continual learning as continuation of a shared manifold. We instantiate this view within Support-Preserving Manifold Assimilation (SPMA) and evaluate a geometry-preserving variant, SPMA-OG, that combines sparse replay, output distillation, relational geometry preservation, local smoothing, and chart-assignment regularization on old anchors. On representative compatible-shift CIFAR10 and Tiny-ImageNet runs, SPMA-OG improves over sparse replay baselines in old-task retention and representation-preservation metrics while remaining competitive on new-task accuracy. On a controlled synthetic atlas-manifold benchmark, it achieves near-perfect anchor-geometry preservation while also improving new-task accuracy over replay. These results provide evidence that geometry-aware anchor regularization is a useful inductive bias when continual learning should preserve a shared latent support rather than create a new one.

AI.tldr: Proposes SPMA-OG, a geometry-preserving continual learning method that maintains a shared latent manifold via anchor-based regularization and limited replay; it improves old-task retention and representation preservation, with competitive new-task accuracy, and achieves near-perfect anchor-geometry preservation on a synthetic atlas-manifold benchmark.
AI.motivation: To address catastrophic forgetting not just at the parameter or output level but at the latent representation geometry, by enforcing that old and new data lie on the same latent support (shared manifold).
AI.method: Introduce SPMA-OG (geometry-preserving variant of Support-Preserving Manifold Assimilation) which combines sparse replay, output distillation, relational geometry preservation, local smoothing, and chart-assignment regularization on old anchors to preserve anchor geometry while learning new tasks; evaluate on compatible-shift CIFAR10 and Tiny-ImageNet and a synthetic atlas-manifold benchmark.
AI.result: SPMA-OG outperforms sparse replay baselines in old-task retention and representation-preservation metrics on CIFAR10/Tiny-ImageNet while remaining competitive on new-task accuracy; on the synthetic atlas-manifold benchmark it achieves near-perfect anchor-geometry preservation and also improves new-task accuracy over replay.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware anchor regularization is a useful inductive bias for continual learning when preserving a shared latent support rather than creating a new one.

title: Adaptive Greedy Frame Selection for Long Video Understanding
summary: Large vision--language models (VLMs) are increasingly applied to long-video question answering, yet inference is often bottlenecked by the number of input frames and resulting visual tokens. Naive sparse sampling can miss decisive moments, while purely relevance-driven selection frequently collapses onto near-duplicate frames and sacrifices coverage of temporally distant evidence. We propose a question-adaptive greedy frame selection method that jointly optimizes query relevance and semantic representativeness under a fixed frame budget. Our approach constructs a 1~FPS candidate pool (capped at 1000) with exact timestamp alignment, embeds candidates in two complementary spaces (SigLIP for question relevance and DINOv2 for semantic similarity), and selects frames by greedily maximizing a weighted sum of a modular relevance term and a facility-location coverage term. This objective is normalized, monotone, and submodular, yielding a standard (1-1/e) greedy approximation guarantee. To account for question-dependent trade-offs between relevance and coverage, we introduce four preset strategies and a lightweight text-only question-type classifier that routes each query to its best-performing preset. Experiments on MLVU show consistent accuracy gains over uniform sampling and a strong recent baseline across frame budgets, with the largest improvements under tight budgets.

AI.tldr: Question-adaptive greedy frame selection for long-video VQA that balances frame relevance and semantic coverage under a fixed frame budget, using a 1 FPS candidate pool, dual embeddings (SigLIP for relevance, DINOv2 for similarity), and a submodular objective with a (1-1/e) approximation guarantee; includes four presets and a lightweight question-type classifier.
AI.motivation: Inference bottlenecks in long-video VQA due to大量 frames and tokens; naive sampling misses decisive moments and relevance-only selection ignores temporally distant evidence; need a method that jointly optimizes relevance and representativeness under a fixed budget.
AI.method: Construct a 1 FPS candidate pool (up to 1000) with exact timestamps; embed candidates in SigLIP (question relevance) and DINOv2 (semantic similarity); greedily maximize a weighted sum of a modular relevance term and a facility-location coverage term; objective is normalized, monotone, and submodular, enabling a (1-1/e) greedy guarantee; introduce four presets and a lightweight text-only classifier to route queries to the best preset.
AI.result: Experiments on the MLVU dataset show consistent accuracy gains over uniform sampling and a strong recent baseline across frame budgets, with the largest gains under tight budgets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method provides a principled, scalable approach to frame selection for long-video VQA with provable guarantees and demonstrated empirical gains; preset strategies and a lightweight classifier enable adaptation to question types.

title: HUGE-Bench: A Benchmark for High-Level UAV Vision-Language-Action Tasks
summary: Existing UAV vision-language navigation (VLN) benchmarks have enabled language-guided flight, but they largely focus on long, step-wise route descriptions with goal-centric evaluation, making them less diagnostic for real operations where brief, high-level commands must be grounded into safe multi-stage behaviors. We present HUGE-Bench, a benchmark for High-Level UAV Vision-Language-Action (HL-VLA) tasks that tests whether an agent can interpret concise language and execute complex, process-oriented trajectories with safety awareness. HUGE-Bench comprises 4 real-world digital twin scenes, 8 high-level tasks, and 2.56M meters of trajectories, and is built on an aligned 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-Mesh representation that combines photorealistic rendering with collision-capable geometry for scalable generation and collision-aware evaluation. We introduce process-oriented and collision-aware metrics to assess process fidelity, terminal accuracy, and safety. Experiments on representative state-of-the-art VLA models reveal significant gaps in high-level semantic completion and safe execution, highlighting HUGE-Bench as a diagnostic testbed for high-level UAV autonomy.

AI.tldr: Huge-Bench is a diagnostic benchmark for high-level UAV vision-language-action (HL-VLA) tasks that tests concise language grounding into multi-stage, safety-aware trajectories. It features 4 real-world digital twin scenes, 8 tasks, and 2.56M meters of trajectories, built on a 3D Gaussian Splatting-Mesh representation with collision-aware evaluation and new process-oriented and safety metrics.
AI.motivation: Current UAV VLN benchmarks emphasize long, step-by-step route descriptions and goal-centric evaluation, which poorly diagnose real-world performance where operators rely on brief, high-level commands requiring safe, multi-stage behavior.
AI.method: Construct HUGE-Bench with 4 real-world digital-twin scenes, 8 high-level tasks, and 2.56M meters of trajectories. Represent scenes with a 3D Gaussian Splatting–Mesh (3DGS-Mesh) that supports photorealistic rendering and collision-aware geometry. Define process-oriented and collision-aware metrics to evaluate process fidelity, terminal accuracy, and safety. Evaluate representative state-of-the-art VLA models on HL-VLA tasks to assess gaps.
AI.result: Experiments reveal significant gaps in high-level semantic completion and safe execution for current VLA models, indicating that they struggle to interpret concise commands and execute safe, multi-stage trajectories. HUGE-Bench provides a diagnostic platform to measure and diagnose these deficiencies in high-level UAV autonomy.
AI.conclusion: HUGE-Bench serves as a diagnostic testbed for high-level UAV autonomy, enabling evaluation of interpretation of concise language and safe, process-oriented execution across realistic, collision-aware scenarios.

title: Federated Hyperdimensional Computing for Resource-Constrained Industrial IoT
comment: Submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: In the Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) systems, edge devices often operate under strict constraints in memory, compute capability, and wireless bandwidth. These limitations challenge the deployment of advanced data analytics tasks, such as predictive and prescriptive maintenance. In this work, we explore hyperdimensional computing (HDC) as a lightweight learning paradigm for resource-constrained IIoT. Conventional centralized HDC leverages the properties of high-dimensional vector spaces to enable energy-efficient training and inference. We integrate this paradigm into a federated learning (FL) framework where devices exchange only prototype representations, which significantly reduces communication overhead. Our numerical results highlight the potential of federated HDC to support collaborative learning in IIoT with fast convergence speed and communication efficiency. These results indicate that HDC represents a lightweight and resilient framework for distributed intelligence in large-scale and resource-constrained IIoT environments.

AI.tldr: Federated hyperdimensional computing (HDC) enables lightweight, communication-efficient distributed learning for resource-constrained IIoT by exchanging prototype representations in a FL framework.
AI.motivation: IIoT edge devices suffer from strict memory, compute, and bandwidth constraints, making conventional data analytics and FL training challenging. A lightweight, scalable learning paradigm is needed for predictive/prescriptive maintenance.
AI.method: Adapt hyperdimensional computing (HDC) to a federated learning (FL) setting. Devices train locally with HDC representations and exchange only prototype vectors rather than full models, leveraging HDC’s high-dimensional vector properties to enable efficient aggregation and inference.
AI.result: Numerical results indicate fast convergence and reduced communication overhead, showing that federated HDC can support collaborative learning in IIoT with energy-efficient, scalable performance.
AI.conclusion: Federated HDC offers a lightweight, resilient framework for distributed intelligence in large-scale, resource-constrained IIoT environments and is a viable path for edge analytics.

title: VideoSeek: Long-Horizon Video Agent with Tool-Guided Seeking
summary: Video agentic models have advanced challenging video-language tasks. However, most agentic approaches still heavily rely on greedy parsing over densely sampled video frames, resulting in high computational cost. We present VideoSeek, a long-horizon video agent that leverages video logic flow to actively seek answer-critical evidence instead of exhaustively parsing the full video. This insight allows the model to use far fewer frames while maintaining, or even improving, its video understanding capability. VideoSeek operates in a think-act-observe loop with a well-designed toolkit for collecting multi-granular video observations. This design enables query-aware exploration over accumulated observations and supports practical video understanding and reasoning. Experiments on four challenging video understanding and reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that VideoSeek achieves strong accuracy while using far fewer frames than prior video agents and standalone LMMs. Notably, VideoSeek achieves a 10.2 absolute points improvement on LVBench over its base model, GPT-5, while using 93% fewer frames. Further analysis highlights the significance of leveraging video logic flow, strong reasoning capability, and the complementary roles of toolkit design.

AI.tldr: VideoSeek introduces a long-horizon video agent that uses video logic flow and a think-act-observe loop to actively seek evidence, greatly reducing frame usage while matching or surpassing prior methods; it reports a 10.2 absolute LVBench gain over GPT-5 with 93% fewer frames.
AI.motivation: Current agentic video-language systems rely on greedy, dense frame parsing, which is computationally expensive and inefficient for long-horizon reasoning. A method that selectively gathers evidence through a structured logic flow could improve efficiency without sacrificing performance.
AI.method: VideoSeek employs a think-act-observe loop with a toolkit for collecting multi-granular video observations. It uses query-aware exploration over accumulated observations and a video logic flow to guide evidence collection. Evaluations are conducted on four challenging video understanding and reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: VideoSeek achieves strong accuracy while using far fewer frames than prior video agents and standalone LMMs. It delivers a 10.2 absolute-point improvement on LVBench over the base GPT-5 model, with 93% fewer frames. Additional analyses emphasize the effectiveness of video logic flow, robust reasoning, and the complementary roles of the toolkit.
AI.conclusion: Video logic flow and the toolkit-enabled think-act-observe loop enable efficient long-horizon video reasoning. The approach reduces computational cost (fewer frames) while maintaining or improving accuracy, highlighting the importance of structured observation collection and query-aware exploration in video understanding.

title: Fourier Splatting: Generalized Fourier encoded primitives for scalable radiance fields
summary: Novel view synthesis has recently been revolutionized by 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), which enables real-time rendering through explicit primitive rasterization. However, existing methods tie visual fidelity strictly to the number of primitives: quality downscaling is achieved only through pruning primitives. We propose the first inherently scalable primitive for radiance field rendering. Fourier Splatting employs scalable primitives with arbitrary closed shapes obtained by parameterizing planar surfels with Fourier encoded descriptors. This formulation allows a single trained model to be rendered at varying levels of detail simply by truncating Fourier coefficients at runtime. To facilitate stable optimization, we employ a straight-through estimator for gradient extension beyond the primitive boundary, and introduce HYDRA, a densification strategy that decomposes complex primitives into simpler constituents within the MCMC framework. Our method achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality among planar-primitive frameworks and comparable perceptual metrics compared to leading volumetric representations on standard benchmarks, providing a versatile solution for bandwidth-constrained high-fidelity rendering.

AI.tldr: Fourier Splatting introduces scalable, closed-shaped primitives for radiance field rendering by encoding planar surfels with Fourier descriptors and truncating coefficients at runtime to adjust detail, with optimization stability via straight-through gradient extension and HYDRA densification within MCMC.
AI.motivation: To overcome the fidelity-vs-primitives coupling in 3D Gaussian Splatting by enabling arbitrary, runtime-adjustable detail without retraining, while maintaining efficient rendering and competitive quality.
AI.method: Replace fixed primitive count with Fourier-encoded planar surfels that support arbitrary closed shapes. Train with a straight-through estimator to extend gradients beyond primitive boundaries. Introduce HYDRA, a densification strategy that decomposes complex primitives into simpler constituents within an MCMC framework to improve optimization and representational capacity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art rendering quality among planar-primitive methods and competitive perceptual metrics with leading volumetric methods on standard benchmarks, enabling high-fidelity rendering under bandwidth constraints.
AI.conclusion: An inherently scalable primitive approach for radiance-field rendering that decouples quality from primitive count, allowing runtime detail control and maintaining competitive performance with volumetric representations.

title: LumosX: Relate Any Identities with Their Attributes for Personalized Video Generation
comment: ICLR 2026 Camera Ready Version. Code and Models: https://jiazheng-xing.github.io/lumosx-home/
summary: Recent advances in diffusion models have significantly improved text-to-video generation, enabling personalized content creation with fine-grained control over both foreground and background elements. However, precise face-attribute alignment across subjects remains challenging, as existing methods lack explicit mechanisms to ensure intra-group consistency. Addressing this gap requires both explicit modeling strategies and face-attribute-aware data resources. We therefore propose LumosX, a framework that advances both data and model design. On the data side, a tailored collection pipeline orchestrates captions and visual cues from independent videos, while multimodal large language models (MLLMs) infer and assign subject-specific dependencies. These extracted relational priors impose a finer-grained structure that amplifies the expressive control of personalized video generation and enables the construction of a comprehensive benchmark. On the modeling side, Relational Self-Attention and Relational Cross-Attention intertwine position-aware embeddings with refined attention dynamics to inscribe explicit subject-attribute dependencies, enforcing disciplined intra-group cohesion and amplifying the separation between distinct subject clusters. Comprehensive evaluations on our benchmark demonstrate that LumosX achieves state-of-the-art performance in fine-grained, identity-consistent, and semantically aligned personalized multi-subject video generation. Code and models are available at https://jiazheng-xing.github.io/lumosx-home/.

AI.tldr: LumosX introduces a dual data-model approach for personalized multi-subject video generation: a data pipeline with subject-aware relational priors inferred by MLLMs, and a relational attention-based model that enforces explicit subject-attribute dependencies to improve intra-group cohesion and inter-subject separation. It achieves state-of-the-art results on a new benchmark for fine-grained, identity-consistent video generation.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in precise face-attribute alignment and intra-group consistency in personalized text-to-video generation, by combining explicit relational priors from data with structure-aware modeling.
AI.method: Data: a tailored pipeline that aligns captions and visuals from independent videos; MLLMs infer subject-specific dependencies to build relational priors and a benchmark. Model: Relational Self-Attention and Relational Cross-Attention that couple position-aware embeddings with enhanced attention dynamics to encode subject-attribute dependencies and enforce intra-group cohesion while separating subject clusters.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on a dedicated benchmark shows LumosX achieves state-of-the-art performance for fine-grained, identity-consistent, semantically aligned personalized multi-subject video generation.
AI.conclusion: LumosX demonstrates that integrating relational priors at data and modeling levels yields stronger identity-consistent and semantically aligned personalized video generation, supported by a new benchmark and public code/models.

title: Hyper-Connections for Adaptive Multi-Modal MRI Brain Tumor Segmentation
summary: We present the first study of Hyper-Connections (HC) for volumetric multi-modal brain tumor segmentation, integrating them as a drop-in replacement for fixed residual connections across five architectures: nnU-Net, SwinUNETR, VT-UNet, U-Net, and U-Netpp. Dynamic HC consistently improves all 3D models on the BraTS 2021 dataset, yielding up to +1.03 percent mean Dice gain with negligible parameter overhead. Gains are most pronounced in the Enhancing Tumor sub-region, reflecting improved fine-grained boundary delineation. Modality ablation further reveals that HC-equipped models develop sharper sensitivity toward clinically dominant sequences, specifically T1ce for Tumor Core and Enhancing Tumor, and FLAIR for Whole Tumor, a behavior absent in fixed-connection baselines and consistent across all architectures. In 2D settings, improvements are smaller and configuration-sensitive, suggesting that volumetric spatial context amplifies the benefit of adaptive aggregation. These results establish HC as a simple, efficient, and broadly applicable mechanism for multi-modal feature fusion in medical image segmentation.

AI.tldr: Dynamic Hyper-Connections (HC) act as a drop-in replacement for fixed residual connections, boosting volumetric multi-modal brain tumor segmentation across five architectures on BraTS 2021 with up to 1.03 percentage points in mean Dice and negligible parameter overhead; gains are strongest in the Enhancing Tumor and rely on adaptive, modality-aware fusion, especially in 3D.
AI.motivation: Improve multi-modal feature fusion in medical image segmentation by replacing static residuals with adaptive, dynamic aggregation to better leverage complementary information across modalities and improve boundary delineation.
AI.method: Introduce HC as a drop-in replacement for fixed residual connections in nnU-Net, SwinUNETR, VT-UNet, U-Net, and U-Net++, evaluate on BraTS 2021 for 3D segmentation; perform modality ablation to study sensitivity to T1ce, FLAIR, and other sequences; compare 3D vs 2D performance; assess parameter overhead.
AI.result: HC yields up to +1.03 percentage points mean Dice across 3D models with negligible parameter overhead; improvements are most pronounced for the Enhancing Tumor region, indicating better boundary delineation; modality ablations show HC-equipped models develop sharper sensitivity toward dominant sequences (T1ce for Tumor Core and Enhancing Tumor; FLAIR for Whole Tumor), a behavior not seen with fixed connections and consistent across architectures; 2D gains are smaller and more configuration-sensitive.
AI.conclusion: HC provides a simple, efficient, and broadly applicable mechanism for multi-modal feature fusion in medical image segmentation, with strong 3D benefits due to volumetric context; the approach is compatible with multiple architectures and requires minimal overhead.

title: How Out-of-Equilibrium Phase Transitions can Seed Pattern Formation in Trained Diffusion Models
summary: In this work, we propose a theoretical framework that interprets the generation process in trained diffusion models as an instance of out-of-equilibrium phase transitions. We argue that, rather than evolving smoothly from noise to data, reverse diffusion passes through a critical regime in which small spatial fluctuations are amplified and seed the emergence of large-scale structure. Our central insight is that architectural constraints, such as locality, sparsity, and translation equivariance, transform memorization-driven instabilities into collective spatial modes, enabling the formation of coherent patterns beyond the training data. Using analytically tractable patch score models, we show how classical symmetry-breaking bifurcations generalize into spatially extended critical phenomena described by softening Fourier modes and growing correlation lengths. We further connect these dynamics to effective field theories of the Ginzburg-Landau type and to mechanisms of pattern formation in non-equilibrium physics. Empirical results on trained convolutional diffusion models corroborate the theory, revealing signatures of criticality including mode softening and rapid growth of spatial correlations. Finally, we demonstrate that this critical regime has practical relevance: targeted perturbations, such as classifier-free guidance pulses applied at the estimated critical time, significantly improve generation control. Together, these findings position non-equilibrium critical phenomena as a unifying principle for understanding, and potentially improving, the behavior of modern diffusion models.

AI.tldr: Frames diffusion-model generation as non-equilibrium critical phenomena; symmetry-breaking bifurcations become spatial patterns, with empirical mode softening and growing correlations, yielding practical control via targeted perturbations.
AI.motivation: Provide a unifying, physics-informed lens to explain emergent structure and controllability in diffusion models, linking architecture-induced instabilities to collective spatial modes and EFT descriptions.
AI.method: Introduce analytically tractable patch score models; derive generalized symmetry-breaking bifurcations to spatially extended critical phenomena with soft Fourier modes; relate to Ginzburg-Landau EFTs; validate with empirical analyses on trained convolutional diffusion models; test classifier-free guidance pulses at the estimated critical time.
AI.result: Demonstrate signatures of criticality in diffusion models, including mode softening and rapid growth of spatial correlations; show that targeted perturbations at critical time enhance generation control.
AI.conclusion: Non-equilibrium critical phenomena offer a unifying framework for understanding and potentially improving diffusion-model behavior, with architectural constraints steering memorization-driven instabilities into coherent spatial patterns and practical control mechanisms.

title: From Masks to Pixels and Meaning: A New Taxonomy, Benchmark, and Metrics for VLM Image Tampering
comment: Code and data at: https://github.com/VILA-Lab/PIXAR (Accepted in CVPR 2026 Findings, but not opted in)
summary: Existing tampering detection benchmarks largely rely on object masks, which severely misalign with the true edit signal: many pixels inside a mask are untouched or only trivially modified, while subtle yet consequential edits outside the mask are treated as natural. We reformulate VLM image tampering from coarse region labels to a pixel-grounded, meaning and language-aware task. First, we introduce a taxonomy spanning edit primitives (replace/remove/splice/inpaint/attribute/colorization, etc.) and their semantic class of tampered object, linking low-level changes to high-level understanding. Second, we release a new benchmark with per-pixel tamper maps and paired category supervision to evaluate detection and classification within a unified protocol. Third, we propose a training framework and evaluation metrics that quantify pixel-level correctness with localization to assess confidence or prediction on true edit intensity, and further measure tamper meaning understanding via semantics-aware classification and natural language descriptions for the predicted regions. We also re-evaluate the existing strong segmentation/localization baselines on recent strong tamper detectors and reveal substantial over- and under-scoring using mask-only metrics, and expose failure modes on micro-edits and off-mask changes. Our framework advances the field from masks to pixels, meanings and language descriptions, establishing a rigorous standard for tamper localization, semantic classification and description. Code and benchmark data are available at https://github.com/VILA-Lab/PIXAR.

AI.tldr: A pixel-grounded, meaning and language-aware reformulation of VLM image tampering with a new per-pixel tamper benchmark (PIXAR), taxonomy of edit primitives, and metrics for pixel-level localization, semantic classification, and natural language descriptions; evidence that mask-based evaluation is flawed; code/data released.
AI.motivation: Current tampering benchmarks rely on coarse object masks that poorly align with actual edits, mislabeling untouched pixels and missing subtle edits. There is a need to connect low-level pixel changes to high-level semantic meaning and enable language-grounded descriptions of tampering.
AI.method: Propose a taxonomy of edit primitives and tampered object classes; release a dataset with per-pixel tamper maps and paired category supervision; develop training framework and evaluation metrics for pixel-level correctness, localization, semantics-aware classification, and natural language descriptions; re-evaluate existing baselines to reveal mask-based shortcomings.
AI.result: Introduction of a pixel-grounded tampering benchmark (PIXAR) with per-pixel tamper maps and category supervision; a training/evaluation framework for pixel-level localization and semantics; demonstration that mask-only metrics misrepresent performance; public code and data at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: Shifts tampering research from mask-centric to pixel-level reasoning, enabling localization, semantic classification, and language-described tampering, and sets a rigorous standard for tamper localization and description.

title: IsoCLIP: Decomposing CLIP Projectors for Efficient Intra-modal Alignment
summary: Vision-Language Models like CLIP are extensively used for inter-modal tasks which involve both visual and text modalities. However, when the individual modality encoders are applied to inherently intra-modal tasks like image-to-image retrieval, their performance suffers from the intra-modal misalignment. In this paper we study intra-modal misalignment in CLIP with a focus on the role of the projectors that map pre-projection image and text embeddings into the shared embedding space. By analyzing the form of the cosine similarity applied to projected features, and its interaction with the contrastive CLIP loss, we show that there is an inter-modal operator responsible for aligning the two modalities during training, and a second, intra-modal operator that only enforces intra-modal normalization but does nothing to promote intra-modal alignment. Via spectral analysis of the inter-modal operator, we identify an approximately isotropic subspace in which the two modalities are well-aligned, as well as anisotropic directions specific to each modality. We demonstrate that this aligned subspace can be directly obtained from the projector weights and that removing the anisotropic directions improves intra-modal alignment. Our experiments on intra-modal retrieval and classification benchmarks show that our training-free method reduces intra-modal misalignment, greatly lowers latency, and outperforms existing approaches across multiple pre-trained CLIP-like models. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/simomagi/IsoCLIP.

AI.tldr: A training-free method (IsoCLIP) improves intra-modal alignment in CLIP by extracting an approximately isotropic subspace from projection weights and removing modality-specific anisotropic directions, enhancing intra-modal retrieval/classification with lower latency.
AI.motivation: Intra-modal misalignment arises when CLIP's encoders are used for intra-modal tasks (e.g., image-to-image retrieval). Analyzing the projection stage reveals separate inter-modal and intra-modal operators, enabling targeted improvements.
AI.method: Spectral analysis of the inter-modal operator formed by the projection step in CLIP; identify an approximately isotropic subspace where modalities align; show that this subspace can be obtained from projector weights; remove anisotropic directions to improve intra-modal alignment; training-free implementation.
AI.result: The training-free approach reduces intra-modal misalignment, decreases inference latency, and outperforms existing methods on intra-modal retrieval and classification benchmarks across multiple pre-trained CLIP-like models; code available.
AI.conclusion: There exist distinct inter-modal and intra-modal operators in CLIP projections; leveraging the isotropic subspace and suppressing anisotropic directions yields better intra-modal alignment without retraining.

title: MedQ-Engine: A Closed-Loop Data Engine for Evolving MLLMs in Medical Image Quality Assessment
summary: Medical image quality assessment (Med-IQA) is a prerequisite for clinical AI deployment, yet multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still fall substantially short of human experts, particularly when required to provide descriptive assessments with clinical reasoning beyond simple quality scores. However, improving them is hindered by the high cost of acquiring descriptive annotations and by the inability of one-time data collection to adapt to the model's evolving weaknesses. To address these challenges, we propose MedQ-Engine, a closed-loop data engine that iteratively evaluates the model to discover failure prototypes via data-driven clustering, explores a million-scale image pool using these prototypes as retrieval anchors with progressive human-in-the-loop annotation, and evolves through quality-assured fine-tuning, forming a self-improving cycle. Models are evaluated on complementary perception and description tasks. An entropy-guided routing mechanism triages annotations to minimize labeling cost. Experiments across five medical imaging modalities show that MedQ-Engine elevates an 8B-parameter model to surpass GPT-4o by over 13% and narrow the gap with human experts to only 4.34%, using only 10K annotations with more than 4x sample efficiency over random sampling.

AI.tldr: MedQ-Engine is a closed-loop data engine that iteratively improves multimodal medical image QA models via failure-prototype clustering, large-scale retrieval with progressive human-in-the-loop annotation, and quality-assured fine-tuning, achieving strong performance with only 10K annotations.
AI.motivation: To overcome the high cost of descriptive annotations and the static nature of one-shot data collection by enabling iterative, data-efficient improvement that adapts to the model's evolving weaknesses in medical image quality assessment.
AI.method: 1) Identify failure prototypes through data-driven clustering of model outputs; 2) Retrieve from a million-scale image pool using prototypes as anchors; 3) Apply progressive, human-in-the-loop annotation with entropy-guided routing to minimize labeling cost; 4) Perform quality-assured fine-tuning to form a self-improving cycle.
AI.result: On five medical imaging modalities, an 8B-parameter model after the MedQ-Engine loop surpasses GPT-4o by >13% and narrows the gap with human experts to 4.34%, using just 10K annotations with >4x sample efficiency over random sampling.
AI.conclusion: The closed-loop MedQ-Engine framework provides a scalable, data-efficient pathway to elevate MMLMs in clinical imaging QA, combining failure-driven data selection, large-scale retrieval, human-in-the-loop labeling, and iterative fine-tuning to close the AI–expert gap.

title: Trojan horse hunt in deep forecasting models: Insights from the European Space Agency competition
summary: Forecasting plays a crucial role in modern safety-critical applications, such as space operations. However, the increasing use of deep forecasting models introduces a new security risk of trojan horse attacks, carried out by hiding a backdoor in the training data or directly in the model weights. Once implanted, the backdoor is activated by a specific trigger pattern at test time, causing the model to produce manipulated predictions. We focus on this issue in our \textit{Trojan Horse Hunt} data science competition, where more than 200 teams faced the task of identifying triggers hidden in deep forecasting models for spacecraft telemetry. We describe the novel task formulation, benchmark set, evaluation protocol, and best solutions from the competition. We further summarize key insights and research directions for effective identification of triggers in time series forecasting models. All materials are publicly available on the official competition webpage https://www.kaggle.com/competitions/trojan-horse-hunt-in-space.

AI.tldr: Introduces Trojan Horse Hunt: a competition-driven benchmark for detecting backdoor triggers in deep forecasting models for spacecraft telemetry, with task formulation, dataset, protocol, and top approaches.
AI.motivation: Ensure security of ML-driven forecasting in safety-critical space missions by enabling detection of hidden backdoors in training data or model weights.
AI.method: Proposes a novel task formulation for trigger identification in time-series forecasts, builds a benchmark set, defines an evaluation protocol, and aggregates best solutions from 200+ teams.
AI.result: Describes the top-performing strategies and key insights from the competition, establishing directions for future research in time-series backdoor detection.
AI.conclusion: All materials are publicly available; the work lays groundwork for robust detection of triggers in forecasting models and invites ongoing development.

title: SIMPLER: Efficient Foundation Model Adaptation via Similarity-Guided Layer Pruning for Earth Observation
summary: Fine-tuning foundation models for Earth Observation is computationally expensive, with high training time and memory demands for both training and deployment. Parameter-efficient methods reduce training cost but retain full inference complexity, while post-hoc compression optimizes inference only after costly full fine-tuning. We introduce SIMPLER, a pre-fine-tuning architecture selection method that reduces inference and deployment costs by identifying an effective model depth before adaptation. SIMPLER exploits stabilization of representations in deeper layers of pre-trained vision transformers: it computes layer-wise representation similarity on unlabeled task data and applies an automated scoring function to select redundant layers, with no gradients, magnitude heuristics, or hyperparameter tuning required. On Prithvi-EO-2, SIMPLER prunes up to 79% of parameters while retaining 94% of baseline performance, yielding a 2.1x training speedup and 2.6x inference speedup. The method generalizes to TerraMind (a multimodal EO foundation model) and ImageNet-pretrained ViT-MAE, demonstrating applicability across tasks, architectures, and spectral modalities. Code is available at https://gitlab.citius.gal/hpc4rs/simpler.

AI.tldr: SIMPLER selects an effective model depth before fine-tuning by pruning redundant transformer layers using layer-wise representation similarity on unlabeled data, reducing training/inference costs while preserving performance; demonstrated on Prithvi-EO-2 with up to 79% parameter pruning and ~2–3x speedups, and generalized to TerraMind and ViT-MAE.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning foundation models for Earth Observation is costly; existing parameter-efficient methods still incur full inference costs; post-hoc compression delays deployment; need pre-fine-tuning depth selection that reduces both training and deployment costs.
AI.method: Compute layer-wise representation similarity across depth for unlabeled task data, apply an automated scoring function to identify and prune redundant layers without gradients, magnitude heuristics, or hyperparameter tuning; select architecture depth prior to adaptation; results validated on multiple EO tasks/architectures.
AI.result: Prithvi-EO-2: up to 79% parameter pruning with 94% baseline performance; 2.1x training speedup; 2.6x inference speedup. Generalizes to TerraMind and ImageNet-pretrained ViT-MAE; code available.
AI.conclusion: Pre-fine-tuning depth selection via representation similarity offers gradient-free, hyperparameter-free efficiency gains with broad applicability across EO tasks and modalities; effective for reducing both training and deployment costs.

title: GO-GenZip: Goal-Oriented Generative Sampling and Hybrid Compression
summary: Current network data telemetry pipelines consist of massive streams of fine-grained Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) from multiple distributed sources towards central aggregators, making data storage, transmission, and real-time analysis increasingly unsustainable. This work presents a generative AI (GenAI)-driven sampling and hybrid compression framework that redesigns network telemetry from a goal-oriented perspective. Unlike conventional approaches that passively compress fully observed data, our approach jointly optimizes what to observe and how to encode it, guided by the relevance of information to downstream tasks. The framework integrates adaptive sampling policies, using adaptive masking techniques, with generative modeling to identify patterns and preserve critical features across temporal and spatial dimensions. The selectively acquired data are further processed through a hybrid compression scheme that combines traditional lossless coding with GenAI-driven, lossy compression. Experimental results on real network datasets demonstrate over 50$\%$ reductions in sampling and data transfer costs, while maintaining comparable reconstruction accuracy and goal-oriented analytical fidelity in downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: GenAI-driven sampling and hybrid compression reduce data volume by >50% while preserving downstream task performance.
AI.motivation: Massive streams of KPIs from distributed sources overwhelm storage, bandwidth, and real-time analytics; there is a need to optimize both what data is observed and how it is encoded.
AI.method: Joint design of adaptive observation (masking-based adaptive sampling) and encoding (hybrid lossless + GenAI-driven lossy compression) guided by downstream task relevance. Generative modeling identifies informative temporal/spatial patterns to preserve critical features.
AI.result: On real network datasets, the framework achieves over 50% reductions in sampling and data transfer costs, with reconstruction accuracy and goal-oriented analytical fidelity comparable to traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: A goal-oriented GenAI-driven framework can substantially improve efficiency in network telemetry pipelines by reducing data volume without sacrificing task performance.

title: PanORama: Multiview Consistent Panoptic Segmentation in Operating Rooms
summary: Operating rooms (ORs) are cluttered, dynamic, highly occluded environments, where reliable spatial understanding is essential for situational awareness during complex surgical workflows. Achieving spatial understanding for panoptic segmentation from sparse multiview images poses a fundamental challenge, as limited visibility in a subset of views often leads to mispredictions across cameras. To this end, we introduce PanORama, the first panoptic segmentation for the operating room that is multiview-consistent by design. By modeling cross-view interactions at the feature level inside the backbone in a single forward pass, view consistency emerges directly rather than through post-hoc refinement. We evaluate on the MM-OR and 4D-OR datasets, achieving >70% Panoptic Quality (PQ) performance, and outperforming the previous state of the art. Importantly, PanORama is calibration-free, requiring no camera parameters, and generalizes to unseen camera viewpoints within any multiview configuration at inference time. By substantially enhancing multiview segmentation and, consequently, spatial understanding in the OR, we believe our approach opens new opportunities for surgical perception and assistance. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: PanORama delivers multiview-consistent panoptic segmentation for operating rooms in a single forward pass, calibration-free, achieving PQ >70% on MM-OR and 4D-OR, outperforming prior SOTA.
AI.motivation: Enable reliable, cross-view spatial understanding in cluttered, occluded ORs where sparse multi-view data leads to inconsistent predictions; eliminate reliance on camera calibration and post-hoc refinement.
AI.method: In-network cross-view interactions are modeled at the feature level inside the backbone in a single forward pass to enforce view-consistency by design, with no camera parameters required.
AI.result: Achieves >70% Panoptic Quality on MM-OR and 4D-OR datasets, outperforms previous SOTA, generalizes to unseen viewpoints within any multiview configuration during inference.
AI.conclusion: PanORama strengthens multiview segmentation and spatial awareness in the OR, enabling new surgical perception and assistance opportunities; code will be released upon acceptance.
