title: Conditioning Protein Generation via Hopfield Pattern Multiplicity
summary: Protein sequence generation via stochastic attention produces plausible family members from small alignments without training, but treats all stored sequences equally and cannot direct generation toward a functional subset of interest. We show that a single scalar parameter, added as a bias to the sampler's attention logits, continuously shifts generation from the full family toward a user-specified subset, with no retraining and no change to the model architecture. A practitioner supplies a small set of sequences (for example, hits from a binding screen) and a multiplicity ratio that controls how strongly generation favors them. The method is agnostic to what the subset represents: binding, stability, specificity, or any other property. We find that the conditioning is exact at the level of the sampler's internal representation, but that the decoded sequence phenotype can fall short because the dimensionality reduction used to encode sequences does not always preserve the residue-level variation that defines the functional split. We term this discrepancy the calibration gap and show that it is predicted by a simple geometric measure of how well the encoding separates the functional subset from the rest of the family. Experiments on five Pfam families (Kunitz, SH3, WW, Homeobox, and Forkhead domains) confirm the monotonic relationship between separation and gap across a fourfold range of geometries. Applied to omega-conotoxin peptides targeting a calcium channel involved in pain signaling, curated seeding from 23 characterized binders produces over a thousand candidates that preserve the primary pharmacophore and all experimentally identified binding determinants. These results show that stochastic attention enables practitioners to expand a handful of experimentally characterized sequences into diverse candidate libraries without retraining a generative model.

AI.tldr: A scalar bias to the sampler's attention logits steers protein sequence generation toward a user-defined functional subset without retraining, supported by an analysis of a calibration gap and demonstrated across Pfam families and omega-conotoxin peptides.
AI.motivation: Enable targeted exploration of protein sequence space by conditioning a training-free generative model on a small seed set to enrich for functional variants while avoiding retraining.
AI.method: Add a single scalar as a bias to the sampler's attention logits. Use a small seed set (e.g., binding hits) and a multiplicity ratio to control how strongly generation favors them. Analyze exact conditioning at the internal representation level and a calibration gap between internal conditioning and decoded sequence phenotypes. Define a geometric measure of how well the encoding separates the functional subset from the rest and relate it to the gap.
AI.result: Demonstrates a monotonic relationship between separation and calibration gap across diverse geometries (Kunitz, SH3, WW, Homeobox, Forkhead). On omega-conotoxin targets, seeding from 23 binders yields over a thousand candidates that preserve the primary pharmacophore and binding determinants, expanding a small seed into large candidate libraries without retraining.
AI.conclusion: A simple, architecture-agnostic bias in attention enables targeted expansion of sequence libraries from small experimental seeds, with a predictable calibration gap that can be anticipated by the encoding geometry.

title: SegVGGT: Joint 3D Reconstruction and Instance Segmentation from Multi-View Images
summary: 3D instance segmentation methods typically rely on high-quality point clouds or posed RGB-D scans, requiring complex multi-stage processing pipelines, and are highly sensitive to reconstruction noise. While recent feed-forward transformers have revolutionized multi-view 3D reconstruction, they remain decoupled from high-level semantic understanding. In this work, we present SegVGGT, a unified end-to-end framework that simultaneously performs feed-forward 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation directly from multi-view RGB images. By introducing object queries that interact with multi-level geometric features, our method deeply integrates instance identification into the visual geometry grounded transformer. To address the severe attention dispersion problem caused by the massive number of global image tokens, we propose the Frame-level Attention Distribution Alignment (FADA) strategy. FADA explicitly guides object queries to attend to instance-relevant frames during training, providing structured supervision without extra inference overhead. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SegVGGT achieves the state-of-the-art performance on ScanNetv2 and ScanNet200, outperforming both recent joint models and RGB-D-based approaches, while exhibiting strong generalization capabilities on ScanNet++.

AI.tldr: SegVGGT is a unified end-to-end framework for 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation from multi-view RGB images. It introduces object queries with multi-level geometric features and a Frame-level Attention Distribution Alignment (FADA) strategy to guide attention to instance-relevant frames, achieving state-of-the-art results on ScanNet variants and good generalization to ScanNet++.
AI.motivation: Current 3D instance segmentation pipelines rely on high-quality point clouds or RGB-D scans and are multi-stage and sensitive to reconstruction noise. While feed-forward transformers improve multi-view reconstruction, they lack integrated high-level semantic understanding. A unified end-to-end framework combining 3D reconstruction with instance segmentation can leverage shared visual geometry features and improve robustness.
AI.method: SegVGGT jointly performs feed-forward 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation from multi-view RGB images using object queries that interact with multi-level geometric features within a visual geometry grounded transformer. To mitigate attention dispersion from many global image tokens, it introduces Frame-level Attention Distribution Alignment (FADA), which guides object queries to attend to instance-relevant frames during training without adding inference overhead.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on ScanNetv2 and ScanNet200, outperforming recent joint models and RGB-D-based approaches, and demonstrates strong generalization on ScanNet++.
AI.conclusion: SegVGGT provides a unified end-to-end solution for simultaneous 3D reconstruction and instance segmentation from RGB imagery by embedding instance understanding into the VGT framework and using FADA to focus attention, delivering SOTA results and good generalization.

title: Revisiting Gene Ontology Knowledge Discovery with Hierarchical Feature Selection and Virtual Study Group of AI Agents
summary: Large language models have achieved great success in multiple challenging tasks, and their capacity can be further boosted by the emerging agentic AI techniques. This new computing paradigm has already started revolutionising the traditional scientific discovery pipelines. In this work, we propose a novel agentic AI-based knowledge discovery-oriented virtual study group that aims to extract meaningful ageing-related biological knowledge considering highly ageing-related Gene Ontology terms that are selected by hierarchical feature selection methods. We investigate the performance of the proposed agentic AI framework by considering four different model organisms' ageing-related Gene Ontology terms and validate the biological findings by reviewing existing research articles. It is found that the majority of the AI agent-generated scientific claims can be supported by existing literatures and the proposed internal mechanisms of the virtual study group also play an important role in the designed agentic AI-based knowledge discovery framework.

AI.tldr: A novel agentic AI-based virtual study group for knowledge discovery in aging biology using aging-related GO terms selected by hierarchical feature selection; validated across four organisms; majority AI-generated claims supported by literature; internal mechanisms of the group are important.
AI.motivation: To accelerate aging biology knowledge discovery by integrating agentic AI with GO-term–driven knowledge discovery, leveraging hierarchical feature selection to focus on highly aging-related terms.
AI.method: Propose and implement an agentic AI-based virtual study group framework; select aging-related Gene Ontology terms via hierarchical feature selection; evaluate on four model organisms; validate AI-generated claims by literature review; analyze the role of internal mechanisms in the virtual group.
AI.result: Most AI-generated claims are supported by existing literature; the internal mechanisms of the virtual study group significantly contribute to performance; the framework shows promise for knowledge discovery in aging biology.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI-based knowledge discovery with a virtual study group is an effective paradigm for accelerating aging biology research; potential for broader application beyond aging and GO-term analysis; highlights the importance of internal group dynamics and term selection strategy.

title: Beyond Single Tokens: Distilling Discrete Diffusion Models via Discrete MMD
summary:
It is currently difficult to distill discrete diffusion models. In contrast, continuous diffusion literature has many distillation approaches methods that can reduce sampling steps to a handful.
  Our method, Discrete Moment Matching Distillation (D-MMD), leverages ideas that have been highly successful in the continuous domain. Whereas previous discrete distillation methods collapse, D-MMD maintains high quality and diversity (given sufficient sampling steps). This is demonstrated on both text and image datasets. Moreover, the newly distilled generators can outperform their teachers.

AI.tldr: Proposes Discrete Moment Matching Distillation (D-MMD) to distill discrete diffusion models by adapting continuous-domain distillation ideas, using moment matching; preserves high quality and diversity with reduced sampling steps; validated on text and image data; distilled generators can outperform their teachers.
AI.motivation: Discrete diffusion distillation is difficult; continuous diffusion literature has robust distillation methods to reduce sampling steps; bridging these ideas to the discrete domain to improve efficiency and performance.
AI.method: Discrete Moment Matching Distillation (D-MMD) applies moment-matching-based distillation to discrete diffusion models, preserving quality and diversity with fewer sampling steps; demonstrated on text and image datasets; results include distilled generators that can surpass teacher models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows D-MMD maintains high quality and diversity at reduced sampling steps on both text and image tasks; distilled generators can outperform their teachers.
AI.conclusion: D-MMD provides a viable, effective approach to discrete diffusion distillation by importing continuous-domain moment-matching ideas, achieving high-quality, diverse generations with fewer steps and sometimes surpassing teacher models.

title: Kolmogorov-Arnold causal generative models
summary: Causal generative models provide a principled framework for answering observational, interventional, and counterfactual queries from observational data. However, many deep causal models rely on highly expressive architectures with opaque mechanisms, limiting auditability in high-stakes domains. We propose KaCGM, a causal generative model for mixed-type tabular data where each structural equation is parameterized by a Kolmogorov--Arnold Network (KAN). This decomposition enables direct inspection of learned causal mechanisms, including symbolic approximations and visualization of parent--child relationships, while preserving query-agnostic generative semantics. We introduce a validation pipeline based on distributional matching and independence diagnostics of inferred exogenous variables, allowing assessment using observational data alone. Experiments on synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmarks show competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods. A real-world cardiovascular case study further demonstrates the extraction of simplified structural equations and interpretable causal effects. These results suggest that expressive causal generative modeling and functional transparency can be achieved jointly, supporting trustworthy deployment in tabular decision-making settings. Code: https://github.com/aalmodovares/kacgm

AI.tldr: Proposes KaCGM, a causal generative model for mixed-type tabular data where each structural equation is parameterized by a Kolmogorov--Arnold Network (KAN). This enables direct inspection of learned causal mechanisms (symbolic approximations, parent--child visualizations) while preserving query-agnostic generative semantics. A validation pipeline using distributional matching and independence diagnostics on exogenous variables is introduced. Experiments on synthetic and semi-synthetic data show competitive performance; a real-world cardiovascular case study demonstrates extractable simplified structural equations and interpretable causal effects. Code available at the provided URL.
AI.motivation: There is a need for interpretable and auditable causal generative models for high-stakes tabular data. Many deep causal models are highly expressive but opaque, hindering auditability and trust. KaCGM aims to combine expressive modeling with functional transparency.
AI.method: Structural equations are parameterized by Kolmogorov--Arnold Networks (KANs) for mixed-type tabular data. The approach enables inspection of learned causal mechanisms, including symbolic approximations and parent–child visualizations, while preserving query-agnostic generative semantics. Validation uses distributional matching and independence diagnostics on inferred exogenous variables. Evaluations on synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmarks, plus a real-world cardiovascular case study to extract simplified structural equations and interpret causal effects.
AI.result: KaCGM achieves competitive performance against state-of-the-art methods on benchmarks. It provides direct interpretability through symbolic approximations and visualizations of causal structure, and facilitates extraction of simplified structural equations and interpretable causal effects in real data.
AI.conclusion: Expressive causal generative modeling can be paired with functional transparency, enabling trustworthy deployment of causal models in tabular decision-making settings.

title: Timestep-Aware Block Masking for Efficient Diffusion Model Inference
summary: Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DPMs) have achieved great success in image generation but suffer from high inference latency due to their iterative denoising nature. Motivated by the evolving feature dynamics across the denoising trajectory, we propose a novel framework to optimize the computational graph of pre-trained DPMs on a per-timestep basis. By learning timestep-specific masks, our method dynamically determines which blocks to execute or bypass through feature reuse at each inference stage. Unlike global optimization methods that incur prohibitive memory costs via full-chain backpropagation, our method optimizes masks for each timestep independently, ensuring a memory-efficient training process. To guide this process, we introduce a timestep-aware loss scaling mechanism that prioritizes feature fidelity during sensitive denoising phases, complemented by a knowledge-guided mask rectification strategy to prune redundant spatial-temporal dependencies. Our approach is architecture-agnostic and demonstrates significant efficiency gains across a broad spectrum of models, including DDPM, LDM, DiT, and PixArt. Experimental results show that by treating the denoising process as a sequence of optimized computational paths, our method achieves a superior balance between sampling speed and generative quality. Our code will be released.

AI.tldr: Optimizes diffusion model inference by learning per-timestep masks to dynamically skip or reuse features, enabling memory-efficient, architecture-agnostic speedups with timestep-aware training and mask rectification; demonstrates improved speed-accuracy across DDPM, LDM, DiT, and PixArt; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference latency of diffusion probabilistic models by exploiting evolving feature dynamics along the denoising trajectory while avoiding memory-heavy full-backpropagation over the full chain.
AI.method: Train timestep-specific masks that decide which blocks to execute or bypass at each inference step, enabling dynamic computational graphs and feature reuse. Optimize masks per timestep independently to maintain memory efficiency. Introduce a timestep-aware loss scaling to emphasize fidelity during sensitive denoising phases and a knowledge-guided mask rectification to prune redundant spatio-temporal dependencies. Architecture-agnostic, applicable to DDPM, LDM, DiT, PixArt.
AI.result: Demonstrates significant efficiency gains (speedups with maintained or improved generative quality) across a range of diffusion-model architectures. Treats denoising as a sequence of optimized computational paths, achieving favorable speed-quality trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: Per-timestep computational-path optimization is a viable, memory-efficient strategy to accelerate diffusion models, with broad applicability and potential for reuse across architectures; code release anticipated.

title: Survey of Various Fuzzy and Uncertain Decision-Making Methods
comment: Book. Publisher: Neutrosophic Science International Association (NSIA) Publishing House. ISBN: 978-1-59973-883-3. 446 pages
summary: Decision-making in real applications is often affected by vagueness, incomplete information, heterogeneous data, and conflicting expert opinions. This survey reviews uncertainty-aware multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) and organizes the field into a concise, task-oriented taxonomy. We summarize problem-level settings (discrete, group/consensus, dynamic, multi-stage, multi-level, multiagent, and multi-scenario), weight elicitation (subjective and objective schemes under fuzzy/linguistic inputs), and inter-criteria structure and causality modelling. For solution procedures, we contrast compensatory scoring methods, distance-to-reference and compromise approaches, and non-compensatory outranking frameworks for ranking or sorting. We also outline rule/evidence-based and sequential decision models that produce interpretable rules or policies. The survey highlights typical inputs, core computational steps, and primary outputs, and provides guidance on choosing methods according to robustness, interpretability, and data availability. It concludes with open directions on explainable uncertainty integration, stability, and scalability in large-scale and dynamic decision environments.

AI.tldr: Survey of uncertainty-aware MCDM, proposing a task-oriented taxonomy across problem settings, weight elicitation, structure, and solution methods; highlights inputs/steps/outputs, offers guidance on robustness/interpretability/data needs; calls out explainable uncertainty, stability, and scalability as future directions.
AI.motivation: To address vagueness, incomplete information, heterogeneous data, and conflicting expert opinions in real-world decisions by organizing the uncertainty-aware MCDM literature into a concise, actionable taxonomy and providing practical guidance for method selection.
AI.method: Literature survey and synthesis; development of a task-oriented taxonomy covering problem-level settings, weight elicitation schemes (subjective and objective under fuzzy/linguistic inputs), inter-criteria structure and causality modeling, and solution procedures (compensatory scoring, distance-to-reference, compromise, non-compensatory outranking).
AI.result: A structured taxonomy and practical guidance summarizing typical inputs, core computational steps, and outputs for uncertainty-aware MCDM methods; comparison of method families; recommendations on robustness, interpretability, and data availability; identification of gaps and future research directions.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizes explainable uncertainty integration, stability, and scalability in large-scale and dynamic decision environments, outlining open directions for advancing the field.

title: Cov2Pose: Leveraging Spatial Covariance for Direct Manifold-aware 6-DoF Object Pose Estimation
comment: Accepted to the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) 2026
summary: In this paper, we address the problem of 6-DoF object pose estimation from a single RGB image. Indirect methods that typically predict intermediate 2D keypoints, followed by a Perspective-n-Point solver, have shown great performance. Direct approaches, which regress the pose in an end-to-end manner, are usually computationally more efficient but less accurate. However, direct heads rely on globally pooled features, ignoring spatial second-order statistics despite their informativeness in pose prediction. They also predict, in most cases, discontinuous pose representations that lack robustness. Herein, we therefore propose a covariance-pooled representation that encodes convolutional feature distributions as a symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrix. Moreover, we propose a novel pose encoding in the form of an SPD matrix via its Cholesky decomposition. Pose is then regressed in an end-to-end manner with a manifold-aware network head, taking into account the Riemannian geometry of SPD matrices. Experiments and ablations consistently demonstrate the relevance of second-order pooling and continuous representations for direct pose regression, including under partial occlusion.

AI.tldr: Proposes a covariance-pooled, SPD-based representation for end-to-end 6-DoF pose regression from a single RGB image, using a Cholesky-based SPD encoding and a manifold-aware head to exploit second-order statistics and Riemannian geometry, improving robustness under occlusion compared to traditional direct regression.
AI.motivation: Direct pose regression often uses globally pooled features and yields discontinuous pose representations, limiting accuracy and robustness. Incorporating second-order statistics and a continuous SPD representation aims to provide richer, spatially informative cues for pose estimation.
AI.method: Compute covariance pooling of convolutional features to form a symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrix; encode the pose as an SPD matrix via Cholesky decomposition; regress pose end-to-end with a manifold-aware head that respects SPD geometry (Riemannian).
AI.result: Ablation studies and experiments demonstrate that second-order pooling and the continuous SPD representation improve direct pose regression performance, including under partial occlusion.
AI.conclusion: The SPD-based covariance pooling combined with a manifold-aware regression head leverages second-order statistics and geometric structure to enhance direct 6-DoF pose estimation from RGB images, indicating the value of continuous SPD representations for this task.

title: 2K Retrofit: Entropy-Guided Efficient Sparse Refinement for High-Resolution 3D Geometry Prediction
summary: High-resolution geometric prediction is essential for robust perception in autonomous driving, robotics, and AR/MR, but current foundation models are fundamentally limited by their scalability to real-world, high-resolution scenarios. Direct inference on 2K images with these models incurs prohibitive computational and memory demands, making practical deployment challenging. To tackle the issue, we present 2K Retrofit, a novel framework that enables efficient 2K-resolution inference for any geometric foundation model, without modifying or retraining the backbone. Our approach leverages fast coarse predictions and an entropy-based sparse refinement to selectively enhance high-uncertainty regions, achieving precise and high-fidelity 2K outputs with minimal overhead. Extensive experiments on widely used benchmark demonstrate that 2K Retrofit consistently achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and speed, bridging the gap between research advances and scalable deployment in high-resolution 3D vision applications. Code will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: A plug-in framework for efficient 2K-resolution inference on geometric foundation models using fast coarse predictions and entropy-based sparse refinement, without backbone modification or retraining.
AI.motivation: Current geometric foundation models struggle to scale to real-world, high-resolution 2K inputs due to prohibitive compute and memory demands, hindering deployment in robotics, autonomous driving, and AR/MR.
AI.method: A two-stage approach: 1) generate fast coarse predictions with existing backbones; 2) apply entropy-based sparse refinement to high-uncertainty regions to upgrade the output to 2K with minimal overhead, without retraining or modifying the backbone; the framework is model-agnostic and plug-in.
AI.result: Empirical results on standard benchmarks show state-of-the-art accuracy and speed for 2K outputs, with low overhead and good generalization across geometric foundation models; code to be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: 2K Retrofit narrows the gap between research and scalable deployment for high-resolution 3D vision, enabling reliable 2K predictions in real-world applications; future work includes broader benchmarks and optimization.

title: MedSPOT: A Workflow-Aware Sequential Grounding Benchmark for Clinical GUI
summary: Despite the rapid progress of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), their ability to perform reliable visual grounding in high-stakes clinical software environments remains underexplored. Existing GUI benchmarks largely focus on isolated, single-step grounding queries, overlooking the sequential, workflow-driven reasoning required in real-world medical interfaces, where tasks evolve across independent steps and dynamic interface states. We introduce MedSPOT, a workflow-aware sequential grounding benchmark for clinical GUI environments. Unlike prior benchmarks that treat grounding as a standalone prediction task, MedSPOT models procedural interaction as a sequence of structured spatial decisions. The benchmark comprises 216 task-driven videos with 597 annotated keyframes, in which each task consists of 2 to 3 interdependent grounding steps within realistic medical workflows. This design captures interface hierarchies, contextual dependencies, and fine-grained spatial precision under evolving conditions. To evaluate procedural robustness, we propose a strict sequential evaluation protocol that terminates task assessment upon the first incorrect grounding prediction, explicitly measuring error propagation in multi-step workflows. We further introduce a comprehensive failure taxonomy, including edge bias, small-target errors, no prediction, near miss, far miss, and toolbar confusion, to enable systematic diagnosis of model behavior in clinical GUI settings. By shifting evaluation from isolated grounding to workflow-aware sequential reasoning, MedSPOT establishes a realistic and safety-critical benchmark for assessing multimodal models in medical software environments. Code and data are available at: https://github.com/Tajamul21/MedSPOT.

AI.tldr: MedSPOT is a workflow-aware, sequential grounding benchmark for clinical GUI environments, introducing 216 task-driven videos with 597 keyframes, 2–3 interdependent grounding steps per task, a strict sequential evaluation protocol, and a comprehensive failure taxonomy to diagnose model behavior in medical workflows; code and data are released.
AI.motivation: Current GUI grounding benchmarks treat grounding as isolated, single-step tasks and do not capture the sequential, context-dependent reasoning required in real-world medical interfaces, risking unsafe or unreliable model behavior in high-stakes clinical software.
AI.method: Develop MedSPOT by assembling 216 task-driven videos with 597 annotated keyframes, each task comprising 2–3 interdependent grounding steps. Implement a strict sequential evaluation protocol that ends a task at the first incorrect grounding prediction to measure error propagation. Define a comprehensive failure taxonomy (edge bias, small-target errors, no prediction, near miss, far miss, toolbar confusion). Provide code and data for reproducibility.
AI.result: A new benchmark and evaluation framework that emphasizes workflow-aware sequential reasoning for visual grounding in clinical GUI contexts, along with dataset, annotations, and diagnostic taxonomy; enabling analysis of error propagation and model robustness in multi-step medical workflows.
AI.conclusion: MedSPOT shifts evaluation from isolated grounding to workflow-aware sequential reasoning, offering a realistic, safety-critical benchmark for evaluating multimodal models in medical software environments; the authors release code and data to facilitate adoption.

title: Evaluating Test-Time Adaptation For Facial Expression Recognition Under Natural Cross-Dataset Distribution Shifts
summary: Deep learning models often struggle under natural distribution shifts, a common challenge in real-world deployments. Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) addresses this by adapting models during inference without labeled source data. We present the first evaluation of TTA methods for FER under natural domain shifts, performing cross-dataset experiments with widely used FER datasets. This moves beyond synthetic corruptions to examine real-world shifts caused by differing collection protocols, annotation standards, and demographics. Results show TTA can boost FER performance under natural shifts by up to 11.34\%. Entropy minimization methods such as TENT and SAR perform best when the target distribution is clean. In contrast, prototype adjustment methods like T3A excel under larger distributional distance scenarios. Finally, feature alignment methods such as SHOT deliver the largest gains when the target distribution is noisier than our source. Our cross-dataset analysis shows that TTA effectiveness is governed by the distributional distance and the severity of the natural shift across domains.

AI.tldr: TTA improves facial emotion recognition under natural domain shifts, with gains up to 11.34%. Performance depends on shift characteristics and adaptation method: entropy minimization (TENT/SAR) excels when the target is clean; prototype adjustment (T3A) helps with larger distributional changes; feature alignment (SHOT) shines when the target is noisier than the source.
AI.motivation: Assess the effectiveness of Test-Time Adaptation for facial emotion recognition under real-world, natural distribution shifts across datasets, moving beyond synthetic corruptions.
AI.method: Cross-dataset evaluation on standard FER datasets, comparing several TTA approaches: entropy minimization (TENT, SAR), prototype adjustment (T3A), and feature alignment (SHOT). Analyze performance as a function of distributional distance and shift severity.
AI.result: TTA can boost FER performance under natural shifts by up to 11.34%. Entropy minimization methods perform best when the target distribution is clean, prototype adjustment methods excel under larger distributional distance, and feature alignment methods deliver the largest gains when the target is noisier than the source. Overall, TTA effectiveness correlates with distributional distance and shift severity across domains.
AI.conclusion: TTA is a viable strategy for FER under real-world domain shifts; method choice should be guided by the nature of the shift (distance and noisiness). Cross-dataset analysis provides practical guidance on when to deploy entropy minimization, prototype Adjustment, or feature alignment techniques.

title: NEC-Diff: Noise-Robust Event-RAW Complementary Diffusion for Seeing Motion in Extreme Darkness
summary: High-quality imaging of dynamic scenes in extremely low-light conditions is highly challenging. Photon scarcity induces severe noise and texture loss, causing significant image degradation. Event cameras, featuring a high dynamic range (120 dB) and high sensitivity to motion, serve as powerful complements to conventional cameras by offering crucial cues for preserving subtle textures. However, most existing approaches emphasize texture recovery from events, while paying little attention to image noise or the intrinsic noise of events themselves, which ultimately hinders accurate pixel reconstruction under photon-starved conditions. In this work, we propose NEC-Diff, a novel diffusion-based event-RAW hybrid imaging framework that extracts reliable information from heavily noisy signals to reconstruct fine scene structures. The framework is driven by two key insights: (1) combining the linear light-response property of RAW images with the brightness-change nature of events to establish a physics-driven constraint for robust dual-modal denoising; and (2) dynamically estimating the SNR of both modalities based on denoising results to guide adaptive feature fusion, thereby injecting reliable cues into the diffusion process for high-fidelity visual reconstruction. Furthermore, we construct the REAL (Raw and Event Acquired in Low-light) dataset which provides 47,800 pixel-aligned low-light RAW images, events, and high-quality references under 0.001-0.8 lux illumination. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superiority of NEC-Diff under extreme darkness. The project are available at: https://github.com/jinghan-xu/NEC-Diff.

AI.tldr: NEC-Diff: a diffusion-based RAW–event fusion framework for ultra-low-light imaging, using physics-driven dual-modal denoising and SNR-guided adaptive fusion; introduces REAL dataset.
AI.motivation: Imaging in extreme low-light suffers from photon scarcity, severe noise, and texture loss. Events offer motion cues and high DR, but existing methods underutilize their noise characteristics; need robust dual-modal denoising and reconstruction.
AI.method: A diffusion-based framework that leverages RAW linear light-response and event brightness-change to enforce a physics-driven constraint for denoising; dynamically estimates per-modality SNR to guide adaptive feature fusion during diffusion; includes a RAW-event denoising and reconstruction pipeline and a real-world-low-light RAW–event dataset (REAL).
AI.result: Extensive experiments show NEC-Diff achieves superior reconstruction under extreme darkness; code andREAL dataset are publicly available at the given GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: NEC-Diff provides a high-fidelity solution for photon-starved imaging by robustly denoising and fusing RAW and event data within a diffusion framework; REAL enables comprehensive evaluation in ultra-low-light conditions.

title: Diffusion-Based Makeup Transfer with Facial Region-Aware Makeup Features
summary: Current diffusion-based makeup transfer methods commonly use the makeup information encoded by off-the-shelf foundation models (e.g., CLIP) as condition to preserve the makeup style of reference image in the generation. Although effective, these works mainly have two limitations: (1) foundation models pre-trained for generic tasks struggle to capture makeup styles; (2) the makeup features of reference image are injected to the diffusion denoising model as a whole for global makeup transfer, overlooking the facial region-aware makeup features (i.e., eyes, mouth, etc) and limiting the regional controllability for region-specific makeup transfer. To address these, in this work, we propose Facial Region-Aware Makeup features (FRAM), which has two stages: (1) makeup CLIP fine-tuning; (2) identity and facial region-aware makeup injection. For makeup CLIP fine-tuning, unlike prior works using off-the-shelf CLIP, we synthesize annotated makeup style data using GPT-o3 and text-driven image editing model, and then use the data to train a makeup CLIP encoder through self-supervised and image-text contrastive learning. For identity and facial region-aware makeup injection, we construct before-and-after makeup image pairs from the edited images in stage 1 and then use them to learn to inject identity of source image and makeup of reference image to the diffusion denoising model for makeup transfer. Specifically, we use learnable tokens to query the makeup CLIP encoder to extract facial region-aware makeup features for makeup injection, which is learned via an attention loss to enable regional control. As for identity injection, we use a ControlNet Union to encode source image and its 3D mesh simultaneously. The experimental results verify the superiority of our regional controllability and our makeup transfer performance.

AI.tldr: Introduces Facial Region-Aware Makeup features (FRAM) to enhance region-specific makeup transfer in diffusion models via a two-stage approach: (1) makeup CLIP fine-tuning with GPT-3-synthesized data and self-supervised + image-text contrastive learning; (2) identity and facial region-aware makeup injection using learnable tokens querying a makeup CLIP encoder with an attention loss, and ControlNet Union for identity with 3D mesh. Demonstrates improved regional controllability and transfer quality.
AI.motivation: Current diffusion-based makeup transfer methods rely on generic foundation models (e.g., CLIP) that inadequately capture makeup styles and perform global transfer, limiting region-specific control. There is a need for region-aware features and controlled identity-preserving transfer to improve realism and controllability.
AI.method: Two-stage FRAM framework: Stage 1 makeup CLIP fine-tuning by synthesizing annotated makeup style data with GPT-3 and image-editing models, then training a makeup CLIP encoder via self-supervised and image-text contrastive learning. Stage 2 identity and facial region-aware injection: create before-after pairs from stage-1 edits to learn to inject source identity and reference makeup into the diffusion denoising model; use learnable tokens to query the makeup CLIP encoder to extract region-aware makeup features with an attention-loss to enable regional control; identity injection via a ControlNet Union encoding the source image and its 3D mesh.
AI.result: Experiments show superiority in regional controllability and makeup transfer performance, validating the effectiveness of FRAM.
AI.conclusion: FRAM enables region-aware makeup transfer with improved controllability and performance, addressing limitations of prior diffusion-based methods by integrating region-specific makeup features and identity-aware guidance.

title: CFCML: A Coarse-to-Fine Crossmodal Learning Framework For Disease Diagnosis Using Multimodal Images and Tabular Data
summary: In clinical practice, crossmodal information including medical images and tabular data is essential for disease diagnosis. There exists a significant modality gap between these data types, which obstructs advancements in crossmodal diagnostic accuracy. Most existing crossmodal learning (CML) methods primarily focus on exploring relationships among high-level encoder outputs, leading to the neglect of local information in images. Additionally, these methods often overlook the extraction of task-relevant information. In this paper, we propose a novel coarse-to-fine crossmodal learning (CFCML) framework to progressively reduce the modality gap between multimodal images and tabular data, by thoroughly exploring inter-modal relationships. At the coarse stage, we explore the relationships between multi-granularity features from various image encoder stages and tabular information, facilitating a preliminary reduction of the modality gap. At the fine stage, we generate unimodal and crossmodal prototypes that incorporate class-aware information, and establish hierarchical anchor-based relationship mining (HRM) strategy to further diminish the modality gap and extract discriminative crossmodal information. This strategy utilize modality samples, unimodal prototypes, and crossmodal prototypes as anchors to develop contrastive learning approaches, effectively enhancing inter-class disparity while reducing intra-class disparity from multiple perspectives. Experimental results indicate that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods, achieving improvements of 1.53% and 0.91% in AUC metrics on the MEN and Derm7pt datasets, respectively. The code is available at https://github.com/IsDling/CFCML.

AI.tldr: A crossmodal learning method (CFCML) bridges the gap between multimodal medical images and tabular data using a coarse-to-fine framework with hierarchical anchor-based contrastive learning, improving AUC on two medical datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the modality gap between images and tabular data in clinical diagnosis, and to overcome limitations of prior CML methods that emphasize high-level representations and neglect local and task-relevant information.
AI.method: Coarse stage: fuse multi-granularity image encoder features with tabular data to reduce modality gap. Fine stage: create unimodal and crossmodal class-aware prototypes and apply hierarchical anchor-based relationship mining (HRM) using modality samples and prototypes as anchors to drive contrastive learning.
AI.result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art gains, with AUC improvements of 1.53% on MEN and 0.91% on Derm7pt; code released at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: CFCML effectively narrows the modality gap between medical images and tabular data and yields more discriminative crossmodal representations, leading to improved diagnostic performance.

title: Preference-Guided Debiasing for No-Reference Enhancement Image Quality Assessment
summary: Current no-reference image quality assessment (NR-IQA) models for enhanced images often struggle to generalize, as they tend to overfit to the distinct patterns of specific enhancement algorithms rather than evaluating genuine perceptual quality. To address this issue, we propose a preference-guided debiasing framework for no-reference enhancement image quality assessment (EIQA). Specifically, we first learn a continuous enhancement-preference embedding space using supervised contrastive learning, where images generated by similar enhancement styles are encouraged to have closer representations. Based on this, we further estimate the enhancement-induced nuisance component contained in the raw quality representation and remove it before quality regression. In this way, the model is guided to focus on algorithm-invariant perceptual quality cues instead of enhancement-specific visual fingerprints. To facilitate stable optimization, we adopt a two-stage training strategy that first learns the enhancement-preference space and then performs debiased quality prediction. Extensive experiments on public EIQA benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed method effectively mitigates algorithm-induced representation bias and achieves superior robustness and cross-algorithm generalization compared with existing approaches.

AI.tldr: A two-stage, preference-guided debiasing framework for no-reference enhancement IQA (EIQA) that learns enhancement-style embeddings via supervised contrastive learning and debiases the quality representation to improve cross-algorithm generalization.
AI.motivation: NR-IQA models for enhanced images overfit to artifacts of specific enhancement algorithms, limiting generalization to unseen methods and reducing perceptual quality assessment reliability.
AI.method: Develop a preference-guided debiasing framework. Train a continuous enhancement-preference embedding space using supervised contrastive learning so images from similar enhancement styles cluster together. Estimate and remove the enhancement-induced nuisance component from the raw quality representation before regression. Use a two-stage training strategy: (1) learn the enhancement-preference space; (2) learn debiased quality prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on public EIQA benchmarks show reduced algorithm-induced representation bias and improved robustness and cross-algorithm generalization compared with existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively mitigates enhancement-specific biases in EIQA, yielding more robust, algorithm-invariant perceptual quality assessment.

title: Generalizable NGP-SR: Generalizable Neural Radiance Fields Super-Resolution via Neural Graph Primitives
summary: Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) achieve photorealistic novel view synthesis but become costly when high-resolution (HR) rendering is required, as HR outputs demand dense sampling and higher-capacity models. Moreover, naively super-resolving per-view renderings in 2D often breaks multi-view consistency. We propose Generalizable NGP-SR, a 3D-aware super-resolution framework that reconstructs an HR radiance field directly from low-resolution (LR) posed images. Built on Neural Graphics Primitives (NGP), NGP-SR conditions radiance prediction on 3D coordinates and learned local texture tokens, enabling recovery of high-frequency details within the radiance field and producing view-consistent HR novel views without external HR references or post-hoc 2D upsampling. Importantly, our model is generalizable: once trained, it can be applied to unseen scenes and rendered from novel viewpoints without per-scene optimization. Experiments on multiple datasets show that NGP-SR consistently improves both reconstruction quality and runtime efficiency over prior NeRF-based super-resolution methods, offering a practical solution for scalable high-resolution novel view synthesis.

AI.tldr: NGP-SR is a generalizable, 3D-aware super-resolution framework that reconstructs high-resolution radiance fields from low-resolution, posed images using NGP, yielding view-consistent HR novel views without per-scene optimization and with improved efficiency over earlier NeRF-based SR methods.
AI.motivation: High-resolution NeRF rendering is computationally expensive due to dense sampling and larger models; naive 2D super-resolution can break multi-view consistency; need a generalizable solution that scales to unseen scenes without per-scene optimization.
AI.method: Extend Neural Graphics Primitives (NGP) by conditioning radiance predictions on 3D coordinates and learned local texture tokens to reconstruct an HR radiance field directly from LR inputs; no external HR references or post-hoc upsampling; generalizes to unseen scenes; 3D-aware SR integrated into the radiance field.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple datasets shows consistent improvements in reconstruction quality and runtime efficiency over prior NeRF-based SR methods; HR views are produced with maintained multi-view consistency.
AI.conclusion: NGP-SR offers a practical, scalable solution for high-resolution neural radiance field rendering that generalizes across scenes and eliminates the need for per-scene optimization or external HR data.

title: Synergistic Perception and Generative Recomposition: A Multi-Agent Orchestration for Expert-Level Building Inspection
summary: Building facade defect inspection is fundamental to structural health monitoring and sustainable urban maintenance, yet it remains a formidable challenge due to extreme geometric variability, low contrast against complex backgrounds, and the inherent complexity of composite defects (e.g., cracks co-occurring with spalling). Such characteristics lead to severe pixel imbalance and feature ambiguity, which, coupled with the critical scarcity of high-quality pixel-level annotations, hinder the generalization of existing detection and segmentation models. To address gaps, we propose \textit{FacadeFixer}, a unified multi-agent framework that treats defect perception as a collaborative reasoning task rather than isolated recognition. Specifically,\textit{FacadeFixer} orchestrates specialized agents for detection and segmentation to handle multi-type defect interference, working in tandem with a generative agent to enable semantic recomposition. This process decouples intricate defects from noisy backgrounds and realistically synthesizes them onto diverse clean textures, generating high-fidelity augmented data with precise expert-level masks. To support this, we introduce a comprehensive multi-task dataset covering six primary facade categories with pixel-level annotations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \textit{FacadeFixer} significantly outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) baselines. Specifically, it excels in capturing pixel-level structural anomalies and highlights generative synthesis as a robust solution to data scarcity in infrastructure inspection. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: A unified multi-agent framework (FacadeFixer) for facade defect perception, combining detection, segmentation, and generative recomposition to combat data scarcity and pixel imbalance; introduces a six-category pixel-level annotated dataset; shows SOTA improvements and robust data augmentation for infrastructure inspection.
AI.motivation: Overcome extreme geometric variability, low contrast against complex backgrounds, and the scarcity of high-quality pixel-level annotations in façade defect detection, to improve generalization and reliability of detection/segmentation models.
AI.method: A three-agent system where specialized detection and segmentation agents address multi-type defect interference, coordinated with a generative agent that semantically recomposes defects onto clean textures to synthesize high-fidelity, mask-precise augmented data. Accompanied by a comprehensive multi-task dataset with pixel-level annotations across six façade categories.
AI.result: Experimental results show FacadeFixer significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with strong performance on pixel-level structural anomaly detection and demonstrating that generative synthesis is an effective solution to data scarcity in infrastructure inspection.
AI.conclusion: FacadeFixer offers a strong, data-efficient approach for facade defect perception, aided by a publicly available codebase and dataset; the work highlights the value of multi-agent collaboration and synthetic data for robust infrastructure inspection.

title: Can Large Multimodal Models Inspect Buildings? A Hierarchical Benchmark for Structural Pathology Reasoning
summary: Automated building facade inspection is a critical component of urban resilience and smart city maintenance. Traditionally, this field has relied on specialized discriminative models (e.g., YOLO, Mask R-CNN) that excel at pixel-level localization but are constrained to passive perception and worse generization without the visual understandng to interpret structural topology. Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) promise a paradigm shift toward active reasoning, yet their application in such high-stakes engineering domains lacks rigorous evaluation standards. To bridge this gap, we introduce a human-in-the-loop semi-automated annotation framework, leveraging expert-proposal verification to unify 12 fragmented datasets into a standardized, hierarchical ontology. Building on this foundation, we present \textit{DefectBench}, the first multi-dimensional benchmark designed to interrogate LMMs beyond basic semantic recognition. \textit{DefectBench} evaluates 18 state-of-the-art (SOTA) LMMs across three escalating cognitive dimensions: Semantic Perception, Spatial Localization, and Generative Geometry Segmentation. Extensive experiments reveal that while current LMMs demonstrate exceptional topological awareness and semantic understanding (effectively diagnosing "what" and "how"), they exhibit significant deficiencies in metric localization precision ("where"). Crucially, however, we validate the viability of zero-shot generative segmentation, showing that general-purpose foundation models can rival specialized supervised networks without domain-specific training. This work provides both a rigorous benchmarking standard and a high-quality open-source database, establishing a new baseline for the advancement of autonomous AI agents in civil engineering.

AI.tldr: Introduces DefectBench, a multi-dimensional benchmark and a human-in-the-loop annotation framework to unify 12 facade datasets into a hierarchical ontology, evaluating 18 LMMs across Semantic Perception, Spatial Localization, and Generative Geometry Segmentation. Findings: LMMs excel in semantic/topological understanding but lag in precise localization; zero-shot generative segmentation can rival supervised models; provides open-source data and baseline for autonomous civil-engineering AI.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of rigorous, domain-specific evaluation standards for large multimodal models (LMMs) in high-stakes engineering tasks like automated facade inspection; bridge the gap between semantic understanding and actionable spatial reasoning within a standardized ontology.
AI.method: Develop a human-in-the-loop semi-automated annotation framework with expert verification to unify 12 fragmented datasets into a standardized hierarchical ontology. Build DefectBench to assess 18 state-of-the-art LMMs along three cognitive dimensions. Conduct extensive experiments including zero-shot generative segmentation to compare general-purpose foundation models against domain-specific supervised networks. Provide an open-source database.
AI.result: Demonstrates that LMMs have strong semantic perception and topology awareness (understanding what and how) but struggle with metric localization (where). Zero-shot generative segmentation shows competitive performance against specialized models without domain training. DefectBench establishes a new benchmarking standard and a high-quality open dataset for autonomous civil-engineering AI agents.
AI.conclusion: DefectBench offers a rigorous, multi-dimensional evaluation framework and data resource that sets a baseline for future research in autonomous civil-engineering AI, highlighting the need to improve localization precision while leveraging powerful semantic and generative capabilities.

title: EgoForge: Goal-Directed Egocentric World Simulator
summary: Generative world models have shown promise for simulating dynamic environments, yet egocentric video remains challenging due to rapid viewpoint changes, frequent hand-object interactions, and goal-directed procedures whose evolution depends on latent human intent. Existing approaches either focus on hand-centric instructional synthesis with limited scene evolution, perform static view translation without modeling action dynamics, or rely on dense supervision, such as camera trajectories, long video prefixes, synchronized multicamera capture, etc. In this work, we introduce EgoForge, an egocentric goal-directed world simulator that generates coherent, first-person video rollouts from minimal static inputs: a single egocentric image, a high-level instruction, and an optional auxiliary exocentric view. To improve intent alignment and temporal consistency, we propose VideoDiffusionNFT, a trajectory-level reward-guided refinement that optimizes goal completion, temporal causality, scene consistency, and perceptual fidelity during diffusion sampling. Extensive experiments show EgoForge achieves consistent gains in semantic alignment, geometric stability, and motion fidelity over strong baselines, and robust performance in real-world smart-glasses experiments.

AI.tldr: EgoForge is an egocentric goal-directed world simulator that generates coherent first-person video rollouts from minimal inputs (a single egocentric image, a high-level instruction, and an optional exocentric view), augmented by VideoDiffusionNFT, a trajectory-level reward-guided refinement that enforces goal completion, temporal causality, scene consistency, and perceptual fidelity during diffusion.
AI.motivation: Modeling egocentric video is challenging due to rapid viewpoint changes, frequent hand-object interactions, and goal-directed procedures driven by latent human intent; existing methods rely on hand-centric synthesis with limited scene evolution, static view translation, or dense supervisory data.
AI.method: Introduce EgoForge for generating egocentric video from minimal signals, and VideoDiffusionNFT for trajectory-level refinement during diffusion sampling. EgoForge takes a single egocentric image, a high-level instruction, and an optional auxiliary exocentric view to produce coherent rollouts. VideoDiffusionNFT optimizes over trajectories with rewards for goal completion, temporal causality, scene consistency, and perceptual fidelity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows EgoForge yields consistent gains in semantic alignment, geometric stability, and motion fidelity compared to strong baselines, with robust performance demonstrated in real-world smart-glasses experiments.
AI.conclusion: EgoForge, combined with VideoDiffusionNFT, advances the generation of coherent first-person video from minimal inputs by improving intent alignment and temporal consistency, enabling more practical egocentric simulation and planning.

title: TinyML Enhances CubeSat Mission Capabilities
comment: Accepted at the 17th ACM/IEEE International Conference on Cyber-Physical Systems (ICCPS) 2026
summary: Earth observation (EO) missions traditionally rely on transmitting raw or minimally processed imagery from satellites to ground stations for computationally intensive analysis. This paradigm is infeasible for CubeSat systems due to stringent constraints on the onboard embedded processors, energy availability, and communication bandwidth. To overcome these limitations, the paper presents a TinyML-based Convolutional Neural Networks (ConvNets) model optimization and deployment pipeline for onboard image classification, enabling accurate, energy-efficient, and hardware-aware inference under CubeSat-class constraints. Our pipeline integrates structured iterative pruning, post-training INT8 quantization, and hardware-aware operator mapping to compress models and align them with the heterogeneous compute architecture of the STM32N6 microcontroller from STMicroelectronics. This Microcontroller Unit (MCU) integrates a novel Arm Cortex-M55 core and a Neural-ART Neural Processing Unit (NPU), providing a realistic proxy for CubeSat onboard computers. The paper evaluates the proposed approach on three EO benchmark datasets (i.e., EuroSAT, RS_C11, MEDIC) and four models (i.e., SqueezeNet, MobileNetV3, EfficientNet, MCUNetV1). We demonstrate an average reduction in RAM usage of 89.55% and Flash memory of 70.09% for the optimized models, significantly decreasing downlink bandwidth requirements while maintaining task-acceptable accuracy (with a drop ranging from 0.4 to 8.6 percentage points compared to the Float32 baseline). The energy consumption per inference ranges from 0.68 mJ to 6.45 mJ, with latency spanning from 3.22 ms to 30.38 ms. These results fully satisfy the stringent energy budgets and real-time constraints required for efficient onboard EO processing.

AI.tldr: TinyML optimization pipeline for onboard EO classification on STM32N6 MCU enables significant memory and energy reductions with modest accuracy loss; validated on EuroSAT, RS_C11, MEDIC with SqueezeNet, MobileNetV3, EfficientNet, MCUNetV1.
AI.motivation: Enable CubeSat-class onboard processing by overcoming data transmission and compute constraints; reduce bandwidth and energy use while maintaining real-time inference.
AI.method: Structured iterative pruning, post-training INT8 quantization, and hardware-aware operator mapping deployed on STM32N6 (Cortex-M55 + Neural-ART NPU); evaluated on three EO benchmarks (EuroSAT, RS_C11, MEDIC) with four models (SqueezeNet, MobileNetV3, EfficientNet, MCUNetV1).
AI.result: Average RAM reduction 89.55% and Flash reduction 70.09%; accuracy drop 0.4–8.6 percentage points from Float32 baseline; energy per inference 0.68–6.45 mJ; latency 3.22–30.38 ms.
AI.conclusion: The pipeline demonstrates feasible, energy-efficient, hardware-aware onboard CNN inference for CubeSat-class EO tasks, significantly reducing memory and bandwidth needs while maintaining acceptable performance; the MCU+NPU setup provides a realistic proxy for onboard compute.

title: LagerNVS: Latent Geometry for Fully Neural Real-time Novel View Synthesis
comment: IEEE CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition 2026. Project page with code, models and examples: szymanowiczs.github.io/lagernvs
summary: Recent work has shown that neural networks can perform 3D tasks such as Novel View Synthesis (NVS) without explicit 3D reconstruction. Even so, we argue that strong 3D inductive biases are still helpful in the design of such networks. We show this point by introducing LagerNVS, an encoder-decoder neural network for NVS that builds on `3D-aware' latent features. The encoder is initialized from a 3D reconstruction network pre-trained using explicit 3D supervision. This is paired with a lightweight decoder, and trained end-to-end with photometric losses. LagerNVS achieves state-of-the-art deterministic feed-forward Novel View Synthesis (including 31.4 PSNR on Re10k), with and without known cameras, renders in real time, generalizes to in-the-wild data, and can be paired with a diffusion decoder for generative extrapolation.

AI.tldr: LagerNVS is a 3D-aware encoder–decoder for neural view synthesis that uses a 3D-pretrained encoder and a lightweight decoder to achieve state-of-the-art deterministic NVS with real-time rendering and strong generalization, and can be extended with a diffusion-based generator.
AI.motivation: Although recent NVS works can eschew explicit 3D representations, explicit 3D inductive biases and supervision can improve performance, generalization, and realism in novel-view synthesis.
AI.method: Encoder initialized from a 3D reconstruction network pre-trained with explicit 3D supervision; a lightweight decoder; end-to-end training with photometric losses; accommodates known/unknown camera setups; optionally paired with a diffusion decoder for generative extrapolation.
AI.result: State-of-the-art deterministic feed-forward NVS; 31.4 PSNR on Re10k; real-time rendering; generalizes to in-the-wild data; works with known cameras; diffusion-based generative extrapolation possible.
AI.conclusion: 3D-aware latent features combined with explicit 3D supervision yield strong NVS performance, highlighting the value of 3D inductive biases in neural rendering and enabling scalable, real-time inference with potential for generative extensions.

title: Improving Image-to-Image Translation via a Rectified Flow Reformulation
summary: In this work, we propose Image-to-Image Rectified Flow Reformulation (I2I-RFR), a practical plug-in reformulation that recasts standard I2I regression networks as continuous-time transport models. While pixel-wise I2I regression is simple, stable, and easy to adapt across tasks, it often over-smooths ill-posed and multimodal targets, whereas generative alternatives often require additional components, task-specific tuning, and more complex training and inference pipelines. Our method augments the backbone input by channel-wise concatenation with a noise-corrupted version of the ground-truth target and optimizes a simple t-reweighted pixel loss. This objective admits a rectified-flow interpretation via an induced velocity field, enabling ODE-based progressive refinement at inference time while largely preserving the standard supervised training pipeline. In most cases, adopting I2I-RFR requires only expanding the input channels, and inference can be performed with a few explicit solver steps (e.g., 3 steps) without distillation. Extensive experiments across multiple image-to-image translation and video restoration tasks show that I2I-RFR generally improves performance across a wide range of tasks and backbones, with particularly clear gains in perceptual quality and detail preservation. Overall, I2I-RFR provides a lightweight way to incorporate continuous-time refinement into conventional I2I models without requiring a heavy generative pipeline.

AI.tldr: A plug-in reformulation (I2I-RFR) converts standard image-to-image regression networks into continuous-time rectified-flow models by augmenting inputs with a noise-corrupted ground-truth target and training with a t-reweighted pixel loss, enabling ODE-based progressive refinement during inference with only a few solver steps, yielding improved perceptual quality and detail without heavy generative pipelines.
AI.motivation: Pixel-wise I2I regression is stable but often over-smooths ill-posed/multimodal targets; generative alternatives add complexity and task-specific tuning. The work seeks a lightweight, plug-in method to introduce continuous-time refinement into existing I2I models.
AI.method: Augment backbone input by channel-wise concatenation with a noise-corrupted version of the ground-truth target; optimize a t-reweighted pixel loss. This induces a velocity field corresponding to a rectified-flow, enabling ODE-based progressive refinement at inference. Requires only expanding input channels; few explicit solver steps (e.g., 3) for inference; no distillation. Works across various backbones and tasks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across image-to-image translation and video restoration show general performance improvements, particularly in perceptual quality and detail preservation, across multiple tasks and backbones; the method is lightweight and avoids heavy generative pipelines while providing continuous-time refinement.
AI.conclusion: I2I-RFR offers a practical, lightweight way to incorporate continuous-time refinement into conventional I2I models, improving perceptual quality and detail without requiring a heavy generative pipeline or specialized training.

title: MuSteerNet: Human Reaction Generation from Videos via Observation-Reaction Mutual Steering
summary: Video-driven human reaction generation aims to synthesize 3D human motions that directly react to observed video sequences, which is crucial for building human-like interactive AI systems. However, existing methods often fail to effectively leverage video inputs to steer human reaction synthesis, resulting in reaction motions that are mismatched with the content of video sequences. We reveal that this limitation arises from a severe relational distortion between visual observations and reaction types. In light of this, we propose MuSteerNet, a simple yet effective framework that generates 3D human reactions from videos via observation-reaction mutual steering. Specifically, we first propose a Prototype Feedback Steering mechanism to mitigate relational distortion by refining visual observations with a gated delta-rectification modulator and a relational margin constraint, guided by prototypical vectors learned from human reactions. We then introduce Dual-Coupled Reaction Refinement that fully leverages rectified visual cues to further steer the refinement of generated reaction motions, thereby effectively improving reaction quality and enabling MuSteerNet to achieve competitive performance. Extensive experiments and ablation studies validate the effectiveness of our method. Code coming soon: https://github.com/zhouyuan888888/MuSteerNet.

AI.tldr: MuSteerNet introduces observation-reaction mutual steering for video-driven 3D human reaction generation, addressing relational distortion between visual inputs and reaction types; it uses Prototype Feedback Steering and Dual-Coupled Reaction Refinement to improve reaction quality, achieving competitive results with extensive ablations and forthcoming code.
AI.motivation: Existing video-driven reaction generation methods underutilize video cues and suffer from relational distortion between observed visuals and target reaction types, leading to mismatched motions.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) Prototype Feedback Steering refines visual observations via a gated delta-rectification modulator and a relational margin constraint guided by prototypical reaction vectors; (2) Dual-Coupled Reaction Refinement uses rectified visual cues to further steer refinement of generated reaction motions, enabling mutual steering between observations and reactions.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and ablation studies validate the effectiveness of MuSteerNet, showing competitive performance on benchmarks; code release is planned.
AI.conclusion: MuSteerNet offers a simple yet effective solution to mitigate relational distortion in video-driven reaction generation by enabling observation-reaction mutual steering through prototype-guided observation refinement and coupled motion refinement.

title: Wildfire Spread Scenarios: Increasing Sample Diversity of Segmentation Diffusion Models with Training-Free Methods
comment: Accepted at NLDL 2026. This version contains small corrections compared to the initial publication, see appendix for details
summary:
Predicting future states in uncertain environments, such as wildfire spread, medical diagnosis, or autonomous driving, requires models that can consider multiple plausible outcomes. While diffusion models can effectively learn such multi-modal distributions, naively sampling from these models is computationally inefficient, potentially requiring hundreds of samples to find low-probability modes that may still be operationally relevant. In this work, we address the challenge of sample-efficient ambiguous segmentation by evaluating several training-free sampling methods that encourage diverse predictions. We adapt two techniques, particle guidance and SPELL, originally designed for the generation of diverse natural images, to discrete segmentation tasks, and additionally propose a simple clustering-based technique. We validate these approaches on the LIDC medical dataset, a modified version of the Cityscapes dataset, and MMFire, a new simulation-based wildfire spread dataset introduced in this paper. Compared to naive sampling, these approaches increase the HM IoU* metric by up to 7.5% on MMFire and 16.4% on Cityscapes, demonstrating that training-free methods can be used to efficiently increase the sample diversity of segmentation diffusion models with little cost to image quality and runtime.
  Code and dataset: https://github.com/SebastianGer/wildfire-spread-scenarios

AI.tldr: Training-free sampling methods (particle guidance, SPELL, clustering) adapt diffusion models for discrete segmentation to boost multi-modal diversity; evaluated on LIDC, a Cityscapes variant, and the MMFire wildfire dataset; achieved notable gains with modest cost.
AI.motivation: Need for efficient, diverse multi-modal segmentation in uncertain environments. Diffusion models capture multiple plausible outcomes, but naive sampling is computationally expensive; improving sample efficiency is crucial for practical deployment in medical, urban, and environmental contexts.
AI.method: Adapt two diffusion-based diversity techniques (particle guidance, SPELL) to discrete segmentation tasks and introduce a simple clustering-based sampling method. Evaluate in a training-free setting on three datasets (LIDC, Cityscapes variant, MMFire). Compare to naive sampling and analyze trade-offs in diversity, image quality, and runtime.
AI.result: Training-free methods yield significant diversity gains: up to 16.4% HM IoU* on the Cityscapes variant and 7.5% on MMFire, with little degradation to image quality and minimal runtime overhead.
AI.conclusion: Training-free sampling strategies effectively increase the diversity of segmentation diffusion models, enabling more reliable multi-modal predictions in critical tasks without retraining.

title: CoVR-R:Reason-Aware Composed Video Retrieval
summary: Composed Video Retrieval (CoVR) aims to find a target video given a reference video and a textual modification. Prior work assumes the modification text fully specifies the visual changes, overlooking after-effects and implicit consequences (e.g., motion, state transitions, viewpoint or duration cues) that emerge from the edit. We argue that successful CoVR requires reasoning about these after-effects. We introduce a reasoning-first, zero-shot approach that leverages large multimodal models to (i) infer causal and temporal consequences implied by the edit, and (ii) align the resulting reasoned queries to candidate videos without task-specific finetuning. To evaluate reasoning in CoVR, we also propose CoVR-Reason, a benchmark that pairs each (reference, edit, target) triplet with structured internal reasoning traces and challenging distractors that require predicting after-effects rather than keyword matching. Experiments show that our zero-shot method outperforms strong retrieval baselines on recall at K and particularly excels on implicit-effect subsets. Our automatic and human analysis confirm higher step consistency and effect factuality in our retrieved results. Our findings show that incorporating reasoning into general-purpose multimodal models enables effective CoVR by explicitly accounting for causal and temporal after-effects. This reduces dependence on task-specific supervision, improves generalization to challenging implicit-effect cases, and enhances interpretability of retrieval outcomes. These results point toward a scalable and principled framework for explainable video search. The model, code, and benchmark are available at https://github.com/mbzuai-oryx/CoVR-R.

AI.tldr: Zero-shot, reasoning-first CoVR that infers and uses implicit after-effects of edits; introduces CoVR-Reason benchmark with reasoning traces; achieves better recall, esp. on implicit effects; enables explainable video search; code/benchmark released.
AI.motivation: Current CoVR methods rely on modification text that may not specify after-effects (motion, timing, viewpoint, duration). To robustly retrieve target videos, models must infer causal/temporal consequences of edits.
AI.method: A zero-shot approach using large multimodal models to (i) infer causal/temporal after-effects implied by the edit and (ii) generate reasoned queries that are aligned to candidate videos without task-specific finetuning. Evaluation includes CoVR-Reason benchmark with structured reasoning traces and distractors.
AI.result: Outperforms strong retrieval baselines on recall@K, with notable gains on implicit-effect subsets. Automatic and human analyses show higher step-consistency and effect factuality. Demonstrates improved generalization and interpretability; supports a scalable framework for explainable video search.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating reasoning into general-purpose multimodal models enables effective CoVR by accounting for causal/temporal after-effects, reducing supervision, improving generalization, and enhancing interpretability. Provides a principled, scalable approach for explainable video search; code and benchmark available.

title: Deterministic Mode Proposals: An Efficient Alternative to Generative Sampling for Ambiguous Segmentation
summary: Many segmentation tasks, such as medical image segmentation or future state prediction, are inherently ambiguous, meaning that multiple predictions are equally correct. Current methods typically rely on generative models to capture this uncertainty. However, identifying the underlying modes of the distribution with these methods is computationally expensive, requiring large numbers of samples and post-hoc clustering. In this paper, we shift the focus from stochastic sampling to the direct generation of likely outcomes. We introduce mode proposal models, a deterministic framework that efficiently produces a fixed-size set of proposal masks in a single forward pass. To handle superfluous proposals, we adapt a confidence mechanism, traditionally used in object detection, to the high-dimensional space of segmentation masks. Our approach significantly reduces inference time while achieving higher ground-truth coverage than existing generative models. Furthermore, we demonstrate that our model can be trained without knowing the full distribution of outcomes, making it applicable to real-world datasets. Finally, we show that by decomposing the velocity field of a pre-trained flow model, we can efficiently estimate prior mode probabilities for our proposals.

AI.tldr: Deterministic mode proposal models generate a fixed-size set of plausible segmentation masks in a single forward pass, enabling fast, coverage-rich multi-modal predictions without requiring full outcome distribution; priors for modes can be estimated via flow-model velocity decomposition.
AI.motivation: Segmentation tasks are inherently ambiguous; relying on stochastic generative sampling and post-hoc clustering is computationally expensive and slow to infer mode structure. A fast, direct method to produce diverse yet likely outcomes is desirable.
AI.method: Introduce mode proposal models that deterministically output a fixed number of proposal masks per input. Use a confidence mechanism, adapted from object detection, to suppress redundant proposals in high-dimensional segmentation masks. Train without requiring full knowledge of the outcome distribution. Decompose the velocity field of a pre-trained flow model to estimate prior mode probabilities for proposals.
AI.result: Significantly faster inference with a higher ground-truth coverage than existing generative models. Proposals remain diverse and representative of likely outcomes without exhaustive sampling or clustering. The approach is applicable to real-world datasets, even without complete distribution knowledge. A method to estimate mode priors via velocity-field decomposition is proposed.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic mode proposal generation with a confidence-based pruning strategy offers an efficient and effective alternative to stochastic sampling for multi-modal segmentation, enabling real-time or near-real-time performance while preserving high coverage of plausible outcomes; priors can be estimated from pre-trained flow models to further inform proposal selection.

title: MME-CoF-Pro: Evaluating Reasoning Coherence in Video Generative Models with Text and Visual Hints
summary: Video generative models show emerging reasoning behaviors. It is essential to ensure that generated events remain causally consistent across frames for reliable deployment, a property we define as reasoning coherence. To bridge the gap in literature for missing reasoning coherence evaluation, we propose MME-CoF-Pro, a comprehensive video reasoning benchmark to assess reasoning coherence in video models. Specifically, MME-CoF-Pro contains 303 samples across 16 categories, ranging from visual logical to scientific reasoning. It introduces Reasoning Score as evaluation metric for assessing process-level necessary intermediate reasoning steps, and includes three evaluation settings, (a) no hint (b) text hint and (c) visual hint, enabling a controlled investigation into the underlying mechanisms of reasoning hint guidance. Evaluation results in 7 open and closed-source video models reveals insights including: (1) Video generative models exhibit weak reasoning coherence, decoupled from generation quality. (2) Text hints boost apparent correctness but often cause inconsistency and hallucinated reasoning (3) Visual hints benefit structured perceptual tasks but struggle with fine-grained perception. Website: https://video-reasoning-coherence.github.io/

AI.tldr: Introduces MME-CoF-Pro, a video reasoning benchmark with a Reasoning Score to assess reasoning coherence across frames; reports that video generative models exhibit weak coherence, with hints (text/visual) having mixed effects.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of standardized evaluation for reasoning coherence in video generation and to understand how hints influence reasoning and coherence across frames.
AI.method: Dataset: 303 samples across 16 categories (visual logical to scientific reasoning). Three evaluation settings: no hint, text hint, visual hint. Introduces Reasoning Score to quantify process-level intermediate reasoning steps. Evaluated 7 open/closed-source video models.
AI.result: Findings: (1) Video generation models show weak reasoning coherence, decoupled from visual quality. (2) Text hints improve apparent correctness but induce inconsistency and hallucinated reasoning. (3) Visual hints aid structured perceptual tasks but hinder fine-grained perception.
AI.conclusion: MME-CoF-Pro provides a comprehensive framework to evaluate reasoning coherence in video models; hints influence evaluation and reveal underlying mechanisms; results motivate further work to improve coherent, causally consistent video generation. Website provided.
